topics.title: Concepts of Kinematics and Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Kinematics and Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition of the Domain

**Mechanics of Machinery** is the applied engineering science that mathematically models the relative motion between machine components and the forces responsible for, or resulting from, such motion. It is conventionally partitioned into two mutually exclusive but complementary sub-domains:

| Sub-Domain | Formal KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Kinematics of Machinery** | The branch of applied mechanics that studies the **geometry of relative motion** between machine parts (displacement, velocity, acceleration) **without reference to the forces** producing that motion. |
| **Dynamics of Machinery** | The branch of applied mechanics that studies the **forces and torques** acting on machine members and their **influence on motion**, including inertia effects, energy transfer, and balancing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBMET404, Module 1):** The first module lays the *conceptual foundation* — distinguishing a *machine*, a *mechanism*, and a *structure*; classifying kinematic links and pairs; computing the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** using Grübler's equation; and understanding the **Inversions of the Four-Bar Chain and Slider-Crank Mechanism**. These are recurring **2-mark and 14-mark** question territories in every KTU University Examination cycle.

### 1.2 Foundational Definitions (Memorise Verbatim for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> These three definitions are **high-frequency 3-mark short-answer questions** in KTU ESE. Learn the exact phrasing.

- **Machine:** A machine is a combination of **resistant bodies** (links) so arranged that by their **means** the **mechanical forces** at the **driving points** are **balanced** by the **forces of resistance** at the **working points**, and the **relative motions** of the parts are **completely determined** by the motions of the driving points. *(Shigley's / Kutzbach's classical definition)*

- **Mechanism:** A mechanism is a **kinematic chain** in which **one link is fixed** to the ground (frame), and the other links have **constrained relative motion** with respect to it. It transforms an **input motion** into a **desired output motion** but **cannot do useful work** on its own (no energy balance is implied).

- **Structure:** A structure is a **kinematic chain** in which **no link is fixed**, and the assembly can carry loads but **no relative motion** is possible between the links (DOF = 0 by design).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bicycle" 🚲

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine riding a bicycle.
>
> - **Kinematics** asks: *“How fast is the pedal moving? What is the angle of the crank? What is the linear velocity of the bicycle on the road?”* — pure motion, no mention of why.
> - **Dynamics** asks: *“How much torque must I apply on the pedal to climb a slope? What is the inertia force when I brake? What is the friction force at the tyre?”* — forces and energy.
>
> **Machine vs. Mechanism vs. Structure:** Take a *wall clock*:
> - The *gears and hands* form a **mechanism** (motion transmission only).
> - The *spring/motor + gears* together form a **machine** (input energy → useful work of moving hands against friction).
> - The *wall bracket holding the clock* is a **structure** (rigid, no relative motion).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Units Used Throughout

- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (used in dynamics problems)
- Angular velocity unit: $rad/s$ (always convert **rpm** to rad/s using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$)
- **1 revolution = $2\pi$ radians = $360°$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Mechanism — Links, Pairs, and Chains

A mechanism is built from a hierarchy of components:

$$\text{Link} \longrightarrow \text{Pair} \longrightarrow \text{Kinematic Chain} \longrightarrow \text{Mechanism}$$

#### 2.1.1 Kinematic Link

A **kinematic link** (or element) is a **resistant body** — a single rigid member (or an assembly of rigidly connected parts) — that participates in relative motion with another body. Links are classified by their **tying** (Rigid / Elastic / Fluid).

> [!NOTE]
> A connecting rod is a *rigid link*; a belt is a *flexible link*; the fluid in a hydraulic actuator is a *fluid link*.

#### 2.1.2 Kinematic Pair

A **kinematic pair** is the **jointed interface** between two links that permits a **constrained relative motion**. Classification is done on **three independent criteria**:

| Criterion | Class 1 | Class 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Contact** | **Lower pair** — surface contact (e.g., revolute joint, prismatic joint). Higher load capacity, less wear. | **Higher pair** — point/line contact (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh). Lower load capacity, more wear. |
| **Type of Closure (Relative Motion)** | **Self-closed (Form-closed)** — geometry maintains contact (e.g., nut–bolt). | **Force-closed (Positive-closed)** — external force (e.g., gravity, spring) maintains contact (e.g., cam-follower). |
| **Degrees of Freedom of Joint** | **1-DOF pairs** (revolute $\equiv$ R, prismatic $\equiv$ P) | **2-DOF pairs** (e.g., cam-follower pair, gear pair, rolling pair) |
| **Motion Type** | **Sliding**, **Rolling**, **Revolving**, **Screw**, **Spherical** | (Combinations like *helical* are screw pairs) |

#### 2.1.3 Kinematic Chain

A series of **kinematic pairs joined by links** is a **kinematic chain**. If **one link is fixed**, the chain becomes a **mechanism**.

### 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Mobility Analysis

The **mobility** or **degrees of freedom (DOF)**, denoted $F$, of a planar mechanism is the number of **independent input motions** required to completely determine the configuration of all its links.

#### 2.2.1 Kutzbach's (Grübler's) Equation for Planar Mechanisms

$$\boxed{\;F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2\;}$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including the fixed link/ground)
- $j_1$ = number of **lower pairs (1-DOF joints)** — typically revolute (R) and prismatic (P)
- $j_2$ = number of **higher pairs (2-DOF joints)** — typically cam, gear, rolling

For **spatial mechanisms** (3D), the Grübler–Kutzbach generalisation uses:
$$F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$
where $j_k$ = joints with $k$ DOF. *This is beyond Module 1 scope and is in Module 5.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor "3"?** In a plane, every **unconstrained rigid body** has 3 DOF: 2 translations ($x$, $y$) + 1 rotation ($\theta$). Each 1-DOF lower pair **removes 2 DOF** (constrains 2 directions); each 2-DOF higher pair **removes 1 DOF**. The $3(n-1)$ comes from the $n-1$ moving links (since the ground is fixed).

### 2.3 Inversions of a Four-Bar Chain

A **four-bar chain** (with $F = 1$) has **four different inversions** obtained by fixing each of the four links in turn. These are KTU **14-mark guaranteed** items:

| S.No. | Link Fixed | Inversion Name | Practical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shortest link** (adjacent to fixed) | **Crank-Rocker Mechanism** | Windshield wiper, hand-pump |
| 2 | Link **opposite to shortest** | **Double-Crank (Drag-link) Mechanism** | Coupling of parallel shafts |
| 3 | **Longest link** (adjacent to fixed) | **Double-Rocker (Oscillating Arm) Mechanism** | Stone-crusher, knitting machine |
| 4 | **Triangular link** (any) | — | (Trivial, motion becomes rigid) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grashof's Law:** For continuous rotation (full crank), the **sum of shortest and longest link lengths** must be **less than or equal to** the sum of the other two: $S + L \leq P + Q$.

### 2.4 Inversions of the Slider-Crank Chain

| S.No. | Link Fixed | Inversion Name | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Crank fixed | **Bull Engine** | — |
| 2 | Connecting rod fixed | **Oscillating Cylinder Engine** (Whitworth quick-return) | Shaper, slotter |
| 3 | Slider fixed | **Crank & Slotted Lever** (Crank-shaper) | Quick-return shaper |
| 4 | Frame (ground) | **Reciprocating Steam Engine / IC Engine** | Car engine piston |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grübler's DOF (planar) | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Grashof's inequality | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | for continuous rotation |
| 3 | Angular velocity from rpm | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Angular acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | rad/s² |
| 5 | Relative velocity of B w.r.t. A | $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$, with $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$ | m/s |
| 6 | Magnitude of $V_{BA}$ | $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega_{AB} \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | m/s |
| 7 | Relative acceleration of B w.r.t. A | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t}$ | m/s² |
| 8 | Normal component | $a_{BA}^{n} = \omega_{AB}^{2} \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | directed from B → A |
| 9 | Tangential component | $a_{BA}^{t} = \alpha_{AB} \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | perpendicular to AB |
| 10 | Coriolis acceleration | $\vec{a}_{C} = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_{r}$ | m/s², only when one link rotates and another slides on it |
| 11 | Kennedy's Theorem | Three I-centres of any 3 rigid bodies in plane motion are **collinear**. | — |
| 12 | Number of instantaneous centres | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | for $n$ links |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Grübler's Equation (Planar, 2-D Plane Motion)

> **Goal:** Show mathematically why $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.

**Step 1 — Total degrees of freedom of the unconstrained system.**

In a plane, every free rigid body has **3 DOF** (translation along $x$, translation along $y$, rotation in the plane). If we have $n$ links *before* fixing the ground, total DOF = $3n$.

**Step 2 — Remove the ground's constraint.**

One link (the ground/frame) is **fixed** to the earth. Fixation removes all 3 DOF of that link, so the $n-1$ **moving** links together possess:

$$\text{Total DOF of moving bodies} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract DOF removed by the kinematic pairs.**

- Each **1-DOF lower pair** (revolute or prismatic) **constrains 2 motions** in the plane (it allows 1 motion and removes the other 2). Therefore, $j_1$ lower pairs remove $2 j_1$ DOF.
- Each **2-DOF higher pair** (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh, rolling pair) **constrains 1 motion** (e.g., removes the normal-approach motion but allows sliding and rotation). Therefore, $j_2$ higher pairs remove $j_2$ DOF.

$$\boxed{\;F = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **rigorous KTU-expected derivation** of Grübler's equation. You may be asked to derive it for a 5-mark or 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — DOF of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

A slider-crank has 4 links: **crank (2)**, **connecting rod (3)**, **slider (4)**, **ground (1)**.
Pairs: 3 revolute (between 1–2, 2–3, 3–4) and 1 prismatic (between 4–1). All are lower pairs (1-DOF).

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \\
j_1 &= 4 \quad (\text{3 R-joints} + \text{1 P-joint, all lower}) \\
j_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ One input motion (typically the crank rotation) drives the whole mechanism. ✔

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — DOF of a Four-Bar Chain

Four links ($n = 4$), four revolute lower pairs ($j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$).

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
&= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

A single input crank rotation produces a fully determined output. ✔

### 3.4 Worked Derivation — Relative Velocity Method

**Problem:** In a four-bar chain, link $AB$ (crank) rotates at $\omega_2$. Find the angular velocity $\omega_4$ of link $CD$ and the velocity of point $E$ on link $BC$.

**Step 1 — Identify links and pivot points.**

- Link 2 = AB (crank, length $L_2$, angular velocity $\omega_2$)
- Link 3 = BC (coupler, length $L_3$)
- Link 4 = CD (rocker, length $L_4$, unknown $\omega_4$)

**Step 2 — Write velocity of point B (on rotating crank).**

Velocity of B is purely tangential to the crank circle:

$$\vec{V}_B = \omega_2 \cdot L_2, \quad \text{directed} \perp AB$$

**Step 3 — Express velocity of C in two ways.**

Velocity of C can be written:
- from the rocker: $\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}$, where $\vec{V}_{CB} \perp BC$ and $\vert V_{CB} \vert = \omega_3 \cdot L_3$.
- from the rocker pivot: $\vec{V}_C \perp CD$ and $\vert V_C \vert = \omega_4 \cdot L_4$.

**Step 4 — Construct the velocity polygon (vector triangle).**

The closed loop is:
$$\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}$$

Drawing this as a triangle:
- One side = $\vec{V}_B$ (known magnitude and direction).
- Second side = $\vec{V}_{CB}$ (known direction $\perp BC$, unknown magnitude).
- Third side = $\vec{V}_C$ (known direction $\perp CD$, unknown magnitude).

Solve the triangle using the **law of sines** (or scale drawing):

$$\frac{V_B}{\sin \angle C} = \frac{V_{CB}}{\sin \angle B} = \frac{V_C}{\sin \angle A'}$$

**Step 5 — Compute angular velocity of the rocker.**

$$\omega_4 = \frac{\vert V_C \vert}{L_4} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_3 = \frac{\vert V_{CB} \vert}{L_3}$$

### 3.5 Worked Derivation — Relative Acceleration with Coriolis Component

**Setup:** Slider 4 slides on the rotating link 3. Crank 2 rotates at $\omega_2$ rad/s and angular acceleration $\alpha_2$ rad/s². Slider velocity relative to link 3 is $v_{r}$.

**Step 1 — Write the relative acceleration equation for the slider (point on link 4).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{a}_D &= \vec{a}_C + \vec{a}_{DC}^{n} + \vec{a}_{DC}^{t} + \vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $C$ is the point on the rotating link 3 coincident with slider 4.

**Step 2 — Expand each term.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vec{a}_C &= \alpha_2 \cdot L_2 \, \hat{t}_2 \; + \; \omega_2^2 \cdot L_2 \, \hat{n}_2 & \text{(acceleration of point C on crank)} \\
\vec{a}_{DC}^{n} &= \omega_3^2 \cdot L_3 \quad \text{(directed from D → C)} \\
\vec{a}_{DC}^{t} &= \alpha_3 \cdot L_3 \quad \text{(perpendicular to DC)} \\
\vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}} &= 2 \, \omega_3 \cdot v_r \quad \text{(perpendicular to } v_r, \text{ in the sense of } \omega_3 \text{ rotation)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many students *forget the Coriolis term* $2\omega v_r$. In a **slider-on-rotating-link** problem (e.g., crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism), Coriolis **MUST be included**, else the answer is wrong by a sign/magnitude.

**Step 3 — Vector polygon construction.**

- The known vector $\vec{a}_C$ is drawn.
- $\vec{a}_{DC}^{n}$ is known in magnitude ($\omega_3^2 L_3$) and direction (D → C).
- $\vec{a}_{DC}^{t}$ is known in direction ($\perp DC$) but unknown in magnitude ($\alpha_3 L_3$).
- $\vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}}$ is known in magnitude ($2 \omega_3 v_r$) and direction (perpendicular to $v_r$).
- $\vec{a}_D$ is along the line of slider motion (known direction, unknown magnitude).

Solve by **scale drawing** or **complex-algebra / sine-rule** in the polygon.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — DOF of Common KTU Mechanisms

| Mechanism | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Four-bar chain | 4 | 4 | 0 | $9 - 8 = 1$ | Single-input |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | $9 - 8 = 1$ | Single-input |
| Engine indicator (Watt) | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | |
| Double slider-crank (ellipse trammel) | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Trammel draws ellipse |
| Whitworth quick-return | 6 | 8 | 0 | $15 - 16 = -1$ | **Structure** (over-constrained) |
| Geneva mechanism (4-slot) | 5 | 4 | 1 | $12 - 8 - 1 = 3$ | Must add **3 constraints** (slots etc.) |
| Differential gear | — | — | — | Uses gear train analysis | Module 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Kinematic Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Resistant Bodies in a Machine"] --> B["Kinematic Links"]
    A --> C["Kinematic Pairs"]
    A --> D["Kinematic Chain"]
    A --> E["Mechanism / Machine / Structure"]

    B --> B1["Rigid Link"]
    B --> B2["Flexible Link"]
    B --> B3["Fluid Link"]

    C --> C1["Lower Pair - Surface Contact"]
    C --> C2["Higher Pair - Point or Line Contact"]
    C --> C3["By Closure - Self or Force Closed"]

    C1 --> C1a["Revolute - 1 DOF"]
    C1 --> C1b["Prismatic - 1 DOF"]
    C1 --> C1c["Screw - 1 DOF"]
    C1 --> C1d["Spherical - 3 DOF"]

    C2 --> C2a["Cam Follower - 2 DOF"]
    C2 --> C2b["Gear Mesh - 2 DOF"]
    C2 --> C2c["Rolling Pair - 2 DOF"]

    D --> D1["Open Chain - Crane Type"]
    D --> D2["Closed Chain - Four Bar"]
    D2 --> D3["Inversion 1 - Crank Rocker"]
    D2 --> D4["Inversion 2 - Double Crank"]
    D2 --> D5["Inversion 3 - Double Rocker"]

    E --> E1["Mechanism - one link fixed DOF greater than 0"]
    E --> E2["Machine - mechanism doing useful work"]
    E --> E3["Structure - no relative motion DOF equals 0"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture for Velocity/Acceleration Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1["Given: Geometry of Mechanism"] --> StepA["Identify Links and Pairs"]
    Input2["Given: Input Motion omega2 alpha2"] --> StepA
    StepA --> StepB["Compute Known Acceleration of Reference Point"]
    StepB --> StepC["Set Up Relative Acceleration Equation"]
    StepC --> StepD{"Does slider slide on rotating link?"}
    StepD -- Yes --> StepE["Add Coriolis Component 2 omega v sub r"]
    StepD -- No --> StepF["Skip Coriolis Term"]
    StepE --> StepG["Identify Known and Unknown Magnitudes and Directions"]
    StepF --> StepG
    StepG --> StepH["Draw Acceleration Polygon - Scale or Analytical"]
    StepH --> StepI["Solve Polygon - Sine Rule"]
    StepI --> StepJ["Compute Output - omega3 alpha3 velocity of slider"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Inversions of the Slider-Crank Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC["Slider Crank Chain with 4 links 1 Crank 2 Connecting Rod 3 Slider 4 Ground"]
    SC --> Inv1["Fix 1 Ground - Inversion A - Reciprocating IC Engine - Application - Car Engine"]
    SC --> Inv2["Fix 2 Crank - Inversion B - Bull Engine"]
    SC --> Inv3["Fix 3 Connecting Rod - Inversion C - Oscillating Cylinder"]
    SC --> Inv4["Fix 4 Slider - Inversion D - Crank and Slotted Lever - Application - Shaper"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Crank-and-Slotted-Lever** inversion is **the most asked** inversion in KTU 2024 University Exams. The Coriolis term is the *defining feature* of its analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**
*Define a kinematic pair. How is it classified on the basis of (i) the nature of relative motion and (ii) the type of closure?*

**Model Answer (Memorise):**
A kinematic pair is a jointed interface between two links that permits a **constrained relative motion**.
- **(i) Nature of Relative Motion:** *Sliding pair* (pure translation, e.g., piston–cylinder), *Revolving pair* (pure rotation, e.g., shaft–bearing), *Rolling pair* (e.g., roller–cam), *Screw pair* (helical motion, e.g., nut–bolt), *Spherical pair* (e.g., ball–socket).
- **(ii) Type of Closure:** *Self-closed pair* (geometry maintains contact — no external force needed, e.g., shaft–bearing) and *Force-closed pair* (external force like spring/gravity maintains contact, e.g., cam–follower).

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - July 2024, Sample Paper]`**
*Distinguish between a machine, a mechanism, and a structure. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
A **mechanism** is a constrained kinematic chain with one link fixed; it transmits motion only (e.g., sewing machine needle mechanism). A **machine** is a mechanism that **does useful work** by transforming input energy (e.g., lathe, IC engine). A **structure** has **no relative motion** between members (DOF = 0) and only transmits forces (e.g., a building frame, a wall bracket).

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: In every 14-mark question, there is **internal choice** — answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both options below have full sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat sketch, derive Kutzbach's equation for the degrees of freedom of a planar mechanism. State the assumptions clearly.*

**Model Solution:**

1. Consider a mechanism with $n$ links, $j_1$ lower pairs, and $j_2$ higher pairs, in **planar (2-D) motion**.
2. **Assumption:** All links are rigid, all joints are ideal (no clearance, no friction), and the motion is purely 2-D.
3. A free rigid body in a plane has 3 DOF ($x$, $y$, $\theta$).
4. For $n$ links without ground fixing, total DOF $= 3n$.
5. Fixing the ground removes 3 DOF, leaving $3(n-1)$ for the moving system.
6. Each **1-DOF lower pair** (revolute / prismatic) allows 1 motion and removes 2, so $j_1$ such pairs remove $2j_1$ DOF.
7. Each **2-DOF higher pair** allows 2 motions and removes 1, so $j_2$ such pairs remove $j_2$ DOF.
8. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2\;}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ has links $AB = 15$ cm, $BC = 40$ cm, $CD = 30$ cm, and the fixed link $AD = 35$ cm. Using Grashof's law, identify the type of mechanism and sketch the inversion.*

**Model Solution:**

Identify the lengths: $S = 15$ (shortest), $L = 40$ (longest), $P = 30$, $Q = 35$.

Check Grashof: $S + L = 15 + 40 = 55$; $P + Q = 30 + 35 = 65$.

$$S + L < P + Q \implies \text{Grashof satisfied (Class I)}$$

Since the **shortest link (AB)** is **adjacent to the fixed link (AD)**, the mechanism is a **Crank-Rocker Mechanism**: link AB can rotate fully (crank), while link CD oscillates (rocker).

Inversion: $AD$ is the ground; the mechanism is the **crank-rocker** inversion of the four-bar chain, used in **windshield wipers, hand pumps, and sewing machines**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - July 2024, Sample Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the relative velocity method for the velocity analysis of a four-bar mechanism. Use the equation $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$ and clearly show the direction conventions.*

**Model Solution:**

1. The **relative velocity equation** between two points $A$ and $B$ on a rigid link is:

$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$$

where $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is the velocity of $B$ as seen from $A$ (relative velocity).

2. For a **rigid link**, the distance $AB$ is constant, so $B$ can only rotate about $A$:

$$\vert \vec{V}_{BA} \vert = \omega_{AB} \cdot \vert AB \vert, \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$$

3. **Direction convention:** If link $AB$ rotates **counter-clockwise (CCW)** with $\omega_{AB}$, then $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is rotated **90° CCW** from the vector $\vec{AB}$ (i.e., from $A$ to $B$).

4. **Procedure:**
   - Identify the known $\vec{V}_A$ (input point).
   - Identify the two unknown vectors: $\vec{V}_{BA}$ (direction $\perp AB$, magnitude $\omega_{AB} \cdot AB$) and $\vec{V}_B$ (direction depends on next link).
   - Construct a closed vector triangle $\vec{V}_B - \vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_{BA}$.
   - Solve using the **sine rule** (analytical) or scale drawing.
   - The angular velocity of the next link is found from $\omega = V/L$.

5. **Engine indicator diagram example:** Use the polygon to find the velocity of the pencil tip, then construct the indicator diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *In a crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism, the crank rotates at 120 rpm and the crank length is 50 mm. If the slot angle is 60°, find the velocity and acceleration of the slotted lever at the instant when the crank is perpendicular to the slotted lever. Use the relative velocity and relative acceleration methods, and include the Coriolis component.*

**Model Solution (Numerical Walkthrough):**

**Given:**
- $N = 120$ rpm $\implies \omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi \times 120}{60} = 4\pi \approx 12.566$ rad/s
- Crank length $r = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- Slot angle $\phi = 60°$ at the instant of consideration
- Assume uniform crank speed, so $\alpha_2 = 0$

**Step 1 — Velocity of crank pin (point C on crank).**

$$\vert V_C \vert = \omega_2 \cdot r = 12.566 \times 0.05 = 0.6283 \;\text{m/s}$$

$\vec{V}_C$ is perpendicular to the crank.

**Step 2 — Velocity equation for the slider (point on slotted lever).**

The slider $D$ moves in the slot of the lever $O_4D$. Decompose $\vec{V}_C$ into:
- Component along the slot (causes the slider to slide within the slot): $V_r = V_C \sin\phi$
- Component perpendicular to the slot (drives the lever rotation about $O_4$): $V_C \cos\phi$

$$V_r = 0.6283 \times \sin 60° = 0.6283 \times 0.8660 = 0.5442 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$V_D = V_C \cos\phi = 0.6283 \times 0.5 = 0.3142 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of the slotted lever.**

$$\omega_4 = \frac{V_D}{O_4 D}$$

(With $O_4 D$ measured from the given figure; the textbook value is the lever length at that instant.)

**Step 4 — Acceleration of crank pin C.**

Since $\alpha_2 = 0$, only the centripetal component exists:
$$a_C^n = \omega_2^2 \cdot r = (12.566)^2 \times 0.05 = 7.896 \;\text{m/s}^2 \;\;(\text{directed from C towards O}_2)$$

**Step 5 — Acceleration equation for the slider (with Coriolis).**

$$\vec{a}_D = \vec{a}_C + \vec{a}_{\text{rel}} + \vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}}$$

- $\vec{a}_C$ is known (magnitude $a_C^n$, direction C → O₂).
- $\vec{a}_{\text{rel}}$ is the acceleration of the slider along the slot, $\perp$ direction is not yet known.
- **Coriolis acceleration:** $a_{\text{Cor}} = 2 \omega_4 V_r = 2 \times \omega_4 \times 0.5442$, directed $\perp$ to the slot, in the sense of $\omega_4$ rotation.
- $\vec{a}_D$ is $\perp$ to the lever $O_4 D$.

**Step 6 — Solve the acceleration polygon (sine rule).**

$$\boxed{\;a_{\text{Coriolis}} = 2 \,\omega_4 \, V_r\;\;[\text{m/s}^2]\;}$$

The exact numerical answer depends on the lever length $O_4D$, but the **inclusion of Coriolis** is the **key KTU mark-winning step**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Loss Points in PBMET404 Module 1:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis component** in any slider-on-rotating-link problem (Crank-and-Slotted Lever, Rotary IC engine). 1 to 2 marks lost instantly.
> 2. **Sign/Direction errors** in the acceleration polygon. Always label *known direction* and *unknown direction* with arrows, and use a consistent convention (e.g., CCW positive).
> 3. **Not fixing the ground in DOF calculations** — students often count 3 revolute joints but forget the prismatic ground joint in slider-crank → wrong DOF. Always count **all four** pairs in a slider-crank.
> 4. **Mixing up rpm and rad/s** — failure to convert $N$ to $\omega$ via $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ is a silent 1-mark killer.
> 5. **Confusing "Grashof satisfied" with mechanism type** — Grashof only tells you the class (Class I/II/III); you still need to check **which link is shortest and where it sits** to identify crank-rocker vs. double-crank.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- **Kinematics = motion alone; Dynamics = forces + motion.** Memorise this one-line distinction.
- **Machine > Mechanism > Structure**: machine = mechanism + energy source doing useful work; structure = no relative motion.
- **Link** = rigid/flexible/fluid body; **Pair** = jointed interface; **Chain** = series of pairs.
- **Grübler's equation** (planar): $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. Always state assumptions (rigid links, ideal joints, planar motion).
- **Grashof's law** for four-bar continuous rotation: $S + L \leq P + Q$, with the shortest link determining the inversion type when adjacent to ground.
- **Four-bar inversions:** Crank-Rocker, Double-Crank (Drag-link), Double-Rocker — know one practical application each.
- **Slider-crank inversions:** Reciprocating engine, Bull engine, Oscillating cylinder, Crank-and-Slotted Lever.
- **Relative velocity:** $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$ with $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$ and $\vert V_{BA} \vert = \omega \cdot AB$.
- **Relative acceleration:** $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{BA}^{n} + \vec{a}_{BA}^{t} + \vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}}$.
- **Coriolis** appears **only** when one link rotates and a second link slides on it; $a_C = 2 \omega v_r$, perpendicular to $v_r$ in the sense of $\omega$ rotation.
- **Kennedy's theorem** (next sub-topic): three instantaneous centres of three rigid bodies in plane motion are collinear.
- **Number of I-centres:** $N = n(n-1)/2$ for $n$ links.
- **Always convert rpm to rad/s** before plugging into $\omega$.
- **Draw neat, labelled vector polygons** — in KTU ESE, the polygon is worth **3 to 4 marks** by itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms and Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanisms and Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Machine — A Working Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine (KTU Formal Definition):**
> A *machine* is a combination of resistant bodies (links) so arranged that the forces developed between them by the energy supplied to the machine are transmitted from one link to another in a definite manner, and the relative motion between the links is **definite and constrained**.

A machine is a **transformation device**. It receives energy in one form (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, mechanical) and delivers it in a more useful form. Crucially, it transmits **force AND motion** along a predetermined path.

### 1.1.1 Real-World Analogy
Think of a **ceiling fan** connected to a switchboard. The electrical energy flowing through the wiring is the *input energy*. The motor coils and the electromagnetic field between the stator and rotor are the *resistant bodies*. The power passes through the shaft, the bearings, and the blades, and finally comes out as a rotational motion that pushes air downward. The shaft speed is **definite** for a given supply frequency (50 Hz in India) — the motion is *constrained* and *predictable*. That is what makes a ceiling fan a *machine*, and not just a bundle of wires.

## 1.2 The Mechanism — Motion Without the Power-Transmission Emphasis

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism (KTU Formal Definition):**
> A *mechanism* is a kinematic chain in which **one link is fixed** to the frame (ground) and the other links move in a definite, predetermined manner relative to it, purely to **transmit or transform motion** (not energy).

A mechanism is essentially a machine **stripped of its power/load considerations**. It is the *kinematic skeleton* of a machine.

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy
Picture a **sewing machine's needle-bar drive**. The flywheel, the crank, the connecting rod, and the needle bar all move in a perfectly synchronised up-and-down path. If you take the same set of links and use them to drive a heavy press, you still get the *same motion pattern*. The motion is the **mechanism**; the energy/force aspect is the **machine**.

## 1.3 The Kinematic Chain — A Machine Minus the Ground

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Chain (KTU Formal Definition):**
> A *kinematic chain* is an assembly of links (rigid bodies) connected together by means of *kinematic pairs* such that each link moves relative to every other link in a constrained fashion. When one of these links is designated as the *frame* (ground), the chain becomes a **mechanism**.

## 1.4 The Link — The Atom of Every Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Link (Rigid Body / Kinematic Element):**
> A *link* is a single resistant body, or an assembly of rigidly connected parts, that participates in motion as a **single unit** and is joined to other links by kinematic pairs.

### 1.4.1 The Three Material Categories of Links

| # | Type of Link | Defining Characteristic | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rigid Link** | Does **not** deform appreciably under the applied forces. | Crank shaft, connecting rod, gear tooth. |
| 2 | **Flexible Link** | Deforms significantly — primarily through bending or stretching. | Belt drives, chain drives, ropes, cables, springs. |
| 3 | **Fluid Link** | The transmission medium is a *fluid* (gas or liquid) that fills a defined volume. | Hydraulic oil column in a hydraulic jack, air column in a pneumatic cylinder. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** The question *"Classify kinematic links"* is asked almost every year. Always mention the *fluid link* — many students forget it. A fluid link transmits pressure, but since its boundary (the pipe) is fixed, the transmission is treated kinematically as a link.

## 1.5 The Kinematic Pair — Where Motion Meets Constraint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Pair (KTU Formal Definition):**
> A *kinematic pair* is a **joint between two links** that permits a *relative motion* of a *definite type* between them. The pair is the *atom of constraint* in a mechanism.

### 1.5.1 Real-World Analogy
Imagine a **door hinge**. The two leaves of the hinge are *linked*; the hinge pin is the *joint*. The door can only **rotate** about the pin — it cannot slide up, down, left, right, or any other way. That *one allowed motion* (rotation) and the *two eliminated translations* is exactly what makes a hinge a *turning pair*, a *lower pair*, and a *self-closed pair* — three classifications we will meet in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Independent Axes of Classification for Kinematic Pairs

A kinematic pair is **not classified by one rule** — it is classified along **three independent axes**, and the complete name of a pair uses one label from each axis.

### Axis A — By Nature of Contact

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important classification** for KTU problems and Gruebler's equation.

- **Lower Pair (Surface / Area Contact):**
  - Two links have *surface (area) contact* at the joint.
  - The contact stress is low; suitable for high loads.
  - 2 Degrees Of Freedom (DOF) are *removed* — i.e. 2 constraints are imposed.
  - *Examples:* Revolute (pin) joint, prismatic (slider) joint, screw joint, spherical joint.

- **Higher Pair (Point or Line Contact):**
  - Two links have *point* or *line* contact at the joint.
  - The contact stress is high; suitable only for low loads.
  - 1 Degree Of Freedom is *removed* — i.e. 1 constraint is imposed.
  - *Examples:* Cam-follower, gear-tooth mesh, ball-bearing ball-races, wheel-rail contact.

### Axis B — By Type of Relative Motion

| # | Pair Name | Relative Motion Permitted | DOF | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sliding (Prismatic)** | Pure translation along a line. | 1 | Piston in cylinder, ram in lathe. |
| 2 | **Turning (Revolute / Hinge)** | Pure rotation about a fixed axis. | 1 | Door hinge, crank-shaft journal. |
| 3 | **Rolling** | Pure rolling of one body on another. | 1 (line contact) | Ball-bearing balls on race. |
| 4 | **Screw (Helical)** | Combined rotation + translation along the axis. | 1 | Lead screw, bolt-and-nut. |
| 5 | **Spherical (Ball-and-Socket)** | Rotation about **any** axis through a common point. | 3 | Trailer hitch, human shoulder joint. |

### Axis C — By Type of Closure (How the Pair is Held Together)

- **Self-Closed Pair (Form-Closed Pair):**
  The two links are *geometrically shaped* such that they cannot be separated by any external force. *Examples:* Belt-pulley pair (the belt wraps around), a key inside a keyway.

- **Force-Closed Pair (Energised Pair):**
  An *external force* (usually a spring) is needed to keep the two links in contact. *Example:* Cam-follower pair — the cam pushes the follower down, but a **return spring** pushes the follower back up. Without the spring, the follower would fall off the cam.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** A *force-closed pair* can be identified because **gravity or a spring** must always be present in the drawing. In a *self-closed pair*, no such auxiliary force is drawn.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and Mobility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOF (Mobility), M:**
> The *number of independent inputs* (usually independent rotations or translations) that must be specified to bring the mechanism to a known configuration.

A mechanism with **M = 1** is a *useful* mechanism — one input gives one deterministic output. A mechanism with **M = 0** is a *structure* (no motion possible). A mechanism with **M > 1** is *indeterminate* — you need multiple inputs and the motion is ambiguous.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **only** equations you need to memorise for Module 1 problems. Every Part-B problem on mobility reduction will be solved by one of them.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Field of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = total links; $j_1$ = lower pairs; $j_2$ = higher pairs | **Kutzbach (Gruebler) Criterion** — plane mechanisms. |
| 2 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5$ | $j_1$ to $j_5$ = pairs with 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 DOF | **Kutzbach Criterion** — spatial (3-D) mechanisms. |
| 3 | $s + l \le p + q$ | $s$ = shortest link; $l$ = longest link; $p,q$ = other two | **Grashof's Law** — for a four-bar chain. |
| 4 | $\omega_{coupler} = \omega_{crank} \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L_3}$ (instantaneously, for specific cases) | $L_1$ = crank length; $L_3$ = coupler | **Velocity ratio** of a four-bar. |
| 5 | $n = j + 1 - M$ | Rearranged form of Kutzbach's equation | Quick count of *number of links* if mobility & pairs are known. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipeline Table Syntax Rule:** Notice the use of `\le` for $\le$ and `\dfrac` for $\dfrac{...}{...}$ — never use the bare pipe character `≤` or the `|` symbol inside a markdown table cell. Pipes break the table parser.

## 2.4 Grashof's Law — A Deep Look

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grashof's Law (Statement):**
> *A four-bar chain will have at least one link capable of making a complete revolution if, and only if, the sum of the lengths of the shortest and the longest links is **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two links.*

Mathematically:

$$
s + l \;\le\; p + q
$$

where $s, l, p, q$ are the four link lengths with $s$ = shortest and $l$ = longest.

### 2.4.1 The Four Grashof Sub-Cases

| Condition | Inequality | Behaviour | Mechanism Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grashof, strict | $s + l < p + q$ | **All** links can rotate fully if the *shortest* is the frame. | **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)**. |
| Grashof, strict | $s + l < p + q$ | One link can rotate fully (the *shortest*). | **Crank-Rocker**. |
| Non-Grashof, strict | $s + l > p + q$ | **No** link can rotate fully. | **Double-Rocker (Triple-Rocker)**. |
| Grashof, boundary | $s + l = p + q$ | Continuous change — two extreme positions become coincident. | **Change-Point Mechanism**. |

### 2.4.2 Real-World Engineering Use of Grashof's Law

- **Crank-Rocker:** Walking-beam pumping engine, oscillating-arm wipers, foot-pedal.
- **Double-Crank (Drag-Link):** Coupling rod of a steam locomotive, parallelogram folding umbrella.
- **Double-Rocker:** Car-window regulator, rock-shovel boom, human knee.
- **Change-Point:** Paralympic prosthetic devices (deliberate instability at dead-centre).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kutzbach Criterion (Kutzbach, 1929)

The derivation below is the **textbook-style** proof, written out in full — every algebraic step is shown.

### Step 1 — Choose a reference frame

Consider a planar mechanism consisting of $n$ links. One of them, by convention, is the **frame (ground)**. We will count the degrees of freedom of the *moving* links only.

### Step 2 — Unconstrained mobility of a single free link in a plane

A free rigid body in a plane has **three** independent motions:
- Translation along $x$ — 1 DOF.
- Translation along $y$ — 1 DOF.
- Rotation about its own centre — 1 DOF.

So a single free moving link in a plane contributes **3 DOF**.

### Step 3 — Total unconstrained DOF

Since there are $(n - 1)$ moving links, the total unconstrained DOF (i.e. the DOF if **nothing** constrained the links) is:

$$
\text{Total free DOF} = 3 \times (n - 1)
$$

### Step 4 — DOF removed by each kinematic pair

A *lower pair* (surface contact) constrains the joint to remove **two** of the three possible relative motions at the contact — e.g. a revolute joint removes both relative translations but leaves the rotation. So each lower pair contributes **$-2$** to the DOF count.

A *higher pair* (point/line contact) removes only **one** of the three possible relative motions at the contact — e.g. a cam-follower leaves the follower free to translate and the cam free to rotate, but constrains the contact itself. So each higher pair contributes **$-1$** to the DOF count.

### Step 5 — Subtract the constraints

Let $j_1$ = number of lower pairs and $j_2$ = number of higher pairs. The actual mobility $M$ is:

$$
M = 3(n - 1) \;-\; 2 j_1 \;-\; j_2
$$

### Step 6 — Final arrangement (standard textbook form)

$$
\boxed{\,M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Kutzbach criterion**, valid for any *planar* mechanism with **single-DOF** lower pairs. (If a lower pair has 2 DOF, the Kutzbach coefficient for that pair is $3 - 2 = 1$ instead of $2$.)

## 3.2 Worked Example — DOF of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

> **Question:** Find the degrees of freedom of a single slider-crank mechanism (as in an IC engine piston-crank assembly).
>
> **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Count the links ($n$):**
1. Frame / ground (cylinder block).
2. Crank.
3. Connecting rod.
4. Piston (slider).

So $n = 4$.

**Step 2 — Count the lower pairs ($j_1$):**
1. Crank-frame: *Revolute* (R) — lower pair.
2. Crank-connecting rod: *Revolute* (R) — lower pair.
3. Connecting rod-piston: *Revolute* (R) — lower pair.
4. Piston-frame: *Prismatic* (P) — lower pair.

So $j_1 = 4$.

**Step 3 — Count the higher pairs ($j_2$):** None. (All four joints are surface-contact.) So $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Apply Kutzbach's equation:**

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
M = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** $M = 1$ — the slider-crank is a **deterministic, single-DOF mechanism**, exactly as we expect from one crank input producing one piston output.

## 3.3 Worked Example — DOF of a Four-Bar Chain

> **Question:** A four-bar chain consists of 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Find the DOF.
>
> **Solution:**

- $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (all four joints are turning pairs, which are lower pairs), $j_2 = 0$.
- Apply Kutzbach:

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

So the four-bar chain has **M = 1** — one input link, one deterministic output link.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Grashof's Law for a Steering Mechanism

> **Question:** A four-bar steering mechanism has link lengths 25 mm, 50 mm, 60 mm, and 75 mm. The shortest link is the **input crank**. Identify the mechanism and verify whether the input link can rotate fully.
>
> **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify $s$ and $l$:**
- $s = 25 \text{ mm}$ (shortest).
- $l = 75 \text{ mm}$ (longest).
- $p = 50 \text{ mm}$, $q = 60 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's inequality:**

$$
s + l = 25 + 75 = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
p + q = 50 + 60 = 110 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
100 \;\le\; 110 \quad \text{✓ (Grashof's law is satisfied)}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the mechanism type:**

Since the inequality is *strict* ($<$) and the **shortest link is the crank** (input), the input link **can** rotate fully. The output link (opposite the shortest) cannot. Hence the mechanism is a **Crank-Rocker** (the 25 mm link is the crank, the 75 mm link is the rocker — they are on opposite sides of the chain).

**Conclusion:** It is a **Crank-Rocker** mechanism, and the input crank will rotate fully.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Planar Linkages

| Linkage Type | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | $M$ | Continuous Input? | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Four-bar | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Yes (one link) | Walking-beam, wiper, steering. |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Yes (crank) | IC engine, compressor. |
| Whitworth QRM | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Yes (crank) | Shaper machine. |
| Scotch yoke | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Yes (crank) | Sinusoidal motion generator. |
| Elliptical trammel | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | Yes (any one slider) | Drawing ellipses. |
| Epicyclic gear train | 4 | 3 (R) + 1 (gear mesh) | 1 (mesh) | 1 | Yes (planet carrier) | Automobile gearbox, planetary mixers. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** For an **epicyclic gear train**, students often forget that the *gear mesh* is a *higher pair* (point/line contact). Treat each mesh as 1 higher pair, even though the *axes* are joined by revolute pairs (lower pairs). The two classes of pairs must be counted **separately** in the Kutzbach equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Machine vs. Mechanism vs. Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Assembly of Links Joined by Pairs] --> N2{Kinematic Chain? Plus a Fixed Link}
    N2 --> N3{Does it Transmit Power AND Motion?}
    N3 -- YES --> N4[Machine]
    N3 -- NO, Motion only --> N5{Mechanism: Motion Transmission Only}
    N2 -- With NO motion possible --> N6[Structure: M = 0]
    N4 --> N4A[Examples: Engine, Fan, Lathe]
    N5 --> N5A[Examples: Clock Hands, Sewing Needle Drive]
    N6 --> N6A[Examples: Bridge Truss, Building Frame]
```

## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Kinematic Pairs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Kinematic Pair] --> B1[By Nature of Contact]
    A1 --> B2[By Type of Relative Motion]
    A1 --> B3[By Type of Closure]
    B1 --> C1[Lower Pair: Surface Contact]
    B1 --> C2[Higher Pair: Point or Line Contact]
    C1 --> C1A[Revolute Hinge]
    C1 --> C1B[Prismatic Slider]
    C1 --> C1C[Screw Joint]
    C1 --> C1D[Spherical Joint]
    C2 --> C2A[Cam Follower]
    C2 --> C2B[Gear Mesh]
    C2 --> C2C[Ball Bearing]
    B2 --> D1[Sliding]
    B2 --> D2[Turning]
    B2 --> D3[Rolling]
    B2 --> D4[Screw]
    B2 --> D5[Spherical]
    B3 --> E1[Self Closed Pair]
    B3 --> E2[Force Closed Pair]
    E1 --> E1A[Examples: Hinge, Belt Pulley]
    E2 --> E2A[Examples: Cam Follower with Spring]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Slider-Crank

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Link 1 Frame or Ground] -- REVOLUTE PAIR --> L2[Link 2 Crank or Input]
    L2 -- REVOLUTE PAIR --> L3[Link 3 Connecting Rod or Coupler]
    L3 -- REVOLUTE PAIR --> L4[Link 4 Piston or Slider]
    L4 -- PRISMATIC PAIR --> L1
    I1[Input Energy: Rotation of Crank] --> L2
    L4 --> O1[Output Motion: Reciprocation of Piston]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:**
> - The **nodes** labelled `L1` to `L4` are the four links.
> - The **edge labels** (`REVOLUTE PAIR`, `PRISMATIC PAIR`) are the kinematic joints.
> - The four links form a **closed kinematic chain** of four pairs — three revolute and one prismatic.
> - The **input arrow** enters at the crank (Link 2) and the **output arrow** leaves from the piston (Link 4).

## 4.4 Block Architecture of a Four-Bar Chain and Its Inversions

```mermaid
graph TD
    FB[Four Bar Chain] --> INV1[Inversion 1: Crank Rocker]
    FB --> INV2[Inversion 2: Double Crank or Drag Link]
    FB --> INV3[Inversion 3: Double Rocker]
    FB --> INV4[Inversion 4: Beam Engine]
    FB --> INV5[Inversion 5: Coupling Rod of Locomotive]
    FB --> INV6[Inversion 6: Pantograph]
    FB --> INV7[Inversion 7: Oldhams Coupling]
    INV1 --> APP1[Walking beam, Foot pedal]
    INV2 --> APP2[Locomotive coupling, Parallelogram linkage]
    INV3 --> APP3[Rock shovel, Car window regulator]
    INV4 --> APP4[Beam pumping engine]
    INV5 --> APP5[Steam locomotive drive]
    INV6 --> APP6[Scaling and copying drawings]
    INV7 --> APP7[Shaft coupling with slight misalignment]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram of the Kutzbach Mobility Calculation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify the Mechanism] --> S2[Step 2: Count Number of Links n]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify All Joints]
    S3 --> S4{Joint Type?}
    S4 -- SURFACE CONTACT --> S5[Count as Lower Pair j1]
    S4 -- POINT OR LINE CONTACT --> S6[Count as Higher Pair j2]
    S5 --> S7[Apply M equals 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 4: Interpret M]
    S8 -- M equals 1 --> R1[Deterministic Mechanism]
    S8 -- M equals 0 --> R2[Structure with No Motion]
    S8 -- M greater than 1 --> R3[Indeterminate or Redundant]
```

> [!TIP]
> The above is the **standard valuation sequence** for KTU Part-B questions on DOF calculation. Examiners look for these four explicit steps. Missing step 3 (joint classification) is the most common reason for **2-mark** deductions in the KTU answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q:** Differentiate between a **machine** and a **mechanism** with a suitable example each.

**Model Answer:**

A *machine* is a device that **transmits both force and motion** to perform useful work. A *mechanism* is a kinematic chain with **one link fixed**, used purely to **transmit motion** (force/energy considerations are not studied).

| Aspect | Machine | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Considers** | Force, motion, energy. | Motion only. |
| **Purpose** | Transform energy into useful work. | Transform one type of motion into another. |
| **Example** | A lathe machine, an IC engine. | The slider-crank chain of a sewing machine. |
| **Kinematic chain + ground + power** | Yes. | Yes (no power). |

> **[3 marks]** — Award **1 mark** each for definition, **1 mark** for the force/motion distinction, **1 mark** for the comparative example.

### Question 2 (Conceptual) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **Q:** Define a *kinematic pair*. Classify kinematic pairs based on the **nature of contact**.

**Model Answer:**

A *kinematic pair* is a joint between two links that permits a definite, constrained relative motion between them.

Based on the **nature of contact**, kinematic pairs are classified as:

1. **Lower Pair:** Two links have *surface (area)* contact. 2 DOF are removed; suitable for high loads. *Example:* Revolute joint, prismatic joint, screw joint, spherical joint.
2. **Higher Pair:** Two links have *point* or *line* contact. 1 DOF is removed; suitable for low loads. *Example:* Cam-follower, gear mesh, ball bearing.

> **[3 marks]** — Award **1 mark** for the definition, **1 mark** for lower pair + example, **1 mark** for higher pair + example.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Convention:** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part is for **7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

> **(a)** Define a kinematic link. Classify kinematic links with examples. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** State and prove **Kutzbach criterion** for the mobility of plane mechanisms. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

A *kinematic link* is a single resistant body, or an assembly of rigidly connected parts, that participates in motion as a single unit and is joined to other links by kinematic pairs. **[2 marks]**

Classification of links:

| Type | Property | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid link** | Does not deform appreciably under load. | Crank-shaft, connecting rod. |
| **Flexible link** | Deforms primarily by bending or stretching. | Belt, rope, chain, spring. |
| **Fluid link** | A fluid confined in a chamber transmits pressure. | Hydraulic oil in a hydraulic jack, air in a pneumatic cylinder. |

> *Fluid link is often missed by students — examiners reward you for mentioning it.* **[3 marks for the table]**
> Provide neat sketch / schematic for at least one type of link to gain the **2 sketch marks**. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Statement of Kutzbach's Criterion:**
The mobility $M$ of a planar mechanism is given by the difference between the total unconstrained DOF of the moving links and the total number of constraints imposed by the kinematic pairs. **[2 marks]**

**Proof:** See Section 3.1 above for the **full derivation**. The expected derivation is:

1. Unconstrained DOF of $n$ links with one fixed = $3(n - 1)$. **[1 mark]**
2. Each lower pair removes 2 DOF; each higher pair removes 1 DOF. **[1 mark]**
3. Hence $M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$. **[2 marks]**
4. State **two conditions** for a successful mechanism: $M = 1$ for deterministic motion; $M \ge 0$ for the chain to be movable. **[1 mark]**

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

> **(a)** What is *Grashof's Law*? With reference to a four-bar chain, explain the **four** possible sub-cases. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Find the **degrees of freedom** of (i) a slider-crank mechanism, and (ii) an epicyclic gear train with one sun, one planet and one ring gear. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Grashof's Law (statement):**
A four-bar chain will have at least one link capable of making a full revolution if and only if the sum of the lengths of the **shortest and longest** links is **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two. **[2 marks]**

Mathematically: $s + l \le p + q$. **[1 mark]**

**The Four Sub-Cases:** **[4 marks — 1 mark each]**

1. **$s + l < p + q$ — Grashof (strict).** All four links *can* rotate fully only if the **shortest** is the frame; otherwise a *crank-rocker* (one rocker, one crank).
2. **$s + l = p + q$ — Grashof (boundary).** Two extreme positions coincide; called a *change-point* mechanism.
3. **$s + l > p + q$ — Non-Grashof (strict).** No link can rotate fully; the chain is a *double-rocker* (or *triple-rocker* if the longest is fixed).
4. **Identification of the crank.** The link *opposite* the fixed *shortest* link is the **crank** (full-revolution link) in a Grashof chain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Slider-crank mechanism** — already solved in Section 3.2. Result: $M = 1$. **[3 marks]**

**(ii) Epicyclic gear train** with 1 sun + 1 planet + 1 ring:

- $n = 4$ (sun, planet, ring, carrier-arm).
- Lower pairs: 3 revolute joints (planet-sun, planet-ring, planet-carrier) = $j_1 = 3$. **[1 mark]**
- Higher pairs: 2 gear meshes (planet-sun, planet-ring) = $j_2 = 2$. **[1 mark]**
- Apply Kutzbach:

$$
M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - (2) = 9 - 6 - 2 = 1
$$

So the epicyclic gear train has **M = 1** — a single input (e.g. carrier rotation) deterministically gives the output. **[2 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the frame:** Always state explicitly which link is *fixed*. The frame is **not** part of the *mobility count* of moving links.
> 2. **Counting gear meshes wrong:** Each gear-tooth mesh is **one** higher pair, not two. Each revolute joint at a gear shaft is **one** lower pair.
> 3. **Mixing up lower and higher pairs:** A *ball-bearing ball* on a *race* is a higher pair (point contact). A *ball-bearing inner ring* on a *shaft* is a lower pair (cylindrical fit, surface contact).
> 4. **Omitting the fluid link:** In the *types of links* question, students often forget the *fluid link* — the examiner docks **2 marks** for this.
> 5. **Grashof inequality sign:** Note the **$\le$** (not $<$ alone) — the boundary case $s + l = p + q$ is a *Grashof* chain (change-point), and the examiner expects this nuance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Mechanisms and Machines:**

- **Machine** = Transmits *both* force and motion (transformation of energy). **Mechanism** = Transmits *only* motion. **Structure** = Transmits *only* force, no motion. **[Core distinction]**
- **Link** = Single resistant body that moves as a unit. **Three types** — Rigid, Flexible, **Fluid** (often missed).
- **Kinematic pair** = Joint between two links. Classified on **three independent axes** — *nature of contact*, *type of relative motion*, *type of closure*.
- **Lower pair** = Surface contact → **2 DOF removed**. **Higher pair** = Point/line contact → **1 DOF removed**.
- **Five types of relative motion:** Sliding, Turning, Rolling, Screw, Spherical.
- **Self-closed vs Force-closed** — Self-closed needs no external force; force-closed needs spring or weight.
- **DOF / Mobility** $M$ = number of independent inputs needed. **M = 0** → structure, **M = 1** → useful mechanism, **M > 1** → indeterminate.
- **Kutzbach (plane)**: $\,M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2\,$. **Kutzbach (space)**: $\,M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5\,$.
- **Grashof's Law**: $s + l \le p + q$. Strict `<` = Grashof chain; boundary `=` = change-point; `>` = non-Grashof (no full-revolution link).
- **Grashof sub-cases:** *Double-crank* (shortest = frame, Grashof); *Crank-rocker* (Grashof, shortest is non-frame); *Double-rocker* (non-Grashof); *Change-point* (boundary).
- **Slider-crank DOF** = 1 (4 links, 4 lower pairs, 0 higher pairs). **Four-bar DOF** = 1. **Epicyclic gear** = 1 (treating each gear mesh as one higher pair).
- **Quick reference** — Revolute joint (R) = lower; Prismatic (P) = lower; Screw (H) = lower; Spherical (S) = lower; Cam-follower = higher; Gear mesh = higher; Ball-bearing race = higher.
- **Always** show the **four-step Kutzbach working** in DOF problems (count links → classify joints → apply formula → interpret result). Examiners reward explicit methodology.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU valuation, the first **1 to 2 marks** of a Part-B question are awarded for a *neat labelled sketch* and a *defining statement*. Do not skip the diagram — even a Mermaid-style block diagram, when copied neatly onto the answer sheet, will fetch you the *easy marks* that most students lose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planar and Spatial Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planar and Spatial Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism:** A mechanism is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected together by joints (kinematic pairs) intended to transmit or transform motion in a predetermined, constrained manner. It converts an input motion (rotary or linear) into a desired output motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine vs. Mechanism:** A *machine* is a mechanism (or a combination of mechanisms) which, besides transmitting motion, also transmits **force** and performs **useful work** (e.g., an engine, lathe). A *mechanism* only transmits motion (e.g., a clock escapement, a sewing machine needle drive).

### 1.2 Key Building Blocks of a Mechanism

Every mechanism is built from four fundamental primitives. Mastering these is **mandatory** for KTU Module 1 derivations.

| # | Primitive | Formal Definition | Analogy (Plain English) |
|---|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Link** | A rigid body (or a combination of rigid bodies) that has no relative motion between its parts. | Think of it as one "bone" in the skeleton of a machine. |
| 2 | **Kinematic Pair** | A joint between two links that allows a **specific, constrained relative motion**. | The "elbow" or "knee" — it allows only one type of motion (e.g., bending). |
| 3 | **Kinematic Chain** | A series of links connected by kinematic pairs, with one link fixed (ground). | A skeleton before being pinned to a wall. |
| 4 | **Mechanism** | A kinematic chain where **one link is designated as the frame/ground**, and at least one other link is capable of motion relative to it. | A skeleton now nailed to a wall — it can now move predictably. |

### 1.3 Planar vs. Spatial — The Core Distinction

- **Planar Mechanism:** All links move in **parallel planes** (i.e., motion is essentially 2-D). The trajectories of all points are confined to parallel planes. *Example:* A sewing machine needle drive, an internal combustion engine slider-crank.
- **Spatial Mechanism:** Links move in **3-D space**; at least one link traces a non-planar path. *Example:* A universal joint, a robot arm, a differential gear of a car.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** If you can lay the mechanism flat on a table and watch it work as a shadow on a single sheet of paper — it is **planar**. If it needs depth (z-axis) to operate — it is **spatial**.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Used in This Module)

- **Degrees of freedom of a free rigid body in 3-D space = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations). In a plane = **3** (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- **Gruebler's constants (Kutzbach approach):** Lower pair DOF = **1**, Higher pair DOF = **2** (for planar).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Pairs

Kinematic pairs are classified on **two independent axes** in KTU examinations.

#### (A) Based on Nature of Contact
- **Lower Pair (Surface/Closed Pair):** Two links have *surface* contact. Examples: revolute (pin), prismatic (sliding), screw (helical), cylindrical, spherical, planar.
- **Higher Pair (Line/Point/Conjugate Pair):** Two links have *line or point* contact. Examples: cam-follower, gear teeth, ball bearings.

#### (B) Based on Type of Relative Motion
- **Sliding Pair** (Prismatic): Pure translation, 1 DOF.
- **Turning Pair** (Revolute/Pin): Pure rotation, 1 DOF.
- **Rolling Pair:** Pure rolling, 1 DOF (line contact).
- **Screw Pair (Helical):** Translation + rotation coupled, 1 DOF.
- **Spherical Pair (Ball-and-Socket):** Rotation about any axis through the centre, 3 DOF.
- **Cylindrical Pair:** Rotation + translation along the axis, 2 DOF.
- **Planar Pair:** Both surfaces slide over each other, 3 DOF (2 translation + 1 rotation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Lower = Lots of contact = Lasts longer (wear spread out). Higher = High stress = Higher wear."*

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet for Module 1)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | DOF of free body in plane | $F_{\text{free}} = 3$ | — | Reference baseline |
| 2 | DOF of free body in 3-D space | $F_{\text{free}} = 6$ | — | Reference baseline |
| 3 | **Planar Mobility (Kutzbach/Grübler Equation)** | $\boxed{F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}$ | $n=$ total links, $j_1=$ lower pairs (1 DOF), $j_2=$ higher pairs (2 DOF) | Four-bar, slider-crank |
| 4 | **Spatial Mobility (Full Kutzbach Equation)** | $\boxed{F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5}$ | $j_i=$ number of pairs with $i$ DOF | Universal joint, robot arms |
| 5 | Spatial Mobility (Simplified, single-DOF lower + higher) | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_2$ | Most common exam form | Quick spatial DOF |
| 6 | Number of independent loops in a chain | $L = j - n + 1$ | $j=$ total pairs, $n=$ total links | Structural check |
| 7 | Number of binary links for an $n$-bar | $n = 2L + 1$ | $L=$ independent loops | Quick design check |

> [!WARNING]
> In any KTU derivation, always **list $n$, $j_1$, $j_2$ (and $j_3, j_4, j_5$ for spatial) explicitly** before plugging them in. The examiner awards 2 marks for this step alone.

### 2.3 Why These Equations Exist — The "Why" Behind the Math

**The "Why":** Every joint in a mechanism **removes** some of the 6 (or 3 in a plane) independent motions a free body could have. A lower pair in 3-D removes 5 DOF (it allows only 1). A higher pair removes 3 DOF (it allows 2). Counting what remains gives the **mobility** — i.e., how many *independent inputs* you must feed the mechanism to fully control its motion.

- $F = 1$ → Mechanism moves predictably with one input (most real machines).
- $F = 0$ → **Structure** (no motion, e.g., a bicycle frame).
- $F < 0$ → **Preloaded structure** (statically indeterminate, e.g., a truss with redundant members).
- $F > 1$ → **Uncontrolled mechanism** (e.g., a sewing machine needle bar without a guide = useless).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Planar mechanism analysis** underlies the design of all internal combustion engines, sewing machines, printing presses, and folding mechanisms in daily appliances.
- **Spatial mechanism analysis** is the foundation of **robotics** (serial and parallel manipulators), **vehicle suspensions**, **helicopter rotor linkages**, and **human joint biomechanics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Planar Mobility Equation (Kutzbach Criterion)

**Starting Principle:** A free rigid body in a plane possesses **3 degrees of freedom** (2 translations along $x$ and $y$ axes, and 1 rotation about the $z$-axis). Hence, a system of $n$ free, unconnected links in a plane has a total of $3n$ degrees of freedom.

**Step 1 — Fix the ground link.**
One link must be immobilised to act as the frame of reference. This removes its **3** DOF from the count.

$$
\text{Remaining DOF} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint of a lower pair.**
A planar lower pair (e.g., a pin joint, a slider) connects two links but allows only **1** relative DOF (either a rotation or a translation). It therefore removes $(3 - 1) = 2$ DOF from the system.

If there are $j_1$ such lower pairs, total DOF removed $= 2j_1$.

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint of a higher pair.**
A planar higher pair (e.g., cam-follower contact, gear teeth contact) allows **2** relative DOF. It removes $(3 - 2) = 1$ DOF from the system.

If there are $j_2$ such higher pairs, total DOF removed $= 1 \cdot j_2$.

**Step 4 — Final mobility equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{planar}} &= \text{Total free DOF} - \text{DOF removed by joints} \\
&= 3n - 3 - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Kutzbach criterion** for planar mechanisms, valid only when **all higher pairs are line-contact (no point-only contact)** and the mechanism has **no redundant DOF** or **passive DOF** (special cases that need Grübler's extended equations).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Spatial Mobility Equation (Full Kutzbach)

**Starting Principle:** A free rigid body in 3-D space has **6** independent DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). For $n$ unconnected bodies, total free DOF $= 6n$.

**Step 1 — Fix the ground (frame) link.**
The fixed link loses all 6 of its DOF.

$$
\text{Remaining DOF} = 6n - 6 = 6(n-1)
$$

**Step 2 — Classify joints by how many DOF they preserve.**
- A joint that preserves $1$ DOF (e.g., revolute, prismatic, screw) **removes 5** DOF → counted as $j_1$.
- A joint that preserves $2$ DOF (e.g., cylindrical) **removes 4** DOF → counted as $j_2$.
- A joint that preserves $3$ DOF (e.g., planar, spherical with 3 rotational axes treated as $j_3$ here) **removes 3** DOF → counted as $j_3$.
- A joint that preserves $4$ DOF **removes 2** DOF → $j_4$.
- A joint that preserves $5$ DOF **removes 1** DOF → $j_5$.

**Step 3 — Sum all the DOF removed and subtract from the free count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{spatial}} &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most **exam-frequently used** simplified version of this equation is the one for mechanisms composed of **single-DOF lower pairs** (revolute, prismatic, screw) and **two-DOF higher pairs** (general cam, gear-mesh):
> $$F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_2$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — DOF of a Four-Bar Planar Linkage

A four-bar linkage has 4 links (including the ground) and 4 revolute (lower) joints. Let us verify that it has $F = 1$.

**Given:** $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (revolute pairs), $j_2 = 0$ (no higher pair).

**Substitute into the planar Kutzbach equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
  &= 3(3) - 8 \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Conclusion:** The four-bar linkage is a **1-DOF mechanism** — feed it one input (say, a constant crank rotation), and the entire chain's motion is fully determined. This matches physical reality.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — DOF of a Spatial RSSR Mechanism

The RSSR (Revolute-Spherical-Spherical-Revolute) spatial mechanism is a classic *spatial four-bar*. It has 4 links and 4 joints: two revolute ($j_1$) and two spherical ($j_3$).

**Given:** $n = 4$, $j_1 = 2$, $j_3 = 2$, all other $j_i = 0$.

**Substitute into the full spatial Kutzbach equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_3 \\
  &= 6(4-1) - 5(2) - 3(2) \\
  &= 6(3) - 10 - 6 \\
  &= 18 - 16 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** Unlike a planar four-bar ($F=1$), the *spatial* RSSR has **2 inputs** required for fully controlled motion. The extra DOF comes from the out-of-plane rotation allowed by the spherical joints.

> [!TIP]
> Always cross-check spatial DOF against physical intuition: a Hooke's universal joint (Cardan joint) has 2 links, 2 revolute pairs, 1 revolute-axis (the cross). It must permit the carrier fork to rotate. Compute its $F$ and confirm it equals 2 — this is a frequent KTU 2-mark verification question.

---

### 3.5 Inversion of a Mechanism — Conceptual Derivation

An **inversion** is obtained by fixing a *different* link of the kinematic chain (instead of the original ground) and observing the resulting motion of the other links. The number of links and the joint structure remain the same; only the choice of frame changes.

For a four-bar chain, there are exactly **4** distinct inversions. Two classical examples:
- **Slider-crank mechanism** ← fix the shortest link of a four-bar chain, and the opposite link becomes a slider on a prismatic joint.
- **Whitworth quick-return** ← fix the input link differently to obtain unequal forward and return strokes.

The DOF remains invariant under inversion (a property called *kinematic invariance*). This is a frequently asked 3-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Kinematic Pairs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Kinematic Pair]
    A0 --> A1[Based on Contact Type]
    A0 --> A2[Based on Relative Motion]
    A1 --> A1a[Lower Pair Surface Contact]
    A1 --> A1b[Higher Pair Line or Point Contact]
    A1a --> A1a1[Revolute Pin]
    A1a --> A1a2[Prismatic Slider]
    A1a --> A1a3[Screw Helical]
    A1a --> A1a4[Spherical Ball Socket]
    A1a --> A1a5[Cylindrical]
    A1a --> A1a6[Planar]
    A1b --> A1b1[Cam Follower]
    A1b --> A1b2[Gear Teeth Mesh]
    A1b --> A1b3[Ball Bearing Contact]
    A2 --> A2a[Sliding 1 DOF]
    A2 --> A2b[Turning 1 DOF]
    A2 --> A2c[Rolling 1 DOF]
    A2 --> A2d[Screw 1 DOF coupled]
    A2 --> A2e[Spherical 3 DOF]
    A2 --> A2f[Cylindrical 2 DOF]
    A2 --> A2g[Planar 3 DOF]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Planar vs Spatial Mechanism Analysis (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Planar[PLANAR MECHANISM ANALYSIS]
        P1[Input: n j1 j2] --> P2[Apply Kutzbach Planar]
        P2 --> P3[Formula: F equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
        P3 --> P4[Output: Mobility F]
    end
    subgraph Spatial[SPATIAL MECHANISM ANALYSIS]
        S1[Input: n j1 j2 j3 j4 j5] --> S2[Apply Kutzbach Spatial]
        S2 --> S3[Formula: F equals 6 of n minus 1 minus 5 j1 minus 4 j2 minus 3 j3 minus 2 j4 minus j5]
        S3 --> S4[Output: Mobility F]
    end
    P4 --> Decision{Is F equals 1}
    S4 --> Decision
    Decision -->|Yes| Good[Well Defined Mechanism]
    Decision -->|No| Bad[Recheck Structure or Add Constraint]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Structural Build-up of a Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Rigid Body Link] --> R2[Two or More Links Joined]
    R2 --> R3[Kinematic Pair Joint]
    R3 --> R4[Series of Pairs and Links]
    R4 --> R5[Kinematic Chain]
    R5 --> R6[Fix One Link as Ground Frame]
    R6 --> R7[Mechanism Ready for Motion Analysis]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DOF Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Identify Links and Joints]) --> n1[Count n total links]
    n1 --> j1[Count j1 lower pairs]
    j1 --> j2[Count j2 higher pairs]
    j2 --> plane{Spatial or Planar}
    plane -->|Planar| fp[Compute F equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    plane -->|Spatial| fs[Compute F equals 6 of n minus 1 minus 5 j1 minus 3 j2]
    fp --> check{Value of F}
    fs --> check
    check -->|F equals 1| mech[Valid Single Input Mechanism]
    check -->|F equals 0| struct[Static Structure]
    check -->|F greater than 1| multi[Multi Input Mechanism]
    check -->|F less than 0| locked[Locked Preloaded Structure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a *lower pair* and a *higher pair* with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A **lower pair** has surface (area) contact between the two mating links, e.g., a *revolute pin joint* in a four-bar mechanism. A **higher pair** has point or line contact, e.g., a *cam-follower pair*. Lower pairs are more wear-resistant and can carry higher loads; higher pairs are used where rolling/sliding at a point is essential (gears, cams).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the Kutzbach equation for mobility of a planar mechanism with 3 binary links and 3 lower pairs.

**Model Answer:**
The planar Kutzbach equation is $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. Here, $n = 3$ (ground + 2 binary links), $j_1 = 3$ lower pairs, $j_2 = 0$.

$$
F = 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 0 = 6 - 6 = 0
$$

This is therefore a **structure**, not a mechanism. ✔️

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For DOF problems, students often forget to **include the ground link in the count of $n$**. The ground link *is* a link and *must* be counted. Similarly, the joint connecting a link to the ground must be counted in $j_1$ or $j_2$. Losing this 1 mark is the most common reason for a 13/14 score.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

A mechanism consists of **6 links** and **7 lower pairs** (1-DOF), all moving in a single plane. Determine:
**(a)** Whether the mechanism is a structure, mechanism, or preloaded. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If one higher pair (2-DOF) replaces one lower pair, recompute the mobility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**
Given: $n = 6$, $j_1 = 7$, $j_2 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
  &= 3(5) - 14 \\
  &= 15 - 14 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ The mechanism has **mobility F = 1**. It is a **valid single-input mechanism**.

---

**(b) Solution:**
New conditions: $n = 6$ (unchanged), $j_1 = 6$ (one lower pair replaced), $j_2 = 1$ (new higher pair).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(6-1) - 2(6) - 1 \\
  &= 15 - 12 - 1 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

The new mechanism now requires **2 independent inputs** for fully controlled motion. It is a **two-DOF mechanism** (e.g., behaviour like a 5-bar chain).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A spatial mechanism has **5 links**, **4 single-DOF lower pairs** (revolute/prismatic/screw), and **1 two-DOF higher pair**.
**(a)** Calculate the mobility using the full Kutzbach spatial equation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Justify, with one physical example, why this mechanism has the DOF you calculated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**
Given: $n = 5$, $j_1 = 4$ (each removes 5 DOF), $j_2 = 1$ (removes 3 DOF). All other $j_i = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_2 \\
  &= 6(5-1) - 5(4) - 3(1) \\
  &= 6(4) - 20 - 3 \\
  &= 24 - 23 \\
\therefore \quad F &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The mechanism has **mobility F = 1**.

---

**(b) Justification:**
A classic physical example is a **single-DOF robotic finger or a screw-and-nut drive with a cam-feel guide**, where the cam forms the single higher pair and the screw and pins form the four lower pairs. Physically, driving the input link (e.g., rotating the screw) constrains all other links to follow a unique, fully-determined trajectory — consistent with $F = 1$. If any joint were removed or added, the mechanism would either become a structure ($F = 0$) or uncontrolled ($F > 1$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **mechanism** transmits *motion*; a **machine** transmits *motion + power*.
- [x] The four primitives are **Link, Pair, Chain, Mechanism** (in that build order).
- [x] **Planar** = 2-D motion; **Spatial** = 3-D motion.
- [x] A **lower pair** has *surface contact*; a **higher pair** has *line/point contact*.
- [x] **Planar Kutzbach:** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- [x] **Spatial Kutzbach (full):** $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- [x] **Spatial Kutzbach (simplified, exam form):** $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_2$
- [x] Always **count the ground link** in $n$, and the **ground joints** in $j_1$/$j_2$.
- [x] $F = 0 \Rightarrow$ structure; $F = 1 \Rightarrow$ valid mechanism; $F > 1 \Rightarrow$ under-constrained; $F < 0 \Rightarrow$ preloaded/locked.
- [x] **Inversion** = fixing a different link of the same chain. Number of distinct inversions of a four-bar = 4.
- [x] Grashof's law (preview for Module 2) governs the existence of a continuous rotation in a four-bar.
- [x] **Independent loops:** $L = j - n + 1$ — useful for verifying mechanism structure.
- [x] Common spatial joints and their DOF: Revolute=1, Prismatic=1, Screw=1, Cylindrical=2, Spherical=3, Planar=3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF) / Mobility of a Mechanism:**
> The **Degree of Freedom (F)**, also called **Mobility (M)**, of a kinematic chain or mechanism is defined as the *minimum number of independent inputs (driving links or coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration (position) of every link in the system with respect to a fixed reference (frame/ground).*

In simpler terms, it is the **number of independent relative motions** that a body or a system of connected rigid bodies can perform without violating any of the constraints imposed by the joints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are playing with a set of Lego pieces on a flat table:

- A **single Lego brick** floating freely can move in 3 ways: slide left-right (X), slide forward-back (Y), and rotate (spin). Hence, a free rigid body in a plane has **3 DOF**.
