- Now imagine **gluing one corner** of the brick to the table. The glued corner cannot move. But the brick can still rotate about that glued point and slide along arcs. So the brick has lost 2 motions. It now has **1 DOF** (it can only rotate).
- If you attach **two more bricks** with **pin joints** (allowing rotation only), the system is constrained further.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Every joint (constraint) *removes* certain possible motions. **DOF = Free motions available − motions removed by joints.**

For a planar mechanism with $n$ links (including the ground/frame) and various joint types, we can **count** the links, count the joints, and then compute the surviving independent motions using **Kutzbach's Equation** (the most widely used planar mobility criterion in KTU exams).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- For a **free rigid body in 2-D (planar)**: DOF = **3** (translation along $X$, $Y$, and rotation about $Z$).
- For a **free rigid body in 3-D (spatial)**: DOF = **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Binary Joint** (2 links joined): most common joint form used in inversions.
- **Lower Pair**: Joint with **surface (line/area) contact** — typically allows 1 DOF (e.g., revolute, prismatic, screw, cam).
- **Higher Pair**: Joint with **point/line contact** — allows 2 DOF (e.g., gear mesh, cam-follower, ball-bearing contact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DOF Matters (Engineering Utility):**
> A machine with **F = 0** is a **structure** (no motion possible — it acts like a bridge/truss).
> A machine with **F = 1** is the **most desirable and common mechanism** — one input gives fully determined output (e.g., four-bar linkage, slider-crank).
> **F ≥ 2** means **indeterminate motion** (multiple drivers needed); such systems are usually avoided in machinery but are useful in robotics.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kutzbach's Equation (Planar Mobility Criterion)

The general mobility equation for a **planar mechanism with $n$ links and only single DOF and two-DOF joints** is:

$$F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $F$ = Degrees of freedom (mobility) of the mechanism.
- $n$ = Total number of links including the fixed frame (ground).
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints) — revolute, prismatic, etc.
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints) — cam-follower contact, gear mesh.

**Why this formula?**
1. A free link in the plane has **3 DOF**.
2. There are $(n-1)$ *moving* links (excluding the fixed ground link).
3. If unconstrained, total DOF = $3(n-1)$.
4. Each **lower pair** removes 2 independent relative motions (e.g., a pin joint removes 2 translations, leaves 1 rotation).
5. Each **higher pair** removes 1 motion (e.g., cam-follower leaves the follower free to translate and rotate independently along/around the cam surface, removing 1 constraint only).

### 2.2 Gruebler's Criterion (Special Case — Single DOF Joints Only)

If all joints are 1-DOF joints (e.g., all revolute or prismatic), then $j_2 = 0$, and the equation reduces to the **Gruebler form**:

$$F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1$$

This is the most commonly tested form in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 2.3 Planar vs. Spatial Mobility — Side-by-Side

| Mechanism Type | Free Body DOF | Constraint Form | Mobility Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Planar (2D)** | **3** | 2-DOF joints allowed | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ |
| **Spatial (3D)** | **6** | 1- to 5-DOF joints | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Equation Explained:** Each spatial joint of class $k$ removes $(6-k)$ degrees of freedom. A revolute joint ($k=1$) removes 5 DOF; a universal joint ($k=2$) removes 4; a spherical joint ($k=3$) removes 3; and so on.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach's Equation** (Planar mechanism) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$ | **Gruebler's form** (only 1-DOF joints) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Spatial mobility** (3D mechanism) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{\text{free body, plane}} = 3$ | A single unconstrained link in a plane | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_{\text{free body, space}} = 6$ | A single unconstrained link in 3D | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Constraints removed by lower pair} = 2$ | Per lower pair (e.g., pin) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Constraints removed by higher pair} = 1$ | Per higher pair (e.g., cam) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $n = n_{\text{moving}} + 1$ | Total links = moving links + 1 (ground) | Count |
| 9 | $\text{Loop closure eqs} = j_1 + j_2$ | For planar chain (used in loop equations) | Count |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always remember to include the **ground link** in $n$. Forgetting it is the most common mistake that costs 1–2 marks.

### 2.5 Joint Classification Summary (Vital for KTU)

| Joint Type | Class | DOF Allowed | Constraint Removed | Pair Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (Pin) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Lower |
| Prismatic (Slider) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Lower |
| Screw | 1 | 1 | 5 (spatial) | Lower |
| Cam-Follower | 2 | 2 | 1 | Higher |
| Gear Mesh | 2 | 2 | 1 | Higher |
| Spherical (Ball) | 3 | 3 | 3 (spatial) | Lower |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (Planar)

We start from first principles — counting independent motions of each link in a planar kinematic chain.

**Step 1: Count the unconstrained motions of each moving link.**
In a plane, each rigid body has $3$ independent motions: two translations (along $X$ and $Y$ axes) and one rotation (about the $Z$-axis normal to the plane). So, for a single free link, the DOF count is $3$.

**Step 2: Total unconstrained motions of the chain.**
The mechanism has $n$ links in total, but **one of them is the fixed frame (ground)**. Only $(n - 1)$ links are free to move. Therefore, the total unconstrained degrees of freedom of the system (before applying joint constraints) is:

$$F_{\text{unconstrained}} = 3(n - 1)$$

**Step 3: Count the constraints introduced by each joint.**
- A **lower pair (1-DOF joint)** reduces the relative motion between the two joined links by $2$ (i.e., it imposes $2$ constraints).  
  - Example: A revolute joint (pin) permits rotation but **prevents** the two links from translating relative to each other in both $X$ and $Y$. Hence, $2$ translations are removed; only $1$ rotation remains.
  - Number of constraints imposed by all lower pairs: $2j_1$.
- A **higher pair (2-DOF joint)** reduces the relative motion by $1$.  
  - Example: A cam-follower has point/line contact — it prevents the follower from penetrating the cam, but the follower can still both rotate and translate along/around the surface.
  - Number of constraints imposed by all higher pairs: $1 \cdot j_2$.

**Step 4: Subtract constraints from unconstrained DOF.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= F_{\text{unconstrained}} - \text{Total Constraints} \\[6pt]
F &= 3(n - 1) - \left( 2 j_1 + 1 \cdot j_2 \right) \\[6pt]
F &= 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}$$

This is the celebrated **Kutzbach Criterion** (also called the Kutzbach–Gruebler mobility equation for planar mechanisms).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $F$ is the net number of *independent* generalized coordinates needed to fully describe the mechanism's configuration. If $F = 1$, one input (driver) is enough.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Spatial Mobility Equation

In 3D space, a free rigid body has $6$ independent motions: 3 translations ($X, Y, Z$) and 3 rotations (roll, pitch, yaw). Repeating the same logic:

**Step 1:** Unconstrained DOF of the chain = $6(n - 1)$.

**Step 2:** Each spatial joint of class $k$ removes $(6 - k)$ independent motions. A joint of class $k$ is one that **permits $k$ relative motions** between the two links it connects.

**Step 3:** Total constraints removed = $5j_1 + 4j_2 + 3j_3 + 2j_4 + j_5$.

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(n - 1) - (5j_1 + 4j_2 + 3j_3 + 2j_4 + j_5) \\[6pt]
F &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Spatial Kutzbach Equation** (Chebyshev–Gruebler criterion for 3D chains).

### 3.3 Worked Examples — Step-by-Step Application

#### **Example A: Four-Bar Mechanism**

- Links: $n = 4$ (1 ground + 3 moving: crank, coupler, rocker).
- Joints: All are revolute (lower pairs). $j_1 = 4$ (one at each connection). $j_2 = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\[4pt]
F &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\[4pt]
F &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ One input (say, the crank) is enough to fully determine the motion of all other links.

#### **Example B: Slider-Crank Mechanism**

- Links: $n = 4$ (ground, crank, connecting rod, slider/piston).
- Joints: 3 revolute + 1 prismatic, all lower pairs. $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 \\[4pt]
F &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ One input (crank rotation) drives the piston linearly.

#### **Example C: Cam-Follower (Offset Translation Cam)**

- Links: $n = 3$ (ground, cam, follower).
- Joints: 1 revolute (cam-ground) + 1 prismatic (follower-ground) = $j_1 = 2$. Plus 1 higher pair (cam-follower contact) = $j_2 = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 1 \\[4pt]
F &= 6 - 4 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ One driver (cam rotation) controls the follower.

#### **Example D: Gear Pair (Simple Two-Gear System)**

- Links: $n = 3$ (ground, gear A, gear B).  
- Joints: 2 revolute joints (gear-ground) — $j_1 = 2$. 1 higher pair (gear-gear mesh) — $j_2 = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(3 - 1) - 2(2) - 1 \\[4pt]
F &= 6 - 4 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ One driver gear fully dictates the driven gear.

#### **Example E: Six-Bar Stephenson or Watt Mechanism**

- Links: $n = 6$, $j_1 = 7$ (all revolute), $j_2 = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(6 - 1) - 2(7) = 15 - 14 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ One input is sufficient.

#### **Example F: Hinged Four-Bar with Redundant Constraint (Structure)**

- Links: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 5$ (e.g., extra link pinned diagonally) — this is **statically indeterminate** or a structure.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(5) = 9 - 10 = -1
\end{aligned}$$

✓ Negative DOF means it is a **statically indeterminate structure** (no motion possible, and constraints are redundant — used in trusses, frames).

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — DOF of Common Mechanisms

| S.No. | Mechanism | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | DOF $F$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Four-bar | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | All revolute |
| 2 | Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 3R + 1P |
| 3 | Double-slider | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 2R + 2P |
| 4 | Cam-follower (translating) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1R + 1P + 1H |
| 5 | Gear pair | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2R + 1H |
| 6 | Whitworth quick-return | 6 | 6 | 0 | 3 | Multi-driver |
| 7 | Six-bar Watt | 6 | 7 | 0 | 1 | All revolute |
| 8 | Robot arm (spatial 6R) | 7 | 6 | 0 | 6 | Spatial, not planar |
| 9 | Truss (4-bar fixed) | 4 | 5 | 0 | −1 | Structure |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Mobility Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify a Kinematic Chain] --> B{Links present in 2D or 3D?}
    B -- Planar 2D --> C[Count n = total links including ground]
    B -- Spatial 3D --> D[Use Spatial Kutzbach: 6n-1 minus constraints]
    C --> E[Count j1: lower pairs 1-DOF joints]
    E --> F[Count j2: higher pairs 2-DOF joints]
    F --> G[Apply Formula: F = 3n-1 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    G --> H{Result of F?}
    H -- F equals 1 --> I[DESIRABLE: Single input mechanism, e.g., four-bar, slider-crank]
    H -- F equals 0 --> J[STRUCTURE: No relative motion, e.g., truss, frame]
    H -- F greater than 1 --> K[INDETERMINATE: Needs multiple drivers, common in robotics]
    H -- F less than 0 --> L[STATICALLY INDETERMINATE: Redundant constraints]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Joint Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Joint in a Mechanism] --> B{Contact type?}
    B -- Area or Surface Contact --> C[LOWER PAIR]
    B -- Point or Line Contact --> D[HIGHER PAIR]
    C --> C1[Revolute R - 1 DOF]
    C --> C2[Prismatic P - 1 DOF]
    C --> C3[Screw - 1 DOF]
    C --> C4[Spherical - 3 DOF]
    D --> D1[Cam Follower - 2 DOF]
    D --> D2[Gear Pair - 2 DOF]
    D --> D3[Ball Bearing Race - 2 DOF]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Diagram — DOF Reduction by Joints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Rigid Link in Plane] -- has --> B[DOF equals 3]
    B -- pin joint adds constraint --> C[DOF drops to 1 around pin]
    C -- second link pinned --> D[System with 2 pinned links and 1 ground]
    D -- prismatic joint added --> E[DOF becomes 1 overall = mechanism]
    E -- extra redundant link --> F[DOF equals 0 = structure]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — DOF Decision Workflow for a Designer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Designer receives task] --> B[Define desired output motion]
    B --> C{Number of independent inputs needed?}
    C -- One --> D[Target F equals 1]
    C -- Multiple --> E[Target F equals 2 or more]
    C -- None --> F[Target F equals 0: Build a structure]
    D --> G[Choose a four-bar, slider-crank, cam, or gear]
    E --> H[Use multi-loop or differential mechanisms]
    F --> I[Use redundant constraints to form a rigid frame]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Degree of Freedom** of a mechanism. State Kutzbach's criterion for a planar mechanism with $n$ links, $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The degree of freedom of a mechanism is the *minimum number of independent inputs required to completely specify the configuration of all its moving links* relative to a fixed frame. It is also called mobility.
- **Equation (1 mark):** $F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **Term explanation (1 mark):** $n$ = total number of links including ground; $j_1$ = number of 1-DOF (lower) pairs; $j_2$ = number of 2-DOF (higher) pairs.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between a **lower pair** and a **higher pair** with **two examples each**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lower Pair (1.5 marks):** A joint in which the two connected links have **surface (line or area) contact**. The contact stress is low, and it is generally capable of carrying heavy loads.  
  *Examples:* (i) Revolute (pin) joint, (ii) Prismatic (slider) joint.
- **Higher Pair (1.5 marks):** A joint in which the two connected links have **point or line contact**. The contact stress is high, and it is generally used in motion transmission.  
  *Examples:* (i) Cam-follower, (ii) Gear-tooth pair.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Derive the **Kutzbach mobility equation** for a planar mechanism from first principles, clearly stating the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For the mechanism shown in the figure (a **five-bar planar linkage with one cam pair** — assume $n = 5$, $j_1 = 5$, $j_2 = 1$), compute the degree of freedom and comment on whether the mechanism is a structure, a determinate mechanism, or indeterminate. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

> See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. The student must write:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** A free rigid link in a plane has **3 independent motions** — translation along $X$, translation along $Y$, and rotation about $Z$. For a mechanism with $n$ links (one being the ground), there are $(n - 1)$ moving links, so unconstrained DOF = $3(n-1)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Each **lower pair** removes 2 motions (e.g., a pin removes two translations, leaves one rotation). Each **higher pair** removes 1 motion (e.g., cam-follower allows both translation along and rotation about the contact point, removing only the normal penetration motion). So total constraints = $2j_1 + j_2$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Subtracting constraints from unconstrained DOF:

$$F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention that this is called the **Kutzbach criterion** and is the standard planar mobility equation.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 5$ (1 ground + 4 moving), $j_1 = 5$ (lower pairs — revolute and prismatic), $j_2 = 1$ (one cam pair).

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Apply the Kutzbach equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\[4pt]
F &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) - 1 \\[4pt]
F &= 3 \times 4 - 10 - 1 \\[4pt]
F &= 12 - 11 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Interpretation:** $F = 1$ means the mechanism is a **determinate mechanism** — a single input (e.g., a driving crank) will completely determine the position and motion of every other link in the system.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Comment:** This is the most commonly desired configuration in machine design. The mechanism is **neither a structure (F = 0)** nor **indeterminate (F ≥ 2)**. It is a usable, well-constrained single-DOF mechanism.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches: (i) **Revolute joint**, (ii) **Prismatic joint**, (iii) **Cam pair**, (iv) **Spherical pair**. Classify each as a lower or higher pair and state its DOF. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **four-bar mechanism has the following joint modifications**: An additional **prismatic joint** is introduced between the coupler and the rocker (treating them as now $n = 5$, $j_1 = 5$, $j_2 = 0$). Compute the new DOF. State whether the modified system is a mechanism or a structure, and justify your answer with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Joint classifications with sketches (7 marks):**

| Joint | Sketch (textual) | Class | Pair Type | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Revolute (R) | Cylinder inside a hollow cylinder; bolt passing through both | 1-DOF (rotation only) | Lower | 1 |
| (ii) Prismatic (P) | A slider moving inside a rectangular guide | 1-DOF (translation only) | Lower | 1 |
| (iii) Cam pair | A curved cam profile touching a flat or roller follower at a point | 2-DOF | Higher | 2 |
| (iv) Spherical (Ball) | A ball inside a socket | 3-DOF (3 rotations) | Lower | 3 |

*Note for student:* Mention the **mathematical meaning of class** — a class-$k$ joint allows $k$ independent relative motions. **(Award 1.5 marks per joint, plus 0.5 for a clear sketch.)**

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Given: Modified four-bar with $n = 5$, $j_1 = 5$, $j_2 = 0$.

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Apply the Gruebler form (since $j_2 = 0$).

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 \\[4pt]
F &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) \\[4pt]
F &= 12 - 10 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Interpretation:** $F = 2$ means the mechanism is **indeterminate** — it has 2 independent inputs (drivers) required to fully determine the motion. This is uncommon in classical machinery but is the **basis of robotic manipulators** (e.g., a 2-DOF planar robot arm).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Real-world example:** A **planar 2R robotic arm** has $n = 3$ (ground + 2 links), $j_1 = 2$ (two revolute joints), $F = 3(2) - 2(2) = 2$. This needs 2 motor inputs (one at the shoulder, one at the elbow) to position the end-effector in a plane.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link:** A frequent mistake is using $n - 1$ as the count of *all* links. The ground/frame is **always** link number 1 and must be included in $n$.
> 2. **Confusing DOF with class of joint:** DOF of the *whole mechanism* ≠ DOF of an *individual joint*. Be precise with your vocabulary.
> 3. **Missing the higher pair in cam/gear systems:** Many students set $j_2 = 0$ for cam-followers and gear pairs. This gives wrong answers like $F = 2$ for a single cam, when the correct answer is $F = 1$.
> 4. **Planar vs. Spatial confusion:** Use $3(n-1)$ for 2D, $6(n-1)$ for 3D. Mixing them up costs heavy marks.
> 5. **Sign of DOF:** A negative DOF is *not* "impossible" — it simply means a statically indeterminate structure, which is **valid engineering** (e.g., a bridge truss).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- **Degree of Freedom (F)** = the number of independent inputs required to fully define a mechanism's configuration.
- A **free rigid body** has **3 DOF in a plane** and **6 DOF in space**.
- **Lower pair** = surface contact (e.g., revolute, prismatic) — removes 2 motions.
- **Higher pair** = point/line contact (e.g., cam, gear) — removes 1 motion.
- **Kutzbach's Equation (Planar):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **Gruebler's Form (All 1-DOF joints):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$.
- **Spatial Mobility:** $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **$F = 0$** → Structure (no motion).  
  **$F = 1$** → Desirable mechanism (one driver).  
  **$F \geq 2$** → Indeterminate (multi-driver, robotics).  
  **$F < 0$** → Statically indeterminate structure.
- **Always include the ground link** in $n$.
- A **revolute joint** removes 2 DOF, leaves 1 (rotation only).
- A **prismatic joint** removes 2 DOF, leaves 1 (translation only).
- A **cam-follower pair** removes 1 DOF, leaves 2 (translation + rotation about contact).
- A **gear pair** removes 1 DOF, leaves 2 (rotation of each gear independently).
- A **spherical (ball) joint** in 3D removes 3 DOF, leaves 3.
- KTU examiners **love** questions where you must compute DOF of four-bar, slider-crank, and cam-follower — practice these.
- **Real-world applications of DOF analysis:** robotics (multi-DOF arms), suspension design in automobiles, landing-gear mechanism design, prosthetic limb design, robotic surgery, and 3D printer kinematics.
- **Common errors:** forgetting ground link, mixing higher/lower pair, applying spatial equation to a planar problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobility Analysis - Kutzbach and Grubler's Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mobility Analysis - Kutzbach and Grubler's Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility (M)**, also called the **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mechanism, is defined as the *minimum number of independent inputs (coordinates or parameters) required to completely specify the configuration of every link of the mechanism relative to a fixed reference frame (the ground link).*

In rigid-body kinematics, every unconstrained rigid link in a **2D plane** possesses exactly **3 degrees of freedom** — translation along the **X-axis**, translation along the **Y-axis**, and rotation about the **Z-axis** (perpendicular to the plane). When two or more links are joined by kinematic pairs (joints), each joint removes one or more of these freedoms. The mobility analysis quantifies the *net remaining freedom* after all constraints are applied.

**Kutzbach's Criterion** and **Grubler's Criterion** are two mathematically equivalent, classical algebraic equations used to compute the mobility of planar (and spatial) mechanisms composed of rigid links and ideal joints. Both yield the same scalar integer for non-degenerate, non-special geometries.

### 1.2 The Two Names — Same Math, Different Histories

> [!NOTE]
> **Kutzbach's Equation** was proposed by **Ludwig Kutzbach** (Germany) in **1929**, derived independently for planar linkages.
>
> **Grubler's Criterion** was proposed earlier by **Martin Grübler** (Switzerland) in **1883**, and is more general — it works for both **planar and spatial** mechanisms.
>
> For purely planar (2D) mechanisms, the two equations collapse to the **same algebraic form**, hence the dual naming convention in KTU textbooks.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Puppet on Strings" Analogy

Imagine a wooden marionette puppet (the *output link*) suspended from the ceiling (the *ground link*) by several inextensible strings (the *kinematic pairs*).

*   Each string, when taut, restricts **one direction of motion** of the puppet.
*   If you have **3 strings**, the puppet can still *rotate* and *tilt* in 3D — i.e., it still has *mobility*.
*   If you have **6 non-redundant strings** properly arranged, the puppet becomes **completely immobilized** (Mobility = 0) — it is now a *structure*, not a mechanism.
*   If you have **5 strings**, you can wiggle the puppet with **one independent input** (Mobility = 1) — this is the *simplest mechanism*.

In a real planar linkage, the *strings* are the **kinematic pairs (joints)**, and the *puppet* is the *moving link assembly*. The math simply *counts* how many freedoms are left after every constraint is applied.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

*   **DOF per unconstrained rigid link in 2D:** **3** (Two translations + one rotation)
*   **DOF per unconstrained rigid link in 3D:** **6** (Three translations + three rotations)
*   **DOF removed by a lower pair (1-DOF joint, e.g., pin, slider):** **2** in 2D
*   **DOF removed by a higher pair (2-DOF joint, e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh):** **1** in 2D
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Foundation — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The derivation of mobility rests on a *deficit-counting* argument:

**Step 1 — Total Unconstrained Freedom:**
If we have *n* rigid links in a planar mechanism (one of which is the fixed *ground link*), and we ignore all joints, each link can move independently with 3 degrees of freedom.
Total unconstrained DOF = **3n**

**Step 2 — Removing the Ground Constraint:**
The ground link is fixed, so it has 0 DOF. We therefore subtract the 3 freedoms belonging to the ground.
Net available DOF for moving links = **3(n − 1)**

**Step 3 — Accounting for Joint Constraints:**
*   A **lower pair** (1-DOF joint) connects two links and removes **2 degrees of freedom** from their relative motion (it constrains 2 of the 3 planar coordinates).
*   A **higher pair** (2-DOF joint, like a point/line contact) removes only **1 degree of freedom** (it permits sliding and rolling, but constrains penetration).

If *j₁* = number of lower pairs and *j₂* = number of higher pairs, then total DOF removed = **2j₁ + j₂**.

**Step 4 — Net Mobility:**
The remaining freedom is the mobility M.

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Result:**
*   If **M = 1** → mechanism is a *simple chain* with one input (e.g., four-bar linkage).
*   If **M = 0** → it is a *structure* (statically determinate, no motion).
*   If **M > 1** → *multiple inputs* required; could indicate a redundant mechanism.
*   If **M < 0** → *over-constrained* / impossible geometry (unless special geometry resolves redundancies — *Meyerhof's paradox*).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula (Planar) | Formula (Spatial) | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Mobility / DOF** | $M$ | $3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | dimensionless | Kutzbach/Grubler |
| Number of links | $n$ | — | — | count | includes ground |
| Lower pairs (1-DOF joints) | $j_1$ | — | — | count | pin, slider, revolute, prismatic |
| Higher pairs (2-DOF joints) | $j_2$ | — | — | count | cam, gear mesh, ball-on-plane |
| Intermediate pairs | $j_3$ | — | — | count | only in spatial |
| Number of independent loops | $L$ | $L = n - 1 - j_1$ | $L = n - 1 - j_1 - j_2$ | count | topology check |
| Identical mobility equation (planar) | $M$ | $3L - 2j_2$ | — | dimensionless | alternate form |
| Four-bar special case | $M$ | $3(4) - 2(4) - 0 = 1$ | — | dimensionless | canonical example |
| Slider-crank special case | $M$ | $3(4) - 2(4) - 0 = 1$ | — | dimensionless | one revolute replaced by prismatic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Table Note:** The spatial Grübler formula includes joint types *j₃*, *j₄*, *j₅* with corresponding DOF removal of 3, 2, and 1 respectively. For KTU 2024 Module 1, the **planar equation** is the primary focus.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mobility analysis is the *gatekeeper* of every mechanism design:

*   **Automotive Engineering:** Verifying that a windshield wiper, wiper linkage, or suspension has exactly M = 1.
*   **Robotics:** Determining if a serial robot arm has the right DOF to reach a 6-DOF task space.
*   **Machine Tools:** Checking that a planar slider-crank has the correct mobility for conversion of rotary → linear motion.
*   **Structural Engineering:** Confirming that a truss is a *structure* (M = 0) and not accidentally a *mechanism* (which would collapse).
*   **Biomechanics:** Modelling human joints and limb chains to count physiological DOF.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem is given, *always list n, j₁, j₂ explicitly* before substituting. Board examiners award partial credit for systematic tabulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (Planar)

We start from the *rigid body in plane* principle.

**Step 1:** Consider a mechanism with **n** rigid links, one of which is the *ground* (frame) link.

**Step 2:** Each unconstrained rigid body in a 2D plane has exactly **3 degrees of freedom** (translation in x, translation in y, rotation about z).

**Step 3:** Total degrees of freedom if all links were free to move independently:

$$DOF_{total} = 3 \times n = 3n$$

**Step 4:** The ground link is fixed (welded to earth). It cannot move. Therefore, subtract its 3 DOFs from the total:

$$DOF_{available} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$$

**Step 5:** Now consider the kinematic pairs. Each joint between two links *removes* some relative freedom.

A **lower pair** (e.g., revolute or prismatic joint) in 2D removes **2 relative DOFs** between the two connected links.

If there are *j₁* lower pairs, total DOFs removed by lower pairs:

$$\text{Constraint}_{lower} = 2 \times j_1 = 2j_1$$

A **higher pair** (e.g., cam-follower contact, gear teeth) in 2D removes only **1 relative DOF** (penetration is prevented, but sliding and rotation about the contact normal are still permitted).

If there are *j₂* higher pairs:

$$\text{Constraint}_{higher} = 1 \times j_2 = j_2$$

**Step 6:** Net mobility of the mechanism:

$$M = DOF_{available} - \text{Constraint}_{lower} - \text{Constraint}_{higher}$$

Substituting all terms:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

This is the canonical **Kutzbach Equation** for planar mechanisms. (Q.E.D.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Grubler's Criterion (General Form)

Grübler's approach uses *loop topology* rather than free-body counting.

**Step 1:** In any closed kinematic chain, the **number of independent loops** *L* is given by:

$$L = j_1 + j_2 - n + 1 \quad \text{(for planar)}$$

Solving for *n − 1*:

$$n - 1 = j_1 + j_2 - L$$

**Step 2:** Substituting into the Kutzbach expression:

$$M = 3(j_1 + j_2 - L) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Expand the 3-multiplication:

$$M = 3j_1 + 3j_2 - 3L - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Combine like terms:

$$M = (3j_1 - 2j_1) + (3j_2 - j_2) - 3L$$

$$\boxed{M = j_1 + 2j_2 - 3L}$$

This is the **Grübler form** of mobility. It is mathematically equivalent to Kutzbach's equation but expressed in terms of the *number of independent loops* L, which is often more intuitive for multi-loop mechanisms.

**Step 3:** Special case: If a mechanism has **no higher pairs** ($j_2 = 0$), the formula reduces to:

$$M = j_1 - 3L$$

For a single-loop four-bar: *j₁* = 4, *L* = 1 → M = 4 − 3 = **1** ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A planar mechanism has **n = 7 links**, **j₁ = 9 revolute joints**, and **j₂ = 1 cam pair**. Find its mobility.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Total links | $n$ | 7 |
| Lower pairs | $j_1$ | 9 |
| Higher pairs | $j_2$ | 1 |

**Step 2 — Apply Kutzbach's equation:**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and expand:**

$$M = 3(7-1) - 2(9) - (1)$$

$$M = 3(6) - 18 - 1$$

$$M = 18 - 18 - 1$$

$$\boxed{M = -1}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A negative mobility means the mechanism is **statically over-constrained (a structure, not a mechanism)** under generic geometry. It can only move if there is a *special geometric coincidence* (passive DOF, e.g., collinear pivots in a Stephenson or Watt six-bar with coincident points).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** A negative M does *not* automatically mean an error. You **must** check for *special geometry* (e.g., equal link lengths, coincident pivots) before declaring the mechanism a structure. This is called **Meyerhof's Paradox** and is a frequent 14-mark question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Planar Mechanism Templates

| Mechanism | n | j₁ (revolute/prismatic) | j₂ (cam/gear) | L (loops) | M | Status |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Four-bar linkage | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Five-bar (two-input) | 5 | 5 | 0 | 2 | 2 | Two-DOF mechanism |
| Six-bar Watt | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Six-bar Stephenson | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Truss (triangulated) | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | 0 | Structure |
| Gear train (simple) | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | Mechanism |
| Cam-follower | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Mechanism |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mobility Analysis Decision Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *logical decision flow* a KTU student should follow when solving any mobility problem. This is the *engineering thought process* in visual form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Mechanism] --> B[Count Number of Links n]
    B --> C[Include Ground Link in Count]
    C --> D[Classify All Joints]
    D --> E{Is Joint a Lower Pair?}
    E -->|Yes - pin, slider| F[Increment j1 Counter]
    E -->|No - proceed| G{Is Joint a Higher Pair?}
    G -->|Yes - cam, gear| H[Increment j2 Counter]
    G -->|No| I[Reclassify Joint Type]
    F --> J{More Joints to Check?}
    H --> J
    J -->|Yes| E
    J -->|No| K[Compute M using Kutzbach Formula]
    K --> L[M = 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    L --> M{What is the value of M?}
    M -->|M equals 1| N[Valid Single DOF Mechanism]
    M -->|M equals 0| O[Structure - No Motion]
    M -->|M greater than 1| P[Multi DOF Mechanism - Needs Multiple Inputs]
    M -->|M less than 0| Q[Check for Special Geometry or Redundant Constraints]
    Q --> R{Is Geometry Special?}
    R -->|Yes| S[Passive DOF Exists - Mechanism may Move]
    R -->|No| T[Over-Constrained Structure]
    N --> U[End - Mobility Analysis Complete]
    O --> U
    P --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Joint Classification Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kinematic Pair] --> B{Contact Type}
    B -->|Area Contact Lower Pair| C[Lower Pair j1 - Removes 2 DOF]
    B -->|Point or Line Contact Higher Pair| D[Higher Pair j2 - Removes 1 DOF]
    C --> E[Examples: Revolute, Prismatic, Screw, Cylindrical, Spherical, Planar]
    D --> F[Examples: Cam Follower, Gear Mesh, Wheel on Road]
    C --> G[Constraint Value = 2 per joint]
    D --> H[Constraint Value = 1 per joint]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Mapping — Kutzbach vs Grübler Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanism with n links] --> B[Method 1: Kutzbach Approach]
    A --> C[Method 2: Grübler Approach]
    B --> D[Compute 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    C --> E[Compute Loops L equals j1 plus j2 minus n plus 1]
    E --> F[Compute M equals j1 plus 2 j2 minus 3 L]
    D --> G[Final Mobility Value M]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Both Methods Give Same M?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Mechanism is Kinematically Valid]
    H -->|No| J[Recount Links and Joints]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C`...) and labels contain plain uppercase text only. No bold/italic markdown inside quoted labels to prevent rendering failure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term *mobility* of a mechanism. State Kutzbach's equation and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility (M)** of a mechanism is the total number of independent inputs (or degrees of freedom) required to bring the mechanism to a definite configuration.

**Kutzbach's Equation (for planar mechanisms):**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
*   **M** = mobility (degrees of freedom)
*   **n** = total number of links (including the fixed ground link)
*   **j₁** = number of lower pairs (1-DOF joints such as revolute, prismatic)
*   **j₂** = number of higher pairs (2-DOF joints such as cam-follower, gear teeth)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the difference between a *lower pair* and a *higher pair*? Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A **lower pair** is a kinematic pair in which the two links have *surface* (area) contact. It generally has 1 degree of freedom and removes 2 relative DOFs.
> **Examples:** Revolute (pin) joint, Prismatic (slider) joint.

A **higher pair** is a kinematic pair in which the two links have *point* or *line* contact. It generally has 2 degrees of freedom and removes 1 relative DOF.
**Examples:** Cam-follower contact, Gear teeth mesh.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A planar mechanism consists of **8 links** connected by **10 revolute joints** and **1 cam pair**. Determine its mobility using Kutzbach's criterion. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the *Grubler's criterion* for mobility analysis. A six-bar Watt linkage has 7 lower pairs. Find its mobility and number of independent loops. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Number of links | $n$ | 8 |
| Lower pairs (revolute) | $j_1$ | 10 |
| Higher pairs (cam) | $j_2$ | 1 |

**Formula (Kutzbach):**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 1 — Compute the unconstrained freedom term:**

$$3(n-1) = 3(8-1) = 3 \times 7 = 21$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lower-pair constraint term:**

$$2j_1 = 2 \times 10 = 20$$

**Step 3 — Compute the higher-pair constraint term:**

$$j_2 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Kutzbach's equation:**

$$M = 21 - 20 - 1$$

$$\boxed{M = 0}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since M = 0, the mechanism is a **structure** (statically determinate, no input DOF) under generic geometry. For it to function as a mechanism, the cam pair contact or one revolute joint must release a passive degree of freedom.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Grubler's Criterion Statement:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grubler's Criterion** states that the mobility of a planar mechanism is given by:
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> or equivalently, in terms of independent loops L:
> $$M = j_1 + 2j_2 - 3L$$
> where $L = j_1 + j_2 - n + 1$.

**For a six-bar Watt linkage with 7 lower pairs:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Number of links | $n$ | 6 |
| Lower pairs | $j_1$ | 7 |
| Higher pairs | $j_2$ | 0 |

**Step 1 — Compute the number of independent loops L:**

$$L = j_1 + j_2 - n + 1 = 7 + 0 - 6 + 1 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute mobility using Grübler's loop form:**

$$M = j_1 + 2j_2 - 3L$$

$$M = 7 + 2(0) - 3(2)$$

$$M = 7 - 6 = 1$$

$$\boxed{M = 1, \quad L = 2}$$

The Watt six-bar is a single-DOF mechanism with two independent loops.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** What is *Meyerhof's paradox*? Explain with a suitable example why the Kutzbach equation may give a *negative mobility* for an actually movable mechanism. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A mechanism has **n = 6 links**, **j₁ = 8 revolute joints**, and **j₂ = 1 cam-follower pair**. Compute the mobility. If a redundant constraint is present, identify whether the mechanism can still move. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Meyerhof's Paradox** refers to the apparent contradiction in which Kutzbach's equation gives a mobility of **zero or negative** for a mechanism that, by inspection, has *one or more passive degrees of freedom* due to special geometric conditions (e.g., coincident pivots, collinear axes, or equal-length links arranged in a specific way).

**Classic Example:** A **Stephenson III six-bar linkage** with three collinear pivots in a single ternary link.

**Explanation:**
The Kutzbach/Grübler equation is a *generic* (topological) formula — it counts only the *type* of joint, not its *specific geometric arrangement*. When two or more joints share the *same physical location* (e.g., two revolute pivots superimposed), they form a *passive DOF* (a *redundant* or *idle* degree of freedom) that the generic count does not register. The mechanism can move, but the equation reports M = 0 or M < 0.

To resolve this, the student must:
1.  Identify special geometric coincidences.
2.  Reduce the effective link or joint count by removing the redundancy.
3.  Re-apply the formula to get the *actual* mobility.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Number of links | $n$ | 6 |
| Lower pairs (revolute) | $j_1$ | 8 |
| Higher pairs (cam) | $j_2$ | 1 |

**Step 1 — Apply Kutzbach's equation:**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

$$M = 3(6-1) - 2(8) - (1)$$

$$M = 3(5) - 16 - 1$$

$$M = 15 - 16 - 1$$

$$\boxed{M = -2}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

A negative mobility of **−2** indicates **two redundant constraints** in the generic geometry. The mechanism has *too many* lower pairs for the number of links.

**Step 3 — Check for passive DOFs:**

For the mechanism to actually move, the geometry must release at least **2 passive DOFs**. This could occur if, for example:
*   Two revolute joints are *coincident* (passive DOF = 1 each, contributing 2 to the count).
*   One cam pair is in *rolling-without-slipping* contact, releasing 1 additional passive DOF.

In the absence of such special geometry, the mechanism is a **rigid structure**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Mobility Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1.  **Forgetting the ground link:** Always include the *frame* (ground) in the count of *n*. Many students write *n* = 4 for a four-bar, but if the question gives the data with ground excluded, you must add 1. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 2.  **Wrong joint classification:** A *ball-and-socket* joint is a *spherical pair* (3-DOF lower pair) in 3D, but in 2D it degenerates to a *pin* (1-DOF). Misclassifying it costs *2 marks*.
> 3.  **Ignoring higher pairs:** A cam-follower or gear-mesh pair is *not* a revolute joint. It contributes to *j₂*, not *j₁*. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 4.  **Blindly accepting negative M:** A negative mobility is not the *end* of the problem. You must check for special geometry (Meyerhof) and conclude with a clear interpretation. *Loss: up to 3 marks.*
> 5.  **No units or state convention:** Always state "M is dimensionless" or write M = 1 (no units). *Loss: 0.5 mark* in strict KTU valuation.
> 6.  **Skipping the "interpretation" step:** Board examiners require a concluding sentence: *"Therefore, the mechanism is a single-DOF mechanism."* *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mobility Analysis (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

*   **Mobility (M)** = number of independent inputs needed to define the configuration of a mechanism.
*   **Kutzbach Equation (Planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
*   **Grubler Equation (Planar, in terms of loops):** $M = j_1 + 2j_2 - 3L$
*   **Grubler Equation (General Spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
*   **Number of independent loops:** $L = j_1 + j_2 - n + 1$ (planar)
*   **Always include the ground link** in the count of *n*.
*   **Lower pair (j₁):** 1-DOF joint, removes 2 DOFs in 2D — examples: revolute, prismatic.
*   **Higher pair (j₂):** 2-DOF joint, removes 1 DOF in 2D — examples: cam, gear.
*   **M = 1** → single-input mechanism (most common, e.g., four-bar).
*   **M = 0** → structure (statically determinate).
*   **M > 1** → multi-input mechanism (e.g., five-bar with two cranks).
*   **M < 0** → over-constrained, but check for *passive DOFs* (Meyerhof's paradox).
*   **Meyerhof's Paradox:** Generic mobility formula may underestimate actual mobility when special geometry (coincident pivots, equal links) releases *redundant* or *passive* degrees of freedom.
*   **Kutzbach vs Grübler:** Same equation for planar; Grübler is more general (handles spatial cases).
*   **For a four-bar linkage (canonical example):** *n* = 4, *j₁* = 4, *j₂* = 0 → **M = 1** ✓
*   **For a slider-crank:** *n* = 4, *j₁* = 4, *j₂* = 0 → **M = 1** ✓
*   **For a five-bar two-crank:** *n* = 5, *j₁* = 5, *j₂* = 0 → **M = 2** ✓
*   **Topological check (consistency test):** $L = n - 1 - j_1$ for simple single-pair chains.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Mobility Analysis: Kutzbach and Grubler's Criterion**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PBMET404 Mechanics of Machinery*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grashof's Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grashof's Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — GRASHOF'S CRITERION

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grashof's Criterion (Grashof's Law of Four-Bar Linkages):**
> *"In a four-bar kinematic chain, if the sum of the lengths of the shortest link and the longest link is **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two links, then **at least one link can make a complete revolution** about its pivot. If the inequality is violated, **no link** can complete a full rotation, and the mechanism becomes a triple-rocker."*

Mathematically, if the four link lengths are denoted as $s$ (shortest), $l$ (longest), $p$, and $q$ (the other two links):

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

This single inequality governs the **entire motion-behavior classification** of planar four-bar linkages, making it the foundational kinematic design rule in machine design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swinging Door" Intuition

Imagine a **window-shutter mechanism** (the common stay-arms you see on roadside shops):

- If the **shutter-arm (short link)** is small enough, the **shopkeeper can rotate the shutter arm fully** around the hinge to open or close the window completely. The arm can "go around" — this is a **rotating link (crank)**.
- If the shutter-arm is **too long** (making it the longest link of the four), the **shutter can only oscillate** back and forth; it gets "stuck" before completing a full circle. This is a **rocker** (oscillating link).

The critical threshold at which the behavior **flips** from "can rotate fully" to "can only oscillate" is exactly what **Grashof's Criterion** quantifies. It is the *tipping point* of motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples:**
> - **Crank-Rocker:** Pumping of bicycle pedals, sewing machine drive.
> - **Double-Crank (Drag-Link):** Parallelogram-type steering linkages in older vehicles.
> - **Double-Rocker (Grashof):** Wagon-tail-gate mechanisms.
> - **Triple-Rocker (Non-Grashof):** Stone-crushing jaw mechanisms, foldable furniture hinges.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Symbols (Universal Kinematic Convention):**
> - $s$ — Length of **shortest link** (**m** or **mm**)
> - $l$ — Length of **longest link** (**m** or **mm**)
> - $p, q$ — Lengths of the **two remaining links** (**m** or **mm**)
> - The **sum constant** $\sigma = s + l + p + q$ (total material/geometry factor)
> - **Grashof's inequality** is **dimensionless** (lengths cancel, ratio-based test)

No universal physical constants (like $g$, $h$, $k$) are needed because Grashof's Criterion is a **purely geometric** feasibility condition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Background — The Four-Bar Linkage Foundation

A planar **four-bar chain** consists of four rigid links connected in a closed loop by four revolute (pin) joints. The link fixed to the ground (or frame) is called the **fixed link (frame, ground link)**, and the link opposite to it is the **coupler link**. The two links connected to the frame are the **side-links (or cranks/rockers depending on motion)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification of Links in a Four-Bar:**
> - **Fixed link (frame)** — usually denoted $a$ or 1
> - **Input side-link (crank or rocker)** — usually denoted $b$ or 2
> - **Coupler (floating link)** — usually denoted $c$ or 3
> - **Output side-link (crank or rocker)** — usually denoted $d$ or 4

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation of Grashof's Law

Grashof's Law emerges from analyzing whether a closed quadrilateral can be assembled where one vertex traces a **complete circle** (full revolution) around its adjacent pivot, while the other three links adjust their orientations to maintain closure.

**Step 1: Rank the four link lengths.**
Sort the link lengths and identify:
- $s$ → shortest
- $l$ → longest
- $p, q$ → intermediate links

**Step 2: Apply the Grashof Inequality.**
$$s + l \leq p + q$$

**Step 3: Classify the mechanism type** based on (a) the inequality outcome and (b) which link is the **shortest link**.

**Step 4: Determine the type of motion** for each of the four links (full rotation vs. oscillation).

---

## 2.3 Mechanism Classification Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Inequality | Shortest Link Position | Mechanism Type | Motion Behavior |
|---|------------|------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $s + l < p + q$ (strict) | Fixed (ground) link | **Crank-Rocker Mechanism** | Shortest link rotates fully (crank); opposite side-link oscillates (rocker) |
| 2 | $s + l < p + q$ (strict) | Adjacent to ground (side-link) | **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)** | Both side-links rotate fully; coupler oscillates-or-flips |
| 3 | $s + l < p + q$ (strict) | Coupler (opposite to ground) | **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** | Only coupler rotates fully; both side-links oscillate |
| 4 | $s + l > p + q$ (strict) | Any | **Triple-Rocker (Non-Grashof)** | **No link** can complete a full rotation; all three movable links oscillate |
| 5 | $s + l = p + q$ (equality) | Any | **Change-Point Mechanism** | Boundary case; degenerate motion (link can be locked or fully rotate depending on exact pose) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "SFA → CDR / DR / TR":**
> - **S**hortest link is **F**ixed → **C**rank-**R**ocker
> - **S**hortest link is **A**djacent → **D**ouble-cRank
> - **S**hortest link is **O**pposite (Coupler) → **D**ouble-**R**ocker
> - If inequality fails → **T**riple-**R**ocker

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---------|------------|---------|-------------|
| Grashof's Inequality | $s + l \leq p + q$ | Test for full-rotation capability | Initial design feasibility check |
| Grashof's Ratio (F) | $F = \dfrac{s + l}{p + q}$ | Dimensionless motion index | $F < 1$ (Grashof), $F = 1$ (change-point), $F > 1$ (non-Grashof) |
| Special Case | $s + l = p + q$ | Degenerate change-point linkage | Lockable or full-rotation linkage |
| Crank Existence Condition | $s + l \leq p + q$ AND $s$ = ground or adjacent | At least one link rotates fully | Crank-rocker, drag-link validation |
| Non-Grashof Condition | $s + l > p + q$ | Triple-rocker | All links oscillate, no full rotation |
| Transmission Angle (μ) | $\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2 + 2ad\cos\theta_2}{2bc}\right)$ | Force-transmission quality | Checked alongside Grashof (Module 2) |

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠️ **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables (NOT `|`)** — see `$\vert s+l\vert$` style. The above table avoids raw pipes inside math for markdown safety.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Grashof's Criterion

1. **Preliminary Design Filter:** Before performing detailed kinematic synthesis (position, velocity, acceleration analysis), Grashof's criterion instantly tells the designer whether the desired motion (full rotation or oscillation) is even *physically possible*.
2. **Linkage Synthesis:** When designing machines like presses, agitators, or oscillating-fan mechanisms, designers deliberately select link lengths to *engineer* the Grashof type they need.
3. **Quick Failure Diagnosis:** If a prototype linkage "jams" or fails to complete motion, Grashof's check is the first diagnostic — typically a non-Grashof configuration was inadvertently built.
4. **Path & Motion Generation:** The Grashof condition is foundational for advanced synthesis techniques like the **Freudenstein equation** and **Burmester theory** in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Geometric Derivation of Grashof's Inequality

Consider a four-bar linkage $ABCD$ with:
- Fixed link (ground) = $AD$ of length $a$
- Input link (driver) = $AB$ of length $b$
- Coupler = $BC$ of length $c$
- Output link (follower) = $CD$ of length $d$

Place $A$ at the origin, with $D$ on the x-axis at distance $a$. Let the input link rotate by angle $\theta_2$, and let the output link be at angle $\theta_4$.

### 3.1.1 Closure of the Vector Loop

The four links form a closed polygon, so the sum of position vectors is zero:

$$\vec{AB} + \vec{BC} + \vec{CD} + \vec{DA} = 0$$

In complex-exponential form, with $j = \sqrt{-1}$:

$$b \cdot e^{j\theta_2} + c \cdot e^{j\theta_3} - d \cdot e^{j\theta_4} - a = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$b \cdot e^{j\theta_2} - a = d \cdot e^{j\theta_4} - c \cdot e^{j\theta_3}$$

### 3.1.2 Magnitude Condition for Crank (Link $b$) to Fully Rotate

For link $b$ to rotate a complete $360°$ around $A$, the pivot $B$ must trace a **complete circle** of radius $b$. For this circle to permit a corresponding position of $C$ (and hence $D$) on the other side, the distance from $B$ to $D$ must be physically realizable for *all* positions of $\theta_2 \in [0, 2\pi]$.

The distance $BD$ must satisfy the triangle inequality for triangle $BCD$ **and** triangle $ABD$ simultaneously, for every angular position.

**Triangle ABD (with link $b$ fully rotating):**
The link $AD = a$ is fixed. As $B$ traces a circle of radius $b$, the distance $BD$ varies between:

$$BD_{\min} = \vert a - b \vert \quad \text{and} \quad BD_{\max} = a + b$$

**Triangle BCD (with link $c$ as coupler and $d$ as output):**
For the triangle $BCD$ to close physically (i.e., for the three links to form a valid triangle), the triangle inequality must hold:

$$BD \leq c + d \quad \text{(sum of two sides ≥ third side)}$$

The **worst case** (most restrictive) is when $BD$ is at its **maximum**, i.e., $BD = a + b$. So we require:

$$a + b \leq c + d$$

This is the **classic Grashof condition** when the input is the shortest link!

### 3.1.3 Generalization — The Grashof Inequality for Any Shortest Link

By symmetry, the same analysis applies regardless of which link is the shortest, provided the **shortest link** is the one allowed to fully rotate. Sorting all four link lengths into $s$ (shortest), $l$ (longest), and $p, q$ (the others), the Grashof condition becomes:

$$\boxed{\,s + l \leq p + q\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Inequality:**
> - $s + l$ is the sum of the **extreme** (shortest + longest) link lengths.
> - $p + q$ is the sum of the **intermediate** link lengths.
> - If the extremes' sum is **less than or equal** to the intermediates' sum, full rotation is geometrically permitted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Grashof Ratio $F$

Define the **Grashof Ratio** as the test quantity:

$$F = \frac{s + l}{p + q}$$

**Step 1 — Substitution of the inequality:**

$$s + l \leq p + q \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{s+l}{p+q} \leq 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad F \leq 1$$

**Step 2 — Classification using $F$:**

$$F < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Grashof Linkage (full rotation possible)}$$

$$F = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Change-Point (boundary)}$$

$$F > 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Non-Grashof (no full rotation)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Classification Walkthrough

**Problem:** A four-bar linkage has link lengths:
- $L_1 = 80 \text{ mm}$ (fixed/ground)
- $L_2 = 30 \text{ mm}$
- $L_3 = 100 \text{ mm}$ (coupler)
- $L_4 = 70 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Sort the links in ascending order:**
$$30,\; 70,\; 80,\; 100$$

So:
- $s = 30$ mm (shortest)
- $p = 70$ mm
- $q = 80$ mm
- $l = 100$ mm (longest)

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's inequality:**

$$s + l = 30 + 100 = 130 \text{ mm}$$

$$p + q = 70 + 80 = 150 \text{ mm}$$

$$130 \leq 150 \quad \checkmark \;\; \text{GRASHOF}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Grashof ratio:**

$$F = \frac{130}{150} = 0.867 < 1 \;\; \text{(Grashof confirmed)}$$

**Step 4 — Classify based on shortest-link position:**
- The shortest link is $L_2 = 30$ mm.
- $L_1 = 80$ mm is the ground (fixed) link.
- $L_2$ is **adjacent** to ground (since it's the input side-link).

**Step 5 — Final classification:**

$$\boxed{\text{Grashof, shortest link adjacent to ground} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{DOUBLE-CRANK (DRAG-LINK) MECHANISM}}$$

Both side-links (input $L_2$ and output $L_4$) can rotate fully; the coupler $L_3$ oscillates-or-flips.

---

## 3.4 The Inverse Problem — Designing for a Target Mechanism

If the engineer **wants** a crank-rocker mechanism with a specific input link length, the design must satisfy:

1. **Grashof's condition:** $s + l \leq p + q$
2. **Shortest link must be the ground link** (so input is a crank).
3. **Input link** must be the **shortest** for it to be a crank.

**Example design requirement:** Build a crank-rocker with input $b = 25$ mm (this will be $s$), output $d = 60$ mm, coupler $c = 70$ mm.

Find the maximum allowable ground link $a$ (this will be $l$).

Grashof requires $s + l \leq p + q$, so:

$$25 + a \leq 60 + 70 = 130$$

$$a \leq 130 - 25 = 105 \text{ mm}$$

Thus the ground link must be **≤ 105 mm** for the mechanism to function as a crank-rocker.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Grashof Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Four-Bar Linkage with Links S, P, Q, L"] --> B["Identify Shortest Link S and Longest Link L"]
    B --> C["Compute Sum S Plus L"]
    B --> D["Compute Sum P Plus Q"]
    C --> E{"Is S Plus L Less Than or Equal to P Plus Q"}
    D --> E
    E -->|"YES (Grashof)"| F{"Where is the Shortest Link"}
    E -->|"NO (Non-Grashof)"| G["TRIPLE ROCKER MECHANISM - No link rotates fully"]
    F -->|"Fixed (Ground)"| H["CRANK ROCKER - Input rotates, Output oscillates"]
    F -->|"Adjacent (Side-Link)"| I["DOUBLE CRANK DRAG LINK - Both side-links rotate fully"]
    F -->|"Opposite (Coupler)"| J["DOUBLE ROCKER GRASHOF - Only coupler rotates fully"]
    E -->|"EQUALITY (S+L = P+Q)"| K["CHANGE POINT - Degenerate boundary case"]
    H --> L["End Classification"]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    G --> L
    K --> L
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Grashof Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["INPUT BLOCK: Link Lengths L1, L2, L3, L4"] --> rankA["RANKING BLOCK: Sort Ascending to Obtain S, P, Q, L"]
    rankA --> computeA["COMPUTE BLOCK: Calculate S plus L and P plus Q"]
    computeA --> testA{"DECISION BLOCK: Grashof Inequality Test"}
    testA -->|"S+L is less than P+Q"| grashA["GRASHOF SUBGRAPH"]
    testA -->|"S+L equals P+Q"| specialA["SPECIAL CASE: Change Point"]
    testA -->|"S+L is greater than P+Q"| nonA["NON GRASHOF: Triple Rocker"]
    grashA --> posA{"POSITION TEST: Where is Shortest Link"}
    posA -->|"On Ground"| outA1["OUTPUT: Crank Rocker"]
    posA -->|"Adjacent to Ground"| outA2["OUTPUT: Double Crank"]
    posA -->|"Opposite to Ground"| outA3["OUTPUT: Double Rocker"]
    outA1 --> endA["End of Classification"]
    outA2 --> endA
    outA3 --> endA
    specialA --> endA
    nonA --> endA
```

---

## 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Block-Level Representation)

Since Grashof's Criterion is a geometric condition on a four-bar linkage, a Mermaid block schematic is the cleanest representation. The physical interpretation is:

| Component | Physical Interpretation |
|-----------|-------------------------|
| Four links $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ | Four rigid bars connected by 4 pin joints (revolute pairs) |
| Ground link $L_1$ | The fixed reference link (often the longest in crank-rockers) |
| Side-links $L_2, L_4$ | The two links adjacent to ground; can be cranks (rotating) or rockers (oscillating) |
| Coupler $L_3$ | The "floating" link opposite the ground |
| Motion cycle | Trace of coupler point → coupler curve (relevant for Module 3) |

> [!NOTE]
> For a visual sketch of the linkage types, refer to standard textbook figures: the **crank-rocker** has one short side-link rotating fully with the opposite side-link oscillating; the **double-crank** has both side-links parallel-or-crossing with full rotations; the **double-rocker** has a short coupler that rotates while side-links oscillate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Grashof's Law for a four-bar linkage. What is its significance in mechanism design?**

**Model Answer:**

Grashof's Law states that in a four-bar kinematic chain, the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links must be less than or equal to the sum of the lengths of the other two links for at least one link to make a complete revolution. Mathematically:

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

**Significance:**
1. It is a preliminary **design feasibility test** that determines whether the desired motion (full rotation or oscillation) is geometrically possible.
2. It classifies the mechanism into one of four types: crank-rocker, double-crank, double-rocker, or triple-rocker.
3. It saves design time by eliminating infeasible configurations **before** detailed kinematic analysis.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**A four-bar mechanism has links of lengths 50 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, and 130 mm. Identify the type of mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Sort the links ascending:**
$$50,\; 80,\; 100,\; 130$$

- $s = 50$ mm (shortest)
- $p = 80$ mm
- $q = 100$ mm
- $l = 130$ mm (longest)

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's inequality:**

$$s + l = 50 + 130 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

$$p + q = 80 + 100 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

$$180 \leq 180 \quad \checkmark \;\; \text{(equality case)}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\text{Change-Point Mechanism (Boundary Case)}}$$

Since $s + l = p + q$, this is a **change-point** linkage — depending on the exact angular configuration, the mechanism is on the verge of either full rotation or complete jamming.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** *State and explain Grashof's criterion for a four-bar linkage. Classify the various possible mechanisms based on Grashof's rule.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A four-bar linkage has three links of lengths 25 mm, 40 mm, and 50 mm. The fourth link (ground) is to be designed such that the mechanism operates as a crank-rocker. Find the range of permissible lengths of the ground link.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Grashof's Criterion:**
For a four-bar chain with link lengths $s$ (shortest), $l$ (longest), $p$, and $q$, the criterion states:

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

**Classification Table:**

| Mechanism Type | Grashof Condition | Shortest Link Position | Motion |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Crank-Rocker | $s + l < p + q$ | Ground | One crank + one rocker |
| Double-Crank (Drag-Link) | $s + l < p + q$ | Adjacent to ground | Both cranks rotate |
| Double-Rocker (Grashof) | $s + l < p + q$ | Coupler (opposite) | Both rockers oscillate; coupler rotates |
| Triple-Rocker (Non-Grashof) | $s + l > p + q$ | Any | No full rotation; all oscillate |
| Change-Point | $s + l = p + q$ | Any | Boundary (locking or rotating) |

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Given:** Three links: $25$ mm, $40$ mm, $50$ mm. Let the ground link have length $L_g$.

**Step 1 — For a crank-rocker, the ground link must be the longest, and the shortest link (25 mm) must be the input (a crank rotating fully).**

**Step 2 — Therefore:**
- $s = 25$ mm (shortest, fixed as input)
- Intermediate links: $40$ mm and $50$ mm
- $l = L_g$ (ground link, must be longest)

**Step 3 — Apply Grashof's inequality for crank-rocker validity:**

$$s + l \leq p + q$$

$$25 + L_g \leq 40 + 50 = 90$$

$$L_g \leq 90 - 25 = 65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Also enforce that $L_g$ is the longest link:**

$$L_g \geq 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the constraints:**

$$\boxed{50 \text{ mm} \leq L_g \leq 65 \text{ mm}}$$

The ground link must be designed in this range for a successful crank-rocker mechanism.

---

### 🔵 QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a)** *Derive the Grashof inequality $s + l \leq p + q$ using the triangle inequality for a four-bar linkage.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For a four-bar linkage with links 60 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, and 120 mm (where the 60 mm link is the fixed/ground link), classify the mechanism and state which links can rotate fully and which oscillate.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Setup:** Consider four-bar $ABCD$ with lengths $a$ (ground $AD$), $b$ (input $AB$), $c$ (coupler $BC$), $d$ (output $CD$).

**Step 1 — Let link $b$ be the input crank, rotating fully around $A$.**

**Step 2 — Pivot $B$ traces a circle of radius $b$ about $A$. The distance $BD$ varies between:**

$$BD_{\min} = a - b \quad \text{to} \quad BD_{\max} = a + b$$

(assuming $a > b$; otherwise the lower bound is $b - a$.)

**Step 3 — Triangle $BCD$ must remain geometrically valid (triangle inequality) for **every** position of $B$. The most restrictive case is $BD = a + b$ (maximum distance):**

$$BD \leq c + d$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$a + b \leq c + d$$

**Step 5 — Sort all four links: $s$ (shortest), $l$ (longest), $p, q$ (others).** By labeling, this generalizes to:

$$\boxed{s + l \leq p + q} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Sort the link lengths ascending:**

$$60,\; 80,\; 100,\; 120 \text{ mm}$$

- $s = 60$ mm
- $p = 80$ mm
- $q = 100$ mm
- $l = 120$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply Grashof's inequality:**

$$s + l = 60 + 120 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

$$p + q = 80 + 100 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

$$180 \leq 180 \quad \checkmark \;\; \text{(Change-point case)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify shortest link position:**
- The shortest link $s = 60$ mm is the **fixed/ground link** (as given).

**Step 4 — Final Classification:**

$$\boxed{\text{Change-Point Crank-Rocker (Boundary Type)}}$$

In a change-point configuration with shortest link as ground, the input crank and output rocker can rotate/oscillate **without locking** in some assemblies, while in others the mechanism will **lock up** depending on the initial angular configuration. Designers treat this as a **non-ideal** configuration and avoid it in practice by ensuring strict Grashof inequality ($s + l < p + q$).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Grashof's Criterion Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to sort the links first.** Students directly substitute link lengths into $s + l$ and $p + q$ without identifying which is the shortest and longest. **Always sort ascending first.** (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Confusing the position of the shortest link.** The classification (crank-rocker vs. double-crank vs. double-rocker) depends on whether the shortest link is the **ground**, **side**, or **coupler** link. Drawing a proper labelled diagram is essential. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 
> 3. **Treating equality ($s + l = p + q$) as a Grashof linkage.** This is the **change-point** case — it is **not** a Grashof linkage in the strict sense, and the mechanism can lock up. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the dimension check for "longest link = ground"** in crank-rocker design. The ground link must be the longest for the input to act as a crank. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 
> 5. **Skipping the Grashof ratio $F$ calculation** in design problems. Examiners expect you to compute $F$ and explicitly state whether it is less than, equal to, or greater than 1. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grashof's Criterion — Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> ✅ **Core Formula:** $s + l \leq p + q$ (or $F = \dfrac{s+l}{p+q} \leq 1$)
> 
> ✅ **Always sort** the four link lengths into $s$ (shortest), $p$, $q$, $l$ (longest) **before** applying the criterion.
> 
> ✅ **Four Mechanism Types in Grashof Case ($s + l < p + q$):**
> - Shortest link = **ground** → **Crank-Rocker**
> - Shortest link = **adjacent (side-link)** → **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)**
> - Shortest link = **coupler (opposite)** → **Double-Rocker (Grashof type)**
> 
> ✅ **Non-Grashof Case ($s + l > p + q$):** Always a **Triple-Rocker**; no link rotates fully.
> 
> ✅ **Change-Point Case ($s + l = p + q$):** Boundary/degenerate; mechanism may lock up. **Avoid in design.**
> 
> ✅ **Grashof's Criterion is purely geometric** — no mass, force, or time is involved.
> 
> ✅ **Crank definition:** A link capable of **complete $360°$ rotation** about its fixed pivot.
> 
> ✅ **Rocker definition:** A link that can only **oscillate** between two limiting positions (less than $360°$).
> 
> ✅ **For crank-rocker design:** Ground link is the **longest**, input is the **shortest**.
> 
> ✅ **For double-crank (drag-link) design:** The two side-links must both be **shorter** than the ground link.
> 
> ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the **sorting step, inequality evaluation, and the position of the shortest link** explicitly. Drawing a small labelled diagram of the four-bar with link names earns easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Pairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Pairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Kinematic Pair** is defined as a joint or connection between two or more mechanical links (rigid bodies) which permits relative motion between them. The two links in contact forming a kinematic pair are called **Elements** or **Pair Elements**. The relative motion between these elements is not arbitrary; it is constrained by the nature of contact (surface, line, or point) and the geometry of the connection.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the study of kinematic pairs forms the absolute foundation of Module 1 because every mechanism — from a simple door hinge to a complex robotic arm — is essentially an assembly of these pairs. Without a clear understanding of kinematic pairs, students cannot progress to kinematic chains, mechanisms, inversions, or the degrees of freedom (DOF) calculations that follow in this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Every machine is made up of kinematic links connected by kinematic pairs. A pair essentially reduces the *available* degrees of freedom of a body in space (which is 6 for a free rigid body) by introducing geometric constraints. Identifying the correct pair is the **first step** in any DOF problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human arm. The shoulder is a **ball-and-socket joint** (it allows the upper arm to rotate and swing in many directions). The elbow is a **hinge joint** (it only allows the forearm to bend in one plane). The wrist allows the palm to slide and rotate. 

Each of these joints is a **kinematic pair** — a place where two bones (links) meet and are allowed to move in a specific, restricted way relative to each other. The bones cannot simply pass through one another; the joint dictates exactly *how* they can move. In a machine, the "bones" are steel links, and the "joints" are kinematic pairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A kinematic pair is the "rulebook" that two links follow when they touch — it dictates the type and amount of motion allowed between them.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Link** | A rigid body which forms a part of a machine and has relative motion with other parts. |
| **Pair** | A joint between two links allowing constrained relative motion. |
| **Element** | A link of a kinematic pair (sometimes a link is itself called an element when referring to the pair). |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent parameters required to define the configuration of a link in space. |
| **Constraint** | A condition that restricts the relative motion between two links. |

> [!NOTE]
> A free rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A kinematic pair removes some of these, leaving fewer available DOFs. The relationship is: **Available DOFs = 6 − Number of Independent Constraints**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Pairs

Kinematic pairs are classified into multiple categories based on different criteria. Mastering this classification is a **guaranteed high-yield area** for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Nature of Contact

This is the most important classification for KTU exams.

**A) Lower Pair (Surface Contact)**
- When the two elements of a pair have **surface contact** (i.e., a finite area is in contact), the pair is called a **lower pair**.
- They can take up heavy loads because the contact area is large, leading to low contact pressure (less wear).
- **Examples:** Nut and screw, shaft rotating in a bearing, ball-and-socket joint, piston in a cylinder, journal bearing.

**B) Higher Pair (Point or Line Contact)**
- When the two elements of a pair have **point or line contact** (theoretically zero contact area), the pair is called a **higher pair**.
- They can only take up light loads because contact pressure is very high.
- **Examples:** Cam and follower, tooth gearing, belt drive, ball bearing (rolling contact), wheel and rail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick:** "**Lower** pair = **Large** contact area. **Higher** pair = **Tiny** contact area."

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Type of Relative Motion

This classification categorizes pairs based on the *kind* of motion allowed.

| Sl. No. | Type of Pair | Motion Allowed | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sliding Pair** | Pure relative translation (1 DOF) | Piston in cylinder, crosshead in guide |
| 2 | **Revolute (Turning) Pair** | Pure relative rotation (1 DOF) | Door hinge, crankshaft in main bearing |
| 3 | **Rolling Pair** | Pure rolling (1 DOF) | Ball bearing rolling on a race, wheel on road |
| 4 | **Screw Pair** | Helical motion: translation + rotation (1 DOF) | Nut and bolt, lead screw of lathe |
| 5 | **Cylindrical Pair** | Rotation + translation along the same axis (2 DOF) | Shaft in a cylindrical journal bearing |
| 6 | **Spherical Pair** | Rotation about a common point in any direction (3 DOF) | Ball-and-socket joint of human hip, spherical bearing |
| 7 | **Planar Pair** | Independent translation in 2 perpendicular directions + rotation about an axis perpendicular to the plane (3 DOF) | Flat plate sliding on a flat surface |

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Type of Closure (Self vs. Force Closed)

- **Self-Closed Pair:** The contact between the two elements is maintained by the *geometry* of the elements themselves, without any external force. Example: A shaft in a bearing — gravity (and geometry) keeps them in contact. 
- **Force-Closed Pair:** The contact is maintained by an *external force* (often from a spring or gravity). Example: Cam and follower — the cam does not enclose the follower, but the spring forces the follower to stay in contact.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Concepts

The number of independent relative motions possible between the two elements of a pair is called the **degrees of freedom** of the pair.

For a rigid body in 3D space:
- Total DOF of a free body = **6** (3 translations: along x, y, z; 3 rotations: about x, y, z)
- Each independent constraint imposed by a pair reduces 1 DOF.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — Kutzbach / Grübler Equation for DOF of a Pair:**
> 
> The DOF of a kinematic pair in 3D space is given by:
> 
> $$\text{DOF of a pair} = 6 - n$$
> 
> where $n$ is the number of independent constraints imposed by the pair.

| Pair Type | No. of Constraints ($n$) | DOF (6 − n) | Motion Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (Hinge) | 5 | **1** | Rotation about one fixed axis |
| Prismatic (Slider) | 5 | **1** | Translation along one fixed axis |
| Screw | 5 | **1** | Helical motion (combined rotation + translation) |
| Cylindrical | 4 | **2** | Rotation + translation along same axis |
| Spherical (Ball) | 3 | **3** | Rotation in all 3 directions about a point |
| Planar | 3 | **3** | 2 translations + 1 rotation (in the plane) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the names. A **Prismatic pair** is a *sliding* pair (one DOF), and a **Revolute pair** is a *turning* pair (one DOF). Both have 1 DOF but very different motion types.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Engineering:** The piston-cylinder is a sliding pair, the crankshaft-main bearing is a revolute pair, and the connecting rod-crank interface is a revolute pair.
- **Robotics:** Robotic arms are typically built using revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints to achieve desired spatial motion (e.g., 6-DOF industrial robots).
- **Manufacturing (CNC Machines):** Lead screws (screw pairs) convert rotational motion of a motor into precise linear motion of the tool.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Journal bearings (cylindrical pairs) support rotating shafts under heavy radial loads with minimal friction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Analysis of a Revolute Pair

In this section, we exhaustively derive why a **revolute pair (hinge joint)** has exactly **1 DOF** and imposes **5 constraints**.

### Step 1: Define the Reference Configuration

Consider a free rigid body $B$ in 3D Cartesian space. Without any constraints, the position of $B$ is described by 6 independent parameters:
- 3 translations: $x$, $y$, $z$ (position of its centroid)
- 3 rotations: $\theta_x$, $\theta_y$, $\theta_z$ (orientation about each axis)

So, the total configuration DOF = **6**.

### Step 2: Introduce a Revolute Pair
