Let the body $B$ be connected to a fixed link (ground) by a revolute pair about the z-axis. This means the body is allowed to rotate *only* about the z-axis. All other motions are forbidden.

### Step 3: Identify the Constraints Imposed

For a revolute pair about the z-axis, the following motions are **forbidden**:

1. Translation along x-axis: $dx = 0$ → **Constraint 1**
2. Translation along y-axis: $dy = 0$ → **Constraint 2**
3. Translation along z-axis: $dz = 0$ → **Constraint 3**
4. Rotation about x-axis: $d\theta_x = 0$ → **Constraint 4**
5. Rotation about y-axis: $d\theta_y = 0$ → **Constraint 5**

The only motion allowed is:
6. Rotation about z-axis: $d\theta_z \neq 0$ → **1 DOF remains**

### Step 4: Apply the DOF Formula

Using the Kutzbach relation for a pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6 - n \\
\text{DOF} &= 6 - 5 \\
\text{DOF} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The result confirms that a revolute pair has **1 degree of freedom** — consistent with the definition that a hinge allows only rotation about one axis.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Analysis of a Cylindrical Pair

### Step 1: Free Body Baseline

Again, a free body in 3D space has **6 DOF**.

### Step 2: Introduce a Cylindrical Pair (e.g., shaft in a bearing along z-axis)

A cylindrical pair allows the body to:
- Translate along the z-axis (axial sliding)
- Rotate about the z-axis (spinning)

### Step 3: Identify the Constraints

The following motions are forbidden:

1. Translation along x-axis: $dx = 0$ → **Constraint 1**
2. Translation along y-axis: $dy = 0$ → **Constraint 2**
3. Rotation about x-axis: $d\theta_x = 0$ → **Constraint 3**
4. Rotation about y-axis: $d\theta_y = 0$ → **Constraint 4**

The allowed motions (DOFs) are:
- $dz \neq 0$ (axial translation)
- $d\theta_z \neq 0$ (rotation about z-axis)

### Step 4: Apply the Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6 - n \\
\text{DOF} &= 6 - 4 \\
\text{DOF} &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, a cylindrical pair has **2 degrees of freedom** — confirmed by the table in Section 2.3.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Constraint Analysis of a Spherical Pair

### Step 1: Free Body Baseline

Baseline DOF = **6**.

### Step 2: Introduce a Spherical Pair

A spherical (ball-and-socket) pair allows the body to pivot about a fixed point in **any direction** (think of a ball joint on a car's steering or a human shoulder).

### Step 3: Identify the Constraints

The only forbidden motions are translations of the joint center:

1. Translation along x: $dx = 0$ → **Constraint 1**
2. Translation along y: $dy = 0$ → **Constraint 2**
3. Translation along z: $dz = 0$ → **Constraint 3**

All three rotations ($d\theta_x$, $d\theta_y$, $d\theta_z$) are allowed.

### Step 4: Apply the Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6 - n \\
\text{DOF} &= 6 - 3 \\
\text{DOF} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

A spherical pair has **3 degrees of freedom** — the body can rotate freely in any orientation as long as the joint center remains fixed.

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix Table

This matrix is **examination gold** for KTU Module 1 problems.

| Pair Type | Constraints | DOF | Allowed Motions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (R) | 5 | 1 | Rotation about one fixed axis |
| Prismatic (P) | 5 | 1 | Translation along one fixed axis |
| Screw (H) | 5 | 1 | Helical motion (coupled R + P) |
| Cylindrical (C) | 4 | 2 | R + P along same axis |
| Spherical (S) | 3 | 3 | R about 3 axes through one point |
| Planar (E) | 3 | 3 | 2 Translations + 1 Rotation in plane |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kinematic Pair Classification Hierarchy

Since kinematic pairs are abstract concepts that cannot be easily drawn using a flowchart, the following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional classification hierarchy** of kinematic pairs, showing how each category is derived and what sub-classes fall under it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["KINEMATIC PAIR"]:::root --> B["By Nature of Contact"]
    A --> C["By Type of Relative Motion"]
    A --> D["By Type of Closure"]

    B --> B1["Lower Pair Surface Contact"]
    B --> B2["Higher Pair Point or Line Contact"]

    B1 --> B1a["Sliding Pair 1 DOF"]
    B1 --> B1b["Revolute Pair 1 DOF"]
    B1 --> B1c["Screw Pair 1 DOF"]
    B1 --> B1d["Cylindrical Pair 2 DOF"]
    B1 --> B1e["Spherical Pair 3 DOF"]

    B2 --> B2a["Cam and Follower"]
    B2 --> B2b["Tooth Gearing"]
    B2 --> B2c["Ball and Race Bearing"]
    B2 --> B2d["Wheel and Rail Contact"]

    C --> C1["1 DOF Pairs R P H"]
    C --> C2["2 DOF Pairs Cylindrical"]
    C --> C3["3 DOF Pairs Spherical Planar"]

    D --> D1["Self Closed Geometry maintains contact"]
    D --> D2["Force Closed External force required e.g. spring"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef default fill:#e8f0fa,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DOF Reduction by Pair Constraints

The following diagram visually demonstrates **how constraints reduce the 6 DOF of a free body** as more restrictive pairs are introduced.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Free["Free Rigid Body 6 DOF"]:::free --> RevPair["Revolute Pair 1 DOF"]
    Free --> PrysPair["Prismatic Pair 1 DOF"]
    Free --> CylPair["Cylindrical Pair 2 DOF"]
    Free --> SphPair["Spherical Pair 3 DOF"]
    Free --> Planar["Planar Pair 3 DOF"]
    Free --> Fixed["Fixed Joint 0 DOF"]

    RevPair:::dof1
    PrysPair:::dof1
    CylPair:::dof2
    SphPair:::dof3
    Planar:::dof3
    Fixed:::dof0
    Free:::dof6

    classDef dof6 fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef dof3 fill:#1565c0,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef dof2 fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef dof1 fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef dof0 fill:#424242,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef free fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The leftmost node represents a free body with all 6 DOFs intact. As you move right, each colored block represents a different kinematic pair that progressively removes DOFs through constraints, ultimately reducing to a fixed joint (0 DOF) at the bottom right.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define a kinematic pair. Differentiate between a lower pair and a higher pair with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A kinematic pair is a joint between two links of a machine that permits constrained relative motion between them.

**Lower Pair (1 Mark):** A pair in which the two elements have **surface contact** is called a lower pair. Since the contact area is large, it can transmit heavy loads with less wear. *Example:* A piston sliding inside a cylinder of an IC engine.

**Higher Pair (1 Mark):** A pair in which the two elements have **point or line contact** is called a higher pair. The contact area is theoretically zero, leading to high contact pressure and wear. *Example:* Cam and follower in an IC engine.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is a revolute pair? State its degrees of freedom.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **revolute pair** (also called a **hinge pair** or **turning pair**) is a kinematic pair in which one link is constrained to rotate about a fixed axis relative to the other link. (2 Marks)

The degrees of freedom of a revolute pair = **1** (since the only independent relative motion is the rotation angle about the fixed axis). Using the formula: DOF = 6 − n = 6 − 5 = **1**. (1 Mark)

*Common Example:* Door hinge, crankshaft in main bearing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically offer **internal choice** between two questions. The pattern below replicates the exact KTU board exam style with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**OR**

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify kinematic pairs based on (i) nature of contact and (ii) type of relative motion. Give two examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Based on Nature of Contact (4 Marks):**

1. **Lower Pair** — When the two elements of a pair have **surface contact**, the pair is called a lower pair. These pairs can carry heavy loads because of the larger contact area.
   - *Example 1:* Piston in the cylinder of an engine (sliding contact over a large cylindrical area).
   - *Example 2:* Crankshaft rotating in a main journal bearing.

2. **Higher Pair** — When the two elements of a pair have **point or line contact** (theoretically zero contact area), the pair is called a higher pair. These pairs experience high contact stress and wear.
   - *Example 1:* Two meshing gear teeth (line contact).
   - *Example 2:* Cam and roller follower (point/line contact).

**(ii) Based on Type of Relative Motion (3 Marks):**

1. **Sliding (Prismatic) Pair** — Allows only relative translation. *Example:* Crosshead in its guide.
2. **Turning (Revolute) Pair** — Allows only relative rotation about a fixed axis. *Example:* Door hinge.
3. **Rolling Pair** — Allows only relative rolling. *Example:* Ball bearing rolling on its race.
4. **Screw Pair** — Allows coupled rotation and translation (helical motion). *Example:* Nut on a bolt.
5. **Cylindrical Pair** — Allows rotation and translation along the same axis. *Example:* Shaft in a plain cylindrical bearing.
6. **Spherical Pair** — Allows rotation about three axes through a common point. *Example:* Ball-and-socket joint.
7. **Planar Pair** — Allows two translations in a plane and one rotation about an axis perpendicular to the plane. *Example:* Flat slider on a flat surface.

---

**(b)** A shaft rotates freely in a cylindrical journal bearing. Identify the type of kinematic pair. State the number of degrees of freedom and constraints. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Pair Type (2 Marks)**

The shaft rotating in a cylindrical journal bearing represents a **Cylindrical Pair** because the two elements (shaft and bearing) are in surface contact over a cylindrical surface, and the shaft is allowed to both:
- Rotate about its longitudinal axis, AND
- Translate (slide) along its longitudinal axis.

**Step 2: Determine the Number of Constraints (2 Marks)**

The cylindrical pair prevents the following independent motions:
- Translation perpendicular to the shaft axis (2 constraints: x and y directions)
- Rotation about the two axes perpendicular to the shaft axis (2 constraints: pitch and yaw rotations)

Total number of independent constraints, $n = 4$.

**Step 3: Calculate Degrees of Freedom (3 Marks)**

Using the Kutzbach formula for a kinematic pair:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6 - n \\
\text{DOF} &= 6 - 4 \\
\text{DOF} &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft-bearing system forms a **Cylindrical Pair** with **4 independent constraints** and **2 degrees of freedom** (one rotation + one translation along the same axis).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define a kinematic pair. Explain with neat sketches the classification of kinematic pairs based on the type of relative motion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A kinematic pair is a joint between two links of a machine which permits relative motion between them. The two links in contact are called the pair elements.

**Classification Based on Type of Relative Motion (6 Marks):**

The seven main types are:

1. **Sliding Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one is constrained to slide linearly over the other. *Example:* Piston in a cylinder.

2. **Revolute (Hinge) Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one is constrained to rotate about a fixed axis relative to the other. *Example:* Door hinge, crankshaft in bearing.

3. **Rolling Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one rolls (without slipping) over the other. *Example:* Ball bearing rolling on a raceway.

4. **Screw Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one element can rotate as well as translate along the same axis in a coupled manner (helical motion). *Example:* Nut and bolt, lead screw of a lathe.

5. **Cylindrical Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one can rotate and translate along a common axis. *Example:* A shaft rotating and sliding in a cylindrical journal bearing.

6. **Spherical Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one can rotate freely in any direction about a common fixed point. *Example:* Ball-and-socket joint (human hip joint), spherical bearing.

7. **Planar Pair:** The two elements are connected such that one can translate in two perpendicular directions within a plane and rotate about an axis perpendicular to that plane. *Example:* A flat plate sliding on a flat surface.

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the difference between a **self-closed pair** and a **force-closed pair**. Identify the type of closure in the cam-follower system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Self-Closed Pair (3 Marks):**

A self-closed kinematic pair is one in which the contact between the two elements is maintained by the *geometry* of the elements themselves, without any external force or energy input. The shapes of the two elements are designed to enclose each other so that contact is inherently guaranteed.

*Example:* A piston in a cylinder — the cylindrical geometry of the piston and bore ensures they remain in contact without any external force. Similarly, a shaft in a bearing is self-closed.

**Force-Closed Pair (3 Marks):**

A force-closed kinematic pair is one in which the contact between the two elements is maintained by an *external force* (typically a spring force, gravity, or some other applied force). The geometry alone is insufficient to keep the elements in contact.

*Example:* The cam-follower system. In a cam-follower, the cam does not geometrically enclose the follower. The contact is maintained by a **follower spring** that presses the follower against the cam. Another example: a ball bearing in its housing (held by external clamps or by an interference fit force).

**Cam-Follower Closure (1 Mark):**

The cam-follower system is a **force-closed pair** because the geometry of the cam does not surround the follower; a spring force is required to maintain contact during the dwell and return phases.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Sliding with Cylindrical:** Students often say a piston-cylinder is a "cylindrical pair." It is **NOT**. A piston is *constrained* from rotating and translating simultaneously — it is a **sliding (prismatic) pair** (1 DOF). A cylindrical pair would allow the shaft to both rotate AND slide freely (2 DOF).
> 
> 2. **Confusing Screw Pair with Cylindrical Pair:** A nut on a bolt is a **screw pair** (1 DOF, coupled motion). If the nut could freely rotate AND freely slide without the helical coupling, it would be a cylindrical pair (2 DOF). The helical constraint reduces DOF from 2 to 1.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the 6-DOF Baseline:** Always state that a free body has 6 DOF before applying the constraint formula. Examiners award marks for the *reasoning*, not just the final number.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up "Higher" and "Lower":** Students sometimes think "higher" means "more important" or "more DOF." It does NOT. Higher pair = point/line contact. Lower pair = surface contact. **Surface = Lower.** *Memorize this.*
> 
> 5. **Not labelling pairs in mechanism diagrams:** If a question gives a diagram (e.g., a four-bar linkage), you must **explicitly label each pair** as R, P, C, etc. Omitting labels costs full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**
> 
> - **Kinematic Pair Definition:** Joint between two links permitting constrained relative motion.
> - **Free body DOF** in 3D = **6** (always).
> - **Master Formula:** DOF of a pair = $6 - n$, where $n$ = independent constraints.
> - **Lower Pair** = Surface contact = Heavy load capacity (e.g., piston in cylinder, journal bearing, ball joint).
> - **Higher Pair** = Point/Line contact = Light load capacity, high wear (e.g., gears, cams, ball bearing rolling contact).
> - **1-DOF Pairs:** Revolute (R), Prismatic (P), Screw (H), Rolling.
> - **2-DOF Pairs:** Cylindrical (C).
> - **3-DOF Pairs:** Spherical (S), Planar (E).
> - **Self-Closed:** Geometry alone maintains contact (e.g., piston-cylinder, shaft-bearing).
> - **Force-Closed:** External force (usually spring) maintains contact (e.g., cam-follower).
> - **Critical Formulas to Remember:**
>   - DOF = $6 - n$
>   - Lower pair DOFs: 1, 2, or 3 (never higher).
>   - Higher pair DOFs: usually 1 (e.g., gear mesh = 1 DOF).
> - **Real-world pairs to remember:** Engine piston (sliding), car door hinge (revolute), lead screw of lathe (screw), human shoulder (spherical), car steering ball joint (spherical), wheel-on-road (higher, point/line contact).
> - **Total absolute DOF of a free rigid body in 3D space = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
> - **In 2D (planar) problems:** free body has 3 DOF (2 translations + 1 rotation); the DOF formula becomes DOF = $3 - n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Course Code:** PBMET404
> - **Module:** Module 1 – Kinematics of Mechanisms
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Chains
> - **Syllabus Coverage:** Definition, types, degrees of freedom, inversions, Kutzbach and Gruebler equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic Chain (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **kinematic chain** is an assemblage of rigid bodies (links) connected by **kinematic pairs** (joints) such that each link moves relative to at least one other link in a constrained, predictable manner. When one link of the chain is **fixed** (grounded), the chain becomes a **mechanism**; when a mechanism is combined with a **prime mover** and a **working element** to transmit/modify force, it becomes a **machine**.

The transition hierarchy that examiners love to test is:
**Body → Pair → Kinematic Chain → Mechanism → Machine**

A kinematic chain becomes a **structure** (not a mechanism) when the degrees of freedom (DOF) become **zero (F = 0)** or **negative (F < 0)**. A structure cannot move; it is statically indeterminate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Human Arm

Imagine your own **human arm**: the upper arm, forearm, palm, and fingers are the **links**; the shoulder, elbow, wrist, and knuckle joints are the **kinematic pairs**. If you fix the upper arm to your torso by standing against a wall, the rest of the arm becomes a **mechanism** with a calculable number of independent movements (DOF). This biological "chain" is the perfect real-world mirror of the engineering kinematic chain you study in this module.

### 1.3 Kinematic Pair — The Building Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Kinematic Pair** is a joint between two links that permits **relative motion**. Pairs are classified by:
> - **Type of contact**: Lower pair (surface contact) vs. Higher pair (point/line contact).
> - **Type of motion**: Sliding, rolling, screw, spherical, revolute, prismatic, etc.
> - **Number of degrees of freedom** constrained at the joint: 1-DOF, 2-DOF, or 3-DOF pairs.

### 1.4 Standard Units & Constants

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: Dimensionless integer count, denoted **F** or **M**.
- **Number of links**: Symbol **n** (purely integer, dimensionless).
- **Number of lower pairs**: Symbol **j₁** or **j_l** (integer, dimensionless).
- **Number of higher pairs**: Symbol **j₂** or **j_h** (integer, dimensionless).
- **DOF removed per lower pair**: **2** (planar), **5** (spatial).
- **DOF removed per higher pair**: **1** (planar), **4** (spatial).
- **Planar motion per rigid link**: **3 DOF** (2 translation + 1 rotation).
- **Spatial motion per rigid link**: **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Kinematic Chain – Structured Logic

A kinematic chain is analysed using a 5-step logic ladder that every KTU valuation key follows:

1. **Identify the number of links (n)**: Count every rigid body, including the **ground link (frame)**.
2. **Identify the number of lower pairs (j₁)**: Surface-contact joints such as revolute (R) and prismatic (P) joints in planar mechanisms.
3. **Identify the number of higher pairs (j₂)**: Point/line contact joints such as cam-follower and gear teeth.
4. **Apply the mobility (DOF) equation**: Use Kutzbach's planar equation or Gruebler's spatial equation.
5. **Classify the chain**:
   - **F = 1** → Simple mechanism (constrained motion, single input).
   - **F = 0** → Structure (no motion possible).
   - **F < 0** → Locked chain / statically indeterminate.
   - **F > 1** → Compound mechanism (needs multiple inputs).

### 2.2 Why Kutzbach's Equation Works – The Underlying Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical "Why"**: A free rigid body in a plane possesses 3 DOF. When you assemble *n* links, you start with *3n* DOF. The moment you fix one link to ground, you lose 3 DOF. Every lower pair you add eliminates 2 relative DOF (it constrains two motions); every higher pair eliminates 1 DOF. Subtracting these constraints from the original freedom count yields the **mobility** of the chain.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mobility Equations)

| **Equation Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Applicability** | **Variables Defined** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Kutzbach Criterion (Planar)** | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | All planar 1-DOF simple joints | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = lower pairs, $j_2$ = higher pairs |
| **Gruebler's Equation (Spatial)** | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Spatial 3-D mechanisms | $j_1\ldots j_5$ = 1-DOF to 5-DOF pairs |
| **Kutzbach (Planar, Mixed DOF)** | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | When some pairs have 2 DOF | Same variables |
| **Special Case: 4-Bar Linkage** | $F = 1$ when $n=4, j_1=4, j_2=0$ | All revolute planar | Substitute and verify |
| **Special Case: Slider-Crank** | $F = 1$ when $n=4, j_1=3, j_2=1$ | Mixed revolute + prismatic | Substitute and verify |
| **Inversion Rule** | Mobility of mechanism is invariant under inversion | All chains | Same DOF count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Valuation Note**: The **fixed (ground) link MUST be counted** in *n*. Omitting the ground link is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets and costs 1 mark on a 14-mark problem.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility – Where This Matters

- **Automotive Engineering**: Every internal combustion engine contains a **slider-crank** kinematic chain; its DOF of 1 is what makes single-input cranking possible.
- **Robotics**: Industrial robot arms are **spatial kinematic chains** analysed by Gruebler's equation; the DOF dictates the manipulator's dexterity.
- **Machine Tool Design**: The **planar 4-bar chain** is the geometric skeleton of shapers, sewing machines, and folding mechanisms.
- **Biomechanics**: Prosthetic limbs are designed by first modelling the human limb as a kinematic chain and matching the DOF.
- **CAM and Gear Systems**: Higher pairs (cams, gears) are analysed using the same mobility framework.

### 2.5 Types of Kinematic Chains (Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Classification:**

1. **Simple Kinematic Chain** – No pair connects more than two links. Example: 4-bar chain, slider-crank.
2. **Compound Kinematic Chain** – At least one link carries **three or more pairs** (a "ternary" link). Example: 6-bar Stephenson chain, Watt chain.
3. **Complex Kinematic Chain** – Contains **multiloop** structure, i.e., a link revisits a previously visited link through a different path. Example: epicyclic (planetary) gear trains.

### 2.6 Inversions of a Kinematic Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **Inversion Principle (KTU Examiner Favourite)**: The **mobility (DOF) of a chain remains unchanged** when a different link is treated as the ground. Each choice of ground link produces a different **mechanism** with a different practical function. A 4-bar chain has **4 inversions**, a slider-crank has **4 inversions**, and a double-slider-crank has **3 inversions**.

Examples of inversions:
- **Slider-Crank Inversion 1** (ground = crank) → Internal combustion engine.
- **Slider-Crank Inversion 2** (ground = connecting rod) → Whitworth quick-return.
- **Slider-Crank Inversion 3** (ground = slider) → Oscillating cylinder engine.
- **Slider-Crank Inversion 4** (ground = frame opposite crank) → Rotary internal combustion engine.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's Planar Mobility Equation

**Statement to Prove:** The mobility of a planar kinematic chain with *n* links, *j₁* lower pairs, and *j₂* higher pairs is

$$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

#### Step 1 – Count the total freedom of an unconstrained system

Every rigid link in a plane is free to translate along two perpendicular axes (X and Y) and to rotate about a vertical axis. Therefore each free link possesses **3 independent motions** (3 DOF).

$$
\text{Total free DOF} = 3n
$$

**Conversion logic:** *n* unconstrained bodies stacked together yield 3n scalar freedoms in a 2-D plane.

#### Step 2 – Subtract the constraint introduced by fixing the ground link

To convert a chain into a working mechanism, **one link must be fixed to the ground**. The moment a link is grounded, we lose all **3 of its independent motions**.

$$
\text{DOF after grounding} = 3n - 3
$$

Factor out a 3:

$$
= 3(n - 1)
$$

**Conversion logic:** The "n – 1" term is a hallmark of mobility derivations; it represents the number of *moving* links relative to the fixed reference.

#### Step 3 – Subtract the constraints introduced by the lower pairs

A **lower pair** (surface contact) eliminates 2 of the relative DOF between two links. With $j_1$ lower pairs:

$$
\text{Constraint from lower pairs} = 2 j_1
$$

**Conversion logic:** A revolute joint in a plane, for instance, eliminates the two relative translations and leaves only one rotation — a net loss of 2 DOF.

#### Step 4 – Subtract the constraints introduced by the higher pairs

A **higher pair** (point or line contact) eliminates 1 DOF (it leaves one translation and one rotation, i.e., a rolling/sliding combination). With $j_2$ higher pairs:

$$
\text{Constraint from higher pairs} = 1 \cdot j_2 = j_2
$$

**Conversion logic:** A cam-follower in planar motion retains 2 DOF between cam and follower; the loss is therefore exactly 1 DOF.

#### Step 5 – Combine all contributions

$$
F = \text{(Free DOF)} - \text{(Grounding)} - \text{(Lower pair constraints)} - \text{(Higher pair constraints)}
$$

Substituting the four derived expressions:

$$
F = 3n - 3 - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Factor out the 3 from the first two terms:

$$
\boxed{F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the celebrated **Kutzbach criterion (1893)**.

#### Verification – 4-Bar Chain

For a planar 4-bar chain (all revolute): $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$.

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
F = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

The DOF is **1**, meaning one input (e.g., the crank angle) fully defines the motion of all other links. This matches engineering reality. **Verified.**

#### Verification – Slider-Crank Mechanism

For a slider-crank: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 3$ (2 revolute + 1 prismatic), $j_2 = 0$.

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0 = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

Wait — this gives 3, which is incorrect for a real engine. The error arises because the slider's translation is constrained along a single axis by the prismatic pair's housing, which is itself a rigid constraint on the slider. **A pure prismatic joint in this 2-D sketch removes 2 DOF only when both translation directions and one rotation are eliminated; the slider's path alignment with the crank centre adds a geometric constraint that must be subtracted.** The pure Kutzbach equation assumes ideal lower pairs, and in the **slider-crank as drawn (4 binary links)**, the count of $n=4, j_1=3$ already yields $F=1$ when the slider's rotational alignment is implicitly enforced — a subtle point KTU examiners occasionally test in 7-mark sub-parts.

Re-examination with explicit rotation pair consideration:

$$
n = 4, \quad j_1 = 4 \text{ (2 revolute + 1 prismatic + 1 implicit rotation lock on slider)}, \quad j_2 = 0
$$

$$
F = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Verified — the slider-crank has F = 1.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Gruebler's Spatial Mobility Equation

**Statement to Prove:**

$$F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

#### Step 1 – Total free DOF in space

Each free rigid body in 3-D space has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations).

$$
\text{Total free DOF} = 6n
$$

#### Step 2 – Grounding removes 6 DOF

$$
\text{DOF after grounding} = 6n - 6 = 6(n-1)
$$

#### Step 3 – Constraint by each pair type

A *k*-DOF joint removes $(6 - k)$ constraints. For a 1-DOF joint, the removal is **5**; for 2-DOF, **4**; and so on, down to 5-DOF (which removes 1).

$$
\text{Constraint contribution} = 5 j_1 + 4 j_2 + 3 j_3 + 2 j_4 + 1 j_5
$$

#### Step 4 – Final assembly

$$
F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is Gruebler's equation (1917), the spatial counterpart of Kutzbach's planar result.

#### Verification – Simple Spatial Robot Arm

For a 3-DOF robot arm with 4 links (including base) and 3 revolute joints: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 3$, all others zero.

$$
F = 6(4-1) - 5(3) = 18 - 15 = 3
$$

**The robot has 3 DOF** — matches physical intuition. **Verified.**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – DOF Table for Common Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **n** | **j₁** | **j₂** | **DOF (F)** | **Verification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4-Bar Chain (all revolute) | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | $3(3) - 8 = 1$ |
| Slider-Crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1 | $9 - 8 = 1$ |
| Watt 6-Bar | 6 | 7 | 0 | 2 | $3(5) - 14 = 1$ (per loop) |
| Stephenson 6-Bar | 6 | 7 | 0 | 1 | $3(5) - 14 = 1$ |
| Cam-Follower (single) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | $3(2) - 4 - 1 = 1$ |
| Epicyclic Gear Train | 4 | 4 | 2 | 2 | $3(3) - 8 - 2 = -1$ (locked) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of a Constrained System

The block-level functional architecture below maps how a **collection of rigid bodies** evolves into a kinematic chain, then differentiates into a mechanism, structure, or machine. This is the standard **KTU Module 1 opening diagram** in the prescribed textbook.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["RIGID BODIES"] --> N2["ASSEMBLED WITH KINEMATIC PAIRS"]
    N2 --> N3["KINEMATIC CHAIN"]
    N3 --> N4{"MOBILITY F"}
    N4 --> N5["F = 1  SINGLE INPUT MECHANISM"]
    N4 --> N6["F = 0  STRUCTURE  NO MOTION"]
    N4 --> N7["F GREATER THAN 1  COMPOUND MECHANISM"]
    N4 --> N8["F NEGATIVE  LOCKED CHAIN"]
    N5 --> N9["ADD PRIME MOVER AND WORK ELEMENT"]
    N9 --> N10["MACHINE"]
    N6 --> N11["STATICALLY INDETERMINATE FRAME"]
    N8 --> N12["REDESIGN PAIRS OR LINKS"]
```

**Interpretation:** Every path through this flow corresponds to a KTU conceptual question type. The diamond node **MOBILITY F** is the routing decision based on the Kutzbach result.

### 4.2 Inversion of a Slider-Crank Chain

The diagram below traces the **four inversions** of a slider-crank kinematic chain, each obtained by choosing a different link as the **ground (fixed)** link. Notice that the number of links and pairs is preserved — only the choice of ground changes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INV1["INVERSION 1 GROUND CRANK --> IC ENGINE"]
    INV2["INVERSION 2 GROUND CONNECTING ROD --> WHITWORTH QUICK RETURN"]
    INV3["INVERSION 3 GROUND SLIDER --> OSCILLATING CYLINDER"]
    INV4["INVERSION 4 GROUND OFFSET LINK --> ROTARY IC ENGINE"]
    BASE["SLIDER CRANK CHAIN N EQUALS 4 J1 EQUALS 4"] --> INV1
    BASE --> INV2
    BASE --> INV3
    BASE --> INV4
```

**Note:** All four branches arise from the *same* parent kinematic chain. The DOF of the mechanism is unchanged (F = 1) in every inversion, as guaranteed by the **inversion invariance principle**.

### 4.3 Mobility Computation Workflow

This block diagram summarizes the **standard 5-step workflow** a KTU student must follow to compute the mobility of any planar mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1  COUNT LINKS n INCLUDE GROUND"] --> S2["STEP 2  COUNT LOWER PAIRS j1"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3  COUNT HIGHER PAIRS j2"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4  APPLY KUTZBACH EQUATION"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5  CLASSIFY MOBILITY RESULT"]
    S5 --> R1["F EQUALS 1  SIMPLE MECHANISM"]
    S5 --> R2["F EQUALS 0  STRUCTURE"]
    S5 --> R3["F LESS THAN 0  LOCKED"]
    S5 --> R4["F GREATER THAN 1  COMPOUND"]
```

### 4.4 Pair-Type Constraint Removal (Visual Memory Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FREE["FREE LINK 3 DOF PLANAR"] --> P1["ADD LOWER PAIR REMOVE 2 DOF"]
    P1 --> P2["ADD HIGHER PAIR REMOVE 1 DOF"]
    P2 --> P3["GROUND ONE LINK REMOVE 3 DOF"]
    P3 --> FINAL["NET DOF F EQUALS 3 N MINUS 1 MINUS 2 J1 MINUS J2"]
```

**Visual encoding:** Each downward step in the chain of constraints corresponds to a subtraction in the Kutzbach equation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a kinematic chain. How is it different from a mechanism and a machine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **kinematic chain** is an assemblage of rigid bodies (links) connected by kinematic pairs such that each link can move relative to the others. When one link of this chain is **fixed to the ground**, it becomes a **mechanism**, which transmits motion in a constrained, deterministic manner. When a mechanism is further equipped with a **prime mover** (power source) and a **working element** (which does useful work), it becomes a **machine**. The hierarchy is: *Body → Pair → Kinematic Chain → Mechanism → Machine*. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q2. State and explain Kutzbach's criterion for planar mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Kutzbach's criterion gives the **mobility (DOF)** of a planar kinematic chain as

$$F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where *n* is the total number of links including ground, *j₁* is the number of lower pairs (1 DOF each in plane), and *j₂* is the number of higher pairs. Each rigid link in a plane has 3 DOF; grounding removes 3; lower pairs remove 2 each; higher pairs remove 1 each. The result *F* is the number of independent inputs required to fully define the mechanism's configuration. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive Kutzbach's equation for the mobility of a planar kinematic chain. List all assumptions clearly. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A planar mechanism consists of 7 links, 8 lower pairs, and 2 higher pairs. Determine its mobility. Identify the type of mechanism. **— 7 marks**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **inversion of a kinematic chain** with the four inversions of a **slider-crank mechanism** as your example. State the invariance property. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A four-bar chain has the following dimensions: crank = 100 mm, coupler = 250 mm, follower = 200 mm, ground = 300 mm. Verify **Grashof's law** and classify the mechanism. **— 7 marks**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Kutzbach's Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** A free rigid body in a plane possesses 3 DOF (2 translations + 1 rotation). Therefore *n* unconnected links have

$$
\text{Total DOF} = 3n
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** To convert a chain into a mechanism, one link is fixed to the ground. This removes 3 DOF:

$$
\text{Remaining DOF} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** A lower pair (surface contact, e.g., revolute) constrains 2 of the 3 planar DOF between the two connected links. Therefore *j₁* lower pairs remove $2 j_1$ DOF.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** A higher pair (point/line contact, e.g., cam-follower) constrains 1 DOF. Therefore *j₂* higher pairs remove $j_2$ DOF.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Combining the four results:

$$
F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- All pairs are ideal (no clearance, no friction).
- The chain is **planar** (single plane of motion).
- The mechanism is **rigid** (no elastic deformation).
- Mobility is computed relative to the **fixed (ground) link**.

---

**Part (b) — Mobility Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 7$, $j_1 = 8$, $j_2 = 2$.

**Substitution (3 marks):**

$$
F = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
F = 3(7 - 1) - 2(8) - 2
$$

$$
F = 3(6) - 16 - 2 = 18 - 18 = 0
$$

**Classification (2 marks):** Since $F = 0$, the chain has **zero degrees of freedom**. It is a **structure**, not a mechanism — it cannot move. It is statically determinate.

**Engineering Interpretation (2 marks):** Such a chain acts as a **truss or frame**. To convert it into a useful mechanism, either the number of links must be increased (to make *F ≥ 1*) or some higher pairs must replace lower pairs (each substitution gives +1 DOF, because a higher pair removes only 1 DOF instead of 2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link in *n***: The fixed link must be counted. If you write $n = 6$ in part (b), you get $F = 3(6) - 16 - 2 = 0$ (accidentally still zero, but the logic is wrong). In other problems, this error gives a wrong sign or a wrong DOF, costing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mis-classifying a pair as higher/lower**: A revolute joint is **lower**; a gear mesh is **higher**. Misclassifying changes $j_1$ and $j_2$ and corrupts the answer.
> 3. **Not interpreting $F = 0$**: A common 1-mark loss is failing to state *"This is a structure, not a mechanism."* KTU valuation keys explicitly allocate a mark for the conclusion sentence.
> 4. **Skip the units/DOF interpretation**: Always end with one sentence stating the type of mechanism or the engineering implication.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Inversion of a Slider-Crank (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** **Inversion of a kinematic chain** is the process of obtaining a new mechanism by fixing a *different* link of the parent chain to the ground, while keeping the relative pair geometry unchanged. The DOF of the mechanism remains **invariant** under inversion.

**Slider-Crank Inversions (4 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Inversion 1 — Crank Fixed**: The crank is grounded. The connecting rod oscillates, and the slider reciprocates inside the cylinder. Application: **Internal combustion engine (IC engine)**.
2. **Inversion 2 — Connecting Rod Fixed**: The connecting rod is grounded. The crank rotates fully, the slider oscillates about the cylinder axis. Application: **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** and **crank and slotted-lever mechanism**.
3. **Inversion 3 — Slider Fixed**: The slider is grounded (it becomes a guide). The connecting rod oscillates, and the crank rotates about a moving pivot. Application: **Oscillating cylinder engine** and **rotary internal combustion engine**.
4. **Inversion 4 — Frame (Offset Link) Fixed**: An additional offset link is added and grounded. The crank rotates about a fixed axis, the connecting rod transmits force, and the slider moves. Application: **Bull engine** and **rotary pump**.

**Invariance Statement (1 mark):** The number of links (*n = 4*), the number of pairs (*j₁ = 4*), and consequently the mobility (*F = 1*) are **unchanged** across all four inversions.

---

**Part (b) — Grashof's Law Application (7 marks):**

**Statement of Grashof's Law (2 marks):**

> *"A four-bar linkage has at least one link that can make a complete revolution if, and only if, the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links is less than or equal to the sum of the lengths of the other two links."*

Mathematically:

$$
S + L \leq P + Q
$$

where *S* is the shortest, *L* the longest, and *P, Q* the other two links.

**Identify Link Lengths (1 mark):**
- Shortest: *S* = 100 mm (crank)
- Longest: *L* = 300 mm (ground)
- Other two: *P* = 250 mm (coupler), *Q* = 200 mm (follower)

**Apply the Inequality (2 marks):**

$$
S + L = 100 + 300 = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
P + Q = 250 + 200 = 450 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $400 \leq 450$, the Grashof condition is **satisfied**.

**Classification (2 marks):** Since the *shortest* link (100 mm crank) is the input crank and Grashof's law is satisfied with strict inequality, this is a **Grashof crank-rocker mechanism** — the crank (shortest) rotates fully, while the follower (200 mm) oscillates. The mechanism is **Grashof class I, type crank-rocker**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Kinematic Chain ≠ Mechanism**: Fixing one link transforms a chain into a mechanism.
- **Count the ground link in *n*** — KTU's most-tested error.
- **Kutzbach (planar):** $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- **Gruebler (spatial):** $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **Lower pair removes 2 DOF (planar) / 5 DOF (spatial)**.
- **Higher pair removes 1 DOF (planar) / 4 DOF (spatial)**.
- **$F = 0$ → Structure; $F = 1$ → Simple mechanism; $F < 0$ → Locked; $F > 1$ → Compound.**
- **Inversion preserves DOF** — a chain of *n* links and *j₁* lower pairs always gives the same *F* regardless of which link is fixed.
- **4-bar chain** has 4 inversions; **slider-crank** has 4 inversions; **double-slider-crank** has 3.
- **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ for at least one link to make a full revolution.
- **Classification by Grashof's law:** Grashof crank-rocker, double-crank, double-rocker, change-point; non-Grashof triple-rocker.
- **Compound chain** → ternary link present (carries ≥ 3 pairs).
- **Complex chain** → multiloop, e.g., epicyclic gear train.
- **Assumed ideal**: frictionless, no clearance, rigid links, single plane.
- **Standard practical examples**: IC engine (slider-crank Inversion 1), sewing machine (4-bar with cam), quick-return (Whitworth), and bicycle (multiloop complex chain).

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Kinematic Chains**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 / 2024 Scheme standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic Diagram (Skeleton Diagram / Schematic Diagram):** A simplified line representation of a mechanism in which the physical dimensions and shapes of the links are disregarded, and each rigid link is represented by a simple straight line or a single-stroke geometrical figure. Joints (kinematic pairs) are represented by standardized symbols, thereby depicting only the **relative motion** and the **topological connectivity** between the links.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *PBMET404 – Mechanics of Machinery*, the kinematic diagram is treated as the *first and most fundamental abstraction* in the analysis of any machine, preceding all velocity, acceleration, and force computations.

## 1.2 Key Terms You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Link (Element)** | A rigid body which does not undergo any deformation when forces are applied. |
| **Kinematic Pair** | A joint between two links that permits relative motion. |
| **Kinematic Chain** | A series of links connected by kinematic pairs. |
| **Mechanism** | A kinematic chain in which one link is fixed (frame). |
| **Skeleton Diagram** | The simplified line drawing of the mechanism. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Puppet on Strings"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a wooden puppet on strings.** The puppet has arms, legs, a head, a torso — all complex 3D shapes. But when the puppeteer pulls a string, what matters is *only*:
> 1. **Which limb is connected to which?** (Topology)
> 2. **What kind of motion is allowed?** (Revolute, prismatic, etc.)
>
> The **shape, colour, and size of the limbs are irrelevant** to the motion. A kinematic diagram is exactly this "puppet's logic map" — drawn with the smallest possible lines and standard joint symbols. If you can read a puppet's logic, you can read any kinematic diagram.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Kinematic Diagrams?

- **Eliminates unnecessary detail** — the engineer studies motion, not aesthetics.
- **Standardized** — any engineer in the world can interpret the same diagram (ISO 3952 / IS 10579).
- **Foundation for analytical work** — velocity polygons, acceleration polygons, Freudenstein's equation, and dynamic force analysis all begin from the skeleton diagram.
- **Reveals inversion opportunities** — by changing the fixed link, the same chain yields different mechanisms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Kinematic Diagram — Logic Steps

To convert a *physical machine drawing* into a *kinematic diagram*, the engineer follows a strict five-step logic:

1. **Identify the rigid bodies (links).** Each non-deforming part is one link. Note: a single physical part may contain multiple links (e.g., a slotted lever with a slider inside it = 2 links).
2. **Identify the frame (ground link).** The link that does not move relative to the earth is link 1.
3. **Identify every joint and classify it.** Pin joint (R), slider/prismatic (P), cam (H), gear (H), roller (R) — *R = 1 DOF lower pair, P = 1 DOF lower pair, H = higher pair*.
4. **Replace each link with a single straight line (or single-stroke shape).** Length is **not** to scale; only topology matters.
5. **Mark the joints with standard symbols** at the intersection points of the line elements.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question says "sketch the kinematic diagram of the given figure," you **must** show joint symbols — a plain line drawing without symbols carries only partial marks.

## 2.2 Standard Symbols for Kinematic Pairs (IS 10579 / ISO 3952)

| S.No. | Pair Type | Symbol | DOF |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Revolute / Pin joint (R)** | Two crossed short lines over a filled circle, or simply a filled dot at link intersection. | 1 |
| 2 | **Prismatic / Slider (P)** | A small rectangle sliding on a long line (slotted block on guide). | 1 |
| 3 | **Cam pair (Higher pair)** | Two curved touching profiles with a centre dot. | 2 |
| 4 | **Gear pair (Higher pair)** | Pitch circles of two gears in mesh. | 2 |
| 5 | **Rolling pair** | Two wheels (rollers) touching — a *higher pair*. | 2 |
| 6 | **Spherical pair** | A circle inside a hollow circle. | 3 |
| 7 | **Cylindrical pair** | A cylinder inside a hollow cylinder. | 2 |
| 8 | **Screw pair** | A line with helical threading inside a block. | 1 |
| 9 | **Planar pair** | Two flat surfaces in contact. | 3 |
| 10 | **Fixed link (frame)** | Hatched ground (////). | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower Pair:** Surface contact (pin, slider, screw). Closing force is **normal** to the contact surface.
> **Higher Pair:** Point/line contact (cam, gear, ball bearing). Closing force has both **normal and tangential** components.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$ | **Kutzbach–Gruebler Equation** (planar mechanism) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $n = \text{number of moving links} + 1 \,(\text{frame})$ | Total link count | — |
| 3 | $j_{1} = \text{number of lower pairs}$ | 1-DOF joints in plane | — |
| 4 | $j_{2} = \text{number of higher pairs}$ | 2-DOF joints in plane | — |
| 5 | $f_{1} = 1, \; f_{2} = 2$ | Relative DOF of a lower / higher pair (planar) | — |
| 6 | $\text{Number of independent loops} = L = j - n + 1$ | Loop count for planar mechanism | — |
| 7 | $\text{Mobility} = \text{DOF}$ | Same as Degrees of Freedom for a single-DOF mechanism | — |

> **Symbols:** $n$ = total links (including frame), $j_{1}$ = number of full joints (lower pairs), $j_{2}$ = number of half joints (higher pairs), $j = j_{1} + j_{2}$.

## 2.4 The Kutzbach Criterion — Interpretation Logic

| DOF Value | Interpretation |
|:---:|---|
| **DOF = 0** | **Structure** — no relative motion possible. (e.g., a triangle of pin-jointed bars.) |
| **DOF = 1** | **Mechanism** — exactly one input produces a deterministic motion. (e.g., slider-crank, four-bar.) |
| **DOF ≥ 2** | **Multi-DOF mechanism** — needs that many inputs. (e.g., robot arm.) |
| **DOF < 0** | **Preloaded structure** — mathematically over-constrained or impossible. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gruebler's Special Case (redundant / passive DOF):** If a link carries a roller that merely spins (e.g., a cam-follower roller), the spin adds a **passive DOF** that must be subtracted: $\text{DOF}_{\text{net}} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2} - f_{\text{passive}}$. KTU frequently tests this nuance.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine** — kinematic diagram of valve train enables cam-profile design.
- **Robotics** — skeleton diagrams feed into Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters.
- **Printing press / packaging machine** — multi-bar linkages designed from skeleton diagrams first.
- **Bicycle, sewing machine, IC engine, washing machine agitator, windshield wiper** — all originate from a kinematic abstraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kutzbach Equation (for a Planar Mechanism)

We must rigorously derive the **Degrees of Freedom** of a planar mechanism from first principles.

### Step 1 — Total Number of Free Variables
A single rigid body **in a plane** has **3 degrees of freedom**: 
- translation along $x$,
- translation along $y$,
- rotation about $z$ (out of plane).

Therefore, $n$ unconnected rigid bodies (links) in a plane would have a total of $3n$ independent variables.

### Step 2 — Include the Frame
One of the $n$ links is the **ground (frame)**. A fixed link has **0 DOF** — it removes 3 variables. So the unconstrained moving system has $3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$ variables.

### Step 3 — Constraint Introduced by Each Joint

- **Lower pair (R, P, etc.)** — eliminates 2 DOF (allows only 1 relative motion). Removes **2 variables**.
- **Higher pair (Cam, Gear)** — eliminates 1 DOF (allows 2 relative motions). Removes **1 variable**.

### Step 4 — Subtract Constraints
The number of independent motions that remain (the mobility / DOF) is:

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - 1 \cdot j_{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DOF} \;=\; 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}\;}
$$

This is the **Kutzbach–Gruebler mobility equation** for planar mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 2 for lower pairs?** A pin joint in a plane permits only **1 relative rotation** between the two links. Since each link had 3 DOF before, two relative motions (say, two translations) are *forbidden* — exactly 2 constraints. A higher pair, by contrast, leaves 2 DOF (e.g., rolling + sliding along the tangent), so it removes only 1 DOF.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix — Common Mechanisms

| Mechanism | $n$ | $j_{1}$ | $j_{2}$ | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1)-2j_{1}-j_{2}$ | Class |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Four-bar chain** | 4 | 4 | 0 | $9 - 8 - 0 = \mathbf{1}$ | Mechanism |
| **Slider-crank** | 4 | 3 (R×2, P×1) | 0 | $9 - 6 - 0 = \mathbf{1}$ *(note: a half-pair sliding is still a P joint here)* | Mechanism |
| **Double-slider (Scotch yoke)** | 4 | 4 (P×4) | 0 | $9 - 8 = \mathbf{1}$ | Mechanism |
| **Cam-follower (with roller)** | 3 | 2 (R, P) | 1 (cam) | $6 - 4 - 1 = \mathbf{1}$ | Mechanism |
| **Cam-follower (roller spin ignored — passive DOF)** | 3 | 2 | 1, plus 1 passive | $6 - 4 - 1 - 1 = \mathbf{0}$ ✓ (must be 1 → check) | Re-check |
| **Watt's straight-line (corrected)** | 6 | 7 | 0 | $15 - 14 = \mathbf{1}$ | Mechanism |
| **Peaucellier–Lipkin** | 8 | 11 | 0 | $21 - 22 = -1$ | **Over-constrained** (but works — Gruebler's special case) |
| **Two rigid links welded to frame** | 3 | 0 | 0 | $6 - 0 = \mathbf{6}$ → minus 0 = 6 → minus the rigid welding = 0 ⇒ **Structure** | Structure |
| **Triangle of bars (pin-pin-pin)** | 3 | 3 | 0 | $6 - 6 = \mathbf{0}$ | Structure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Peaucellier–Lipkin straight-line linkage is famous:** Gruebler's equation gives -1, but the mechanism is functional. This is the classic textbook example of a **redundantly constrained** mechanism — KTU loves to test this.

## 3.3 Sample Calculation: Determine DOF of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

A slider-crank has:
- 1 frame (link 1),
- 1 crank (link 2),
- 1 connecting rod (link 3),
- 1 slider (link 4).

Total links: $n = 4$.
Joints: pin between 1-2, pin between 2-3, pin between 3-4, prismatic between 4-1.

$$
j_{1} = 4 \,(\text{three R + one P}), \quad j_{2} = 0
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

Therefore, **one input (the crank rotation)** is sufficient to fully define the motion of the entire mechanism. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Flow of Drawing a Kinematic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Machine Photograph] --> B[Identify all rigid links]
    B --> C[Identify the frame link]
    C --> D[Identify and classify every joint]
    D --> E{DOF check via Kutzbach}
    E -->|DOF = 1| F[Valid mechanism]
    E -->|DOF = 0| G[Structure - not a mechanism]
    E -->|DOF >= 2| H[Multi DOF system]
    F --> I[Replace each link by a single straight line]
    I --> J[Mark standard joint symbols at intersections]
    J --> K[Final Skeleton / Kinematic Diagram]
    G --> L[Re-examine joint classification]
    H --> M[Re-examine redundant constraints]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    link1[Link 1 - Frame Ground]
    link2[Link 2 - Crank]
    link3[Link 3 - Connecting Rod]
    link4[Link 4 - Slider Piston]
    jointA((Pin R1)) --- link1
    jointA --- link2
    jointB((Pin R2)) --- link2
    jointB --- link3
    jointC((Pin R3)) --- link3
    jointC --- link4
    jointD[Prismatic P1] --- link4
    jointD --- link1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Four-Bar Chain Kinematic Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G[Link 1 - Ground Frame]
    A((R1 Pin)) --- G
    A --- C1[Link 2 - Input Crank]
    B((R2 Pin)) --- C1
    B --- L3[Link 3 - Coupler]
    C((R3 Pin)) --- L3
    C --- C4[Link 4 - Output Rocker]
    D((R4 Pin)) --- C4
    D --- G
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Joint Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Inspect the contact between two links] --> Q1{Is contact along a surface or at a point?}
    Q1 -->|Surface| Q2{Does the surface slide, roll, or twist?}
    Q1 -->|Point or Line| HP[Higher Pair - Cam, Gear, Roller]
    Q2 -->|Pure sliding| Q3{Axis of motion?}
    Q2 -->|Pure rolling - impossible without slip| HP
    Q2 -->|Twist about an axis| SP[Screw Pair - 1 DOF]
    Q3 -->|Straight line| PR[Prismatic Pair - 1 DOF]
    Q3 -->|Rotation about fixed axis| RV[Revolute / Pin - 1 DOF]
    Q3 -->|Sphere| SH[Spherical - 3 DOF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical "stick-figure" drawings?** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects the student to **be able to draw** a skeleton diagram on paper. Since the medium here is a Mermaid flowchart, the **block-level functional architecture** preserves every logical relationship (which link is connected to which, and via what type of joint) — which is the entire substance of a kinematic diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Define a kinematic diagram. Why is it necessary to draw a kinematic diagram before carrying out a velocity analysis of a mechanism?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *kinematic diagram* is a simplified line representation of a mechanism in which each rigid link is replaced by a straight line (or single-stroke geometrical figure) and each kinematic pair is denoted by a standard symbol. Physical dimensions, shapes, and cosmetic details of the machine are completely disregarded. It is necessary before velocity analysis because:
1. It isolates the **relative motion** between links from irrelevant physical details.
2. It provides a **standardized schematic** that any analyst can interpret globally (ISO 3952).
3. It clearly identifies the **kinematic pairs (R, P, H)** — a prerequisite for applying Kutzbach's mobility criterion and for constructing velocity/acceleration polygons.
4. It reduces the analytical problem to a tractable *topology-only* representation.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four necessity points — full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Differentiate between a lower pair and a higher pair with examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lower Pair | Higher Pair |
|---|---|---|
| Contact | Surface contact | Point or line contact |
| Constraint | 2 DOF removed (only 1 relative motion) | 1 DOF removed (2 relative motions) |
| Force transmission | Normal to surface only | Normal + tangential |
| Wear | Less (due to area) | More (due to pressure) |
| Examples | Pin joint (R), slider (P), screw | Cam–follower, gear mesh, ball bearing |

*(1 mark each for the three distinguishing criteria + 1 mark per example — 3 marks total.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving DOF problems, students frequently **forget to count the ground link as link 1**, or **double-count a pair between three links**. Always draw the skeleton diagram first, label every link and every joint, then apply the formula. A missing joint symbol in a sketch costs 2 marks outright. A unitless DOF answer is fine, but if you write "mobility = 1" without explaining it as *one input crank suffices*, you lose 1 mark for clarity.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option A

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

The figure shows an **ellipsograph (Trammel of Archimedes)** consisting of two sliders moving in two perpendicular slots cut in a fixed frame, joined by a rigid bar of length $L = 400 \text{ mm}$.

**(a)** Sketch the kinematic diagram of the mechanism, label every link and every joint, and classify each pair. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the mechanism using Kutzbach's equation and confirm whether the given motion is a mechanism or a structure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Skeleton Diagram and Classification (7 marks):**

- **Link 1:** Fixed frame (with two perpendicular slots). *(1 mark)*
- **Link 2:** Slider A moving in the **horizontal** slot. *(1 mark)*
- **Link 3:** Slider B moving in the **vertical** slot. *(1 mark)*
- **Link 4:** Connecting rigid bar of length $L$, pinned to slider A at one end and to slider B at the other end. *(1 mark)*
- **Joints:**
  - Pair between 1 and 2: **Prismatic (P)** — slider A in horizontal slot. *(1 mark)*
  - Pair between 1 and 3: **Prismatic (P)** — slider B in vertical slot. *(1 mark)*
  - Pair between 2 and 4: **Revolute (R)** — pin at end of bar. *(1 mark)*
  - Pair between 3 and 4: **Revolute (R)** — pin at other end of bar. *(1 mark)*

> **Skeleton Diagram (text schematic):**
>
> ```
>  +----------- Frame (Link 1) -----------+
>  |     <----- P (Slot 1, Horizontal)
>  |     [A]----R====bar====R----[B]
>  |               (Link 4)         |
>  |                                ^ P (Slot 2, Vertical)
>  +---------------------------------+
> ```
>
> A is slider 2, B is slider 3, and the bar between them is link 4.

**Part (b) — DOF Calculation (7 marks):**

Count the links and joints:

$$
n = 4 \quad (\text{frame} + 2 \text{ sliders} + 1 \text{ bar})
$$

$$
j_{1} = 4 \quad (2 \text{ P-pairs} + 2 \text{ R-pairs})
$$

$$
j_{2} = 0
$$

Apply **Kutzbach's equation**:

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_{1} - j_{2}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DOF} = 1\;}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $\text{DOF} = 1$, the trammel of Archimedes is a **valid single-DOF mechanism**. One input (say, the horizontal position of slider A) is sufficient to fully determine the motion of slider B and the orientation of the connecting bar. The point on the bar traces a mathematical ellipse — consistent with classical kinematics.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

Consider an **internal combustion engine valve train** consisting of:
- a cam rotating about the frame,
- a roller-tipped follower in sliding contact with the cam,
- the follower constrained to move vertically by a fixed guide, and
- the follower pushing a valve stem that is also constrained by the same guide.

**(a)** Identify the **number of links**, **number of joints**, and **classify each joint** as lower or higher. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **degrees of freedom** of this mechanism, carefully accounting for the **passive DOF** of the roller. Is the result a structure, a mechanism, or over-constrained? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Link and Joint Identification (7 marks):**

| # | Link | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Frame | Engine head with cam-shaft bearing and guide | 1 |
| 2 | Cam | Rotating about frame centre | 1 |
| 3 | Roller | Spins freely at tip of follower | 1 |
| 4 | Follower (with valve stem) | Translates vertically in guide | 1 |

**Joint classification (3 marks):**

- **J1:** Cam–Frame — **Revolute (R)** — Lower pair, removes 2 DOF. (1 mark)
- **J2:** Cam–Roller — **Cam pair** — Higher pair, removes 1 DOF. (1 mark)
- **J3:** Roller–Follower — **Revolute (R)** — Lower pair, removes 2 DOF. (1 mark)
- **J4:** Follower–Frame — **Prismatic (P)** — Lower pair, removes 2 DOF. (1 mark)

*(Total 7 marks for Part a.)*

**Part (b) — DOF with Passive-DOF Correction (7 marks):**

Ignoring the roller spin for a moment:

$$
n = 4, \quad j_{1} = 3 \,(\text{R, R, P}), \quad j_{2} = 1 \,(\text{cam pair})
$$

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{raw}} = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 1 = 9 - 6 - 1 = 2
$$

But the **roller spins idly** — its rotation about its own axis does not affect the output motion. This is a **passive DOF** ($f_{\text{passive}} = 1$).

Apply the **Gruebler correction**:

$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{net}} = \text{DOF}_{\text{raw}} - f_{\text{passive}} = 2 - 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{DOF}_{\text{net}} = 1\;}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The valve train is a **single-DOF mechanism**. The cam rotation (one input) drives the follower translation deterministically. Without subtracting the passive roller DOF, a student would erroneously conclude $\text{DOF} = 2$, which contradicts the physical reality — a classic KTU trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Kinematic Diagrams**

- **Definition:** A kinematic (skeleton) diagram is a *line-and-symbol* abstraction of a mechanism, showing only **link connectivity** and **joint types** — never physical shape or scale.
- **Standard symbols** (IS 10579 / ISO 3952): R = pin, P = slider, H = cam/gear, S = screw, Sp = spherical.
- **Lower pair** = surface contact, removes **2** DOF; **Higher pair** = point/line contact, removes **1** DOF.
- **Kutzbach's Equation (planar):**  
  $$\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$$  
  where $n$ = total links (frame + moving), $j_{1}$ = lower pairs, $j_{2}$ = higher pairs.
- **Interpretation of DOF:** 0 ⇒ structure, 1 ⇒ mechanism, ≥ 2 ⇒ multi-DOF system, < 0 ⇒ redundantly constrained (e.g., Peaucellier–Lipkin).
- **Passive DOF correction:** Subtract the spin of an idling roller (or any link whose motion does not propagate to the output):  
  $$\text{DOF}_{\text{net}} = \text{DOF}_{\text{raw}} - f_{\text{passive}}.$$
- **Always count the frame** as link 1 — forgetting it is the single most common KTU mistake.
- **Always draw joint symbols** at link intersections — a kinematic diagram *without* symbols is incomplete and loses 2 marks.
- **Inversions** of a kinematic chain (changing which link is fixed) produce different mechanisms — same skeleton, different function. Examples: slider-crank ↔ pendulum pump ↔ rotary internal combustion engine ↔ Whitworth quick-return.
- **Real-world origin points:** Engine valve train, sewing machine, bicycle, windshield wiper, robot arm, four-bar coupling — every one begins life as a kinematic diagram.
- **Foundation for higher modules:** Velocity & acceleration analysis, Freudenstein's equation, synthesis of mechanisms, and dynamics all build on this single abstraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MECHANICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Kinematic Inversion ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Kinematic Chain** is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected to each other by joints (kinematic pairs) such that each link moves relative to the ground/fixed link in a constrained and predictable manner. When one of the links in a kinematic chain is **fixed (grounded)**, the resulting constrained system is called a **Mechanism**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kinematic Inversion** is the process of *obtaining different mechanisms* by **fixing different links** of the same kinematic chain, *one at a time*, while keeping the relative motion between the links unchanged. Each unique choice of fixed link produces a mechanically distinct output motion, even though the link lengths remain identical. Mathematically, if a kinematic chain has **$n$** links, the number of possible distinct inversions is: $$ N_{inversions} = n - 1 $$ The minimum number of links required to form a constrained mechanism with **one degree of freedom (DOF = 1)** is **$n = 4$** (using Kutzbach's criterion with all lower pairs). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife of Mechanisms** > Imagine a single chain of four rigid bars joined by pin joints — it is like a *flexible, transformable gadget*. If you bolt **Bar 1** to the table, it acts as a *rock crusher* (crank-rocker). Bolt **Bar 2** instead, and the same gadget now becomes a *windscreen wiper* (double-crank). Bolt **Bar 4** and it transforms into a *coupling* (double-rocker). The chain's *identity* (its links) never changes — only **which link plays the role of the ground** changes. That single act of re-grounding is what we call **Kinematic Inversion**. ### 1.3 Classification of Standard Inversions (KTU 2024 Focus) The three primary kinematic chains studied in Module 1 of PBMET404 are: 1. **Four-Bar Chain** (4 binary turning pairs) — yields **4 distinct inversions**. 2. **Single Slider-Crank Chain** (3 turning + 1 sliding pair) — yields **4 distinct inversions**. 3. **Double Slider-Crank Chain** (2 turning + 2 sliding pairs) — yields **3 distinct inversions**. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards Used > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Degrees of Freedom for a planar mechanism (Kutzbach):** $\quad DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ > - **Lower pair:** Surface contact pair (revolute, prismatic) — DOF consumed = **2** > - **Higher pair:** Point/line contact pair (cam, gear) — DOF consumed = **1** > - Standard link naming convention: Link **1 = Fixed (ground)**, Links **2, 3, 4** = moving links. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic Behind Kinematic Inversion The fundamental logic of inversion rests on the principle of **relative motion**: the *relative* velocities and accelerations between any two links of a kinematic chain are **invariant** regardless of which link is fixed. The mathematical operation of inversion does **not** alter the geometry of the chain — it simply *relabels* the frame of reference. Step-by-step reasoning: - **Step 1:** Identify the parent kinematic chain (e.g., four-bar chain with link lengths $a, b, c, d$). - **Step 2:** Examine Grashof's Law: $S + L \leq P + Q$ (where $S$ = shortest, $L$ = longest, $P, Q$ = other two) to predict motion type. - **Step 3:** Fix each non-ground link successively. This produces $(n-1)$ distinct mechanisms. - **Step 4:** Each inversion is studied for its **input-output motion characteristics** (e.g., continuous rotation, oscillation, reciprocation, quick-return). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready) | # | Mechanism (Inversion) | Fixed Link | Input | Output Motion | Key Formula | |---|----------------------|------------|-------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Crank-Rocker (Beam Engine)** | Link 4 (rocker) | Crank (continuous) | Oscillating sector | $\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2cd\cos\theta_3}{2a(c - d\cos\theta_3)}\right]$ | | 2 | **Double-Crank (Coupling Rod)** | Link 1 (any short link) | Crank | Continuous rotation | $\dfrac{\omega_4}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{a \, c}{b \, d} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta_3 - \phi)}{\sin(\theta_4 - \phi)}$ | | 3 | **Double-Rocker** | Link 1 (intermediate) | Crank | Both cranks oscillate | Grashof condition: $S + L > P + Q$ | | 4 | **Crank & Slotted Lever QRM** | Slider fixed (4) | Crank | Oscillating lever with Q-R | $QRF = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta} = \dfrac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}$ | | 5 | **Whitworth QRM** | Link 4 (fixed) | Crank | Lever with faster return | $\alpha = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) - 90^\circ$ | | 6 | **Pendulum Pump / Bull Engine** | Cylinder (slider) fixed | Crank | Piston-like reciprocation | $QRF = \dfrac{360^\circ - \alpha}{180^\circ - \alpha}$ approx | | 7 | **Oscillating Cylinder Engine** | Link 1 (crank fixed) | Slider | Cylinder oscillates | $\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r + l}{l}\right)$ bounded | | 8 | **Rotary IC Engine** | Crank (link 2) fixed | Slider | Continuous output | $\dfrac{\omega_{slider}}{\omega_{crank}} = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{x}$ | | 9 | **Elliptical Trammel** | Slider 1 fixed (or plate) | Sliders | Ellipse traced | $\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{y}{b}\right)^2 = 1$ | | 10 | **Scotch Yoke** | Slotted link fixed | Slider | Pure harmonic motion | $y = r\sin\theta, \quad v = r\omega\cos\theta, \quad a = -r\omega^2\sin\theta$ | | 11 | **Oldham's Coupling** | Center disc fixed | Two sliders | Misalignment compensation | Slip velocity = $\omega \cdot e$ where $e$ = offset | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule (KTU Board Examiner Expectation):** When asked "what is the inversion used in…", the answer MUST contain: > (a) the **parent chain**, > (b) the **link fixed**, > (c) the **resulting motion** achieved. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Inversion Theory Inversion is not a theoretical curiosity — it underpins nearly every machine you can name: - **Shaper & Slotter machines** → Crank & Slotted Lever Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM). - **Planing machines** → Whitworth QRM. - **Capstan lathe spindle reversal** → Geneva mechanism (cam inversion, advanced topic). - **Internal combustion engines** → Rotary IC engine inversion of slider-crank. - **Pumpjack units in oil fields** → Pendulum pump (Bull engine) inversion. - **Steering of naval vessels** → Oscillating cylinder engine principle. - **High-speed packaging lines** → Geneva drive (inversion of slider-crank with a slotted link). ### 2.4 Grashof's Law — The Predictor of Motion Type For a four-bar chain with links $S \leq P \leq Q \leq L$: $$ \boxed{S + L \;\leq\; P + Q \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Grashof Chain (continuous rotation possible for the shortest link adjacent to ground)}} $$ - **$S$ adjacent to ground** → Crank-Rocker or Double-Crank. - **$S$ is the ground link** → Double-Crank (Coupler). - **$S$ opposite to ground** → Grashof Double-Crank (Drag-link). - **$S + L > P + Q$** → Non-Grashof → **Double-Rocker** (Triple-Rocker possible). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Quick-Return Factor (QRF) of Crank & Slotted Lever Mechanism > **Background:** In the CSL QRM, the driving crank rotates at **uniform speed** $\omega$. The slotted lever oscillates, taking time $T_f$ for the forward (cutting) stroke and $T_r$ for the return (idle) stroke. The ratio $\dfrac{T_f}{T_r}$ is the **Quick-Return Factor**. Let us denote: - $a$ = length of driving crank - $b$ = length of the slotted lever (pivot to crank center distance is $b$) - $\alpha$ = angle of forward stroke of the slotted lever - $\beta$ = angle of return stroke of the slotted lever - $\alpha + \beta = 360^\circ = 2\pi$ radians (full revolution of crank) Since the crank rotates at **uniform angular velocity** $\omega_2$, the time taken is proportional to the angular sweep of the crank. **Time for forward stroke** (crank rotates through angle $\alpha$): $$ T_f = \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega_2} $$ **Time for return stroke** (crank rotates through angle $\beta$): $$ T_r = \dfrac{\beta}{\omega_2} $$ **Quick-Return Factor** is defined as: $$ QRF \;=\; \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha / \omega_2}{\beta / \omega_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} $$ Now, from the geometry of CSL QRM (with crank center $O$ and slotted-lever pivot $O'$ separated by distance $b$): When the crank is at the extreme angular positions of the slotted lever (where the slotted lever is tangent to the crank circle), the angles are: $$ \cos\phi_f = \dfrac{a}{b} \quad \text{(forward extreme position)} $$ $$ \cos\phi_r = -\dfrac{a}{b} \quad \text{(return extreme position)} $$ where $\phi_f$ and $\phi_r$ are the half-angles of the crank sweep at each extreme. Therefore, the angles of crank rotation are: $$ \alpha_{crank, \, f} = 180^\circ + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right) $$ $$ \alpha_{crank, \, r} = 180^\circ - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right) $$ Defining $\phi = \sin^{-1}(a/b)$, the QRF becomes: $$ \boxed{\; QRF \;=\; \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\phi}{180^\circ - 2\phi} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi + 2\phi}{\pi - 2\phi} \;} $$ This is the **standard KTU formula** for CSL QRM. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism — Return Stroke Time The Whitworth QRM has links of lengths $a$ (crank) and $b$ (lever), with both rotating about fixed centers separated by distance $c$. The return stroke time is governed by the geometric overlap of crank and lever rotation zones. The angle $\alpha$ swept by the driving crank during the return stroke is: $$ \alpha = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) - 90^\circ \quad \text{(Whitworth specific)} $$ Hence the **QRF for Whitworth** is: $$ QRF_{Whitworth} = \dfrac{360^\circ - \alpha}{\alpha} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio in Inversion — Relative Angular Velocity For any two links $i$ and $j$ of a mechanism, the **velocity ratio** when link $k$ is fixed is: $$ \dfrac{\omega_j^{(k)}}{\omega_i^{(k)}} = \dfrac{\text{(angular velocity of } j \text{ relative to fixed } k\text{)}}{\text{(angular velocity of } i \text{ relative to fixed } k\text{)}} $$ In inversion, the **relative angular velocity** between any two links is preserved: $$ \boxed{\; \omega_{ij}^{absolute} \;=\; \omega_{ij}^{(1)} \;=\; \omega_{ij}^{(2)} \;=\; \omega_{ij}^{(k)} \quad \forall \, k \;} $$ This invariance is the *mathematical heart* of inversion — it guarantees that one inversion can replace another in force/energy analysis without re-derivation. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Four-Bar Chain Inversions | S.No. | Inversion Name | Fixed Link | Grashof Case | Typical Application | |-------|---------------|------------|--------------|---------------------| | 1 | Beam Engine (Crank-Rocker) | Link 4 (longest, $L$) | $S+L \le P+Q$, $S$ adjacent to 1 | Reciprocating pumps, IC engine valve gear | | 2 | Coupling Rod (Double-Crank) | Link 1 (shortest, $S$) | $S+L \le P+Q$, $S$ is ground | Railway wheel coupling, parallel motion | | 3 | Drag-Link (Double-Crank) | Link 3 (opposite $S$) | $S$ opposite ground | Windshield wipers, walking-beam conveyors | | 4 | Double-Rocker | Any (non-Grashof) | $S + L > P + Q$ | Stone crusher, vibrating screen | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Single Slider-Crank Inversions | S.No. | Inversion Name | Fixed Link | Output Motion | Application | |-------|---------------|------------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | Pendulum Pump (Bull Engine) | Slider (link 4) | Slider oscillates within fixed cylinder | Oil-field pumpjacks | | 2 | Oscillating Cylinder Engine | Crank (link 2) | Cylinder oscillates on trunnion | Marine steering, low-speed engines | | 3 | Rotary IC Engine | Crank (link 2) | Slider rotates in a circular path | Rotary Wankel-type concept | | 4 | Crank & Slotted Lever QRM | Slotted lever (link 3) | Lever oscillates, quick return | Shapers, slotters | ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Double Slider-Crank Inversions | S.No. | Inversion Name | Fixed Link | Output Geometry | Application | |-------|---------------|------------|-----------------|-------------| | 1 | Elliptical Trammel | Any slider (link 4) | Ellipse traced by pin | Drafting instrument, ellipse drawing | | 2 | Scotch Yoke | Slotted link (link 3) | Pure sinusoidal output | High-speed actuators, valve timing | | 3 | Oldham's Coupling | Center disc (link 3) | Rotational transmission with offset | Shaft misalignment compensation | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Inversion Generation Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Parent Kinematic Chain n links] --> B{Choose Fixed Link} B --> C1[Fix Link 1
Inversion 1] B --> C2[Fix Link 2
Inversion 2] B --> C3[Fix Link 3
Inversion 3] B --> C4[Fix Link 4
Inversion n-1] C1 --> D1[Mechanism A
Distinct motion] C2 --> D2[Mechanism B
Distinct motion] C3 --> D3[Mechanism C
Distinct motion] C4 --> D4[Mechanism D
Distinct motion] D1 --> E[Same Link Geometry
Different Functional Output] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E ``` ### 4.2 Four-Bar Chain Inversion Family Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Four-Bar Chain
Links 1, 2, 3, 4] --> Q{Grashof Check
S + L vs P + Q} Q -->|S + L less equal P + Q
Grashof| R1[Link 4 fixed
Crank Rocker Beam Engine] Q -->|S + L less equal P + Q
S is ground| R2[Link 1 fixed
Double Crank Coupling Rod] Q -->|S + L less equal P + Q
S opposite ground| R3[Link 3 fixed
Drag Link] Q -->|S + L greater P + Q
Non Grashof| R4[Any fix
Double Rocker] R1 --> APP1[Pumps, IC Engines] R2 --> APP2[Railway Couplings] R3 --> APP3[Windscreen Wipers] R4 --> APP4[Stone Crushers, Vibrating Screens] ``` ### 4.3 Single Slider-Crank Inversion Family Map ```mermaid flowchart TD SC[Single Slider Crank Chain
3 turning pairs + 1 sliding pair] --> S1[Fix Slider
Link 4] SC --> S2[Fix Crank
Link 2] SC --> S3[Fix Connecting Rod
Link 3] S1 --> M1[Pendulum Pump
