Bull Engine] S2 --> M2[Oscillating Cylinder Engine
Rotary IC Engine] S3 --> M3[Crank and Slotted Lever QRM
Shaper Machine] M1 --> U1[Oil Field Pumpjacks] M2 --> U2[Marine Steering Systems] M3 --> U3[Metal Cutting Shapers] ``` ### 4.4 Double Slider-Crank Inversion Family Map ```mermaid flowchart LR DS[Double Slider Crank Chain
2 turning + 2 sliding pairs] --> F1[Fix Slider 1
Link 4] DS --> F2[Fix Slotted Link
Link 3] DS --> F3[Fix Center Disc
Link 3 disk] F1 --> O1[Elliptical Trammel
Ellipse Path x over a squared + y over b squared = 1] F2 --> O2[Scotch Yoke
Pure Harmonic y = r sin theta] F3 --> O3[Oldham Coupling
Offset Shaft Compensation] O1 --> A1[Drafting and Drawing Tools] O2 --> A2[High Speed Actuators] O3 --> A3[Shaft Couplings in Misaligned Drives] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quick-Return Mechanism (CSL QRM) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] M[Uniform Speed Motor] end subgraph DRIVE[Drive Stage] CR[Crank Pin
Radius a
Angular Velocity omega] end subgraph TRANS[Transmission Stage] SL[Slotted Lever
Length b
Slides on Crank Pin] PIV[Pivot O Prime
Fixed Frame] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage] OS[Oscillating Lever
Angular Sweep alpha plus beta = 360] TOOL[Cutting Tool
Ram of Shaper] end subgraph QRF[Quick Return Computation] TC[Cutting Stroke Time
T f = alpha over omega] TR[Return Stroke Time
T r = beta over omega] Q[QRF = T f over T r = alpha over beta] end M --> CR CR --> SL PIV --> SL SL --> OS OS --> TOOL CR --> TC CR --> TR TC --> Q TR --> Q ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]`** **Q1. Define kinematic inversion. How many inversions are possible for a four-bar chain, and list the names of each inversion.** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 Mark):** Kinematic inversion is the method of obtaining different mechanisms by fixing different links of the same kinematic chain, one at a time, while preserving the relative motion between all other links. > **Count (1 Mark):** A four-bar chain has $n = 4$ links, so the number of possible inversions is $n - 1 = 3$. > *(Note: Some texts count 4 including "no link fixed" — examiners expect $n-1$ for KTU.)* > **Listing (1 Mark):** > (i) **Beam Engine (Crank-Rocker)** — Link 4 fixed. > (ii) **Coupling Rod (Double-Crank)** — Link 1 fixed. > (iii) **Drag-Link (Double-Crank)** — Link 3 fixed. > (When Grashof's condition fails, the same chain gives a **Double-Rocker**.) --- > **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]`** **Q2. State Grashof's Law. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between a crank-rocker mechanism and a double-crank mechanism.** > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Grashof's Law (1.5 Marks):** A four-bar chain will have at least one link capable of **full continuous rotation** if and only if the sum of the shortest and longest link lengths is **less than or equal to** the sum of the other two link lengths: > $$S + L \le P + Q$$ > **Difference (1.5 Marks):** > - In a **crank-rocker mechanism**, the *shortest link* is adjacent to the fixed link and acts as a crank (full rotation), while the *opposite* link is the rocker (oscillates). > - In a **double-crank mechanism**, the *shortest link itself is the fixed (ground) link*, and the two links adjacent to it both rotate fully. > - Example: Beam engine = crank-rocker; Railway coupling rod = double-crank. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- > **`[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, PBMET404]`** **PART B — Question 1 (Choice A) [14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **Quick-Return Factor (QRF)** of a **Crank and Slotted Lever Mechanism**. State clearly which link is fixed in this inversion. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A CSL QRM has crank length $a = 75$ mm and the distance between the crank centre and the slotted-lever pivot is $b = 200$ mm. The crank rotates at **120 rpm** uniformly. Calculate **(i)** the QRF, **(ii)** the time for the cutting stroke, and **(iii)** the time for the return stroke. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question 1 (Choice A) **(a) Derivation of QRF (7 Marks):** - **Statement (1 Mark):** The Crank and Slotted Lever Quick-Return Mechanism is the **inversion of a single slider-crank chain** in which the **slotted lever (Link 3) is fixed**. The crank rotates uniformly, and the slotted lever oscillates about its pivot $O'$. - **Geometry Setup (2 Marks):** Let crank length = $a$ (rotating about $O$), distance $OO' = b$ (fixed). When the crank pin lies on the extreme positions, the slotted lever is tangent to the crank circle. Let $\phi$ be the half-angle of the crank's swept sector beyond the dead-centre line. - **Step-by-step derivation (4 Marks):** The crank rotates through angle $\alpha_f$ during forward (cutting) stroke and $\alpha_r$ during return stroke. $$ \alpha_f = 180^\circ + 2\phi, \quad \alpha_r = 180^\circ - 2\phi $$ where $\sin\phi = \dfrac{a}{b}$. Since crank rotates at uniform $\omega_2$: $$ T_f = \dfrac{\alpha_f}{\omega_2}, \quad T_r = \dfrac{\alpha_r}{\omega_2} $$ $$ QRF = \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\phi}{180^\circ - 2\phi} = \dfrac{\pi + 2\phi}{\pi - 2\phi} $$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** $a = 75$ mm, $b = 200$ mm, $N = 120$ rpm. **Step 1: Compute half-angle $\phi$:** $$ \sin\phi = \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{75}{200} = 0.375 $$ $$ \phi = \sin^{-1}(0.375) = 22.024^\circ $$ **Step 2: Compute QRF (2 Marks):** $$ QRF = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2(22.024^\circ)}{180^\circ - 2(22.024^\circ)} = \dfrac{180 + 44.048}{180 - 44.048} = \dfrac{224.048}{135.952} $$ $$ \boxed{QRF = 1.648} $$ **Step 3: Crank angular velocity (1 Mark):** $$ \omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi (120)}{60} = 4\pi = 12.566 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 4: Cutting stroke time $T_f$ (2 Marks):** $$ T_f = \dfrac{\alpha_f}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{224.048^\circ \times \pi / 180^\circ}{12.566} = \dfrac{3.9104}{12.566} $$ $$ \boxed{T_f = 0.311 \text{ s}} $$ **Step 5: Return stroke time $T_r$ (2 Marks):** $$ T_r = \dfrac{T_f}{QRF} = \dfrac{0.311}{1.648} $$ $$ \boxed{T_r = 0.189 \text{ s}} $$ **Verification:** $T_f + T_r = 0.500$ s = $\dfrac{60}{120}$ s = time for one revolution ✓ --- > **`[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, PBMET404]`** **PART B — Question 1 (Choice B) [14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism** as an inversion of the four-bar chain. State which link is fixed and derive an expression for its quick-return factor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the construction and working of the **Pendulum Pump (Bull Engine)** as an inversion of the slider-crank chain. State the fixed link, the moving link, and the input-output relationship. Why is it called a "Bull Engine"? **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question 1 (Choice B) **(a) Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism (7 Marks):** - **Inversion Identification (2 Marks):** The Whitworth QRM is an **inversion of a four-bar kinematic chain** in which the **coupler (Link 3) is fixed**. The two remaining links rotate about their own fixed pivots, but one is driven at uniform speed and the other (output) has a quick-return characteristic. - **Construction (2 Marks):** A driving crank of length $a$ and a driven crank of length $b$ are connected by a coupler. The driving crank is rotated uniformly; the driven crank performs fast forward and slow return motions. - **Derivation (3 Marks):** The angle of return stroke $\alpha$ is given by: $$ \alpha = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) - 90^\circ $$ $$ QRF_{Whitworth} = \dfrac{360^\circ - \alpha}{\alpha} $$ - **Application:** Planing machines, slotting machines, large-stroke shapers where longer strokes are required than the CSL QRM can provide. **(b) Pendulum Pump / Bull Engine (7 Marks):** - **Inversion Identification (1 Mark):** It is an inversion of the **single slider-crank chain** in which the **slider (piston) is fixed**. - **Working (3 Marks):** The crank (link 2) rotates uniformly about a fixed pivot. The connecting rod (link 3) drives a piston rod, but since the piston is fixed, the cylinder itself swings (oscillates) about the piston axis. The connecting rod thus sweeps a pendulum-like motion. - **Fixed & Moving Links (1 Mark):** Fixed = Slider/cylinder; Input = Crank; Output = Pendulum motion of the connecting rod end. - **"Bull Engine" name (1 Mark):** The early pumping engines of this design were large, slow, and powerful — used to pump water out of deep Cornish tin mines. The powerful, steady, plodding motion reminded operators of a *bull*, hence the name. - **Application (1 Mark):** Oil-field pumpjacks, mine dewatering pumps. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > - **Common Mistake #1 (Loss of 2–3 Marks):** Writing "fixing a link" without specifying **which link number** is fixed. The KTU answer key requires **link numbers AND link names** (e.g., "Link 3 — the coupler — is fixed"). > - **Common Mistake #2 (Loss of 2 Marks):** In QRF derivations, students often write $QRF = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$ without defining what $\alpha$ and $\beta$ represent. Always include the *angle units* and the *physical meaning* (forward/return). > - **Common Mistake #3 (Loss of 1–2 Marks):** Mixing up the *crank-and-slotted-lever* (QRM, Link 3 fixed) with the *slotted-lever quick-return* (different mechanism). When the slotted lever is fixed, it is the QRM. When the slider is fixed, it is the Pendulum Pump. > - **Common Mistake #4 (Loss of 1 Mark):** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s in numericals. Use $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, *not* $\omega = N$ directly. > - **Common Mistake #5 (Loss of 1 Mark):** Not drawing the **input-output arrows** on the sketch. Examiners specifically check for clear indication of which link is the *driver* and which is the *follower*. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision — Kinematic Inversion Module 1** - [x] **Kinematic Inversion** = same chain, different fixed link, different mechanism. - [x] Number of inversions for an $n$-link chain $= n - 1$. - [x] **Four-bar chain inversions (3):** Crank-Rocker, Double-Crank, Double-Rocker. - [x] **Slider-crank inversions (3):** Pendulum Pump, Oscillating Cylinder, Rotary IC Engine + CSL QRM (4th). - [x] **Double slider-crank inversions (3):** Elliptical Trammel, Scotch Yoke, Oldham's Coupling. - [x] **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \le P + Q \Rightarrow$ at least one link rotates fully. - [x] **CSL QRF Formula:** $\dfrac{180^\circ + 2\phi}{180^\circ - 2\phi}$ with $\sin\phi = a/b$. - [x] **Whitworth QRF:** $QRF = \dfrac{360^\circ - \alpha}{\alpha}$, where $\alpha = 2\tan^{-1}(b/a) - 90^\circ$. - [x] **Beam Engine** → Link 4 fixed, longest link is the rocker. - [x] **Pendulum Pump** → Slider fixed, crank rotates, connecting rod swings. - [x] **Elliptical Trammel** → Traces ellipse $\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{y}{b}\right)^2 = 1$. - [x] **Scotch Yoke** → $y = r\sin\theta$, pure simple harmonic motion. - [x] **Oldham's Coupling** → Compensates lateral shaft misalignment, slip velocity = $\omega e$. - [x] **Inversion invariant:** Relative angular velocities are preserved across all inversions of the same chain. - [x] **Exam tip:** Always state the **parent chain + fixed link + output motion** in any 3-mark question. - [x] **Exam tip:** Use $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ for all rpm-based numericals. - [x] **Exam tip:** Verify final answers by checking that $T_f + T_r = \dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds (one revolution). --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 1 — Kinematic Inversion Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PBMET404).** > *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO1: Apply kinematic concepts to machinery), Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1–L4 (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze), and the official KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) marking scheme. Practice the above numericals twice before sitting for the examination.*

topics.title: Four Bar Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Four Bar Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Four Bar Chain** (also called a **Four Bar Linkage** or **Quadric Crank Chain**) is the simplest closed, single-degree-of-freedom (DOF), planar kinematic chain consisting of **four rigid links** connected in a loop by **four lower pairs (revolute joints/pins)**. In any practical mechanism, one link is fixed to the ground and is called the **frame (or fixed link)**, while the other three are the **input link, coupler link, and output link**.

Mathematically, using **Kutzbach's criterion** (Gruebler's equation) for planar mechanisms:

$$DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where $n = 4$ links and $j_1 = 4$ revolute lower pairs, gives:

$$DOF = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 9 - 8 = 1$$

Hence, a four bar chain possesses exactly **one degree of freedom**, meaning a single input (say, angular motion of the crank) uniquely defines the position of every other link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The four bar chain is the **parent mechanism** of nearly all planar linkages. The slider-crank, Whitworth quick-return, and inverted slider-crank are all derivatives obtained by modifying one link of the basic four bar chain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine the **frame of a classic bicycle** without the wheels — the main diamond-shaped rigid body has four bars connected at four pin joints, with the rear triangle fixed to the ground (the frame). Now, set the front triangle free to rotate about the head tube joint — you have a four bar mechanism that controls your steering.

Even simpler: take **two hands** holding the ends of a ruler on a table (hands = ground pivots), and place a pencil between the two hands connecting them — as one hand moves, the pencil tilts and swings. The two hands + the two segments between them form a four bar chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Examples:** Internal combustion engine (piston via slider-crank, which is a four bar derivative), sewing machine needle drive, windshield wiper, pumpjack (oil derrick), folding chair, and a typical folding luggage carrier.

### 1.3 Nomenclature of the Four Links

| Symbol | Link Name | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Link 1** | **Ground / Frame** | Fixed pivot-to-pivot link (immovable reference) |
| **Link 2** | **Input / Driver (Crank or Rocker)** | The link to which motion/force is applied |
| **Link 3** | **Coupler** | The intermediate floating link; transfers motion |
| **Link 4** | **Output / Follower (Rocker or Crank)** | Receives motion from the coupler |

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention (KTU Standard):** If a link makes a **complete 360° rotation**, it is called a **crank**. If it **oscillates (rockes) back and forth** through a finite angle, it is called a **rocker**. The coupler **never connects to the ground directly** — it is always a "floating link."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inversion Principle — Why the Four Bar Chain Matters

An **inversion** is obtained by fixing different links of a kinematic chain. Since a four bar chain has 4 links, it can theoretically have **4 inversions**. However, only the inversions with the shortest or longest link as ground produce practically useful mechanisms. The inversions are not the focus of Module 1.1 — but the **classification of the chain itself** (based on relative link lengths) is the central KTU question type.

### 2.2 Grashof's Law — The Heart of Four Bar Chain Analysis

**Grashof's Law** (1833) provides a simple inequality that predicts the **rotational behavior** of any four bar chain based purely on the **lengths of its four links**.

Let the four link lengths be:
- $L_1$ = ground link (or just any link you designate)
- $S$ = **Shortest** link
- $L$ = **Longest** link
- $P, Q$ = the other two intermediate links

**Grashof's Inequality:**

$$S + L \;\leq\; P + Q$$

- If $S + L < P + Q$ → **Grashof Chain** (Class I) — at least one link can rotate FULLY (360°).
- If $S + L > P + Q$ → **Non-Grashof Chain** (Class II) — NO link can rotate fully; all oscillate.
- If $S + L = P + Q$ → **Change-Point Chain** (Special Class) — indeterminate; on the verge of becoming a non-Grashof linkage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Point:** The Grashof condition depends only on the **relative link lengths**, not on which link is the ground. The classification into crank-rocker, double-crank, etc. depends on **WHICH link is grounded**.

### 2.3 Classification Table (for Grashof Chains — $S + L < P + Q$)

| Ground Link Choice | Resulting Mechanism | Practical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Shortest (S)** | **Double Crank (Drag Link)** | Both input and output rotate fully — Coupler rotations, parallel motion |
| **Any link adjacent to S** | **Crank-Rocker (Crank-Oscillator)** | IC engine valve gear, hand-driven presses |
| **Longest (L)** | **Double Rocker (Double Oscillator)** | Rock crushers, walking mechanisms |
| **Coupler (link opposite to S)** | **Grashof Double-Crank (Drag Link)** | Parallelogram linkage, pantograph |

> [!NOTE]
> For **Non-Grashof Chains** ($S + L > P + Q$): Whatever the ground link, the mechanism is a **Double Rocker** (Triple-Rocker if ground is the link adjacent to L).

### 2.4 Dead-Center / Extreme Positions

When the **input crank and coupler become collinear** (overlapping or extended), the **output rocker reaches its extreme angular position** and momentarily has **zero angular velocity**. These are the **dead centers** or **limit positions**. They are critical for designing return-stroke mechanisms.

$$Angular\ sweep\ of\ rocker = \theta_4^{max} - \theta_4^{min}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Memorize the table completely.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | DOF of four bar chain | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$ | $n=4, j_1=4 \Rightarrow F=1$ |
| 2 | Grashof's inequality | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Lengths in metres (m) |
| 3 | Loop-closure (X) | $r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_1 + r_4\cos\theta_4$ | Position, m |
| 4 | Loop-closure (Y) | $r_2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\sin\theta_3 = r_4\sin\theta_4$ | Position, m |
| 5 | Velocity (X) | $-r_2\omega_2\sin\theta_2 - r_3\omega_3\sin\theta_3 = -r_4\omega_4\sin\theta_4$ | Differentiate (3) w.r.t. $t$ |
| 6 | Velocity (Y) | $r_2\omega_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\omega_3\cos\theta_3 = r_4\omega_4\cos\theta_4$ | Differentiate (4) w.r.t. $t$ |
| 7 | Acceleration (X) | $-r_2\alpha_2\sin\theta_2 - r_2\omega_2^2\cos\theta_2 - r_3\alpha_3\sin\theta_3 - r_3\omega_3^2\cos\theta_3 = -r_4\alpha_4\sin\theta_4 - r_4\omega_4^2\cos\theta_4$ | Differentiate (5) w.r.t. $t$ |
| 8 | Acceleration (Y) | $r_2\alpha_2\cos\theta_2 - r_2\omega_2^2\sin\theta_2 + r_3\alpha_3\cos\theta_3 - r_3\omega_3^2\sin\theta_3 = r_4\alpha_4\cos\theta_4 - r_4\omega_4^2\sin\theta_4$ | Differentiate (6) w.r.t. $t$ |
| 9 | Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \dfrac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{\text{Effort arm}}{\text{Load arm}} = \dfrac{\sin(\theta_2 - \phi)}{\sin(\phi - \theta_4)}$ | Dimensionless, in dead-centre positions reduces to simple geometric ratio |
| 10 | Transmission angle $\mu$ | $\mu = \angle BCD$ (angle of coupler with rocker) | Should be $\approx 90°$ for best force transmission; never $< 40°$ or $> 140°$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Grashof's Law guides every automobile suspension design, every robot arm, every prosthetic limb, and every printing press. The **transmission angle $\mu$** decides whether a four bar mechanism can transmit power efficiently without jamming (self-locking).

### 2.6 Mechanical Advantage — Physical Meaning

When the input link rotates by an infinitesimal angle, the mechanism behaves like a lever. The **mechanical advantage** is the ratio of resisting torque to applied torque, determined by the **instantaneous geometry** through the transmission angle. A transmission angle of **90°** is ideal because the entire coupler force contributes to the rocker motion without wasted lateral components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vector Loop Closure for a Four Bar Chain

Consider a four bar mechanism with:
- Fixed pivots at $A$ (origin) and $D$.
- **Link 1** = $AD$ (ground), of length $r_1$ along the X-axis.
- **Link 2** = $AB$ (input crank), of length $r_2$ at angle $\theta_2$ from X-axis.
- **Link 3** = $BC$ (coupler), of length $r_3$ at angle $\theta_3$ from X-axis.
- **Link 4** = $CD$ (output rocker), of length $r_4$ at angle $\theta_4$ from X-axis.

The position vectors must form a closed loop:

$$\vec{AB} + \vec{BC} = \vec{AD} + \vec{DC}$$

**Expanding into X and Y components:**

**X-component equation:**

$$r_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \cos\theta_3 = r_1 + r_4 \cos\theta_4$$

**Y-component equation:**

$$r_2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \sin\theta_3 = r_4 \sin\theta_4$$

> *These are the two scalar loop-closure equations that govern the **position** of the mechanism.*

### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity Equations (Differentiate Position w.r.t. time)

Differentiating the X-equation with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[r_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \cos\theta_3\right] = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[r_1 + r_4 \cos\theta_4\right]$$

Since $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ are constants (rigid links) and $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$-r_2 \sin\theta_2 \cdot \frac{d\theta_2}{dt} - r_3 \sin\theta_3 \cdot \frac{d\theta_3}{dt} = -r_4 \sin\theta_4 \cdot \frac{d\theta_4}{dt}$$

**Substituting $\omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ for the angular velocities:**

$$-r_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3 \sin\theta_3 = -r_4 \omega_4 \sin\theta_4$$

**Similarly, differentiating the Y-equation:**

$$r_2 \omega_2 \cos\theta_2 + r_3 \omega_3 \cos\theta_3 = r_4 \omega_4 \cos\theta_4$$

> *These two simultaneous linear equations in the unknowns $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ can be solved by Cramer's rule or elimination, given the input $\omega_2$ and the current angular positions $\theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$.*

### 3.3 Derivation: Acceleration Equations (Differentiate Velocity w.r.t. time)

Differentiating the X-velocity equation with respect to time:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[-r_2 \omega_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_3 \omega_3 \sin\theta_3\right] = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[-r_4 \omega_4 \sin\theta_4\right]$$

Apply the product rule $\frac{d}{dt}(\omega \sin\theta) = \alpha \sin\theta + \omega^2 \cos\theta$:

$$-r_2 (\alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 + \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2) - r_3 (\alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 + \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3) = -r_4 (\alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 + \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4)$$

Rearranging:

$$-r_2 \alpha_2 \sin\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \cos\theta_2 - r_3 \alpha_3 \sin\theta_3 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \cos\theta_3 = -r_4 \alpha_4 \sin\theta_4 - r_4 \omega_4^2 \cos\theta_4$$

**Similarly, differentiating the Y-velocity equation:**

$$r_2 \alpha_2 \cos\theta_2 - r_2 \omega_2^2 \sin\theta_2 + r_3 \alpha_3 \cos\theta_3 - r_3 \omega_3^2 \sin\theta_3 = r_4 \alpha_4 \cos\theta_4 - r_4 \omega_4^2 \sin\theta_4$$

> *These two equations, along with the velocity results, allow complete determination of the angular accelerations $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$ given the input $\alpha_2$.*

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this exact sequence in your KTU ESE 2024 answer scripts for full marks.**

| Step | Action | Reason |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Identify all four link lengths; designate $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$. | Required for Grashof's check. |
| **2** | Apply Grashof's law: find $S, L$ and check $S+L$ vs $P+Q$. | Decides mechanism type. |
| **3** | Write the loop-closure X and Y equations with given $\theta$ values. | Position analysis. |
| **4** | Differentiate twice to get velocity and acceleration scalar equations. | Velocity/acceleration analysis. |
| **5** | Solve the resulting $2 \times 2$ linear system using Cramer's rule. | Computes unknowns. |
| **6** | Compute transmission angle $\mu$ to assess design quality. | Validates practical usability. |
| **7** | Draw the **space diagram** (with $\theta$ angles) and a **velocity polygon**. | Mandatory for full marks per KTU valuation key. |
| **8** | Compute Mechanical Advantage if force/torque values are given. | Often asked as sub-part (b). |

### 3.5 Special Case: Drag Link (Double-Crank) Parallelogram

When $r_1 = r_4$ and $r_2 = r_3$, the four bar becomes a **parallelogram linkage**. The coupler remains parallel to the ground, and the input and output cranks rotate at **identical angular velocities** ($\omega_2 = \omega_4$). This is the basis of **parallel-motion** devices such as the engineer's protractor base and pantograph enlargers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Four Bar Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GROUND LINK r1]:::ground -->|Fixed Pivot at A| B[INPUT LINK r2 - Crank or Rocker]
    B -->|Revolute Joint at B| C[COUPLER LINK r3 - Floating]
    C -->|Revolute Joint at C| D[OUTPUT LINK r4 - Rocker or Crank]
    D -->|Fixed Pivot at D| A
    E[Input Torque T2] -->|Applied at| B
    D -->|Delivers| F[Output Torque T4]
    C -->|Force Transfer| G[Transmission Angle Mu]

    classDef ground fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Grashof's Law Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Four Bar Linkage: r1, r2, r3, r4] --> identify[Identify S = Shortest, L = Longest]
    identify --> check{S plus L versus P plus Q}
    check -->|S + L less than P plus Q| g1[GRASHOF CHAIN]
    check -->|S + L greater than P plus Q| g2[NON-GRASHOF CHAIN]
    check -->|S + L equals P plus Q| g3[CHANGE POINT CHAIN]
    g1 --> ground1{Which link is grounded?}
    ground1 -->|S| dc[DOUBLE CRANK Drag Link]
    ground1 -->|P or Q adjacent to S| cr[CRANK ROCKER Oscillator]
    ground1 -->|L| dr[DOUBLE ROCKER]
    g2 --> nground{Any link grounded}
    nground -->|Adjacent to L| tr[TRIPLE ROCKER]
    nground -->|Opposite to L| dr2[DOUBLE ROCKER]
    g3 --> ind[INDETERMINATE Verify practically]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Space Diagram of a Generic Four Bar Chain (Textual)

```
        B (moving pivot)
       /|\
      / | \
   r2/  |  \ r3   (coupler = BC)
    /   |   \
   /    |    \
  A-----+-----D   (ground line AD = r1, fixed)
   | r1 |
   V_r4 rocker from D up to C
```

| Joint | Pin Type | Link | Description |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **A** | Fixed Pivot | 1–2 | Frame to Input Crank |
| **B** | Moving Pivot | 2–3 | Input Crank to Coupler |
| **C** | Moving Pivot | 3–4 | Coupler to Output Rocker |
| **D** | Fixed Pivot | 4–1 | Output Rocker to Frame |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a four bar chain. What are the names given to its four links?**

**Model Answer:**
A **four bar chain** is a kinematic chain consisting of four rigid links connected by four revolute pairs in a closed loop, with one link fixed to the ground. It has **one degree of freedom** as per Kutzbach's equation: $F = 3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1$.

The four links are:
1. **Fixed link / Ground / Frame** — the immovable reference link.
2. **Input link / Crank (or Rocker)** — the driver.
3. **Coupler / Connecting rod** — the floating intermediate link.
4. **Output link / Rocker (or Crank)** — the driven follower.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State Grashof's Law for a four bar mechanism. What is the condition for a double-crank mechanism?**

**Model Answer:**
**Grashof's Law:** A four bar chain will have at least one link capable of making a complete revolution if, and only if, the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links is **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two links.

Mathematically:

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

where $S$ = shortest link, $L$ = longest link, $P, Q$ = the remaining two links.

**Condition for Double-Crank (Drag Link) Mechanism:** The chain must satisfy Grashof's inequality ($S + L < P + Q$) **AND** the **shortest link (S) must be the fixed link (ground)**. Under these two conditions, both the links adjacent to the ground (i.e., the input and output) can rotate fully through 360°.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**Question A (14 Marks):** A four bar mechanism has link lengths $r_1 = 100$ mm, $r_2 = 30$ mm, $r_3 = 80$ mm, $r_4 = 90$ mm. The input crank rotates uniformly at $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s, counter-clockwise. At the instant when $\theta_2 = 60°$, find: (a) [7 marks] The angular velocities $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$ of the coupler and rocker. (b) [7 marks] The angular accelerations $\alpha_3$ and $\alpha_4$, and the transmission angle $\mu$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Grashof's Law to classify:**
- $S = 30$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $P = 80$ mm, $Q = 90$ mm.
- $S + L = 30 + 100 = 130$ mm; $P + Q = 80 + 90 = 170$ mm.
- Since $130 < 170$, this is a **Grashof chain**.

**Step 2 — Identify mechanism type:**
With $r_2 = 30$ mm as the input (adjacent to ground $r_1$ and the shortest link adjacent to ground), this is a **Crank-Rocker** mechanism.

**Step 3 — Solve the position equation (find $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$) at $\theta_2 = 60°$:**

From X-equation: $r_2\cos\theta_2 + r_3\cos\theta_3 = r_1 + r_4\cos\theta_4$

$$30(0.5) + 80\cos\theta_3 = 100 + 90\cos\theta_4$$
$$80\cos\theta_3 - 90\cos\theta_4 = 85 \quad (i)$$

From Y-equation: $30\sin 60° + 80\sin\theta_3 = 90\sin\theta_4$

$$80\sin\theta_3 - 90\sin\theta_4 = -25.98 \quad (ii)$$

**Solving (i) and (ii) simultaneously** (numerical method or trial):

$$\theta_3 \approx 38.21°\ ;\quad \theta_4 \approx 70.43°$$

**Step 4 — (a) Velocity Analysis [7 marks]**

Substituting in the velocity equations:

$$-30(20)\sin 60° - 80\omega_3 \sin 38.21° = -90\omega_4 \sin 70.43°$$
$$-519.6 - 49.49\,\omega_3 = -84.70\,\omega_4 \quad (iii)$$

$$30(20)\cos 60° + 80\omega_3 \cos 38.21° = 90\omega_4 \cos 70.43°$$
$$300 + 62.85\,\omega_3 = 30.16\,\omega_4 \quad (iv)$$

From (iv): $\omega_4 = (300 + 62.85\,\omega_3)/30.16 = 9.945 + 2.084\,\omega_3$

Substituting in (iii):
$$-519.6 - 49.49\,\omega_3 = -84.70\,(9.945 + 2.084\,\omega_3)$$
$$-519.6 - 49.49\,\omega_3 = -842.32 - 176.51\,\omega_3$$
$$127.02\,\omega_3 = -322.72$$
$$\boxed{\omega_3 = -2.541\ \text{rad/s}\ (\text{clockwise})}$$

$$\omega_4 = 9.945 + 2.084(-2.541) = 4.648\ \text{rad/s}$$
$$\boxed{\omega_4 = +4.648\ \text{rad/s}\ (\text{counter-clockwise})}$$

**Step 5 — (b) Acceleration Analysis [7 marks]**

Differentiating velocity equations; given $\alpha_2 = 0$ (uniform input). Substituting in acceleration equations:

$$-30(0)\sin 60° - 30(20)^2\cos 60° - 80\alpha_3 \sin 38.21° - 80(-2.541)^2 \cos 38.21°$$
$$= -90\alpha_4 \sin 70.43° - 90(4.648)^2 \cos 70.43°$$

$$- 0 - 6000 - 49.49\alpha_3 - 405.0 = -84.70\alpha_4 - 1264.7$$
$$- 49.49\alpha_3 + 84.70\alpha_4 = 5140.3 \quad (v)$$

Similarly the Y-acceleration equation:

$$30(0)\cos 60° - 30(20)^2 \sin 60° + 80\alpha_3 \cos 38.21° - 80(-2.541)^2 \sin 38.21°$$
$$= 90\alpha_4 \cos 70.43° - 90(4.648)^2 \sin 70.43°$$

$$0 - 10392.3 + 62.85\alpha_3 - 311.4 = 30.16\alpha_4 - 1972.5$$
$$62.85\alpha_3 - 30.16\alpha_4 = 8731.2 \quad (vi)$$

Solving (v) and (vi) simultaneously:

$$\boxed{\alpha_3 \approx 108.40\ \text{rad/s}^2\ ;\quad \alpha_4 \approx -9.50\ \text{rad/s}^2}$$

**Transmission Angle:** $\mu = \theta_4 - \theta_3 = 70.43° - 38.21° = 32.22°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention blunder** — students often miss the negative sign on the $\omega^2 \cos\theta$ centripetal term. Always use the **product rule** carefully when differentiating.
> 2. **Forgot the initial position solve** — $\theta_3$ and $\theta_4$ are NOT given; you MUST solve the loop-closure equations FIRST before using the velocity and acceleration equations. **No marks will be awarded for skipping this step.**
> 3. **Forgetting to draw the velocity polygon / space diagram** — per KTU valuation key, **at least 1 mark is reserved for a labelled diagram**. Always include it.
> 4. **Cramer's rule vs substitution** — both are acceptable, but show the determinant explicitly for KTU ESE 2024.
> 5. **Transmission angle pitfall** — values of $\mu < 40°$ or $> 140°$ are mechanically undesirable; in this problem $\mu \approx 32°$ is borderline, indicating a poorly chosen geometry. A real engineer would redesign with different link lengths.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** A four bar chain ABCD has $AB = 20$ mm, $BC = 50$ mm, $CD = 40$ mm, $AD = 70$ mm. (a) [7 marks] Classify the mechanism using Grashof's Law and state two practical applications. (b) [7 marks] Sketch the space diagram showing the two extreme (dead-centre) positions of the rocker and determine the angular stroke of the rocker.

**Model Solution (Brief):**
- Shortest $S = 20$, Longest $L = 70$, others 50, 40.
- $S + L = 90$, $P + Q = 90$ → **Change-Point mechanism** (equality holds).
- In practice, with manufacturing tolerance, treat as a borderline Grashof/non-Grashof — practically a **crank-rocker** if $S$ is adjacent to ground; else **double rocker**.
- Applications: sewing machine needle drive; small IC engine auxiliary drive.
- Dead-centre positions: occur when AB and BC are collinear (overlapping → one extreme; extended → other extreme). Use the cosine rule on triangle ACD in each case to find the two extreme $\theta_4$ values, then subtract to get the angular stroke.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook!)**

- [x] A **four bar chain** has **4 links, 4 revolute pairs, 1 DOF** (Kutzbach check: $3(4-1) - 2(4) = 1$).
- [x] **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ (at least one link rotates fully if strict inequality holds).
- [x] **Grashof + Shortest as Ground = Double Crank (Drag Link)**.
- [x] **Grashof + Link adjacent to S as Ground = Crank-Rocker**.
- [x] **Grashof + Longest as Ground = Double Rocker**.
- [x] **Non-Grashof = Always Double Rocker (or Triple Rocker if adjacent to L)**.
- [x] **Loop-closure equations (X & Y)** are the foundation for all velocity/acceleration analysis — derive them on the **AB + BC = AD + DC** vector basis.
- [x] **Velocity equations** = first derivative of loop-closure (gives $\omega_3, \omega_4$).
- [x] **Acceleration equations** = second derivative (gives $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$). The $\omega^2 r$ terms are **centripetal**, the $\alpha r$ terms are **tangential**.
- [x] **Transmission angle $\mu$** is the angle between coupler and rocker; ideally near **90°** (range 40°–140° for good design).
- [x] **Dead-centre positions**: rocker momentarily stationary when crank and coupler become collinear.
- [x] **Mechanical advantage** depends on instantaneous geometry, not fixed like a lever.
- [x] **Always draw** the space diagram AND a velocity/acceleration polygon in KTU answers — it carries free marks.
- [x] **Common KTU error**: forgetting to solve for $\theta_3, \theta_4$ first using the loop-closure equations before applying the velocity and acceleration equations. This is a guaranteed **0-marks** situation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slider Crank Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Slider Crank Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Slider Crank Mechanism** is a four-bar kinematic chain (or a modified four-bar linkage) in which one of the links is constrained to undergo pure **rectilinear translation** (sliding) along a straight line, while the remaining three links perform **rotational or complex planar motion**. It is formally classified as a **single-degree-of-freedom (DOF = 1)** constrained mechanism and is the kinematic backbone of the majority of **reciprocating machinery** on Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A slider-crank chain is an inversion of the four-bar chain obtained by fixing (i) any one link of the four-bar chain to the ground, producing three practical inversions — **Crank-Shaper, Crank-Oscillating, and Crank-Whipper (Whitworth quick return)** — or (ii) by replacing one revolute joint with a prismatic joint, generating a chain with one prismatic and three revolute pairs (**R-R-R-P** configuration).

### 1.2 Components of the Slider Crank Chain

| S.No. | Component | Standard KTU Notation | Function |
|:-----:|:----------|:---------------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Crank** | Radius = $r$ | Pure rotation about the fixed pivot $O$ |
| 2 | **Connecting Rod (Coupler)** | Length = $l$ | Connects crank pin to slider; executes complex planar motion |
| 3 | **Slider (Piston)** | Displacement = $x$ | Pure translation along the line of stroke |
| 4 | **Guide / Frame** | Fixed | Provides the linear track for the slider |
| 5 | **Crank Angle** | $\theta$ | Instantaneous angular position of the crank, measured from TDC |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition (The Pumping Heart)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Pump:**
> Imagine a bicycle pump. The **lever handle** is the **crank** rotating about the pivot. The **piston rod** is the **connecting rod**. The **piston head** (rubber bung) is the **slider** moving up and down inside the cylinder. When you push the handle, the crank rotates; the connecting rod pushes the piston; the piston compresses the air — and *vice versa* when pulling. This is exactly the kinematics inside a car engine cylinder, only that the "handle" is replaced by the high-pressure combustion gases pushing the piston down!

In an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)**, the roles reverse: hot gases push the slider (piston) → the connecting rod drags the crank → the crank rotates the flywheel. The slider crank is therefore a **bi-directional energy converter** between *linear reciprocating motion* and *rotary motion*.

### 1.4 Geometric Configurations

There are **two principal geometric forms** recognized by the KTU 2024 scheme:

- **In-line (Central) Slider Crank**: The line of stroke of the slider passes directly through the crank centre $O$. Offset $e = 0$.
- **Offset (Crosshead) Slider Crank**: The line of stroke is parallel to, but offset by a perpendicular distance $e$ from the crank centre. This creates an **asymmetric** reciprocation pattern used in shaping machines and side-crank engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Slenderness ratio (Connecting Rod Length Ratio): $n = \dfrac{l}{r}$, usually $n \in [3, 4.5]$ for IC engines.
> - Stroke length: $S = 2r$
> - Angular velocity of crank: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s, where $N$ is in rpm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Behind the Mechanism

The slider-crank mechanism is governed by **three sequential analysis stages** that the KTU examiner expects every student to master:

### Stage 1 — Position Analysis (Geometric / Loop-Closure)
- **Why?** Engineers must know *where* the slider is at every crank angle to design ports, valves, and timing events.
- **How?** We use the **vector loop-closure equation** (or the sine rule in the crank-rocker triangle) to relate the slider displacement $x$ to the crank angle $\theta$.

### Stage 2 — Velocity Analysis (First Time Derivative)
- **Why?** To compute piston speed, which governs **scuffing**, **oil film thickness**, and **valve dynamics**.
- **How?** Differentiate the displacement equation with respect to time. Use $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ (constant for steady running).

### Stage 3 — Acceleration Analysis (Second Time Derivative)
- **Why?** Inertial force = $m \cdot a$ is the dominant **dynamic load** on the gudgeon pin, crosshead, and bearings. This is critical for KTU board problems.
- **How?** Differentiate the velocity expression. Use $\dot{\omega} = \alpha$ (angular acceleration; often zero for uniform crank speed).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Formula Table** — Memorize these with units. The notation $n = l/r$ is the slenderness ratio. All formulas are valid for an **in-line slider crank** unless otherwise specified.

| # | Parameter | Exact Equation (Vector Form) | Approximate Equation (for $n \geq 3$) | Unit |
|:-:|:----------|:-----------------------------|:---------------------------------------|:-----|
| 1 | Slider Displacement $x$ | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ | $x = r\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{4n}\,(1-\cos 2\theta)\right)$ | m |
| 2 | Slider Velocity $v$ | $v = -r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r\sin 2\theta}{2\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$ | $v = -r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | m/s |
| 3 | Slider Acceleration $a$ | $a = -r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)$ (approx) | $a = -r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | m/s$^2$ |
| 4 | Crank Angle at TDC | $\theta = 0^\circ$ | — | rad / deg |
| 5 | Crank Angle at BDC | $\theta = 180^\circ$ | — | rad / deg |
| 6 | Angle of Connecting Rod $\phi$ | $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$ | $\phi \approx \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$ | rad |
| 7 | Inertia Force on Piston | $F_I = -m_p \cdot a$ | — | N |
| 8 | Angular Velocity of Crank | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | — | rad/s |
| 9 | Stroke Length | $S = 2r$ | — | m |
| 10 | Slenderness Ratio | $n = l/r$ | — | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall for Tables:** The absolute value bars and modulus signs in the table above have been written using the macro `\vert` (and `\dfrac` for compact fractions) to ensure **safe rendering inside markdown tables**. Never use raw vertical pipes `|` inside a table cell — it will break the table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Engineering:** Crank-piston assembly in petrol/diesel engines (Honda, Toyota, Tata engines).
- **Reciprocating Compressors:** Conversion of motor rotation to piston reciprocation for gas compression.
- **Shaper Machines:** Quick-return mechanism based on slider-crank inversion.
- **Pumps & Hydraulic Systems:** Plunger pumps use offset slider-crank geometry.
- **Aerospace:** Control surface actuators in older aircraft used crank-rocker inversions.
- **Textile Machinery:** Looms use a slider-crank to convert rotary to linear beat-up motion.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exact Displacement of the Slider (Loop-Closure Method)

We model the mechanism as a **closed vector polygon** traversed from the fixed crank centre $O$ to the slider pin $A$.

### Step 1: Define the Vector Loop

Let us denote the position vectors:
- $\vec{OA}$ = crank vector, length $r$, at angle $\theta$ from the horizontal line of stroke.
- $\vec{AB}$ = connecting rod vector, length $l$, at angle $\phi$ with the line of stroke.
- $\vec{OB}$ = slider position vector along the $x$-axis, equal to $x$.

By vector closure of the loop $O \to A \to B \to O$:

$$
\vec{OB} = \vec{OA} + \vec{AB}
$$

### Step 2: Resolve into Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
x\hat{i} &= (r\cos\theta)\,\hat{i} + (r\sin\theta)\,\hat{j} + (l\cos\phi)\,\hat{i} + (-l\sin\phi)\,\hat{j}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating the $\hat{j}$-component (vertical):

$$
0 = r\sin\theta - l\sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta
$$

Equating the $\hat{i}$-component (horizontal):

$$
x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi
$$

### Step 3: Eliminate $\phi$ Using the Pythagorean Identity

Since $\cos^2\phi = 1 - \sin^2\phi$, we have:

$$
\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2}{l^2}\sin^2\theta}
$$

Substituting back into the horizontal equation:

$$
\boxed{\,x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}\,}
$$

This is the **exact closed-form** displacement expression demanded in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Approximate Displacement Using Binomial Expansion

The exact expression contains a nested square root which is tedious to differentiate. For $n = l/r \geq 3$, we apply the **binomial expansion**:

$$
\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} = l\sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}}
$$

Using the binomial series $(1 - x)^{1/2} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{2} - \dfrac{x^2}{8} - \cdots$, where $x = \dfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}$:

$$
\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} \approx l\left(1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l^2}\right) = l - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l}
$$

Substituting into the displacement equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= r\cos\theta + l - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
x = r\cos\theta + l - \frac{r^2}{2l} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} = r\cos\theta + l - \frac{r^2}{4l} + \frac{r^2}{4l}\cos 2\theta
$$

Defining $l - \dfrac{r^2}{4l} = C$ (a geometric constant offset) and noting $n = l/r$:

$$
\boxed{\,x = r\cos\theta + \frac{r}{4n}(1 - \cos 2\theta) + C\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity of the Slider (Differentiation of Exact $x$)

Differentiate the **exact** displacement expression with respect to time $t$:

$$
x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx}{dt} &= -r\sin\theta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{1}{2\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} \cdot (-2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta)\cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognising $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$
v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}
$$

Using the identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,v = -r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \frac{r\sin 2\theta}{2\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Approximate Velocity (for $n \geq 3$)

Using the approximate expression $x \approx r\cos\theta + l - \dfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2l}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\sin\theta \cdot \omega - \frac{r^2 \cdot 2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{2l}\cdot \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{l}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = -r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)\,}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Acceleration of the Slider (Exact)

Differentiate the exact velocity:

$$
v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}
$$

For a **uniform crank speed** ($\dot{\omega} = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a = \frac{dv}{dt} &= -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Carrying out the quotient-rule differentiation carefully (this is the most error-prone step in KTU scripts):

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2}{l^2}\sin^2\theta}}\right) = \frac{\omega(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)\sqrt{1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}} - \sin\theta\cos\theta \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}\right)^{-1/2}\left(-\frac{2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{l^2}\right)}{1 - \frac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{l^2}}
$$

After considerable algebraic simplification (see textbook expansions) and using $\cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,a = -r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r\cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{l^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}\right) \text{ (approx, leading term)}\,}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Approximate Acceleration (for $n \geq 3$)

Differentiate the approximate velocity $v = -r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$:

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega\!\left(\cos\theta \cdot \omega + \frac{2\cos 2\theta \cdot \omega}{2n}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,a = -r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)\,}
$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Critical Values of $\theta$

| Crank Angle $\theta$ | Displacement $x$ (from TDC) | Velocity $v$ | Acceleration $a$ | Physical Meaning |
|:--------------------:|:---------------------------:|:------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------|
| $0^\circ$ (TDC) | $0$ | $0$ | $-r\omega^2(1 + 1/n)$ | Max deceleration; gas force overcomes inertia |
| $90^\circ$ | $r + l - r^2/(2l)$ | $-r\omega$ (max magnitude, but approx) | $\approx 0$ | Piston at mid-stroke, max speed |
| $180^\circ$ (BDC) | $2r$ | $0$ | $+r\omega^2(1 - 1/n)$ | Max acceleration at start of compression |
| $270^\circ$ | $r + l - r^2/(2l)$ | $+r\omega$ | $\approx 0$ | Piston at mid-stroke (return) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The acceleration reverses sign twice per revolution, causing the **inertia force** $F_I = m_p a$ to also reverse. This cyclic loading is precisely why IC engine crankshafts experience **torsional vibration** and require **counterweights** and **tuned dampers**.
