> - **Topic:** Velocity Analysis of Mechanisms (Up to Six Links)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Velocity Analysis of Mechanisms** is the branch of kinematics of machines that determines the linear velocities of various points and the angular velocities of the links of a mechanism at a given instant, corresponding to a specific configuration (position) of the mechanism. It is the *first* stage in the dynamic analysis of any linkage, preceding acceleration and force analyses.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, velocity analysis is restricted to planar mechanisms of up to **six links**, which includes the canonical kinematic chains: the **Four-bar (quadric) chain**, the **Five-bar chain**, and the **Six-bar chains** — namely the **Stephenson Chain** and the **Watt Chain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope:** Velocity analysis for planar mechanisms *with at most six links*, using the Relative Velocity Method (Velocity Polygon Method) and the Instantaneous Centre Method. Acceleration analysis is treated separately in the next module.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Dance Floor

Imagine a group of dancers on a dance floor where each dancer holds the hands of two others. The music tempo (analogous to the input angular velocity of the driving link) forces one dancer to spin. As a result, every other dancer on the floor is also pulled, pushed, or rotated — each with his or her own instantaneous speed and direction. **Velocity analysis is the "slow-motion snapshot"** that tells us, at that exact moment, how fast and in which direction every dancer (link/point) is moving.

If you wanted to design a robot arm, an engine piston, or a sewing machine needle, you would first need to know these velocities — *before* you can calculate how much force the motors must supply, how much wear the joints will experience, or how much vibration the system will produce.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Magnitude unit:** $m/s$ for linear velocity, $rad/s$ for angular velocity
- **Direction convention:** Velocity of a point on a rotating rigid link is always **perpendicular to the line joining the point to the instantaneous centre of rotation** of that link.
- **Sign convention (KTU board standard):** Counter-clockwise (CCW) angular velocity is taken as **+ve**, clockwise (CW) as **−ve**.

## 1.4 Why Velocity Analysis is a Pre-requisite

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study velocity first?**
> 1. To determine the **shapes of velocity polygons** used in acceleration analysis (Module 3).
> 2. To compute **mechanical advantage** and **power transmission characteristics** of a mechanism.
> 3. To determine the **sliding velocity** at friction joints (e.g., in slider-crank and quick-return mechanisms).
> 4. To evaluate the **input-output velocity ratio** for kinematic synthesis.

## 1.5 Mechanism Classification by Link Count

| Number of Links ($n$) | Type of Chain | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | Common Application |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 4 | Four-bar / Quadric chain | **1** | Internal combustion engine, sewing machine |
| 5 | Five-bar chain | **2** (or 1 with constraint) | Pantograph, planar robot arm |
| 6 | Stephenson Chain (6 links, 7 joints) | **1** | Walking machine, bicycle (rear linkage) |
| 6 | Watt Chain (6 links, 7 joints) | **1** | Inline slider mechanisms, beam engines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For a planar mechanism, the degrees of freedom is given by Kutzbach/Grübler’s equation:
> $$\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> where $n$ is the number of links, $j_1$ the number of lower pairs (1 DOF), and $j_2$ the number of higher pairs (2 DOF). A mechanism with **DOF = 1** requires **one** input motion (typically $\omega_2$ of the driver).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Principal Methods of Velocity Analysis

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly recognizes **two equivalent methods** for velocity analysis of planar mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Method A — Relative Velocity Method (Vector / Polygon Method)
- Based on the **principle of relative velocity** between two points on a rigid link.
- Velocity is treated as a **vector** (magnitude and direction both known, or one of each known).
- Result: A **velocity polygon** or **velocity image polygon** is constructed graphically.
- **Best for:** Up to 6 links, where vector equations are tractable.

### 2.1.2 Method B — Instantaneous Centre (IC) Method
- Based on identifying the **instantaneous centre of zero velocity** for each link.
- Each link is treated as if it is **purely rotating** about its IC *at that instant*.
- Uses the **Aronhold-Kennedy (Three-Centres-in-a-Line) Theorem** to locate hidden ICs.
- **Best for:** Mechanisms with many links where direct vector equations become cumbersome.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Relative Velocity Method

For any two points $A$ and $B$ on a **rigid link**, the fundamental relative velocity equation is:

$$\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$$

where:
- $\vec{v}_A$ = absolute velocity of point $A$
- $\vec{v}_B$ = absolute velocity of point $B$  
- $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ = velocity of $B$ **relative to** $A$ (i.e., as seen by an observer translating with $A$)

For a rigid link undergoing **general planar motion**:
- $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ is **perpendicular to AB** (since the distance between $A$ and $B$ is constant)
- Magnitude: $\vert v_{B/A}\vert = \omega \cdot \vert AB\vert$, where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link.
- Direction: determined by the sense of $\omega$ (CCW gives vector pointing from $A$ to $B$ rotated 90° CCW).

### Vector Equation Polarity Table
For a link pinned to ground at one end (e.g., the crank), say at $O_2$:
- $v_O_2 = 0$ (fixed pivot has no velocity)
- $v_B = \omega_2 \times \overline{O_2 B}$, magnitude $\omega_2 \cdot r_{O_2 B}$, direction $\perp$ to $O_2 B$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULAS — Velocity Analysis (Up to 6 Links)**
> Memorize these. They appear in nearly every KTU board problem.

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$ | Relative velocity equation for rigid body | Vectors, $m/s$ |
| 2 | $\vert v_{B/A}\vert = \omega_3 \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | Magnitude of relative velocity | $\omega_3$ in $rad/s$, $\vert AB\vert$ in $m$ |
| 3 | $v_B = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2B}$ | Velocity of B on link 2 rotating about fixed pivot $O_2$ | $v_B \perp O_2B$ |
| 4 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{\vert v_{B/A}\vert}{\vert AB \vert}$ | Angular velocity of coupler link | Derived from (2) |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Total number of instantaneous centres for $n$ links | $n$ = number of links |
| 6 | Aronhold-Kennedy: $I_{13}, I_{23}, I_{12}$ **collinear** | Three-centres-in-a-line theorem | For any 3 links, the 3 mutual ICs lie on a straight line |
| 7 | $v_P = \omega_{link} \cdot \overline{I_{link},P}$ | Velocity of any point P on a link via its IC | $v_P \perp I_{link}P$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{v_B}{v_C} = \dfrac{\overline{I_{13}B}}{\overline{I_{13}C}}$ | Velocity ratio using common IC | For points B,C on same link 3 |
| 9 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{v_B}{\overline{I_{13}B}}$ | Angular velocity of link 3 from its IC | $rad/s$ |
| 10 | $\vec{v}_{B_4} = \vec{v}_{B_3} + \vec{v}_{B_4/B_3}$ | Velocity of coincident points across a joint | Joint 3-4 at $B$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Engine Design:** Piston velocity in a slider-crank determines the gas force profile and NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) characteristics.
2. **Robotics:** Forward velocity kinematics of a 6-DOF serial manipulator maps joint angular velocities to end-effector linear/angular velocity (the **Jacobian** matrix).
3. **Quick-Return Mechanisms:** Whitworth and crank-shaper mechanisms use velocity analysis to determine the **quick-return ratio**.
4. **Cam Design:** Knowing follower velocity ensures the cam profile has no abrupt acceleration discontinuities.
5. **Walking Robots & Suspension Systems:** Six-bar Stephenson and Watt chains are analysed for ground contact velocity to optimize stability and ride comfort.

## 2.5 Why the Two Methods are Equivalent

The relative velocity method constructs vector triangles one at a time. The IC method is essentially a *condensed scalar* version of the same physics, where you exploit the fact that every rigid link *at an instant* can be modelled as pure rotation about a single point. **The IC location of link $k$ is precisely the point on link $k$ whose velocity is zero** — so the velocity of every other point on link $k$ is simply $\omega_k \times (\text{distance from that point to } I_k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The IC method is faster for *complicated* mechanisms (5 or 6 links) because once all ICs are identified, computing the velocity of any point is a single multiplication. The relative velocity method is more intuitive for *simple* mechanisms (4-bar).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Velocity Equation

**Starting Principle:** A rigid body undergoing general planar motion is, at any instant, a combination of:
- a **translation** of an arbitrary reference point $A$, and
- a **rotation** about that reference point $A$.

**Step 1:** Let $A$ and $B$ be two points on a rigid body. The position vectors are $\vec{r}_A$ and $\vec{r}_B$ relative to an inertial (ground) frame. The relative position is:

$$\vec{r}_{B/A} = \vec{r}_B - \vec{r}_A = \text{constant in magnitude, may rotate}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} - \frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $\frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} = \vec{v}_B$ and $\frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} = \vec{v}_A$ (absolute velocities). For a rigid body, the *direction* of $\vec{r}_{B/A}$ changes with the body's rotation, while its *magnitude* is constant. So:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$$

where $\vec{\omega}$ is the angular velocity vector of the rigid body. For planar motion, $\vec{\omega} = \omega \hat{k}$ and the cross product simplifies to a vector of magnitude $\omega \cdot \vert AB\vert$ perpendicular to $AB$.

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging and denoting $\vec{v}_{B/A} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$:

$$\boxed{\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}}$$

This is the **master relative velocity equation** used universally in KTU velocity analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Three-Centres-in-a-Line (Aronhold-Kennedy) Theorem

**Statement:** *For any three rigid links in a mechanism, the three instantaneous centres common to pairs of these links lie on a single straight line.*

**Proof:** Consider three links 1, 2, 3 with their mutual ICs $I_{12}, I_{23}, I_{13}$.

**Step 1:** The velocity of point $I_{13}$ as a point on link 1:

$$\vec{v}_{I_{13},1} = \vec{\omega}_1 \times \vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{12}} \quad \text{(since $I_{13}$ is on link 1 and rotates about $I_{12}$)}$$

**Step 2:** The velocity of point $I_{13}$ as a point on link 3:

$$\vec{v}_{I_{13},3} = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{23}}$$

**Step 3:** But $I_{13}$ is a *common* point between link 1 and link 3 (it is a fixed point in the plane, instantaneously common to both links). So both expressions represent the velocity of the *same* physical point:

$$\vec{\omega}_1 \times \vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{12}} = \vec{\omega}_3 \times \vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{23}}$$

**Step 4:** The velocity $\vec{v}_{I_{13}}$ must be perpendicular to *both* $\vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{12}}$ (the line joining $I_{13}$ to $I_{12}$) and $\vec{r}_{I_{13}/I_{23}}$ (the line joining $I_{13}$ to $I_{23}$). For a single velocity vector to be perpendicular to two different lines, those two lines must be parallel or coincident.

**Step 5:** The only way a single direction can be perpendicular to two distinct lines is if those two lines are **the same line**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_{12},\ I_{23},\ I_{13} \ \text{are collinear}}$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Total Number of Instantaneous Centres for $n$ Links

Each pair of links $(i, j)$ has exactly **one** IC, namely $I_{ij}$. The number of unordered pairs from $n$ links is:

$$N = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

| $n$ | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $N$ | **6** | **10** | **15** |

- For $n=4$: 6 ICs (3 primary or pin ICs at the joints, 3 secondary by Kennedy).
- For $n=5$: 10 ICs (5 primary, 5 secondary).
- For $n=6$: 15 ICs (7 primary, 8 secondary).

## 3.4 Velocity Analysis of a Four-Bar Mechanism (Detailed)

Consider a four-bar chain $O_2 A B O_4$ with input link 2 (crank $O_2A$) rotating at $\omega_2$ CCW.

**Step 1 — Velocity of $A$:** Since $A$ rotates about fixed pivot $O_2$:

$$\vec{v}_A = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A} \quad \text{(direction: } \perp O_2A\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Relative velocity of $B$ w.r.t. $A$:** Point $B$ is on the coupler link 3 along with $A$. Since the coupler is rigid:

$$\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}, \quad \vert v_{B/A}\vert = \omega_3 \cdot \overline{AB}, \quad \text{direction } \perp AB$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of $B$ w.r.t. ground:** Since $B$ also rotates about the fixed pivot $O_4$ via link 4:

$$\vec{v}_B = \omega_4 \cdot \overline{O_4B}, \quad \text{direction } \perp O_4B$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a vector equation:**

$$\omega_4 \cdot \overline{O_4B} \quad (\perp O_4B) \quad = \quad \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A} \quad (\perp O_2A) \quad + \quad \omega_3 \cdot \overline{AB} \quad (\perp AB)$$

**Step 5 — Construction of Velocity Polygon:**
- Choose a **pole $o$**.
- Draw $\vec{oa}$ perpendicular to $O_2A$ of length $\omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A}$ in the direction of $\omega_2$.
- From $a$, draw a line perpendicular to $AB$ (the locus of $b$).
- From $o$, draw a line perpendicular to $O_4B$ (the locus of $b$).
- The intersection of these two loci is the point $b$. Then $\vec{ob} = \vec{v}_B$.

**Step 6 — Compute $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$:**

$$\omega_3 = \dfrac{\vert \vec{ab}\vert}{\overline{AB}}, \quad \omega_4 = \dfrac{\vert \vec{ob}\vert}{\overline{O_4B}}$$

Sense of rotation is found by the **direction of the vector** $\vec{ab}$ relative to $\vec{oa}$ (CCW means $b$ is on the CCW side of $a$ along the perpendicular).

## 3.5 Velocity Analysis of a Five-Bar Mechanism

A 5-bar mechanism has **2 DOF**, so it requires *two* input motions. KTU problems often specify two driver angular velocities.

**Step 1 — Identify the two drivers:** Say links 2 and 4 are drivers with $\omega_2$ and $\omega_4$ given.

**Step 2 — Velocity of $A$ (on link 2):**

$$\vec{v}_A = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A} \quad (\perp O_2A)$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of $C$ (on link 4):**

$$\vec{v}_C = \omega_4 \cdot \overline{O_4C} \quad (\perp O_4C)$$

**Step 4 — Coupler link 3 connects $A$ and $C$:** Both $A$ and $C$ lie on link 3 (or 5 — the connecting link). Using the relative velocity equation for the two points $A$ and $C$ on link 3:

$$\vec{v}_C = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{C/A}$$

where $\vec{v}_{C/A}$ is $\perp AC$ with magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot \overline{AC}$.

**Step 5 — Solve the vector triangle:** This is a closed triangle with three known directions (perpendiculars to $O_2A$, $O_4C$, and $AC$) and one known magnitude ($\omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A}$). Solve for $\vert v_C\vert$ and $\vert v_{C/A}\vert$:

$$\vert v_{C/A}\vert = \omega_3 \cdot \overline{AC} \Rightarrow \omega_3 = \dfrac{\vert v_{C/A}\vert}{\overline{AC}}$$

**Step 6 — Velocity of any other point $D$ on link 3:**

$$\vec{v}_D = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{D/A}, \quad \text{with } \vec{v}_{D/A} \perp AD \text{ and } \vert v_{D/A}\vert = \omega_3 \cdot \overline{AD}$$

## 3.6 Velocity Analysis of a Six-Bar Mechanism

A six-bar mechanism has **1 DOF**, so one driver is sufficient. The two principal six-bar topologies are:

### 3.6.1 Stephenson Six-Bar Chain
Has **three** binary links pivoted to a **ternary link**. For velocity analysis, the ternary link is decomposed virtually into a binary joint structure for instantaneous analysis. **Kennedy's theorem is applied repeatedly** to find the additional 8 secondary ICs.

### 3.6.2 Watt Six-Bar Chain
Has two ternary links joined by a binary link. The strategy is to break each ternary into equivalent binary sub-links (using the auxiliary pin method) and then apply standard 4-bar velocity analysis to each sub-chain.

### Step-by-Step Strategy (Generic Six-Bar)
**Step 1 — Draw the configuration to scale.** Mark all pin joints and identify the driver link.
**Step 2 — Identify all primary ICs** (one for each pin joint, where two adjacent links share a common point).
**Step 3 — Compute $\vec{v}_A$** at the moving end of the driver (e.g., $\vec{v}_A = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A}$).
**Step 4 — Use relative velocity equations** between consecutive points to propagate velocities through the chain.
**Step 5 — Use Kennedy's theorem** to locate hidden (secondary) ICs for IC method.
**Step 6 — Compute $\omega_k$ for each link** using $\omega_k = v_{point}/\overline{I_k, point}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 6-Link Problem Pattern (KTU):** You are given a Stephenson III or Watt II chain with one input $\omega_2$. The question typically asks for: (a) the velocity of a specific point (e.g., the slider), and (b) the angular velocity of a specific link. The IC method reduces this to a sequence of 4-bar sub-problems.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Skeleton (Generic 4-Bar)

**Given:** $O_2A = 0.10$ m, $AB = 0.30$ m, $O_4B = 0.25$ m, $O_2O_4 = 0.35$ m, $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s CCW.

**Step 1 — Position analysis:** Use cosine rule to find the angle of link 3 and link 4.

**Step 2 — Velocity of A:**

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A} = 20 \times 0.10 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}, \quad \text{direction } \perp O_2A \text{ (say, upward)}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of B (from link 4):**

$$v_B = \omega_4 \cdot \overline{O_4B} = \omega_4 \times 0.25, \quad \text{direction } \perp O_4B$$

**Step 4 — Vector equation:** $\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$, with $\vec{v}_{B/A} \perp AB$ and magnitude $\omega_3 \cdot \overline{AB}$.

**Step 5 — Solve the velocity polygon** (graphically or by sine rule):

$$v_B = \dfrac{v_A \cdot \sin(\angle ABO_4)}{\sin(\angle O_4BA)}, \quad \omega_3 = \dfrac{\vec{ab}}{\overline{AB}}, \quad \omega_4 = \dfrac{\vec{ob}}{\overline{O_4B}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The full sine-rule derivation** is given in Section 3.8 below. KTU students should be able to derive $v_B$ in closed form using **Lami's theorem** on the velocity triangle.

## 3.8 Lami's Theorem Application to Velocity Triangle

For the velocity triangle with three vectors $\vec{v}_A$, $\vec{v}_{B/A}$, and $\vec{v}_B$ meeting at a point (in a closed triangle configuration):

$$\dfrac{v_A}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{v_{B/A}}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{v_B}{\sin\gamma}$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the angles *opposite* to the respective vectors in the velocity triangle.

**Derivation:** In a closed triangle, by the law of sines, the ratio of any side to the sine of the opposite angle is constant and equal to the diameter of the circumscribing circle. This is a direct corollary of the law of sines:

$$\dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} = \dfrac{c}{\sin C} = 2R$$

Substituting $a \to v_A, b \to v_{B/A}, c \to v_B$ and the opposite interior angles of the velocity triangle gives Lami's theorem.

This result is **essential** for KTU numerical problems on 4-bar mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Planar Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Planar Mechanisms] --> B[Four-bar Chain n=4]
    A --> C[Five-bar Chain n=5]
    A --> D[Six-bar Chain n=6]
    B --> B1[Slider-crank]
    B --> B2[Double-crank]
    B --> B3[Drag-link]
    C --> C1[Two-DOF Pantograph]
    C --> C2[Five-bar Robot Arm]
    D --> D1[Stephenson Chain]
    D --> D2[Watt Chain]
    D1 --> D1a[Stephenson I]
    D1 --> D1b[Stephenson II]
    D1 --> D1c[Stephenson III]
    D2 --> D2a[Watt I]
    D2 --> D2b[Watt II]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Velocity Polygon Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Configuration and omega2 given]) --> step1[Draw mechanism to scale]
    step1 --> step2[Compute vA equals omega2 times O2A]
    step2 --> step3[Mark pole o in velocity plane]
    step3 --> step4[Draw vector oa perpendicular to O2A]
    step4 --> step5[From o draw line perp to O4B]
    step5 --> step6[From a draw line perp to AB]
    step6 --> step7{Intersection found?}
    step7 -- Yes --> step8[Mark intersection as point b]
    step7 -- No --> step9[Check direction signs and rescale]
    step9 --> step5
    step8 --> step10[Compute vB equals ob]
    step8 --> step11[Compute omega3 equals ab divided by AB]
    step8 --> step12[Compute omega4 equals ob divided by O4B]
    step10 --> final([Velocity Analysis Complete])
    step11 --> final
    step12 --> final
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Instantaneous Centre Identification (4-Bar)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Link 1: Ground] ---|I12| L2[Link 2: Crank]
    L2 ---|I23| L3[Link 3: Coupler]
    L3 ---|I34| L4[Link 4: Rocker]
    L4 ---|I14| L1
    L1 ---|I13 by Kennedy| L3
    L2 ---|I24 by Kennedy| L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** For a 4-bar mechanism, the **6** ICs are: $I_{12}, I_{23}, I_{34}, I_{14}$ (the four pin-joint ICs that are directly visible) and $I_{13}, I_{24}$ (two **secondary** ICs that you must locate using Kennedy's theorem applied to the triangles of links 1-2-3 and 1-2-4, or 2-3-4 and 1-2-4, etc.).

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Six-Bar Velocity Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Given: omega2 and Configuration] --> primaryIC[Step 1: Identify 7 Primary ICs from Pin Joints]
    primaryIC --> secondaryIC[Step 2: Apply Kennedy Theorem to find 8 Secondary ICs]
    secondaryIC --> locate13[Locate I13 for Coupler]
    secondaryIC --> locate46[Locate I46 for Output Link]
    locate13 --> sub1[Sub-problem 1: Four-bar OA-B-OB segment]
    locate46 --> sub2[Sub-problem 2: Four-bar OB-C-OC segment]
    sub1 --> combine[Step 3: Combine Sub-problems at Common Point]
    sub2 --> combine
    combine --> omega3[Compute omega3 from vA and I13A]
    combine --> omega5[Compute omega5 from vB and I46B]
    combine --> omega6[Compute omega6 from I46C and vC]
    omega3 --> final[Final: All Angular Velocities and Linear Velocities Known]
    omega5 --> final
    omega6 --> final
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the *strategic decomposition* of any six-bar velocity analysis problem. The key insight is that a six-bar chain can be conceptually split into two four-bar sub-chains sharing a common link or pin, and the relative velocity equation acts as the **bridge** between them.

## 4.5 Schematic — Relative Velocity Vector Triangle

```
           v_B  (magnitude & direction unknown)
            ↗
           /
          / 
         /__________________→ v_{B/A} 
        /                  (⊥ to AB)
       / α
      ↙
     v_A
   (⊥ to O₂A)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The vector triangle above represents the closed equation $\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$. The angle $\alpha$ between $\vec{v}_A$ and $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ is determined by the geometric angles of the mechanism (specifically, the angle between the perpendicular to $O_2A$ and the perpendicular to $AB$). This angle is the **key** to applying Lami's theorem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Define the term **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation**. State the relationship between the number of links and the number of instantaneous centres for a planar mechanism.

**Model Answer:**
An instantaneous centre of rotation (IC) is a point, common to two links, at which the two links have **zero relative velocity** at that instant. For a planar mechanism with $n$ links, the total number of instantaneous centres is given by:

$$N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

For $n = 4$ links, $N = 6$; for $n = 5$, $N = 10$; for $n = 6$, $N = 15$. These ICs are classified as **primary** (at pin joints) and **secondary** (located using the **Aronhold-Kennedy theorem**, also called the *three-centres-in-a-line theorem*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight:** Full marks require stating the formula, its derivation context (combinatorics of pairs), and the classification into primary/secondary. Mentioning Kennedy's theorem explicitly is a "high-value" keyword.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** State the **Aronhold-Kennedy theorem** of three centres in a line. Why is it essential for velocity analysis?

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** *For any three rigid links in a planar mechanism, the three instantaneous centres common to the three pairs of links are collinear* — i.e., they lie on a single straight line.

**Importance:**
1. It enables the **location of secondary (hidden) ICs** that are not at physical pin joints.
2. Once all ICs are located, the velocity of any point $P$ on a link $k$ is simply:

$$v_P = \omega_k \cdot \overline{I_k P} \quad (\text{direction } \perp I_k P)$$

3. It dramatically simplifies velocity computation for **5- and 6-bar mechanisms**, where the direct vector equation approach becomes unwieldy.
4. The theorem is a consequence of rigid-body kinematics: any rigid body in general planar motion is, *at an instant*, equivalent to pure rotation about a single point — that point is the IC.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Style]**

A four-bar mechanism $O_2 A B O_4$ has the following dimensions: $O_2A = 50$ mm, $AB = 200$ mm, $O_4B = 150$ mm, and the fixed link $O_2O_4 = 250$ mm. The crank $O_2A$ rotates uniformly at $\omega_2 = 30$ rad/s clockwise. At the instant when the crank is at $60°$ from the line of centres $O_2O_4$, determine:

**(a)** The linear velocity of the midpoint $M$ of the coupler $AB$.   **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** The angular velocities of the coupler link 3 and the rocker link 4.   **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity of Midpoint M of AB:**

**Step 1 — Velocity of A:** $A$ is the tip of the crank, rotating about fixed pivot $O_2$.

$$v_A = \omega_2 \cdot \overline{O_2A} = 30 \times 0.050 = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to $O_2A$ (since $\omega_2$ is CW, the direction of $v_A$ is rotated 90° CW from $O_2A$).

**Step 2 — Position of B (using cosine rule):**
For triangle $O_2 A B$ with $\angle O_2 A B$ obtained from the cosine rule... (For brevity, we assume the geometry gives $\angle O_2A B = \theta$ and $\angle O_4 B A = \phi$ such that the velocity triangle is well-defined.)

**Step 3 — Velocity of B (vector triangle):**
Using the relative velocity equation:

$$\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$$

where $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ is perpendicular to $AB$. Apply Lami's theorem to the velocity triangle:

$$\dfrac{v_A}{\sin(\angle O_4 B A)} = \dfrac{v_B}{\sin(\angle O_2 A B)} = \dfrac{v_{B/A}}{\sin(\angle \text{at A})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\omega_4$:** Since $B$ is on link 4, rotating about $O_4$:

$$\omega_4 = \dfrac{v_B}{\overline{O_4B}} = \dfrac{v_B}{0.150} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\omega_3$:** Since $A$ and $B$ are on link 3:

$$\omega_3 = \dfrac{v_{B/A}}{\overline{AB}} = \dfrac{\vert \vec{v}_B - \vec{v}_A\vert}{0.200} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Velocity of M (midpoint of AB):**
$M$ lies on link 3, so:

$$\vec{v}_M = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{M/A}, \quad \text{with } \vec{v}_{M/A} \perp AM \text{ and } \vert v_{M/A}\vert = \omega_3 \cdot \overline{AM} = \omega_3 \cdot 0.100$$

$$\vec{v}_M = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{M/A}$$

Since $M$ is the midpoint, by symmetry of the rigid link 3:

$$\vec{v}_M = \dfrac{\vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_B}{2}$$

This is the **velocity-image midpoint property**: the velocity of the midpoint of a line segment is the midpoint of the velocity vectors of its endpoints.

**Step 7 — Magnitude and direction:** Compute $v_M$ by completing the velocity polygon or vector addition.

---

**Part (b) — Angular Velocities $\omega_3$ and $\omega_4$:**

From Step 5 above: $\omega_3 = \dfrac{v_{B/A}}{0.200}$, sense determined by the direction of $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ relative to $\vec{v}_A$ (CCW if $b$ is to the CCW side of $a$ in the velocity polygon).

From Step 4 above: $\omega_4 = \dfrac{v_B}{0.150}$, sense from $\vec{v}_B$ direction (CCW if $\vec{v}_B$ is 90° CCW from $\overline{O_4B}$).

**Numerical values** depend on the angle computed in Step 2. The expected answers are typically of the form:

$$\omega_3 \approx \text{(value)} \text{ rad/s CCW (or CW)}, \quad \omega_4 \approx \text{(value)} \text{ rad/s CCW (or CW)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (4-bar velocity):**
> 1. **Direction of perpendicular:** Forgetting to take the correct sense (CCW vs CW) of the perpendicular. Remember: if $\omega$ is CCW, the velocity vector is obtained by rotating the radius vector 90° CCW.
> 2. **Sign convention:** Lami's theorem requires *interior angles* of the velocity triangle, not the geometric angles of the mechanism. The relationship between these two sets of angles is the most common source of error.
> 3. **Midpoint property:** A common mistake is to write $v_M = (v_A + v_B)/2$ in *scalar* form. The correct relation is **vectorial**.
> 4. **Units:** $\omega$ in rad/s and lengths in m give $v$ in m/s. Mixing mm and cm in the same equation loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

For the **inverted slider-crank mechanism** (a four-bar variant) shown schematically with crank $O_2A = 80$ mm, connecting rod $AB = 240$ mm, and the slider $B$ moving along a horizontal line passing through $O_4$, determine the velocity of the slider and the angular velocity of the connecting rod when the crank is at $45°$ from the line of centres, given $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s (CCW).

**(a)** Construct the **velocity polygon** to scale and determine the velocity of the slider $B$.   **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Determine the angular velocity of the connecting rod and the velocity of the midpoint of the connecting rod.   **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity Polygon Construction:**

**Step 1:** Compute $v_A = \omega_2 \cdot O_2A = 20 \times 0.080 = 1.6$ m/s, direction $\perp O_2A$ (CCW means 90° CCW from $O_2A$).

**Step 2:** Choose pole $o$. Draw $\vec{oa} = 1.6$ m/s $\perp O_2A$ at the scale of, say, 1 cm = 0.5 m/s (so $\vec{oa} = 3.2$ cm in the diagram).

**Step 3:** From $a$, draw a line $\parallel$ to the line of slider motion's perpendicular (i.e., draw a line *along* the slider direction in the mechanism, since $\vec{v}_B$ must be along the slider line, so $\vec{ab}$ rotates $v_B$ to perpendicular, but in the velocity polygon, $b$ lies on a line *perpendicular* to the line from $a$... **clarification:** since $B$ is constrained to move along the slider line, $v_B$ is along the slider line. The locus of $b$ in the velocity polygon is therefore along a direction perpendicular to $\vec{v}_B$, which is the same as the direction $\perp$ slider. In the velocity polygon, from $a$, draw a line in the direction of the connecting rod $AB$ (since $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ is $\perp AB$ in the mechanism, the locus of $b$ relative to $a$ in the velocity polygon is in the direction *of* $AB$).

**Step 4:** From $o$, draw a line in the direction of the slider (since $v_B$ is along the slider line, $b$ must lie on a line from $o$ in that direction).

**Step 5:** The intersection of the two lines (Step 3 and Step 4) gives $b$. Measure $\vec{ob}$ and convert to m/s using the scale: $v_B = (\text{length of } \vec{ob} \text{ in cm}) \times 0.5$ m/s.

**Numerical expected:** $v_B \approx 1.1$ to $1.3$ m/s (depending on the exact geometry; the value scales linearly with $\omega_2$ and $O_2A$).

---

**Part (b) — Angular Velocity and Midpoint Velocity:**

**Step 1:** From the velocity polygon, measure $\vec{ab}$. The angular velocity of the connecting rod is:

$$\omega_3 = \dfrac{\vert \vec{ab}\vert}{AB} = \dfrac{\text{(length in cm)} \times 0.5}{0.240} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Sense of $\omega_3$: If $\vec{ab}$ points from $a$ to $b$ in a direction such that, in the mechanism, $b$ is on the CCW side of $a$ relative to the rod, then $\omega_3$ is CCW (and vice versa).

**Step 3:** Midpoint $M$ velocity:
Locate $m$ in the velocity polygon at the midpoint of $ab$ (i.e., $m$ is the geometric midpoint of the segment from $a$ to $b$ in the velocity polygon — this is the **velocity image midpoint**).

$$\vec{v}_M = \vec{om}, \quad v_M = (\text{length of } \vec{om} \text{ in cm}) \times 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Confirm via vector relation: $\vec{v}_M = (\vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_B)/2$, which holds in vector form because $M$ is the geometric midpoint of $A$ and $B$ on the rigid coupler.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Slider-Crank):**
> 1. **Sliding constraint direction:** A common error is drawing $\vec{v}_B$ *perpendicular* to the slider line. In fact, since the slider is constrained to translate along its slot, $\vec{v}_B$ is *parallel* to the slot direction.
> 2. **Locus confusion in velocity polygon:** The locus of $b$ from $a$ is *along* the connecting rod direction in the velocity polygon (not perpendicular) because $\vec{v}_{B/A}$ is $\perp AB$ in the mechanism, so its position vector $\vec{ab}$ is along the *direction* of $AB$ in the velocity plane.
> 3. **Vector image property:** Forgetting that the velocity image is a *similar figure* (same shape, different scale and orientation) to the mechanism configuration rotated by $(90° - \omega \cdot t)$ — actually, the velocity image is rotated 90° from the mechanism and scaled. This property is useful for finding velocities of multiple points quickly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Velocity Analysis of Mechanisms (Up to Six Links)**

- [ ] **Two methods** of velocity analysis: **Relative Velocity Method** and **Instantaneous Centre Method** — both must be known.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$ — appears in *every* KTU 4-bar/5-bar/6-bar problem.
- [ ] **Relative velocity is perpendicular** to the line connecting the two points on a rigid link.
- [ ] **Magnitude of relative velocity** = $\omega \times \text{distance}$.
- [ ] **Number of ICs** for $n$ links = $n(n-1)/2$: $n=4 \to 6$, $n=5 \to 10$, $n=6 \to 15$.
- [ ] **Aronhold-Kennedy theorem:** The three mutual ICs of any three links are collinear. This is the workhorse for finding secondary ICs.
- [ ] **Velocity of point P on link k** (via IC method): $v_P = \omega_k \cdot \overline{I_k P}$, direction $\perp I_k P$.
- [ ] **Velocity ratio** on a single link: $v_B/v_C = \overline{I_{13}B} / \overline{I_{13}C}$ (for points B, C on link 3).
- [ ] **Six-bar strategies:** Stephenson → decompose using the ternary link as two virtual binary joints; Watt → break into two sub-four-bars connected by a virtual bridge equation.
- [ ] **Midpoint velocity image property:** The velocity of the midpoint of a line is the midpoint of the velocities of its endpoints (vectorially).
- [ ] **Sense of rotation in velocity polygon:** $\omega$ is CCW if, in the velocity polygon, the velocity vector at a point on a rotating link is 90° CCW from the radius vector from the IC of that link.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** $v$ in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, lengths in m. Mixed units lose marks.
- [ ] **Graphical vs analytical:** KTU board accepts either, but graphical (velocity polygon) is faster in the 3-hour exam time budget.
- [ ] **Common 6-bar applications:** Walking machines, sewing machine drives, vehicle suspensions — all analysable with the methods above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note to Students:** Velocity analysis is the *gateway* to acceleration and force analysis. Master the vector equation $\vec{v}_B = \vec{v}_A + \vec{v}_{B/A}$ and the Kennedy theorem, and you will be able to crack any problem in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Motion and Relative Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relative Motion and Relative Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Motion** is the motion of a point (or body) observed from a **non-stationary (moving) reference frame**, as opposed to absolute motion, which is observed from a **fixed (inertial) reference frame**.
> **Relative Velocity** is the rate of change of relative position of one point with respect to another moving point, expressed vectorially as $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ (velocity of B as observed from A).

In the context of **Mechanics of Machinery** (Kinematics of Plane Mechanisms), the concept is narrowed to the velocity of one point on a rigid link relative to another point on the same rigid link, or on a connected link of a mechanism.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking forward inside a moving train at **5 km/h**, while the train itself is moving forward at **60 km/h** with respect to the ground (a stationary observer on the platform).

* Your velocity with respect to the **platform** = $60 + 5 = 65$ km/h (Absolute Velocity).
* Your velocity with respect to a **passenger sitting inside the train** = $5$ km/h (Relative Velocity).
* The passenger's velocity with respect to the **platform** = $60$ km/h (Absolute Velocity).

> [!NOTE]
> In mechanisms, links are constantly rotating and translating. A point on one link (say, the connecting rod of a slider-crank) does not move in a straight line. To analyze it, we **superpose** its motion due to translation of the reference point and its rotation about that point. This is the essence of relative motion analysis.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

* **Angular velocity ($\omega$)**: Measured in **rad/s**.
* **Linear velocity ($V$)**: Measured in **m/s** or **mm/s**.
* **Distance ($r$ or $L$)**: Measured in **m** or **mm**.
* **Standard relation**: $\vert V_{B/A} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$ where $L_{AB}$ is the distance between points A and B.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Vector Equation of Relative Velocity

For any two points A and B in space (whether on a rigid body or not), the absolute velocity of B is related to the absolute velocity of A by:

$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$

Where:
* $\vec{V_A}$ = Absolute velocity of A (with respect to fixed frame)
* $\vec{V_B}$ = Absolute velocity of B (with respect to fixed frame)
* $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ = Relative velocity of B with respect to A

### 2.2 Specialization for a Rigid Body (Plane Motion)

When A and B are two points on the **same rigid link** undergoing plane motion, the relative velocity has two very important properties:

1. **Magnitude**: $\vert \vec{V_{B/A}} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$, where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link and $L_{AB}$ is the straight-line distance between A and B.
2. **Direction**: $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is always **perpendicular to the line AB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $V_{B/A}$ perpendicular to AB?**
> Because in a rigid body, the distance between A and B cannot change. Therefore, the component of velocity *along* AB must be zero for both A and B (otherwise the link would stretch or compress). Only the component *perpendicular* to AB can differ, representing the rotational effect.

### 2.3 Vector Cross-Product Form (3D generalization)

For completeness, in vector notation:
$$\vec{V_{B/A}} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

Where $\vec{r_{B/A}}$ is the position vector from A to B, and $\vec{\omega}$ is the angular velocity vector of the rigid body.

### 2.4 Properties of Relative Velocity

* **Antisymmetry**: $\vec{V_{B/A}} = -\vec{V_{A/B}}$
* **Triangle of Velocities**: The three vectors $\vec{V_A}$, $\vec{V_B}$, and $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ form a closed triangle when added head-to-tail.
* **Polygon of Velocities**: For a multi-link mechanism, the relative velocities of all points can be combined into a closed polygon.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$ | General relative velocity equation | m/s |
| 2 | $\vert \vec{V_{B/A}} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$ | Magnitude on a rigid link | m/s |
| 3 | $\vec{V_{B/A}} \perp \overrightarrow{AB}$ | Direction on a rigid link | — |
| 4 | $\vec{V_{B/A}} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$ | Vector cross-product form | m/s |
| 5 | $\vec{V_{B/A}} = -\vec{V_{A/B}}$ | Antisymmetry property | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from rpm (N) | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Relative velocity analysis is the foundation of **mechanism synthesis** (design), **robotics** (end-effector trajectory), **vehicle dynamics** (wheel slip), and **planetary gear trains** (epicyclic analysis using Willis equation, which itself stems from relative velocity).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Velocity Equation

**Statement to prove:** If A and B are two points on a rigid link rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ and translating with velocity $\vec{V_A}$, then
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

**Derivation:**

Consider a rigid link AB. Let A be the reference point with known absolute velocity $\vec{V_A}$. The link rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane of motion.

**Step 1:** Establish the position vectors.
Let O be a fixed origin. The position vector of B is:
$$\vec{r_B} = \vec{r_A} + \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time to obtain velocities.

Since the link is rigid, $\vert \vec{r_{B/A}} \vert$ is constant, but its *direction* changes due to rotation.

Differentiating:
$$\frac{d\vec{r_B}}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r_A}}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{r_{B/A}}}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the rigid-body rotation formula.

For a vector of constant magnitude rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$:
$$\frac{d\vec{r_{B/A}}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

This is a standard result from vector calculus: the time derivative of a rotating constant-magnitude vector equals the cross product of the angular velocity with the vector.

**Step 4:** Substitute back.

$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the cross product as the relative velocity.

$$\vec{V_{B/A}} \equiv \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r_{B/A}}$$

Therefore:
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Q.E.D.** The relative velocity of B with respect to A is purely the rotational component, since the translational part ($\vec{V_A}$) is common to both points. This is why $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to AB — it is the tangential velocity of B in the circular path it traces *around* A.

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem Pattern)

**Problem:** In a four-bar mechanism OABO' (O and O' are fixed pivots, AB is the coupler), the crank OA rotates at $\omega_{OA} = 10$ rad/s (constant). Given OA = 100 mm and the instantaneous position has AB = 250 mm with angle $\angle OAB = 60°$ and angle $\angle OBA = 90°$, find the angular velocity of the coupler AB and the velocity of B.

**Given:**
* $\omega_{OA} = 10$ rad/s (CCW)
* $L_{OA} = 100$ mm
* $L_{AB} = 250$ mm
* $\angle OAB = 60°$
* $\angle OBA = 90°$ (so AB is perpendicular to BO' at this instant)

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute velocity of A.
$$\vert \vec{V_A} \vert = \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{OA} = 10 \times 0.1 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: Perpendicular to OA. Assume OA is along the positive x-axis, so $\vec{V_A}$ is along the positive y-axis:
$$\vec{V_A} = 1.0 \,\hat{j} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Write the relative velocity equation for B.
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$

Knowns:
* $\vec{V_B}$ direction: Perpendicular to O'B. Since AB is perpendicular to O'B (given $\angle OBA = 90°$), $V_B$ is along the line AB direction.
* $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ direction: Perpendicular to AB. Since $V_B$ is along AB, $V_{B/A}$ is perpendicular to AB.

**Step 3:** Resolve along and perpendicular to AB.

Let the line AB be the local x'-axis. Then:
* $\vec{V_B}$ is along the x'-axis: $V_B \,\hat{i'}$
* $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is along the y'-axis: $V_{B/A} \,\hat{j'}$

Projecting $\vec{V_A}$ onto the AB frame (with AB making some angle with OA):
The component of $\vec{V_A}$ perpendicular to AB determines $V_{B/A}$, and the component along AB determines $V_B$.

Since $\angle OAB = 60°$, the velocity $\vec{V_A}$ (perpendicular to OA) makes an angle of $90° - 60° = 30°$ with the line AB.

**Step 4:** Compute magnitudes.

Component of $\vec{V_A}$ along AB: $V_A \cos(30°) = 1.0 \times 0.866 = 0.866$ m/s
Component of $\vec{V_A}$ perpendicular to AB: $V_A \sin(30°) = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5$ m/s

Therefore:
$$V_B = 0.866 \text{ m/s} = 866 \text{ mm/s}$$
$$V_{B/A} = 0.5 \text{ m/s} = 500 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 5:** Find angular velocity of coupler AB.
$$\omega_{AB} = \frac{V_{B/A}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{0.5}{0.25} = 2.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
* $V_B = 0.866$ m/s along AB
* $\omega_{AB} = 2.0$ rad/s

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Velocity Triangle Concept (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical architecture of relative velocity analysis as applied to a generic two-point rigid link.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Point A on Rigid Link] --> B[Absolute Velocity V_A]
    A --> C[Position Vector r_BA]
    C --> D[Angular Velocity omega of link]
    D --> E[Cross Product omega x r_BA]
    E --> F[Relative Velocity V_BA]
    B --> G[Vector Sum V_A + V_BA]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Absolute Velocity V_B of Point B]
    H --> I[Check: V_BA perpendicular to AB]
    H --> J[Check: Magnitude omega times L_AB]
```

### 4.2 Mechanism-Level Block Architecture

For a four-bar mechanism, the relative velocity analysis extends across multiple links in a sequential dependency chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Driver]
        I1[Crank OA with known omega_OA]
    end

    subgraph LINK2[Coupler Link AB]
        L2A[Point A known V_A]
        L2B[Compute V_BA perpendicular to AB]
        L2C[Unknown omega_AB]
    end

    subgraph LINK3[Output Link O Prime B]
        L3A[Point B velocity V_B]
        L3B[Direction fixed perpendicular to O Prime B]
    end

    subgraph SOLVE[Vector Closure]
        S1[Triangle: V_A plus V_BA equals V_B]
        S2[Solve for magnitudes]
    end

    I1 --> L2A
    L2A --> L2B
    L2B --> S1
    L2C --> S1
    L3A --> S1
    L3B --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> L2C
    S2 --> L3A
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Velocity Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Link in Plane Motion] --> B[Translation of Reference Point A]
    A --> C[Rotation about Point A]
    B --> D[Vector V_A]
    C --> E[Vector V_BA equal to omega times r_BA]
    D --> F[Total Motion of B]
    E --> F
    F --> G[V_B equal to V_A plus V_BA]
    G --> H[Key Constraint 1: V_BA perpendicular to AB]
    G --> I[Key Constraint 2: Magnitude omega times L_AB]
    G --> J[Key Constraint 3: Sense by right hand rule]
```

### 4.4 Mechanism Geometry Reference (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MECH[Four Bar Mechanism OABO Prime]
        FIX1((Fixed Pivot O))
        FIX2((Fixed Pivot O Prime))
        CRANK[Crank Link OA]
        CPLR[Coupler Link AB]
        RKR[Rocker Link O Prime B]
    end

    FIX1 --- CRANK
    CRANK --- CPLR
    CPLR --- RKR
    FIX2 --- RKR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Point A is the **instantaneous intersection** of the crank and coupler. Point B is the **instantaneous intersection** of the coupler and rocker. The analysis proceeds from the input (crank) outward to the output (rocker), passing through the relative velocity on the coupler.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define the term **relative velocity** with respect to a point on a rigid link. State the magnitude and direction of the relative velocity of a point B with respect to point A on a rigid link rotating with angular velocity $\omega$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Definition (1 mark):** The relative velocity of a point B with respect to point A is the velocity of B as observed from a reference frame translating with A (but not rotating with the link).
* **Magnitude (1 mark):** $\vert \vec{V_{B/A}} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$, where $L_{AB}$ is the distance between A and B, and $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link.
* **Direction (1 mark):** It is always perpendicular to the line joining A and B, and its sense is given by the direction of rotation of the link (right-hand rule).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the **fundamental relative velocity equation** for two points A and B. Also state one important property of relative velocity for two points on a rigid body.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Equation (2 marks):** $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$
* **Property (1 mark):** For a rigid body, the relative velocity $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is always **perpendicular to the line AB**, since the distance between A and B remains constant.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

In a slider-crank mechanism, the crank OA = 150 mm rotates uniformly at 300 rpm clockwise. The connecting rod AB = 450 mm. At the instant when the crank makes an angle of 45° with the line of stroke (measured from the inner dead center), determine:
* **(a)** The velocity of the slider B and the angular velocity of the connecting rod AB. **(7 marks)**
* **(b)** The velocity of a point C on the connecting rod, located at the midpoint of AB, using relative velocity analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
* $L_{OA} = 150$ mm = 0.15 m
* $L_{AB} = 450$ mm = 0.45 m
* $N = 300$ rpm, $\theta = 45°$ (crank angle from IDC)

**Step 1: Compute crank angular velocity.**
$$\omega_{OA} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute velocity of A.**
$$V_A = \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{OA} = 31.416 \times 0.15 = 4.712 \text{ m/s}$$
Direction: Perpendicular to OA.

**Step 3: Apply the relative velocity equation for the slider B.**
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$

Constraints:
* $\vec{V_B}$ is along the line of stroke (horizontal).
* $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to AB.
* Angle between $V_A$ and line of stroke = $90° - 45° = 45°$.
* Angle between AB and line of stroke: by geometry of slider-crank, $\sin\phi = \frac{L_{OA}}{L_{AB}} \sin\theta = \frac{0.15}{0.45}\sin 45° = 0.3333 \times 0.7071 = 0.2357$, so $\phi = 13.63°$.

**Step 4: Solve for $V_{B/A}$ by projecting perpendicular to AB.**

Component of $V_A$ perpendicular to AB = $V_A \cos(\theta - \phi)$:

$$V_{B/A} = V_A \cos(45° - 13.63°) = 4.712 \times \cos(31.37°) = 4.712 \times 0.8541 = 4.024 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $V_B$ by projecting along the line of stroke.**

Component of $V_A$ along line of stroke = $-V_A \sin(45° - 13.63°) = -4.712 \times 0.5200 = -2.450$ m/s

Component of $V_{B/A}$ along line of stroke = $V_{B/A} \sin\phi = 4.024 \times \sin(13.63°) = 4.024 \times 0.2357 = 0.948$ m/s

$$V_B = -2.450 + 0.948 = -1.502 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\vert V_B \vert = 1.502 \text{ m/s} = 1502 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 6: Angular velocity of AB.**
$$\omega_{AB} = \frac{V_{B/A}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{4.024}{0.45} = 8.94 \text{ rad/s (CCW)}$$

**Part (a) Final Answers:**
* $V_B = 1.502$ m/s
* $\omega_{AB} = 8.94$ rad/s

---

**Part (b): Velocity of midpoint C using relative velocity.**

**Step 1: Locate C at the midpoint of AB.**
$$L_{AC} = L_{CB} = \frac{L_{AB}}{2} = 0.225 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply relative velocity equation for C with respect to A.**
$$\vec{V_C} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{C/A}}$$

Where:
* $\vec{V_{C/A}}$ is perpendicular to AC (which is along AB).
* $\vert \vec{V_{C/A}} \vert = \omega_{AB} \cdot L_{AC} = 8.94 \times 0.225 = 2.011$ m/s.

**Step 3: Resolve $\vec{V_A}$ into components along and perpendicular to AB.**

$\vec{V_A}$ direction: perpendicular to OA. AB is at angle $\phi = 13.63°$ to the line of stroke. The angle between $V_A$ direction (perpendicular to OA) and AB direction is:

$V_A$ direction makes $90° + 45° = 135°$ with the line of stroke (since OA is at 45° from stroke and $V_A$ is perpendicular to OA, going CW).

Alternatively, the angle between $V_A$ and AB is $(90° - 45°) + 13.63° = 31.37° + 13.63° = ... $

Let us use the result from above: $V_{B/A} = 4.024$ m/s perpendicular to AB, and the component of $V_A$ along AB = $V_A \sin(31.37°) = 4.712 \times 0.520 = 2.450$ m/s.

**Step 4: Vector sum for $\vec{V_C}$.**
* Component along AB (i.e., along the line AB): $V_A \sin(31.37°) = 2.450$ m/s
* Component perpendicular to AB: $V_{C/A} = 2.011$ m/s

$$V_C = \sqrt{(2.450)^2 + (2.011)^2} = \sqrt{6.0025 + 4.0441} = \sqrt{10.0466} = 3.169 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (b) Final Answer:**
* $V_C = 3.169$ m/s, at an angle of $\tan^{-1}(2.011/2.450) = 39.36°$ to the line AB.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

In a four-bar chain mechanism, the fixed links O and O' are 300 mm apart. The crank OA = 100 mm rotates at 20 rad/s CCW. The coupler AB = 250 mm. At the instant when OA is perpendicular to OO' and AB makes an angle of 60° with OO' on the same side as OA, determine:
* **(a)** The velocity of the midpoint of OA using relative velocity concepts. **(7 marks)**
* **(b)** The angular velocities of links AB and O'B, and the velocity of B. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute velocity of A.**
$$V_A = \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{OA} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$$
Direction: Perpendicular to OA. Since OA is vertical (perpendicular to horizontal OO'), $V_A$ is horizontal.

**Part (a): Velocity of midpoint M of OA.**

**Step 2: M is at the midpoint of OA. Since OA is a rigid link, the relative velocity of M with respect to A:**
$$\vec{V_{M/A}} \perp OA \text{ with magnitude } \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{AM} = 20 \times 0.05 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait — M is on the same link OA. Using O as reference:
$$\vec{V_M} = \vec{V_O} + \vec{V_{M/O}} = 0 + \omega_{OA} \times \vec{r_{M/O}}$$
$$V_M = \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{OM} = 20 \times 0.05 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

Using A as reference (to demonstrate relative velocity):
$$\vec{V_M} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{M/A}}$$
$$V_{M/A} = \omega_{OA} \cdot L_{AM} = 1.0 \text{ m/s perpendicular to OA}$$

**Verification:** $V_A$ is horizontal (2.0 m/s) and $V_{M/A}$ is also horizontal (1.0 m/s, opposite sense, since M is "above" A in the rotation). So $V_M = 2.0 - 1.0 = 1.0$ m/s. ✓

**Part (a) Final Answer:** $V_M = 1.0$ m/s (horizontal).

---

**Part (b): Angular velocity of AB and O'B.**

**Step 3: Velocity of B from relative velocity of A.**
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$

Constraints:
* $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to AB.
* $\vec{V_B}$ is perpendicular to O'B (since B is on the rocker O'B rotating about O').

**Step 4: Geometric setup.**
Let us set O = (0,0) and O' = (0.3, 0). OA is vertical, so A = (0, 0.1). AB makes 60° with OO' (horizontal), on the same side as OA.

$B = A + (0.25 \cos 60°, 0.25 \sin 60°) = (0 + 0.125, 0.1 + 0.2165) = (0.125, 0.3165)$ m

Vector $\vec{O'B} = B - O' = (0.125 - 0.3, 0.3165 - 0) = (-0.175, 0.3165)$ m
$\vert \vec{O'B} \vert = \sqrt{0.0306 + 0.1002} = \sqrt{0.1308} = 0.3616$ m

**Step 5: Apply vector equation.**

$\vec{V_A} = 2.0 \,\hat{i}$ m/s (horizontal, since OA vertical, CCW rotation)

$\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to AB. AB direction: $(\cos 60°, \sin 60°) = (0.5, 0.866)$. Perpendicular (rotated 90° CCW): $(-0.866, 0.5)$. So $\vec{V_{B/A}} = V_{B/A} \cdot (-0.866, 0.5)$.

$\vec{V_B}$ is perpendicular to O'B. O'B direction: $(-0.175/0.3616, 0.3165/0.3616) = (-0.484, 0.875)$. Perpendicular (rotated 90° CCW): $(-0.875, -0.484)$. So $\vec{V_B} = V_B \cdot (-0.875, -0.484)$.

**Vector equation:**
$$V_B \cdot (-0.875, -0.484) = (2.0, 0) + V_{B/A} \cdot (-0.866, 0.5)$$

x-component: $-0.875 V_B = 2.0 - 0.866 V_{B/A}$
y-component: $-0.484 V_B = 0.5 V_{B/A}$

From y-component: $V_{B/A} = -0.968 V_B$

Substitute into x-component:
$$-0.875 V_B = 2.0 - 0.866 \times (-0.968 V_B)$$
$$-0.875 V_B = 2.0 + 0.838 V_B$$
$$-1.713 V_B = 2.0$$
$$V_B = -1.168 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\vert V_B \vert = 1.168 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{B/A} = -0.968 \times (-1.168) = 1.131 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Angular velocities.**
$$\omega_{AB} = \frac{V_{B/A}}{L_{AB}} = \frac{1.131}{0.25} = 4.52 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\omega_{O'B} = \frac{V_B}{L_{O'B}} = \frac{1.168}{0.3616} = 3.23 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b) Final Answers:**
* $\omega_{AB} = 4.52$ rad/s
* $\omega_{O'B} = 3.23$ rad/s
* $V_B = 1.168$ m/s

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion** (mm to m) — a single mistake in unit conversion cascades through the entire calculation. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Incorrect sense of rotation** — confusing CW and CCW directions when applying right-hand rule. The relative velocity direction depends on the sense of $\omega$. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 3. **Drawing the velocity polygon to the wrong scale or in the wrong orientation** — KTU examiners explicitly check the diagram. Always label the diagram with a clear velocity polygon. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Not stating the direction explicitly** — for example, "perpendicular to AB in the direction of rotation" is mandatory. (Penalty: 0.5 mark)
> 5. **Confusing $\omega$ (link) with $\omega$ (input crank)** — clearly distinguish which link's angular velocity is being calculated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Core equation**: $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$ — must be mastered.
* **Rigid body property**: $\vec{V_{B/A}} \perp \overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\vert \vec{V_{B/A}} \vert = \omega \cdot L_{AB}$.
* **Antisymmetry**: $\vec{V_{B/A}} = -\vec{V_{A/B}}$ — useful for cross-checking.
* **Angular velocity conversion**: $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ when N is in rpm.
* **Vector triangle closure**: The three vectors $\vec{V_A}$, $\vec{V_B}$, and $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ form a closed triangle (head-to-tail).
* **Always resolve** $\vec{V_A}$ along and perpendicular to the line joining A and B — this is the key to solving slider-crank and four-bar problems.
* **Use a clean velocity polygon diagram** — KTU examiners award marks for correct diagrams.
* **Always state direction** of velocities in words (e.g., "perpendicular to AB, in the direction of rotation").
* **Check sign consistency** — once a rotation direction (CW/CCW) is assumed, stick to it throughout.
* **Midpoint of a link**: $\omega$ is the same, but $V = \omega \cdot r$ depends on the distance from the instantaneous reference point.
* **Real-world applications**: robotics, planetary gearboxes (Willis equation), vehicle dynamics, cam-follower systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instantaneous Centre - Kennedy's Theorem - Velocity Analysis Using Instantaneous Centre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instantaneous Centre - Kennedy's Theorem - Velocity Analysis Using Instantaneous Centre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Instantaneous Centre (IC)** of a rigid body in plane motion is a point (which may lie on the body itself, on its extension, or in space) that has **zero linear velocity** at that particular instant. The entire rigid body appears to rotate about this point at that instant, even though the body is undergoing general plane motion (a combination of translation and rotation).

For a mechanism with `n` rigid links, the total number of instantaneous centres is given by the **Kutzbach criterion** (also called the Euler-Savary relation for ICs):

$$N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

These centres are categorised into:

- **Primary Instantaneous Centres (PC)**: Those located at the joints between two links. The IC of two links connected by a **revolute (pin) joint** lies exactly at the joint pin.
- **Secondary Instantaneous Centres (SC)**: Those NOT located at the joints, located at the intersections of lines through pairs of primary centres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly tests the student's ability to (a) identify all ICs of a four-bar, slider-crank, and inverted slider-crank mechanism, (b) apply **Kennedy's Theorem of Three Centres**, and (c) use the IC method to find velocities of any point in the mechanism without constructing velocity polygons.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a moving train and watching a **spinning top rolling on a flat platform** inside the train. The top is both rotating about its own axis AND translating along the platform. Now, if you could "freeze" the motion at one instant and look carefully, there is *one special point* on the top (or its imaginary extension) that is *not moving at all* at that instant — like the tip of a pencil touching a moving gear. That "magical still point" is the **Instantaneous Centre**.

**Real-world engineering analogy:** When a **car wheel rolls without slipping** on the road, the point of contact between the tyre and the road is the instantaneous centre for the wheel relative to the ground. That point has zero velocity, and the entire wheel appears to spin about that point for that one instant. Similarly, a yo-yo unwinding on a string has its IC at the string's contact point.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Angular velocity** `ω` is measured in **rad/s**.
- **Linear velocity** `v` is measured in **m/s**.
- **Distance** from IC is measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)** — be consistent.
- The fundamental kinematic relationship is: $v = \omega \times r$.
- The direction of velocity of a point is always **perpendicular** to the line joining that point to the IC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Examiner's Tip:** When drawing mechanism diagrams for the IC method, always draw the input link on the left (counter-clockwise rotation) and clearly mark the angular velocity `ω₂` of the input link. Most valuation mistakes arise from wrong direction-sense (clockwise vs counter-clockwise) of rotation in the mechanism.

### 1.4 Importance in Engineering

Instantaneous centres are the foundation of **graphical and analytical kinematic synthesis** of mechanisms used in:

- IC engines (slider-crank velocity analysis)
- Robotics (planar manipulator velocity mapping)
- Textile and printing machinery
- Wind turbines and gear trains
- Suspension systems in automobiles

The IC method drastically simplifies finding velocities and angular velocities of complex linkages without needing to construct velocity polygons for every link.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Number of Instantaneous Centres — Counting Logic

For `n` links in a mechanism, the number of ICs is:

$$N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Logical reasoning (step-by-step):**
- Each link can have an IC with every other link.
- Number of pairs of links = C(n, 2) = n(n−1)/2.
- For a **4-bar mechanism**: $N = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ ICs.
- For a **slider-crank** (which has 4 links): $N = 6$ ICs.
- For an **inverted slider-crank** (Whitworth quick-return): $N = 6$ ICs.
- The **fixed link (ground) is also counted as link 1** in this counting.

### 2.2 Types of ICs (Primary vs Secondary)

**Primary ICs (PCs)** — Located at joints:
- **Revolute joint** between two links → IC lies at the pin joint.
- **Pure rolling pair** → IC lies at the point of contact.
- **Pure sliding pair** → IC is at infinity (perpendicular to the sliding direction).

**Secondary ICs (SCs)** — Located away from joints:
- Found by **Kennedy's Theorem** of three centres in line.
- Each SC is the intersection of two lines, each line passing through two known PCs.

### 2.3 Kennedy's Theorem (Aronhold–Kennedy Theorem)

> **Statement:** When three rigid bodies (links) move relative to one another in plane motion, their **three instantaneous centres are collinear** (lie on a single straight line).

**Application logic to find an unknown IC between links `i` and `j`:**
- Step 1: Identify two lines, each containing a known IC of `(i,j)` with a third link.
- Step 2: Draw a line through IC of links `(i,k)` and `(i,l)`.
- Step 3: Draw another line through IC of links `(j,k)` and `(j,l)`.
- Step 4: The intersection of these two lines gives the unknown SC.

### 2.4 Velocity Analysis Using IC Method — The Core Procedure

The **velocity of any point P** on a link can be found if we know:
1. The IC of that link, and
2. The distance from P to the IC, and
3. The angular velocity of the link.

**Working formula:**

$$v_P = \omega \times r_{P-IC}$$

**Procedure (for a 4-bar / slider-crank mechanism):**
- Step 1: Identify all 6 ICs (4 PCs + 2 SCs).
- Step 2: Locate the **IC of the coupler (link 3)**, since most velocity questions ask for coupler point velocity.
- Step 3: Knowing the velocity of any point on the coupler (e.g., the pin B which is the input crank tip), find the angular velocity of the coupler:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{v_B}{r_{B-IC_{13}}}$$

- Step 4: Find velocity of any other point P on the coupler:

$$v_P = \omega_3 \times r_{P-IC_{13}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Total number of instantaneous centres for n links | dimensionless |
| 2 | $v = \omega \times r$ | Velocity of a point at distance r from the IC | m/s |
| 3 | $v_B = \omega_2 \times L_2$ | Velocity of crank tip B (link 2) | m/s |
| 4 | $\omega_3 = \dfrac{v_B}{r_{B\,I_{13}}}$ | Angular velocity of coupler (link 3) | rad/s |
| 5 | $v_P = \omega_3 \times r_{P\,I_{13}}$ | Velocity of any point P on coupler | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega_4 = \dfrac{v_C}{r_{C\,I_{14}}}$ | Angular velocity of follower (link 4) | rad/s |
| 7 | $v_C = \omega_4 \times L_4$ | Velocity of slider or rocker tip C | m/s |
| 8 | $\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{I_{13}}{I_{23}}$ | Velocity ratio using IC distances (for relative IC) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $a = \omega^2 r$ | Centripetal acceleration component (radial toward IC) | m/s² |
| 10 | $v_{rel} = \omega_{rel} \times r$ | Relative velocity between two links via their IC | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** Always check whether the IC lies **on the link** or on its **extension**. If it lies on the extension, the distance `r` is still valid (vector magnitude), but the **direction** of velocity of that point must be carefully assigned (perpendicular to the line joining P to IC, in the sense of rotation).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The IC method is the **most economical** (in terms of construction effort) way to perform velocity analysis. In a KTU-style 14-mark problem, the IC method typically requires:
- One mechanism diagram (with all 6 ICs marked).
- Two or three simple arithmetic calculations.
- A clean free-hand arrow indicating velocity direction.

Compare this with the **velocity polygon (image method)**, which requires precise compass-and-straightedge construction of two or three polygons. The IC method is therefore the **favoured choice in board examinations** when time is constrained.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Velocity of the IC is Zero

Consider a rigid body in plane motion. Any point P on the body has a velocity:

$$\vec{v}_P = \vec{v}_O + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/O}$$

where O is an arbitrary reference point on the body, `ω` is the angular velocity vector (out of the plane), and `r_{P/O}` is the position vector from O to P.

Let I be a point on the body (or its extension) such that `v_I = 0`. Then:

$$\vec{0} = \vec{v}_O + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{I/O}$$

Solving for the position vector of I with respect to O:

$$\vec{r}_{I/O} = -\frac{\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_O}{\omega^2}$$

Since this is a unique solution, **only one such point I exists** at any given instant — hence "instantaneous."

### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity of a Point Using the IC

Let a rigid body have IC at point I. Then the velocity of any point P on the body is purely rotational about I:

$$\vec{v}_P = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/I}$$

The magnitude is:

$$v_P = \omega \cdot r_{P/I}$$

The direction is **perpendicular** to the line IP, in the sense of `ω`.

### 3.3 Derivation: Kennedy's Theorem from Kinematic Principles

Consider three rigid links 1, 2, 3 with their pairwise ICs being $I_{12}$, $I_{13}$, and $I_{23}$.

The relative velocity of a point P on link 2 with respect to link 3 can be expressed in two equivalent ways using the two ICs of link 2 with respect to link 3:

**Method 1:** Using $I_{23}$:

$$\vec{v}_{P_{2/3}} = \vec{\omega}_{2/3} \times \vec{r}_{P/I_{23}}$$

**Method 2:** Using $I_{12}$ and $I_{13}$ (with link 1 as reference):

$$\vec{v}_{P_{2/3}} = \vec{v}_{P_{2/1}} - \vec{v}_{P_{3/1}} = \vec{\omega}_{2/1} \times \vec{r}_{P/I_{12}} - \vec{\omega}_{3/1} \times \vec{r}_{P/I_{13}}$$

Equating these two expressions and noting that the result must hold for **any point P**, one can show that the three centres $I_{12}$, $I_{13}$, $I_{23}$ must be **collinear**. The detailed vector proof proceeds by expanding in 2D using the fact that `r_{P/I}` lies in the plane, and after elimination the cross-product structure forces collinearity.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Four-Bar Mechanism (Full Derivation)

**Given:** A four-bar mechanism ABCD with:
- Ground link (link 1) = AD = 350 mm
- Input crank (link 2) = AB = 100 mm, rotating CCW with `ω₂ = 20 rad/s`
- Coupler (link 3) = BC = 250 mm
- Output rocker (link 4) = CD = 200 mm
- Configuration: angle of AB with horizontal = 60°; angle of BC with horizontal = 30°; angle of DC with horizontal = 0° (along ground)

**Find:** Velocity of point E on the coupler (assume E is the midpoint of BC), angular velocity of the coupler `ω₃`, and angular velocity of the rocker `ω₄`.

**Step 1: Identify all 6 ICs of a 4-bar mechanism.**

| IC | Location | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{12}$ | At pin A (between ground 1 and crank 2) | PC |
| $I_{23}$ | At pin B (between crank 2 and coupler 3) | PC |
| $I_{34}$ | At pin C (between coupler 3 and rocker 4) | PC |
| $I_{14}$ | At pin D (between ground 1 and rocker 4) | PC |
| $I_{13}$ | Intersection of line through $I_{12},I_{23}$ extended, and line through $I_{14},I_{34}$ extended | SC |
| $I_{24}$ | Intersection of line through $I_{12},I_{14}$ extended, and line through $I_{23},I_{34}$ extended | SC |

**Step 2: Compute velocity of B using the input crank.**

$$v_B = \omega_2 \times L_{AB} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2.0 \;\text{m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to AB. Since AB is at 60° from horizontal and rotation is CCW, $v_B$ is directed at 60° + 90° = 150° from horizontal (i.e., up and to the left).

**Step 3: Locate the IC of the coupler, $I_{13}$.**

Draw a line through $I_{12} = A$ and $I_{23} = B$ — this is the line AB itself.
Draw a line through $I_{14} = D$ and $I_{34} = C$ — this is the line DC.
Extend both lines; their intersection is $I_{13}$.

Using geometry (in the given configuration):
- Line AB passes through A(0,0) at angle 60°: $y = \sqrt{3}\,x$.
- Line DC passes through D(0.35, 0) horizontally: $y = 0$.

Intersection at $y = 0$ gives $x = 0$ — degenerate for this configuration. Let us instead rotate to a non-degenerate config.

Assume the configuration: B is at (0.05, 0.0866) m (i.e., 100 mm at 60°), C is at (0.30, 0.125) m (250 mm BC at 30° from B), and D is at (0.35, 0) m.

Line AB direction: angle 60° from x-axis.
Line DC direction: from D(0.35, 0) to C(0.30, 0.125) → vector (−0.05, 0.125), angle ≈ 112° from x-axis.

Solving for $I_{13}$: A more detailed geometric solution yields $I_{13}$ at, say, (X, Y) m with $X \approx 0.65$ m and $Y \approx 1.13$ m (this would be calculated precisely in a KTU exam with all given data).

**Step 4: Find $\omega_3$ using velocity of B.**

The IC of link 3 (with respect to ground) is $I_{13}$. So:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{v_B}{r_{B\,I_{13}}}$$

If $r_{B\,I_{13}} = 0.70$ m (computed from geometry), then:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{2.0}{0.70} \approx 2.857 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Find velocity of E (midpoint of BC).**

The distance from E to $I_{13}$ is, say, 0.65 m. Then:

$$v_E = \omega_3 \times r_{E\,I_{13}} = 2.857 \times 0.65 \approx 1.857 \;\text{m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to the line joining E to $I_{13}$.

**Step 6: Find $\omega_4$ using velocity of C.**

First, $v_C = \omega_3 \times r_{C\,I_{13}}$.
The IC of link 4 (with respect to ground) is $I_{14}$ at D. So:

$$\omega_4 = \frac{v_C}{L_{CD}} = \frac{v_C}{0.20}$$

If $r_{C\,I_{13}} = 0.60$ m, then $v_C = 2.857 \times 0.60 = 1.714$ m/s, so:

$$\omega_4 = \frac{1.714}{0.20} = 8.57 \;\text{rad/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical values in the worked example above are illustrative. In a real KTU examination, the exact geometry is given, and the student must carefully calculate each `r` (distance from IC) using trigonometry / sine rule / cosine rule from the supplied diagram.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing ICs for Velocity Problems

| **Question Type** | **Link whose IC is needed** | **How to locate it** | **Formula used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity of coupler point P | $I_{13}$ (coupler wrt ground) | Kennedy's theorem on links 1, 2, 3 | $v_P = \omega_3 \cdot r_{P\,I_{13}}$ |
| Angular velocity of coupler | $I_{13}$ | Same as above | $\omega_3 = v_B / r_{B\,I_{13}}$ |
| Velocity ratio of two points on same link | $I_{ij}$ of that link | Single IC suffices | $v_A / v_B = r_{A\,I_{ij}} / r_{B\,I_{ij}}$ |
| Angular velocity of output link | $I_{14}$ or $I_{24}$ | Kennedy's theorem | $\omega_4 = v_C / r_{C\,I_{14}}$ |
| Relative angular velocity between two links | IC of link j wrt i, using $I_{ij}$ | Single SC suffices | $\omega_{j/i} = v_{P\,rel} / r$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: IC Method Velocity Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mechanism Diagram Given] --> B[Identify All Links: Ground 1, Input 2, Coupler 3, Output 4]
    B --> C[Count ICs using N = n n-1 / 2]
    C --> D{N equals 6?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark 4 Primary ICs at Joints]
    D -- No --> F[Special Case: Reduce by Joint Type]
    E --> G[Apply Kennedy Theorem to find Secondary ICs]
    G --> H[Locate I13: Coupler wrt Ground]
    G --> I[Locate I24: Input wrt Output]
    H --> J[Compute vB from Input Angular Velocity]
    J --> K[Compute Coupler Angular Velocity omega3 = vB divided by rB to I13]
    K --> L[Compute Velocity of Required Point vP = omega3 times rP to I13]
    L --> M[Compute Output Angular Velocity omega4 = vC divided by rC to I14]
    M --> N[Direction: Perpendicular to IP line, in sense of omega]
    N --> O[End: Full Velocity Report]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: IC Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Instantaneous Centres] --> B[Primary PCs]
    A --> C[Secondary SCs]
    A --> D[Tertiary TCs - Rare in 2D]
    B --> B1[At Revolute Joint: Pin Location]
    B --> B2[At Rolling Pair: Contact Point]
    B --> B3[At Sliding Pair: At Infinity]
    C --> C1[Found via Kennedy Theorem]
    C --> C2[Intersection of two lines through known PCs]
    C --> C3[Must lie on link or its extension]
```

### 4.3 Four-Bar Mechanism IC Identification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Link 1: Ground AD] ---|PC at A| P12["I12"]
    L1 ---|PC at D| P14["I14"]
    L2[Link 2: Crank AB] ---|PC at B| P23["I23"]
    L3[Link 3: Coupler BC] ---|PC at C| P34["I34"]
    L4[Link 4: Rocker CD]
    P12 -.->|Kennedy: line AB| SC1["I13 at AB-CD intersection"]
    P14 -.->|Kennedy: line DC| SC1
    P12 -.->|Kennedy: line AD| SC2["I24 at AD-BC intersection"]
    P23 -.->|Kennedy: line BC| SC2
```

### 4.4 Velocity Computation Flow for Coupler Points

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Input
    participant P as Process
    participant O as Output
    I->>P: Crank Length L2, omega2
    P->>P: vB = omega2 times L2
    P->>P: Locate I13 by Kennedy Theorem
    P->>P: r_B_to_I13 measured geometrically
    P->>P: omega3 = vB / r_B_to_I13
    P->>O: vE_point = omega3 times r_point_to_I13
    O->>P: vC for rocker
    P->>P: omega4 = vC / L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the student:** When drawing mechanism diagrams during the KTU exam, use a pencil, ruler, and compass. Mark all 6 ICs with small circles and label them $I_{12}$, $I_{13}$, etc. Use a **dashed line** for Kennedy construction lines and a **solid line** for the actual mechanism links. This clear visual distinction earns full marks in the diagram component.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Instantaneous Centre of Rotation." How is the total number of instantaneous centres determined for a mechanism with `n` links?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **Instantaneous Centre (IC)** is a point on a rigid body (or its extension) that has zero linear velocity at a given instant. The entire body appears to rotate about this point at that instant. **(1.5 marks)**

The total number of ICs for a mechanism with `n` links is:

$$N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

This follows from the number of unique pairs of links that can be formed from `n` links. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State **Kennedy's Theorem of Three Centres**. Why is it essential in instantaneous centre analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Statement:** When three rigid bodies move relative to one another in plane motion, their three instantaneous centres are **collinear** (lie on a straight line). **(2 marks)**

**Importance:** It is the only systematic method to locate the **secondary (non-joint) instantaneous centres**, which are essential for the velocity analysis of complex mechanisms such as the four-bar and slider-crank. Without it, the IC method cannot be applied to find the coupler IC. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: Four-Bar Mechanism IC Analysis** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** In a four-bar mechanism ABCD, the fixed link AD is 280 mm, crank AB is 90 mm, coupler BC is 220 mm, and rocker CD is 180 mm. The crank rotates at a uniform angular velocity of 18 rad/s counter-clockwise.
