100°) = \cos(-10°) = 0.9848$$ $$-0.1736\,K_1 - 0\,K_2 + K_3 = 0.9848 \quad (2)$$ **Position 3** ($\theta_2 = 120°$, $\theta_4 = 130°$): $$K_1\cos 130° - K_2\cos 120° + K_3 = \cos(120° - 130°) = \cos(-10°) = 0.9848$$ $$-0.6428\,K_1 + 0.5000\,K_2 + K_3 = 0.9848 \quad (3)$$ **Step 2: Eliminate $K_3$.** $(1) - (2)$: $0.3472\,K_1 - 0.5000\,K_2 = -0.0451 \quad (4)$ $(3) - (2)$: $-0.4692\,K_1 + 0.5000\,K_2 = 0.0000 \quad (5)$ **Step 3: Solve.** From (5): $K_2 = 0.9384\,K_1$ Substitute into (4): $$0.3472\,K_1 - 0.5000(0.9384\,K_1) = -0.0451$$ $$0.3472\,K_1 - 0.4692\,K_1 = -0.0451$$ $$-0.1220\,K_1 = -0.0451$$ $$K_1 = 0.3697$$ $$K_2 = 0.9384 \times 0.3697 = 0.3469$$ **Step 4: Compute $K_3$ from (2).** $$K_3 = 0.9848 + 0.1736 \times 0.3697 = 0.9848 + 0.0642 = 1.0490$$ **Step 5: Recover link lengths with $d = 1$.** $$a = \frac{1}{K_1} = \frac{1}{0.3697} = 2.7049$$ $$c = \frac{1}{K_2} = \frac{1}{0.3469} = 2.8826$$ For $b$: $$K_3 = \frac{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2}{2ac}$$ $$b^2 = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2acK_3$$ $$b^2 = (2.7049)^2 + (2.8826)^2 + 1^2 - 2(2.7049)(2.8826)(1.0490)$$ $$b^2 = 7.3165 + 8.3094 + 1 - 16.3554 = 0.2705$$ $$b = 0.5201$$ **Step 6: Final link lengths.** $$\boxed{\;a = 2.705, \quad b = 0.520, \quad c = 2.883, \quad d = 1.000\;}$$ **Step 7: Grashof Check.** | Link | Length | Rank | |---|---|---| | $b$ | 0.520 | **Shortest** | | $d$ | 1.000 | — | | $a$ | 2.705 | — | | $c$ | 2.883 | **Longest** | $$s + \ell = 0.520 + 2.883 = 3.403$$ $$p + q = 1.000 + 2.705 = 3.705$$ $$s + \ell = 3.403 \;\le\; p + q = 3.705 \;\;\checkmark$$ > **Grashof's condition is satisfied.** The shortest link $b$ is the **coupler** — this is a *double-crank* (drag-link) mechanism, where both input and output links can complete full rotations. **Step 8: Transmission Angle Check.** $$\cos\mu = \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2}{2bc} = \frac{0.2705 + 8.3094 - 7.3165 - 1}{2(0.5201)(2.8826)}$$ $$\cos\mu = \frac{0.2634}{2.9989} = 0.0878 \;\Rightarrow\; \mu = 84.96°$$ > $40° \le \mu = 84.96° \le 140°$ ✓ — **Good force transmission.** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize List for KTU Module 3 Exam** - [ ] **Three synthesis types** — function (input→output angle), path (point traces curve), body (coupler pose). Always name them in Part A answers. - [ ] **Precision point** = exact match position; **structural error** = deviation between precision points. - [ ] **Freudenstein's Equation** (memorize in this exact form): $K_1\cos\theta_4 - K_2\cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$. - [ ] **Constant definitions** (do not mix up!): $K_1 = d/a$, $K_2 = d/c$, $K_3 = (a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2)/(2ac)$. - [ ] **Three-position procedure**: Write 3 equations → subtract to eliminate $K_3$ → solve 2×2 linear system → recover $K_3$ → choose $d$ → compute $a, c, b$. - [ ] **Chebyshev spacing** for $n$ points: $\theta_i = \theta_s + \frac{\theta_e - \theta_s}{2}[1 + \cos((2i-1)\pi/2n)]$. - [ ] **Grashof's Law** check: $s + \ell \le p + q$. If not satisfied, redesign (either change the precision points or add a slider). - [ ] **Transmission angle** $40° \le \mu \le 140°$ for good force transmission. $\cos\mu = (b^2 + c^2 - a^2 - d^2)/(2bc)$. - [ ] **Burmester theory** is for path generation and body guidance. For 2 positions: 4 loci (2 center points + 2 circle points). For 3: Burmester curves. For 4: a Burmester point. For 5+: no exact 4-bar solution. - [ ] **Loop-closure equation** is the foundation: $a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = d + c\cos\theta_4$ and $a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4$. - [ ] **Coupler-tracer point** in path generation: a single point on link 3 traces the desired curve. Two extra design parameters (its location) bring total DOF to 6. - [ ] **Degrees of freedom check** — the four-bar has 4 link lengths as design variables → 3 precision points in function generation is solvable; 4–5 in theory but requires non-linear methods. - [ ] **Units**: All Freudenstein constants are **dimensionless**. The scale is set by choosing $d$ (or any one link). - [ ] **Sign convention**: $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$ are measured **CCW from $O_2O_4$** and **CCW from $O_4O_2$** respectively. Always draw the diagram to clarify. > **Time Management Tip for KTU 14-Mark Questions:** > 1. (~3 min) State the equation and unknowns. > 2. (~3 min) Substitute the three precision points; form the system. > 3. (~3 min) Solve the 2×2 system for $K_1, K_2$. > 4. (~2 min) Find $K_3$ from any one equation. > 5. (~2 min) Recover $a, b, c, d$ and verify Grashof + transmission angle. > 6. (~1 min) Final answer box + units.

topics.title: Precision Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Precision Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Precision Points (KTU 2024 Standard Definition)**
> Precision points are the **specific, discrete instants (cam angles $\theta$ or input positions)** in the motion cycle of a cam-follower system (or any function-generating mechanism) at which the **generated displacement, velocity, and acceleration exactly match the designer-specified values**. They are the mathematical anchor positions where the polynomial cam function is forced to pass through prescribed boundary values.

In polynomial cam design, each precision point imposes one **boundary condition** on the polynomial. A polynomial of degree $n$ has $n+1$ arbitrary coefficients, and therefore can satisfy exactly $n+1$ precision points.

### 1.2 Two Categories of Precision Points

1. **Mandatory Boundary Precision Points** — fixed at the start ($\theta = 0$) and end ($\theta = \beta$) of every motion segment. Six standard boundary conditions exist for a smooth dwell-to-dwell rise:
   - $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = 0$, $y''(0) = 0$
   - $y(\beta) = h$, $y'(\beta) = 0$, $y''(\beta) = 0$
2. **Optional Internal Precision Points** — placed inside the motion segment when additional shape control is required (e.g., matching cycloidal segments or imposing midpoint constraints). These are positioned using **Chebyshev spacing** for optimal accuracy.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Mountain Trail" Concept**
> Imagine you must draw a smooth mountain profile (the cam) so that a traveller (the follower) reaches **specific elevations at specific times** (precision points). If you have only **2 checkpoints** (start and end of the climb), a simple curve works. With **6 checkpoints** (3 at start: position, velocity, acceleration = 0; 3 at end: position = $h$, velocity, acceleration = 0), you must use a higher-order polynomial — the famous **3-4-5 polynomial**. The "spaced out" checkpoints follow a special rule called **Chebyshev spacing** to minimize the worst error between your drawn curve and the ideal path. The number of checkpoints you choose directly dictates the polynomial you must use.

### 1.4 Key Constants and Metrics

- Standard cam rotation speeds for design problems: **100 to 1000 rpm**.
- Typical follower lift ($h$): **10 mm to 100 mm**.
- Boundary conditions are always satisfied to **zero order of accuracy** at the endpoints — any deviation is permitted only **inside** the motion interval.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Precision Points Matter

The complete design of a polynomial cam proceeds as a **boundary value problem**:

1. **Choose the number of precision points** $P$ based on smoothness requirements.
2. **Select polynomial degree** $n = P - 1$ (since a degree-$n$ polynomial has $n+1$ coefficients).
3. **Impose boundary conditions** (zero velocity and acceleration at dwell transitions to avoid impact).
4. **Solve the linear system** for the polynomial coefficients.
5. **Place internal precision points at Chebyshev nodes** if extra shape control is needed.

### 2.2 The Chebyshev Criterion for Internal Precision Points

> [!IMPORTANT] **Chebyshev's Spacing Theorem**
> Given $n$ internal precision points in a normalised interval $[0, 1]$, the placement that **minimises the maximum deviation** of the approximating polynomial from the desired function is given by the Chebyshev nodes:
> $$\boxed{\;x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2k - 1}{2n}\pi\right)\right],\quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n\;}$$
> These correspond to the **extrema of the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind** $T_{n}(x) = \cos(n \arccos x)$ mapped into the cam interval.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

**Table 2.A — Standard Cam Motion Profiles and Their Precision Point Counts**

| Motion Profile | Displacement Equation (for rise $h$ in angle $\beta$) | No. of Precision Points |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** | $y = h\!\left[\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | **2** (endpoints only) |
| **Cycloidal Motion** | $y = h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | **2** (endpoints only) |
| **Parabolic (Uniform Acceleration)** | $y = 2h\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{2}$ for $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$ | **2** (endpoint + midpoint kink) |
| **3-4-5 Polynomial** | $y = h\!\left[10\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{3} - 15\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{4} + 6\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{5}\right]$ | **6** (3 start + 3 end) |
| **4-5-6-7 Polynomial** | $y = h\!\left[35 x^{4} - 84 x^{5} + 70 x^{6} - 20 x^{7}\right]$, $x = \theta/\beta$ | **8** (4 start + 4 end) |

**Table 2.B — Velocity and Acceleration Magnitudes (normalised)**

| Motion | $\left(\dfrac{dy/dx}{h}\right)_{\max}$ | $\left(\dfrac{d^{2}y/dx^{2}}{h}\right)_{\max}$ | $\dfrac{v_{\max} \cdot \beta}{h \cdot \omega}$ | $\dfrac{a_{\max} \cdot \beta^{2}}{h \cdot \omega^{2}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SHM | $1.571$ ($\pi/2$) | $4.934$ ($\pi^{2}/2$) | $1.571$ | $4.934$ |
| Cycloidal | $2.000$ | $6.283$ ($2\pi$) | $2.000$ | $6.283$ |
| Parabolic | $2.000$ (at mid) | $4.000$ | $2.000$ | $4.000$ |
| **3-4-5 Polynomial** | $1.875$ | $5.770$ | $1.875$ | $5.770$ |
| 4-5-6-7 Polynomial | $1.760$ | $5.413$ | $1.760$ | $5.413$ |

**Table 2.C — Standard Boundary Conditions Imposed as Precision Points**

| Boundary | Displacement | Velocity | Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Start of rise** ($\theta = 0$) | $y = 0$ | $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = 0$ | $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} = 0$ |
| **End of rise** ($\theta = \beta$) | $y = h$ | $\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} = 0$ | $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} = 0$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine camshafts** use 3-4-5 or 4-5-6-7 polynomials for smooth valve operation.
- **High-speed printing presses** demand minimal acceleration peaks, favouring 4-5-6-7 polynomials.
- **Packaging machinery** often uses cycloidal motion for moderate speed with no abrupt velocity changes.
- **Robotic finger actuators** use precision-point matching to coordinate multi-link motion trajectories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-4-5 Polynomial via Six Precision Points

**Problem:** A cam follower must rise by $h$ during cam angle $\beta$ with **zero velocity and zero acceleration at both endpoints** (i.e., 6 boundary precision points). Determine the polynomial of minimum degree that satisfies all six.

**Step 1 — General Polynomial Form**

Let $x = \theta/\beta$, so $x \in [0, 1]$. The most general degree-5 polynomial is:

$$y(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2} + a_3 x^{3} + a_4 x^{4} + a_5 x^{5}$$

This polynomial has **6 unknown coefficients**, matching exactly the 6 precision points.

**Step 2 — First Three Precision Points (at $x = 0$)**

The first three boundary conditions require $y = 0$, $y' = 0$, $y'' = 0$ at $x = 0$:

- **Condition 1** — Position: $y(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_0 = 0$
- **Condition 2** — Velocity: $y'(0) = a_1 = 0$
- **Condition 3** — Acceleration: $y''(0) = 2 a_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 = 0$

**Step 3 — Three Remaining Coefficients from End Precision Points (at $x = 1$)**

Substituting the three known zeros $a_0 = a_1 = a_2 = 0$, the polynomial simplifies to:

$$y(x) = a_3 x^{3} + a_4 x^{4} + a_5 x^{5}$$

Computing the derivatives:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 3 a_3 x^{2} + 4 a_4 x^{3} + 5 a_5 x^{4}$$

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = 6 a_3 x + 12 a_4 x^{2} + 20 a_5 x^{3}$$

Applying the three endpoint precision points at $x = 1$:

- **Condition 4** — Position: $a_3 + a_4 + a_5 = h$
- **Condition 5** — Velocity: $3 a_3 + 4 a_4 + 5 a_5 = 0$
- **Condition 6** — Acceleration: $6 a_3 + 12 a_4 + 20 a_5 = 0$

**Step 4 — Solving the Linear System**

The augmented matrix is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & h \\
3 & 4 & 5 & 0 \\
6 & 12 & 20 & 0
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Row Operation 1:** $R_2 \to R_2 - 3 R_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & h \\
0 & 1 & 2 & -3h \\
6 & 12 & 20 & 0
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Row Operation 2:** $R_3 \to R_3 - 6 R_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & h \\
0 & 1 & 2 & -3h \\
0 & 6 & 14 & -6h
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Row Operation 3:** $R_3 \to R_3 - 6 R_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & h \\
0 & 1 & 2 & -3h \\
0 & 0 & 2 & 12h
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Back-substitution:**

- $2 a_5 = 12h \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{a_5 = 6h}$
- $a_4 + 2(6h) = -3h \;\Rightarrow\; a_4 = -3h - 12h = \boxed{-15h}$
- $a_3 + (-15h) + 6h = h \;\Rightarrow\; a_3 = h + 9h = \boxed{10h}$

**Step 5 — Final Polynomial (3-4-5 Motion)**

$$\boxed{\;y(\theta) = h\left[10\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{3} - 15\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{4} + 6\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^{5}\right]\;}$$

The exponents 3, 4, 5 give the motion its name.

**Step 6 — Verification of All Six Precision Points**

At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ ✓, $y' = 30h(0)^2(1-0)^2 = 0$ ✓, $y'' = 60h(0)(1-0)(1-0) = 0$ ✓
At $x = 1$: $y = 10-15+6 = 1$ → $h$ ✓, $y' = 30h(1)(0) = 0$ ✓, $y'' = 60h(1)(0)(-1) = 0$ ✓

**Step 7 — Maximum Velocity and Acceleration Locations**

Set $y''(x) = 60 h x (1-x)(1-2x) = 0$ for $x \ne 0, 1$:

$$1 - 2x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; y'(0.5) = 30h(0.25)(0.25) = 1.875 h$$

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\max} = 1.875 h$$

Set $y'''(x) = 60h(1 - 6x + 6x^2) = 0$:

$$x = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{12} = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{3}}{12} = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{3}}{6}$$

$$x_1 = 0.2113, \quad x_2 = 0.7887$$

$$\left(\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\right)_{\max} = 5.770 h \text{ (at } x = 0.2113 \text{)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Chebyshev Spacing for Three Internal Precision Points

**Problem:** For a polynomial of degree 5 (the 3-4-5 polynomial) determine the optimal locations of **three internal precision points** that minimise the worst-case deviation.

**Step 1 — Chebyshev Polynomial Extrema**

The Chebyshev polynomial of degree $n$ in $[-1, 1]$ is $T_n(\xi) = \cos(n \arccos \xi)$. It has $n+1$ extrema at:

$$\xi_k = \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 — Map $[-1, 1]$ to $[0, 1]$**

The linear map $x = \frac{1 + \xi}{2}$ transforms the extrema locations to:

$$x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{k\pi}{n}\right)\right], \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n$$

**Step 3 — Apply for $n = 3$ Internal Points (excluding endpoints $k=0$ and $k=n$)**

For $k = 1, 2, 3$:

$$x_1 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + 0.5\right] = 0.750$$

$$x_2 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - 0.5\right] = 0.250$$

$$x_3 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos(\pi)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - 1\right] = 0.000 \text{ (degenerate)}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Standard Form (Most Widely Used)**

By reflecting about the midpoint and using the alternate extrema of $T_{n+1}$:

$$x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

For $n = 3$:

$$x_1 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.866) = 0.067$$

$$x_2 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{6}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0) = 0.500$$

$$x_3 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 + 0.866) = 0.933$$

**Step 5 — Map Back to Cam Angles**

If the rise angle is $\beta$ (in degrees or radians):

$$\boxed{\;\theta_k = \beta \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right]\;}$$

These three precision points are **clustered near the endpoints** and **spread at the middle**, a hallmark of Chebyshev spacing that suppresses oscillation in the polynomial interpolation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — 3-4-5 Polynomial with Chebyshev Precision Points

**Given:** Cam speed $N = 200$ rpm, rise $h = 30$ mm, rise angle $\beta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, motion is 3-4-5 polynomial.

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (200)}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Velocity**

$$v_{\max} = \omega \cdot \frac{h}{\beta} \cdot \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\max} = (20.944)\left(\frac{0.030}{\pi/3}\right)(1.875)$$

$$\frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{20.944}{1.0472} = 20.00 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$v_{\max} = 20.00 \times 0.030 \times 1.875 = 1.125 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Acceleration**

$$a_{\max} = \omega^{2} \cdot \frac{h}{\beta^{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\right)_{\max} = (20.944)^{2}\left(\frac{0.030}{(\pi/3)^{2}}\right)(5.770)$$

$$a_{\max} = 438.65 \times 0.0274 \times 5.770 = 69.30 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Chebyshev Precision Point Angles (for $n = 3$)**

$$\theta_1 = 60° \times 0.067 = 4.02°$$

$$\theta_2 = 60° \times 0.500 = 30.00°$$

$$\theta_3 = 60° \times 0.933 = 55.98°$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Precision Point Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Cam Motion Requirements] --> B{Number of Precision Points P}
    B -->|P = 2| C1[Use Simple Harmonic or Cycloidal]
    B -->|P = 3| C2[Use Parabolic or 3-Point Bezier]
    B -->|P = 6| C3[Use 3-4-5 Polynomial]
    B -->|P = 8| C4[Use 4-5-6-7 Polynomial]
    B -->|P = 10| C5[Use 5-6-7-8-9 Polynomial]
    
    C1 --> D1[Place endpoints only at x = 0 and x = 1]
    C2 --> D2[Add midpoint precision point]
    C3 --> D3[Apply 6 boundary conditions: y, dy, d2y = 0 at endpoints]
    C4 --> D4[Apply 8 conditions: 4 at each endpoint]
    
    D3 --> E{Internal Precision Points Needed?}
    E -->|No| F1[Polynomial ready for cam profile generation]
    E -->|Yes| F2[Use Chebyshev Spacing]
    
    F2 --> G[Compute x_k using Chebyshev formula]
    G --> H[Convert x_k to cam angles theta_k]
    H --> F1
    
    F1 --> I[Check v_max and a_max for dynamic limits]
    I --> J{Within limits?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Design]
    J -->|No| L[Increase P or revise profile]
    L --> B
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Cam Boundary Precision Points

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Dwell Region: y = 0] -->|Precision 1: y = 0| B[Start of Rise at theta = 0]
    B -->|Precision 2: dy/dtheta = 0| C[Zero velocity condition]
    C -->|Precision 3: d2y/dtheta2 = 0| D[Zero acceleration condition]
    D -->|Polynomial rise segment| E[Midpoint: theta = beta/2]
    E -->|Polynomial continues| F[End of Rise at theta = beta]
    F -->|Precision 4: y = h| G[Lift achieved]
    G -->|Precision 5: dy/dtheta = 0| H[Zero velocity at top]
    H -->|Precision 6: d2y/dtheta2 = 0| I[Zero acceleration at top]
    I --> J[Dwell Region: y = h]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Precision Point Matching in Function Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Function]
        IN1[Desired Position vs Time]
        IN2[Desired Velocity Profile]
        IN3[Desired Acceleration Profile]
    end
    
    subgraph PP[Precision Point Extractor]
        P1[Identify boundary conditions]
        P2[Apply Chebyshev spacing for internal points]
        P3[Generate set of N precision points]
    end
    
    subgraph Solver[Polynomial Solver]
        S1[Set up coefficient matrix A]
        S2[Apply boundary vector b]
        S3[Solve A * c = b for c]
    end
    
    subgraph Output[Cam Profile Output]
        O1[Displacement y theta]
        O2[Velocity v theta]
        O3[Acceleration a theta]
    end
    
    IN1 --> P1
    IN2 --> P1
    IN3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
    S3 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Standard Motion Profiles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Motion Profile Selection] --> B1[SHM: 2 Precision Points]
    A --> B2[Cycloidal: 2 Precision Points]
    A --> B3[Parabolic: 2 + 1 Inflection]
    A --> B4[3-4-5 Polynomial: 6 Precision Points]
    A --> B5[4-5-6-7: 8 Precision Points]
    
    B1 --> C1[Lowest v_max: 1.571]
    B2 --> C2[Moderate: v_max = 2.0]
    B3 --> C3[Discontinuous accel at midpoint]
    B4 --> C4[Smoothest in mid-range: v_max = 1.875]
    B5 --> C5[Best smoothness: v_max = 1.760]
    
    C1 --> D1[Best for slow cams]
    C2 --> D2[Best for high speed]
    C3 --> D3[Best for constant accel]
    C4 --> D4[Best for IC engines]
    C5 --> D5[Best for high speed printing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

Define the term **"Precision Points"** in the context of cam design. How does the number of precision points determine the degree of the polynomial used?

**Model Answer:**

Precision points are the specific cam angles (positions) at which the generated displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the follower exactly match the designer's specified values. Each precision point imposes one boundary condition on the cam polynomial.

A polynomial of degree $n$ has $n+1$ arbitrary coefficients, and hence can satisfy exactly $n+1$ precision points. Therefore:
- **2 precision points** → linear / SHM / cycloidal (lowest acceptable)
- **6 precision points** → 3-4-5 polynomial (most common for IC engine cams)
- **8 precision points** → 4-5-6-7 polynomial (high-speed applications)

The number is increased whenever smoother motion (lower peak acceleration) is required, at the cost of higher polynomial degree and greater computational complexity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

State and explain **Chebyshev's spacing criterion** for the placement of internal precision points in cam design.

**Model Answer:**

Chebyshev's criterion states that the $n$ internal precision points (excluding the fixed endpoints) in a normalised cam interval $[0, 1]$ must be placed at locations that minimise the maximum deviation between the approximating polynomial and the desired function. These optimal locations are the **Chebyshev nodes**:

$$x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

These points are clustered near the endpoints and spread at the centre. This non-uniform spacing suppresses the **Runge oscillation** phenomenon, ensuring that even a moderate-degree polynomial approximates the desired cam function with the smallest possible maximum error.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Model)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cam rotating uniformly at **200 rpm** drives a knife-edge follower to rise by **40 mm** in **$90°$** of cam rotation following the **3-4-5 polynomial** motion. Calculate:
1. The angular positions of the three Chebyshev precision points inside the rise interval.
2. The maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of the follower during the rise.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how the **six boundary precision points** at the start and end of the rise interval are used to derive the 3-4-5 polynomial equation. List the standard boundary conditions in a tabular form.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Given Data:**
- $N = 200$ rpm, $h = 40$ mm = 0.040 m, $\beta = 90° = \pi/2$ rad
- Motion: 3-4-5 polynomial

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity and Conversion Factor**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{20.944}{\pi/2} = \frac{20.944}{1.5708} = 13.333 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Chebyshev Precision Point Angles (for $n = 3$)**

Using $x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{6}\right)\right]$:

$$x_1 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos(30°)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.8660) = 0.0670$$

$$x_2 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos(90°)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0) = 0.5000$$

$$x_3 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos(150°)\right] = \frac{1}{2}(1 + 0.8660) = 0.9330$$

**Corresponding cam angles:**

$$\theta_1 = 90° \times 0.0670 = 6.03°$$

$$\theta_2 = 90° \times 0.5000 = 45.00°$$

$$\theta_3 = 90° \times 0.9330 = 83.97°$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Velocity**

$$v_{\max} = 1.875 \times h \times \frac{\omega}{\beta} = 1.875 \times 0.040 \times 13.333 = 1.000 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Acceleration**

$$a_{\max} = 5.770 \times h \times \left(\frac{\omega}{\beta}\right)^{2} = 5.770 \times 0.040 \times (13.333)^{2}$$

$$a_{\max} = 5.770 \times 0.040 \times 177.77 = 41.03 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Final Answer:** $v_{\max} = 1.000$ m/s, $a_{\max} = 41.03$ m/s², with precision points at $\theta = 6.03°$, $45.00°$, and $83.97°$.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

The 3-4-5 polynomial must satisfy **six boundary precision points** — three at the start ($x = 0$) and three at the end ($x = 1$) of the rise interval, where $x = \theta/\beta$.

| # | Precision Point Location | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = 0$ (start) | Zero lift at start of rise | $y(0) = 0$ |
| 2 | $x = 0$ (start) | Zero velocity to avoid impact | $y'(0) = 0$ |
| 3 | $x = 0$ (start) | Zero acceleration for smooth start | $y''(0) = 0$ |
| 4 | $x = 1$ (end) | Full lift $h$ achieved | $y(1) = h$ |
| 5 | $x = 1$ (end) | Zero velocity at top dwell | $y'(1) = 0$ |
| 6 | $x = 1$ (end) | Zero acceleration for smooth end | $y''(1) = 0$ |

Assume the most general degree-5 polynomial:

$$y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^{2} + a_3 x^{3} + a_4 x^{4} + a_5 x^{5}$$

Conditions 1, 2, 3 yield $a_0 = a_1 = a_2 = 0$. Substituting into the remaining three conditions:

$$a_3 + a_4 + a_5 = h$$
$$3 a_3 + 4 a_4 + 5 a_5 = 0$$
$$6 a_3 + 12 a_4 + 20 a_5 = 0$$

Solving this linear system by elimination (as shown in Section 3.1) gives:

$$a_3 = 10h, \quad a_4 = -15h, \quad a_5 = 6h$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{y = h\left[10x^{3} - 15x^{4} + 6x^{5}\right], \quad x = \frac{\theta}{\beta}}$$

This is the **3-4-5 polynomial**, whose name comes from the exponents of $x$. It satisfies all six boundary precision points exactly, ensuring smooth dwell-to-dwell motion of the follower.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cam rotating at **300 rpm** gives a rise of **30 mm** in **$60°$** with simple harmonic motion followed by a return of **30 mm** in **$90°$** with **3-4-5 polynomial** motion. Determine the maximum velocity and acceleration during **both** the rise and the return. Compare which phase is more critical from a dynamic-load viewpoint.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of neat sketches, compare the **displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams** for **Simple Harmonic Motion**, **Cycloidal Motion**, and **3-4-5 Polynomial** motion. State at least **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using the 3-4-5 polynomial over SHM.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Rise (SHM) | Return (3-4-5) |
|---|---|---|
| Lift $h$ | 30 mm = 0.030 m | 30 mm = 0.030 m |
| Angle $\beta$ | 60° = $\pi/3$ rad | 90° = $\pi/2$ rad |
| Motion | SHM | 3-4-5 Polynomial |
| Peak $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\max}$ | $1.571$ | $1.875$ |
| Peak $\left(\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\right)_{\max}$ | $4.934$ | $5.770$ |

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Rise (SHM) Dynamics**

$$\frac{\omega}{\beta_{\text{rise}}} = \frac{31.416}{\pi/3} = 30.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$v_{\max,\text{rise}} = 1.571 \times 0.030 \times 30.0 = 1.414 \text{ m/s}$$

$$a_{\max,\text{rise}} = 4.934 \times 0.030 \times (30.0)^{2} = 133.2 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Return (3-4-5 Polynomial) Dynamics**

$$\frac{\omega}{\beta_{\text{return}}} = \frac{31.416}{\pi/2} = 20.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$v_{\max,\text{return}} = 1.875 \times 0.030 \times 20.0 = 1.125 \text{ m/s}$$

$$a_{\max,\text{return}} = 5.770 \times 0.030 \times (20.0)^{2} = 69.24 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison**

| Phase | $v_{\max}$ (m/s) | $a_{\max}$ (m/s²) |
|---|---|---|
| Rise (SHM, 60°) | **1.414** | **133.2** |
| Return (3-4-5, 90°) | 1.125 | 69.24 |

**Conclusion:** The **rise phase (SHM)** is more critical for dynamic loading because although its peak velocity is only ~26% higher, its peak acceleration is **nearly twice** that of the return phase. The smaller rise angle concentrates the motion in a shorter time, drastically increasing the inertial forces. Hence, in practice, designers prefer 3-4-5 polynomial for **both** rise and return to keep peak accelerations low.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Qualitative Comparison of the Three Motion Profiles:**

| Property | SHM | Cycloidal | 3-4-5 Polynomial |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak velocity coefficient | $1.571$ (lowest) | $2.000$ | $1.875$ |
| Peak acceleration coefficient | $4.934$ (lowest) | $6.283$ (highest) | $5.770$ |
| Velocity continuity at ends | Smooth | Smooth | Smooth |
| Acceleration continuity at ends | Discontinuous (jerk exists) | Continuous | Continuous |
| Internal jerk | Finite | Zero (infinite smoothness) | Finite (minimal) |
| Mid-rise velocity | $v_{\max}$ (at $\beta/2$) | $v_{\max}$ (at $\beta/2$) | $v_{\max}$ (at $\beta/2$) |
| Suitability | Low-speed cams | High-speed precision | IC engine valves |

**Two Advantages of 3-4-5 Polynomial over SHM:**

1. **Continuous acceleration at endpoints** — Both $a(0) = 0$ and $a(\beta) = 0$, whereas SHM has finite acceleration jumps at the start and end, producing undesirable **infinite jerk** in theory. This makes 3-4-5 polynomial **much smoother** for high-speed applications like IC engine valves.

2. **Configurable peak velocity** — By using a higher-degree polynomial, the peak velocity can be reduced (e.g., 4-5-6-7 gives 1.760, lower than SHM's 1.571... wait, SHM is lower; correction: 3-4-5 gives *moderate* velocity with very low peak acceleration, ideal balance).

**Two Disadvantages of 3-4-5 Polynomial over SHM:**

1. **Higher computational complexity** — Solving a 6×6 linear system instead of a 1-step cosine formula; manual calculations are tedious and error-prone.
2. **Slightly higher peak velocity** — 1.875 vs. 1.571, which may marginally increase spring/follower mass inertia effects.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Chebyshev nodes with equally-spaced points.** Students often use $\theta_k = k \beta / (n+1)$ (equal spacing). The KTU answer key specifically demands the **cosine-based** Chebyshev formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\omega/\beta$ factor in velocity and $\omega^{2}/\beta^{2}$ in acceleration.** Many students compute only the dimensionless derivatives $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)$ and stop, losing **3 to 4 marks**.
> 3. **Sign errors in the 3-4-5 polynomial** (using $+15$ instead of $-15$ for the $x^4$ term). This is the most common algebraic slip in KTU exam scripts.
> 4. **Not verifying the boundary precision points** at the end of the derivation. Examiners award partial marks for showing the verification step $y(1) = h$ explicitly.
> 5. **Mixing up SHM and cycloidal peak acceleration values** ($4.934$ vs. $6.283$). Remember: **cycloidal has the highest** peak acceleration of the standard profiles.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final Summary — Master Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Definition:** Precision points are discrete cam positions where the generated motion matches the desired motion exactly; they are the **boundary conditions** of the cam polynomial.
- **Polynomial degree rule:** $n + 1$ precision points require a polynomial of degree $n$.
- **Mandatory boundary conditions** for any smooth rise: $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = 0$, $y''(0) = 0$, $y(\beta) = h$, $y'(\beta) = 0$, $y''(\beta) = 0$ — these are **6 precision points**.
- **3-4-5 polynomial equation (memorise this):**
  $$y = h\left[10x^{3} - 15x^{4} + 6x^{5}\right], \quad x = \frac{\theta}{\beta}$$
- **Peak velocity of 3-4-5:** $v_{\max} = 1.875 \, h \omega / \beta$ at $x = 0.5$.
- **Peak acceleration of 3-4-5:** $a_{\max} = 5.770 \, h \omega^{2} / \beta^{2}$ at $x = 0.211$ and $0.789$.
- **Chebyshev spacing formula (must memorise):**
  $$x_k = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
- **Chebyshev spacing property:** Points cluster near the endpoints and spread at the middle — opposite of equal spacing — to **minimise Runge oscillation**.
- **Conversion factors to remember:** $v = (dy/d\theta)\omega = (dy/dx)(\omega/\beta)$; $a = (d^{2}y/d\theta^{2})\omega^{2} = (d^{2}y/dx^{2})(\omega/\beta)^{2}$.
- **Profile selection guide:** Use SHM for low-speed, Cycloidal for high-speed, 3-4-5 for IC engines, 4-5-6-7 for very high-speed precision.
- **Boundary velocity and acceleration MUST be zero** at dwell-to-rise and rise-to-dwell transitions to prevent follower jump and impact loading.
- **Standard rpm to rad/s conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
- **Typical exam trap:** Examiners expect the **complete solution of the linear system** (or at least the matrix setup) when deriving a polynomial — do not skip the algebraic steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chebychev Spacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chebychev Spacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Chebychev Spacing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chebychev Spacing** is a mathematical criterion, derived from **Chebychev's Theorem of Polynomial Approximation**, used in the kinematic design of cams and followers. It prescribes the optimal angular positions of a cam (i.e., the **spacing of the rise/fall matching points**) such that the **maximum deviation** between the actual follower displacement function and the desired displacement function is **minimized and equalized** over the entire interval of follower motion.

In simpler KTU-board language:

> [!NOTE]
> When a designer wishes to match a follower displacement curve to a mathematically defined function (e.g., a polynomial of degree *n*), the cam must pass through *n* exact matching points. The question is: **where on the cam angle should these *n* matching points be placed?** Chebychev Spacing provides the mathematically optimal answer by placing them at the **roots of the Chebychev Polynomial of degree *n***.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **landscape architect** asked to plant 5 trees along a curved garden path so that the path looks as close to a perfect smooth arc as possible. If you naively plant the trees at equal distances along the path, the gap between the trees and the true arc will be large in some regions and small in others — a lopsided approximation. 

**Chebychev's insight** is this: shift the trees away from the equal-spacing positions and bunch them slightly towards the *ends* of the path, while leaving the middle a bit more sparse. The result: the **maximum gap** between the trees and the true arc is now the **same everywhere** — a *minimax* (minimum of the maximum) approximation.

**Real-world engineering translation:**
- The **path** = the cam angle (θ) from 0 to β (the rise angle).
- The **trees** = the *n* points where the follower displacement is *forced* to exactly match the desired function.
- The **true arc** = the desired polynomial follower motion.
- The **gap** = the deviation between the actual cam profile and the desired motion.

By placing the matching points at the **roots of the Chebychev polynomial**, the **largest deviation anywhere along the rise is made as small as mathematically possible**, and all the *n+1* local maxima of the deviation are made **equal in magnitude**.

**Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics used:**

- **Chebychev Polynomial of the First Kind**: $T_n(x) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} x)$ — defined for $-1 \le x \le 1$.
- **Interval of Interest**: For cam design, the angular domain is mapped from $[0, \beta]$ (mechanical degrees) to the normalized domain $[0, 1]$, or equivalently to $[-1, +1]$.
- **Degree *n*** = the number of terms in the assumed follower displacement polynomial, equal to the number of matching points.

---

### Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module 3)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 3 typically covers Cam Design.** The Chebychev Spacing criterion is a *high-weightage* topic, often examined in Part B (14-mark) questions under "Cam Design with Polynomial Functions" or "Optimization of Cam Profiles." Expected cognitive levels: **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Mathematical Problem

Let the cam rotate through an angle $\beta$ during the rise of the follower. The desired follower displacement $S(\theta)$ is approximated by a polynomial of degree $n$ in the normalized variable $x$:

$$\phi(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_n x^n$$

where $x = \theta / \beta$ is the normalized cam angle (so $x$ ranges from 0 to 1).

The **error function** between the cam and the desired motion is:

$$E(x) = S(\theta) - \phi(x)$$

There are $n+1$ coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ and hence we can satisfy $n+1$ conditions — most commonly, the displacement and its derivatives at the start ($x=0$) and end ($x=1$) of the rise. This fixes **all** coefficients. The matching is *exact* at $n+1$ points if we choose to enforce the boundary conditions strictly. But often we relax this and **force exact matching at $n$ interior points** instead, leaving one degree of freedom to minimize the error.

**The Chebychev question:**
> *If we are free to choose where the $n$ interior matching points are placed, where should we place them to make the maximum error as small as possible?*

The answer comes from Chebychev's theorem.

---

## 2.2 Chebychev's Theorem (1859)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chebychev's Theorem:** Among all monic polynomials (leading coefficient = 1) of degree $n$ on the interval $[-1, +1]$, the polynomial
> $$\frac{T_n(x)}{2^{n-1}}$$
> has the **smallest maximum absolute value** (the *minimax* property). Equivalently, it has the smallest **uniform norm**:
> $$\left\| \frac{T_n(x)}{2^{n-1}} \right\|_{\infty} = \min_{p_n \text{ monic}} \left\| p_n(x) \right\|_{\infty} = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$$

In words: **no other polynomial of the same degree and leading coefficient can stay uniformly closer to zero over the entire interval**.

The Chebychev polynomial itself:

$$T_n(x) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} x)$$

It has the remarkable property that its **$n$ real roots** all lie in the open interval $(-1, 1)$ and it **oscillates** between $-1$ and $+1$ exactly $n+1$ times, attaining equal extreme values at the *Chebychev nodes*.

---

## 2.3 The Connection to Cam Design — Why It Works

For a cam rise approximated by an $n$-th degree polynomial whose coefficients are chosen to satisfy $n+1$ boundary conditions, the error function $E(x)$ is essentially a polynomial of degree $n$ with a specific leading coefficient. By Chebychev's theorem, the **maximum of $|E(x)|$ is minimized** if — and only if — $E(x)$ is proportional to $T_n(x)$ mapped onto the rise interval.

This occurs precisely when the $n$ interior matching points coincide with the **$n$ roots of the mapped Chebychev polynomial**. The result:
1. The maximum error is reduced by a factor of $2^{n-1}$ compared to equal spacing.
2. The $n+1$ local extrema of the error are **all equal in magnitude** (the *equalization of maxima* property).

---

## 2.4 Derivation of the Chebychev Spacing Formula

Let the rise angle be $\beta$, and the cam angle be $\theta \in [0, \beta]$. Define:

$$x = \frac{\theta}{\beta} \in [0, 1]$$

To apply $T_n$, we map $x \in [0, 1]$ to $u \in [-1, +1]$ via:

$$u = 2x - 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x = \frac{u + 1}{2}$$

The roots of $T_n(u)$ in $[-1, +1]$ are obtained by setting $T_n(u) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} u) = 0$, giving:

$$n \cos^{-1} u_k = \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_k = \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2n} \right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

Mapping back to the cam angle $\theta_k$:

$$x_k = \frac{u_k + 1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1 + \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2n} \right) \right]$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta_k = \frac{\beta}{2}\left[ 1 + \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2n} \right) \right], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n\;}$$

These are the **Chebychev spacing positions** on the cam.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the cosine argument. The points are **symmetrically bunched towards the ends** ($\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$) of the rise, leaving the middle region sparser — exactly the intuition of the landscape architect analogy.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Domain / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Chebychev Polynomial | $T_n(x) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} x)$ | $x \in [-1, +1]$ | Defined for integer $n \ge 0$ |
| 2 | Normalized Cam Angle | $x = \theta / \beta$ | dimensionless | $x \in [0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Mapping to $[-1,1]$ | $u = 2x - 1$ | dimensionless | Standard linear map |
| 4 | Roots of $T_n(u)$ | $u_k = \cos\!\left[ (2k-1)\pi / (2n) \right]$ | $k = 1, \ldots, n$ | $n$ real roots in $(-1, 1)$ |
| 5 | **Chebychev Spacing on Cam** | $\theta_k = (\beta/2)\{ 1 + \cos[(2k-1)\pi/(2n)]\}$ | **radians or degrees** | **THE master formula** |
| 6 | Equalized Maximum Error | $E_{\max} = h / 2^{n-1}$ | length units (m, mm) | $h$ = total rise; $n$ = polynomial degree |
| 7 | Minimax Property | $\vert E(\theta) \vert \le h / 2^{n-1}$ | length units | Smallest possible uniform bound |
| 8 | Number of Equal Maxima | $n + 1$ extrema | — | Between $n+1$ matching constraints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE (avoid KTU mark-loss):** In numerical substitution, use the **degree mode** of your calculator correctly: $\beta$ in **degrees** ⇒ $(2k-1)\pi/(2n)$ must be in **radians**. Many KTU toppers lose marks by mistakenly mixing degrees and radians in the cosine.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Chebychev Spacing |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| **High-speed cam profiles** (I.C. engine valves, automatic machines) | Minimizes residual vibration after polynomial cam segments are joined |
| **CNC machining interpolation** | Chebychev nodes used in polynomial interpolation of tool paths to reduce maximum contour error |
| **Robotics trajectory planning** | Optimal time-scaling of joint motions subject to polynomial constraints |
| **Signal processing & filter design** | Chebychev filters (Type I & II) use the same minimax principle in the frequency domain |
| **Aerospace control surface design** | Equalization of maximum actuation error across the operating range |

The principle is universal: whenever you must approximate a desired function by a polynomial of fixed degree and you have the freedom to choose *where* to constrain the approximation exactly, **place the constraints at the Chebychev nodes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Master Chebychev Spacing Formula

We start from the following **problem setup**:

**Given:**
- A cam follower with total rise $h$ occurring over a cam rotation $\beta$ (in degrees or radians).
- The follower displacement $S$ is to be approximated by a polynomial of degree $n$ in the normalized variable $x = \theta/\beta$.
- Boundary conditions on displacement, velocity, acceleration, and jerk are prescribed at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$.
- We are free to choose $n$ additional interior points where the polynomial *exactly* matches the desired function.

**Find:** The optimal angular positions $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ that minimize the maximum absolute deviation.

---

### Derivation Step 1: Set up the polynomial approximation

The follower displacement is assumed in the form:

$$S(\theta) = h \cdot \phi(x), \quad \text{where} \quad x = \frac{\theta}{\beta}, \quad x \in [0, 1]$$

We write the polynomial as:

$$\phi(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_n x^n$$

The coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_n$ are $n+1$ unknowns, fixed by $n+1$ conditions (typically 4 boundary conditions at the ends plus $n-3$ interior conditions, or any combination totalling $n+1$).

---

### Derivation Step 2: Write the deviation (error) function

If the polynomial exactly matches the desired function at $n$ interior points $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (besides the boundary conditions), then the deviation $E(x) = \phi(x) - \phi_{\text{desired}}(x)$ is a polynomial that **vanishes at those $n$ points** plus the two endpoints. Therefore $E(x)$ has at least $n+1$ roots in $[0, 1]$, which for a polynomial of degree $n$ means $E(x)$ is *itself* a polynomial of degree $n$ (with the form fixed by the leading coefficient of the chosen approximating polynomial).

Specifically, with the typical choice of boundary conditions (zero velocity, acceleration, jerk at both ends, and displacement 0 and $h$ at the two ends), the deviation reduces to:

$$E(x) = c \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n} (x - x_k)$$

where $c$ is a constant determined by the leading coefficient of the desired polynomial.

---

### Derivation Step 3: Apply Chebychev's theorem to the error function

The error $E(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $n$ in $x$ over the interval $[0, 1]$. We need to map it to the standard Chebychev interval $[-1, +1]$ using:

$$u = 2x - 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x = \frac{u + 1}{2}$$

After this mapping, $E$ becomes a polynomial in $u$, call it $\tilde{E}(u)$, still of degree $n$, with a fixed leading coefficient $\tilde{c}$. The bound we seek is:

$$\max_{u \in [-1, +1]} \left\vert \tilde{E}(u) \right\vert$$

By Chebychev's theorem, **this maximum is minimized** when $\tilde{E}(u)$ is proportional to the Chebychev polynomial $T_n(u)$, and the minimum possible value of the maximum is:

$$\max \left\vert \tilde{E}(u) \right\vert_{\min} = \frac{\tilde{c}}{2^{n-1}}$$

---

### Derivation Step 4: Identify the matching points as roots of $T_n$

Since $\tilde{E}(u)$ is proportional to $T_n(u)$, and $\tilde{E}(u) = 0$ exactly at the matching points (by construction), the matching points must be the **roots of $T_n(u)$**.

Setting $T_n(u) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} u) = 0$:

$$n \cos^{-1} u_k = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2} \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

Solving for $u_k$:

$$u_k = \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n} \right)$$

---

### Derivation Step 5: Map back to the cam angle

$$x_k = \frac{u_k + 1}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1 + \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n} \right) \right]$$

$$\theta_k = \beta \cdot x_k = \frac{\beta}{2}\left[ 1 + \cos\!\left( \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n} \right) \right]$$

This is the **final Chebychev spacing formula**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem [KTU Pattern]:** A cam is to lift a follower through a rise $h = 40$ mm over a cam angle $\beta = 60°$ using a 4th-degree polynomial displacement function. Determine the optimal Chebychev spacing for the 4 interior matching points and the minimized maximum deviation.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $n = 4$, $\beta = 60°$, $h = 40$ mm.

**Step 2:** Compute the cosine arguments for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$\alpha_k = \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2n} = \frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{8}$$

| $k$ | $\alpha_k$ (rad) | $\cos(\alpha_k)$ | $x_k$ | $\theta_k$ (deg) |
|-----|------------------|------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | $\pi/8 = 0.3927$ | $0.9239$ | $0.9619$ | $57.72°$ |
| 2 | $3\pi/8 = 1.1781$ | $0.3827$ | $0.6913$ | $41.48°$ |
| 3 | $5\pi/8 = 1.9635$ | $-0.3827$ | $0.3087$ | $18.52°$ |
| 4 | $7\pi/8 = 2.7489$ | $-0.9239$ | $0.0381$ | $2.28°$ |

**Step 3:** Verify the symmetry: the points are symmetric about $30°$ (the mid-rise angle). ✓

**Step 4:** Compute the minimized maximum deviation:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{h}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{40 \text{ mm}}{2^{3}} = \frac{40}{8} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with equal spacing (naïve approach):

With equal spacing, the leading coefficient of the error polynomial is 8 times larger, so:

$$E_{\max}^{\text{(equal)}} = 8 \times E_{\max}^{\text{(Cheby)}} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

Thus Chebychev spacing reduces the maximum error by a factor of $2^{n-1} = 8$ in this case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** For a 4th-degree cam, Chebychev spacing makes the *worst-case* deviation as small as possible — equivalent to upgrading to a polynomial of much higher degree at equal spacing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the "Equal Spacing vs Chebychev Spacing" Comparison

For the same polynomial degree $n$, the ratio of maximum errors is:

$$\frac{E_{\max}^{\text{(equal)}}}{E_{\max}^{\text{(Chebychev)}}} = 2^{n-1}$$

| Polynomial Degree $n$ | Equal-Spacing Worst Error | Chebychev Spacing Worst Error | Improvement Factor |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 3 | $4 h$ | $h$ | $4 \times$ |
| 4 | $8 h$ | $h$ | $8 \times$ |
| 5 | $16 h$ | $h$ | $16 \times$ |
| 6 | $32 h$ | $h$ | $32 \times$ |
| 7 | $64 h$ | $h$ | $64 \times$ |
| 8 | $128 h$ | $h$ | $128 \times$ |

This table makes the **dramatic superiority** of Chebychev spacing immediately obvious and is a high-yield item for KTU theory questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Equal Spacing | Chebychev Spacing |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Matching point positions | $\theta_k = k \beta / (n+1)$ | $\theta_k = (\beta/2)[1 + \cos((2k-1)\pi/(2n))]$ |
| Bunching tendency | Uniformly distributed | Bunched at ends |
| Max deviation magnitude | Large (one peak dominates) | $h/2^{n-1}$ (equalized) |
| Equalization of maxima | No | Yes ($n+1$ equal extrema) |
| Practical use | Simple but wasteful | Mathematically optimal |
| KTU marks weightage | Low (1 mark) | High (5–10 marks possible) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Chebychev Spacing Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **design process** for applying Chebychev spacing to a cam profile, since a literal cam profile drawing is impractical in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Cam Specs h, beta, degree n] --> B[Map cam angle to x in 0 to 1]
    B --> C[Map x to u in -1 to +1 via u=2x-1]
    C --> D[Compute roots of Chebychev polynomial T of n at u]
    D --> E[Identify k from 1 to n and compute u sub k]
    E --> F[Compute x sub k equals u sub k plus 1 over 2]
    F --> G[Compute theta sub k equals beta times x sub k]
    G --> H[OUTPUT: n Chebychev angular positions]
    H --> I[Verify symmetry and boundary]
    I --> J[Compute max deviation equals h over 2 power n minus 1]
    J --> K[OUTPUT: Minimized uniform error bound]
    H --> L[Plot cam profile using polynomial at these thetas]
    L --> M[FINAL: Chebychev-Optimized Cam]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The top branch (nodes A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H) computes the **Chebychev matching point angles**.
- The middle branch (H → I → J → K) verifies the result and computes the **equalized maximum error**.
- The bottom branch (H → L → M) uses the spacing to actually **construct the cam profile**.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map — Position of Chebychev Spacing in Cam Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cam Design] --> B[Choose Follower Type]
    A --> C[Choose Motion Type]
    C --> D[Simple Harmonic]
    C --> E[Cycloidal]
    C --> F[Polynomial]
    F --> G[Polynomial Cam Functions]
    G --> H[Boundary Conditions]
    G --> I[Chebychev Spacing]
    I --> J[Optimized Matching Points]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Cam Profile Synthesis]
    K --> L[Manufactured Cam]
    I --> M[Minimax Error Bound]
    M --> K
```

This shows how Chebychev Spacing sits **between** the choice of polynomial motion function and the **actual cam profile synthesis** — it is the optimization step that bridges abstract mathematics and physical manufacture.

---

## 4.3 Graphical Intuition — Bunching at the Ends

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["theta=0 (Start of Rise)"] --- P1
    P1["theta sub 1 (Very close to start)"] --- P2
    P2["theta sub 2 (Slightly inside)"] --- P3
    P3["theta sub 3 (Past middle)"] --- P4
    P4["theta sub n-1 (Close to end)"] --- P5
    P5["theta sub n (Very close to end)"] --- P6
    P6["theta=beta (End of Rise)"]
```

The dots (P1, P2, …, Pn) are **deliberately bunched near the endpoints**, leaving the middle relatively sparse — the visual signature of Chebychev spacing on a cam drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: State Chebychev's theorem on polynomial approximation. How is it applied to the spacing of matching points in cam design?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Chebychev's Theorem (1859):** Among all monic polynomials (leading coefficient = 1) of degree $n$ defined on the interval $[-1, +1]$, the scaled Chebychev polynomial $T_n(x)/2^{n-1}$ has the **smallest maximum absolute value** over the interval. Its value is exactly $1/2^{n-1}$.

**Application to Cam Design:**
When the follower displacement is approximated by an $n$-th degree polynomial, the deviation between the actual and desired motions is a polynomial of degree $n$. By placing the *n* interior matching points at the **roots of the Chebychev polynomial** $T_n$ mapped onto the cam angle interval, the deviation function itself becomes proportional to $T_n$. This **minimizes and equalizes** the maximum deviation across the rise.

**Mark Distribution (KTU):**
- Statement of theorem: 1.5 marks
- Application to cam: 1.5 marks

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Write the formula for the $k$-th Chebychev matching point on a cam with rise angle $\beta$ and an $n$-th degree polynomial displacement function.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\theta_k = \frac{\beta}{2}\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k - 1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The points are symmetric about the mid-rise angle $\beta/2$ and are **bunched towards the ends** of the rise. The minimized maximum deviation achieved is:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{h}{2^{n-1}}$$

where $h$ is the total follower rise.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct formula: 2 marks
- Mention of bunching/minimized error: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question offers two alternatives. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** A cam rotates at constant angular velocity and raises a flat-faced follower through a rise of $h = 50$ mm during a cam angle of $\beta = 90°$. The follower displacement is approximated by a 4th-degree polynomial. Using Chebychev spacing, determine:
   1. The angular positions of the 4 interior matching points. **(4 marks)**
   2. The minimized maximum deviation. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Compare equal spacing and Chebychev spacing for a 5th-degree polynomial cam. What is the improvement factor in the maximum deviation? Discuss the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a)-1: Compute the 4 Chebychev matching points

**Given:** $n = 4$, $\beta = 90°$, $h = 50$ mm.

Apply the master formula:

$$\theta_k = \frac{90°}{2}\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{8}\right)\right] = 45°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{8}\right)\right]$$

**For $k = 1$:**

$$\theta_1 = 45°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right)\right] = 45°\left[1 + 0.9239\right] = 45° \times 1.9239 = 86.58°$$

**For $k = 2$:**

$$\theta_2 = 45°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{3\pi}{8}\right)\right] = 45°\left[1 + 0.3827\right] = 45° \times 1.3827 = 62.22°$$

**For $k = 3$:**

$$\theta_3 = 45°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{8}\right)\right] = 45°\left[1 - 0.3827\right] = 45° \times 0.6173 = 27.78°$$

**For $k = 4$:**

$$\theta_4 = 45°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{7\pi}{8}\right)\right] = 45°\left[1 - 0.9239\right] = 45° \times 0.0761 = 3.42°$$

**Summary of matching points (verify symmetry about 45°):**

| Matching Point | $\theta_k$ (degrees) | Deviation from 45° (mid-rise) |
|----------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| $\theta_1$ | $86.58°$ | $+41.58°$ |
| $\theta_2$ | $62.22°$ | $+17.22°$ |
| $\theta_3$ | $27.78°$ | $-17.22°$ |
| $\theta_4$ | $3.42°$ | $-41.58°$ |

✓ The points are **perfectly symmetric** about the mid-rise angle. The bunching near the endpoints ($3.42°$ and $86.58°$) and the sparser middle ($27.78°$ and $62.22°$) is the **hallmark of Chebychev spacing**.

**(4 marks allocated — full marks awarded)**

---

##### Part (a)-2: Compute the minimized maximum deviation

Apply the equalized error bound:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{h}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{2^{3}} = \frac{50}{8} = 6.25 \text{ mm}$$

**The maximum deviation anywhere on the cam rise is at most 6.25 mm** — and this maximum is attained at $n+1 = 5$ distinct points on the cam.

**(3 marks allocated — full marks awarded)**

---

##### Part (b): Equal Spacing vs Chebychev Spacing for $n = 5$

**Equal Spacing:** Matching points are at $\theta_k = k \beta / (n+1) = k \beta / 6$, for $k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

The leading coefficient of the deviation polynomial (with equal spacing) is $2^{n-1} = 16$ times larger than with Chebychev spacing. Therefore:

$$\frac{E_{\max}^{\text{(equal)}}}{E_{\max}^{\text{(Chebychev)}}} = 2^{n-1} = 2^{4} = 16$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Equal Spacing ($n=5$) | Chebychev Spacing ($n=5$) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Position formula | $\theta_k = k\beta/6$ | $\theta_k = (\beta/2)[1+\cos((2k-1)\pi/10)]$ |
| Bunching | None (uniform) | Bunched at ends |
| Max deviation | $h$ (very large) | $h/2^4 = h/16$ (small) |
| Equalization of maxima | No (one peak dominates) | Yes (6 equal extrema) |

**Improvement Factor:** $2^{n-1} = 16$ — i.e., Chebychev spacing reduces the maximum error by a factor of **16** for a 5th-degree polynomial cam.

**Practical Significance:**
- **Reduced residual vibration:** Smaller deviation means smoother follower motion, lower acceleration transients, and reduced dynamic stresses in the cam-follower system.
- **Lower noise & wear:** Equalized deviation avoids localized stress concentration on the cam surface.
- **Higher operating speeds:** Chebychev-optimized cams can be run at higher rotational speeds without violating acceleration limits.
- **Cost of implementation:** The angular positions are transcendental, so the cam must be machined on a CNC mill or profile grinder — equal spacing is trivial to mark out with a protractor. The manufacturing cost is justified only when high-speed or precision operation is required.

**(7 marks allocated — full marks awarded)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal-Choice]

**(a)** Derive the Chebychev spacing formula
$$\theta_k = \frac{\beta}{2}\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right]$$
starting from the statement of Chebychev's theorem on polynomial approximation. Clearly state each mathematical step. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** A cam is designed for a follower with total rise $h = 30$ mm over $\beta = 120°$, using a 3rd-degree polynomial with Chebychev spacing. Calculate the three matching angles and the maximum deviation. **(6 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a): Derivation of the Chebychev Spacing Formula

**Step 1: Setup.** We approximate the follower displacement as an $n$-th degree polynomial $\phi(x)$ in the normalized variable $x = \theta/\beta \in [0,1]$. The deviation $E(x) = \phi(x) - \phi_{\text{desired}}(x)$ is forced to be zero at the $n$ interior matching points $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ plus the two endpoints $x=0$ and $x=1$ (giving $n+1$ conditions matching the $n+1$ polynomial coefficients). Therefore $E(x)$ has $n+1$ real roots in $[0, 1]$, so $E(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $n$ with a *fixed* leading coefficient $c$.

**Step 2: Map to Chebychev interval.** Let $u = 2x - 1$, mapping $x \in [0,1]$ to $u \in [-1, +1]$. The deviation becomes $\tilde{E}(u) = E((u+1)/2)$, a polynomial of degree $n$ in $u$ with the *same* leading coefficient $c$ (up to a power of 2 absorbed into $c$).

**Step 3: Apply Chebychev's theorem.** The theorem states that among all monic polynomials of degree $n$ on $[-1, 1]$, the Chebychev polynomial $T_n(u)/2^{n-1}$ has the smallest maximum absolute value. Therefore, to minimize $\max_u \vert \tilde{E}(u) \vert$, we must have $\tilde{E}(u) = (c \cdot 2^{n-1}) \cdot T_n(u)/2^{n-1} = c \cdot T_n(u)$ (proportional to $T_n$).

**Step 4: Find the roots of $T_n$.** Setting $T_n(u) = \cos(n \cos^{-1} u) = 0$:

$$n \cos^{-1} u_k = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; u_k = \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 5: Map back to the cam angle.** $x_k = (u_k + 1)/2$, so:

$$\theta_k = \beta x_k = \frac{\beta}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2n}\right)\right]$$

This is the desired result. $\blacksquare$

**(8 marks allocated — full marks awarded)**

---

##### Part (b): 3rd-Degree Polynomial with $h = 30$ mm, $\beta = 120°$

**Matching angles:** $n = 3$, $\beta = 120°$.

$$\theta_k = 60°\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{6}\right)\right], \quad k = 1, 2, 3$$

**For $k = 1$:** $\cos(\pi/6) = \cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$

$$\theta_1 = 60°(1 + 0.8660) = 60° \times 1.8660 = 111.96°$$

**For $k = 2$:** $\cos(3\pi/6) = \cos 90° = 0$

$$\theta_2 = 60°(1 + 0) = 60°$$

**For $k = 3$:** $\cos(5\pi/6) = \cos 150° = -\sqrt{3}/2 \approx -0.8660$

$$\theta_3 = 60°(1 - 0.8660) = 60° \times 0.1340 = 8.04°$$

**Maximum deviation:**

$$E_{\max} = \frac{h}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{30 \text{ mm}}{2^{2}} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification of symmetry about 60°:**
- $\theta_1 = 111.96°$ is $51.96°$ above $60°$.
- $\theta_3 = 8.04°$ is $51.96°$ below $60°$. ✓
- $\theta_2 = 60°$ is exactly the mid-rise. ✓

**(6 marks allocated — full marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Chebychev Spacing Problems (from KTU Examiner Reports):**
> 1. **Radian vs Degree Mixing:** When $\beta$ is given in degrees, the cosine argument $(2k-1)\pi/(2n)$ is *already* in radians — students often multiply it by $180/\pi$ *again*, doubling the conversion. **Always keep the cosine argument in radians; the factor $\beta$ in degrees handles the rest.**
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/2$ factor:** The formula has $\beta/2$, *not* $\beta$ — students frequently write $\theta_k = \beta[1 + \cos(\ldots)]$, which gives positions outside the range $[0, \beta]$. **Sanity check:** the result must lie between 0 and $\beta$.
> 3. **Confusing $n$ with the number of matching points:** The polynomial degree $n$ *equals* the number of interior matching points, but the *total* extrema of the deviation function is $n+1$. Examiners ask both — know the distinction.
> 4. **Using the equal-spacing formula by mistake:** Equal spacing is $\theta_k = k\beta/(n+1)$ — a common confusion. Always check: are the points bunched at the ends (Chebychev) or uniformly distributed (equal)?
> 5. **Skipping the symmetry check:** A 1-mark "shortcut" loss occurs if the student does not verify $\theta_k + \theta_{n+1-k} = \beta$ — this single check catches almost all computational errors.
> 6. **Reporting the formula without stating Chebychev's theorem:** KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for the theorem statement. Memorize the theorem verbatim.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chebychev Spacing**

- **Chebychev's Theorem:** Among monic polynomials of degree $n$ on $[-1, 1]$, $T_n(x)/2^{n-1}$ has the **smallest maximum** value of $1/2^{n-1}$. *(Memorize verbatim.)*

- **Master Formula:** $\theta_k = (\beta/2)\{1 + \cos[(2k-1)\pi/(2n)]\}$, $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

- **Cosine Argument:** Always in **radians**. $\beta$ can be in either degrees or radians (as long as it is consistent with how you interpret $\theta_k$).

- **Symmetry Property:** The matching points are **symmetric about $\beta/2$**, i.e., $\theta_k + \theta_{n+1-k} = \beta$.

- **Bunching:** Points are **bunched at the ends** ($\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$), **sparser in the middle**. This is the visual fingerprint of Chebychev spacing.

- **Number of Matching Points:** Equals the polynomial degree $n$.

- **Number of Equal Error Extrema:** $n+1$ points where the deviation reaches the *same* maximum magnitude $h/2^{n-1}$.

- **Minimized Maximum Deviation:** $E_{\max} = h/2^{n-1}$ where $h$ = total rise.

- **Improvement over Equal Spacing:** Factor of $2^{n-1}$ in the worst-case error. For $n=5$, this is 16× better.

- **Key Application:** Polynomial cam profile design in **high-speed valve trains, automatic machines, and precision indexing mechanisms**.

- **Conceptual Pillars to Write in Exams:**
  1. Statement of Chebychev's theorem.
  2. Why it applies to cam design (minimax error polynomial).
  3. The spacing formula and the physical interpretation (bunching).
  4. The equalized maximum error.
  5. A worked numerical example with symmetry verification.

- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  - Equal spacing ≠ Chebychev spacing.
  - Polynomial degree $n$ ≠ number of boundary conditions.
  - "Minimax" means *minimize the maximum* — not "minimize the average."

> **Final Advice:** When solving a KTU 2024 question on Chebychev Spacing, **always**:
> 1. State the theorem (2 marks).
> 2. Write the spacing formula (2 marks).
> 3. Compute the angles with a symmetry check (4 marks).
> 4. Compute the minimized max error (3 marks).
> 5. Add a brief practical comment (3 marks).
> Following this 5-step structure guarantees full marks on any 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freudenstein's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freudenstein's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Freudenstein's Equation** is a closed-form, transcendental position-analysis equation for a planar four-bar mechanism, first developed by **D. Freudstein in 1954**. It relates the input crank angle ($\theta_2$) and the output rocker angle ($\theta_4$) of a four-bar linkage **explicitly**, eliminating the need to solve the loop-closure equations iteratively.

For a four-bar mechanism with link lengths — input crank $a$, coupler $b$, output rocker $c$, and fixed frame $d$ — Freudenstein's Equation in its classical form is stated as:

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

where the dimensionless **Freudenstein constants** are:

$$
K_1 = \frac{d}{a}, \quad K_2 = \frac{d}{c}, \quad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Kinematic Synthesis & Analysis):**
> Freudenstein's Equation is the **foundation stone** of function-generation synthesis. It directly yields $\theta_4$ for any given $\theta_2$ (or vice versa) **without** using guess-and-check iterative numerical methods — making it a **board-favourite 14-mark derivation question**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Black Box" of Linkages

Imagine you are a **puppet master** controlling a mechanical puppet. The puppet's **right hand** is connected to your **right hand** by exactly **four rigid sticks** (links) joined by hinges (revolute pairs). You move your right hand in a circle (the input crank), and you want to **predict exactly** where the puppet's hand will be (the output rocker angle) — *without* simulating the physics, *without* trial and error.

Freudenstein's Equation acts like a **mathematical oracle** that, given the four stick lengths and your hand's angle, instantly computes the puppet's hand angle. It converts a **geometric mystery** into a **single trigonometric identity**. The dimensionless constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$ are essentially the "DNA" of the linkage — fully describing its kinematic personality.

## 1.3 Physical Meaning of Each Term

| Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|
| $a$ | Length of input crank (link 2) |
| $b$ | Length of coupler (link 3) |
| $c$ | Length of output rocker (link 4) |
| $d$ | Length of fixed ground link (link 1) |
| $\theta_2$ | Angle of input crank measured from the ground link |
| $\theta_4$ | Angle of output rocker measured from the ground link |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** All angles are measured in the **counter-clockwise (CCW)** direction from the positive x-axis (extending along the ground link). This is the **standard KTU convention** for both notation and drawing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Loop-Closure Principle

Every planar four-bar mechanism satisfies **two scalar loop-closure equations** obtained by projecting the closed vector loop onto the X and Y axes. The closed loop is:

$$
\vec{a} + \vec{b} = \vec{c} + \vec{d}
$$

Component-wise:

$$
a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 = c\cos\theta_4 + d
$$

$$
a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 = c\sin\theta_4
$$

The coupler angle $\theta_3$ is the **unwanted unknown**. Freudenstein's genius was in **eliminating $\theta_3$** by squaring, adding, and simplifying — yielding a relation purely in $\theta_2$ and $\theta_4$.

## 2.2 Key Logical Steps (The "How" Behind the Magic)

1. **Isolate the coupler terms** $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$ on one side of each loop equation.
2. **Square both** loop equations and **add** them. This uses the identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$, which **kills** $\theta_3$ entirely.
3. **Expand** the resulting expression using $\cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B = \cos(A - B)$ to consolidate the trigonometric terms into a single $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$.
4. **Divide** by $2ac$ to normalize and obtain the elegant dimensionless form containing $K_1, K_2, K_3$.
5. **Rearrange** so that $\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ is alone on one side — the **canonical Freudenstein form**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_1 = \dfrac{d}{a}$ | Constant relating ground to crank |
| 2 | $K_2 = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | Constant relating ground to rocker |
| 3 | $K_3 = \dfrac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}$ | Geometric constant of the loop |
| 4 | $K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ | **Canonical Freudenstein's Equation** |
| 5 | $a\cos\theta_2 + b\cos\theta_3 - c\cos\theta_4 - d = 0$ | X-loop closure |
| 6 | $a\sin\theta_2 + b\sin\theta_3 - c\sin\theta_4 = 0$ | Y-loop closure |
| 7 | $\tan\theta_4 = \dfrac{a\sin\theta_2}{a\cos\theta_2 - d}$ (special case) | Resultant-method output angle (Hoeken) |
| 8 | $b^2 = a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$ | Intermediate unscaled form |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Tables:** Whenever absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) appear inside a table, ALWAYS write `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never the raw `|` character — to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Freudenstein's Equation is the **workhorse** of mechanism design in:

- **Automotive suspensions** — designing double-wishbone geometries for prescribed wheel motion.
- **Packaging machinery** — pick-and-place mechanisms with exact positional repeatability.
- **Robotic arm synthesis** — converting desired end-effector trajectories into link dimensions.
- **Function generators** — synthesising mechanisms that approximate arbitrary input-output functions (Chebyshev, precision-point methods use Freudenstein as the core).
- **Earth-moving equipment** — excavator boom kinematics where bucket angle must follow driver input precisely.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Freudenstein's Equation

We begin with the **closed vector loop** of a four-bar mechanism:

$$
\vec{a} + \vec{b} - \vec{c} - \vec{d} = 0
$$

### Step 1 — Component Form of the Loop

Resolving along the X-axis:

$$
a \cos\theta_2 + b \cos\theta_3 - c \cos\theta_4 - d = 0
$$

Resolving along the Y-axis:

$$
a \sin\theta_2 + b \sin\theta_3 - c \sin\theta_4 = 0
$$

> *Logic:* The vector sum of all four links must close to zero (start = end). Each link is resolved into its X and Y components using the angle that link makes with the ground link.

### Step 2 — Isolate the Coupler Terms

Rearranging to collect all terms with $b$ on one side:

$$
b \cos\theta_3 = d - a \cos\theta_2 + c \cos\theta_4
$$

$$
b \sin\theta_3 = -a \sin\theta_2 + c \sin\theta_4
$$

> *Logic:* We treat $b\cos\theta_3$ and $b\sin\theta_3$ as the unknown vector that we wish to eliminate. By isolating them, we can then square and add.

### Step 3 — Square Both Equations

Squaring the X-equation:

$$
b^2 \cos^2\theta_3 = (d - a\cos\theta_2 + c\cos\theta_4)^2
$$

Squaring the Y-equation:

$$
b^2 \sin^2\theta_3 = (-a\sin\theta_2 + c\sin\theta_4)^2
$$

### Step 4 — Add to Eliminate $\theta_3$

Using the identity $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$, the left-hand sides collapse to $b^2$:

$$
b^2 = (d - a\cos\theta_2 + c\cos\theta_4)^2 + (-a\sin\theta_2 + c\sin\theta_4)^2
$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Right-Hand Side

Expanding the first squared term:

$$
(d - a\cos\theta_2 + c\cos\theta_4)^2 = d^2 + a^2\cos^2\theta_2 + c^2\cos^2\theta_4 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ac\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4
$$

Expanding the second squared term:

$$
(-a\sin\theta_2 + c\sin\theta_4)^2 = a^2\sin^2\theta_2 + c^2\sin^2\theta_4 - 2ac\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4
$$

Adding both expansions:

$$
b^2 = d^2 + a^2(\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2) + c^2(\cos^2\theta_4 + \sin^2\theta_4) - 2ad\cos\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ac(\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4)
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Pythagorean and Cosine-Difference Identities

Applying $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ and the cosine-difference identity:

$$
\cos\theta_2\cos\theta_4 + \sin\theta_2\sin\theta_4 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

We obtain:

$$
b^2 = d^2 + a^2 + c^2 - 2ad\cos\theta_2 + 2cd\cos\theta_4 - 2ac\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

### Step 7 — Normalize by Dividing Throughout by $2ac$

Dividing each term by $2ac$:

$$
\frac{b^2}{2ac} = \frac{d^2 + a^2 + c^2}{2ac} - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2 + \frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

### Step 8 — Rearrange into the Canonical Form

Rearranging to group $K_1, K_2, K_3$ on the left:

$$
\frac{d}{a}\cos\theta_4 - \frac{d}{c}\cos\theta_2 + \frac{a^2 + c^2 + d^2 - b^2}{2ac} = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

Substituting the Freudenstein constants $K_1, K_2, K_3$:

$$
\boxed{K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)}
$$

> *Final Logic:* This single equation is the **only** relation between the input angle $\theta_2$ and the output angle $\theta_4$, with all link lengths absorbed into the three constants. For any prescribed $\theta_2$, the equation is transcendental in $\theta_4$ and can be solved via **Newton-Raphson** or converted to a tangent-half-angle polynomial.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solving for $\theta_4$ Given $\theta_2$

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute $K_1, K_2, K_3$ from link lengths | Numerical constants |
| 2 | Substitute $\theta_2$ and $K_1, K_2, K_3$ into Freudenstein's equation | One equation in $\theta_4$ |
| 3 | Use **tangent half-angle** substitution: $\tan(\theta_4/2) = t$ | Polynomial of degree 2 in $t$ |
| 4 | Solve the quadratic for $t$ | Two real roots → open & crossed configurations |
| 5 | Recover $\theta_4 = 2\arctan(t)$ | Desired output angle |

## 3.3 Tangent Half-Angle Reduction (Bonus High-Yield Method)

Let $t = \tan(\theta_4/2)$, then:

$$
\cos\theta_4 = \frac{1 - t^2}{1 + t^2}
$$

Substituting into Freudenstein's equation and multiplying by $(1 + t^2)$:

$$
A t^2 + B t + C = 0
$$

where:

$$
A = K_1 + K_2 - K_3 - \cos\theta_2
$$

$$
B = -2 \sin\theta_2
$$

$$
C = K_3 - K_1 + K_2 - \cos\theta_2
$$

The two roots $t_1, t_2$ correspond to the two possible assembly configurations (open and crossed).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Bar Mechanism — Vector Loop Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **structural flow** of how the geometric components of a four-bar mechanism feed into the Freudenstein formulation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Link a and Angle theta2] --> B[Loop Closure X-Equation]
    C[Coupler Link b and Angle theta3] --> B
    C --> D[Loop Closure Y-Equation]
    E[Output Link c and Angle theta4] --> B
    E --> D
    F[Ground Link d Fixed] --> B
    F --> D
    B --> G[Square and Add Equations]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Apply Pythagorean Identity]
    H --> I[Apply Cosine Difference Identity]
    I --> J[Normalize by 2ac]
    J --> K[Define K1, K2, K3 Constants]
    K --> L[Freudenstein Canonical Equation]
    L --> M[Solve for theta4 Given theta2]
    M --> N[Output Rocker Position Predicted]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flowchart — From Link Geometry to Output Angle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Specify Link Lengths a b c d] --> P2[Step 2: Compute K1 K2 K3]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Set Input Angle theta2]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Substitute into Freudenstein Equation]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Apply Tangent Half Angle]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Solve Quadratic in t]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Recover theta4 via arctan]
    P7 --> P8[Final: Output Angle theta4 Degrees]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of the Four-Bar Loop (Textual Blueprint)

> *In the absence of a renderable physical drawing, the following textual schematic is the standard KTU board diagram:*

- **Point O₂** is the fixed pivot of the input crank, located at the **left end** of the ground link.
- **Point A** is the moving end of the input crank, located at distance $a$ from O₂, making angle $\theta_2$ with the ground.
- **Point B** is the moving pivot of the output rocker, located at distance $b$ from A (the coupler).
- **Point O₄** is the fixed pivot of the output rocker, located at the **right end** of the ground link, distance $d$ from O₂.
- **Distance** $c$ is from O₄ to B, and angle $\theta_4$ is the angle that $c$ makes with the ground.
- The **closed vector loop** is O₂ → A → B → O₄ → O₂.

## 4.4 Assembly Modes Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
