| **Lift / Stroke ($h$)** | The maximum displacement of the follower from its lowest position. |
| **Outstroke (Rise)** | The angular rotation of the cam during which the follower moves from its lowest to highest position. |
| **Return Stroke** | The angular rotation during which the follower returns to its lowest position. |
| **Dwell** | Angular intervals where the follower is *stationary* (zero velocity). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:**
> - For **knife-edge follower**: Pitch curve ≡ Cam profile → $R_p = R_b$
> - For **roller follower (radial)**: $R_p = R_b + r_{roller}$
> - For **roller follower (offset)**: $R_p = \text{offset distance}$ and $R_b = R_p - r_{roller}$

## 1.3 Types of Followers Covered in This Module

**A. By Tip Geometry:**
- **Knife-Edge Follower** — A perfectly sharp edge in sliding contact. Theoretically ideal but suffers from rapid wear; used here purely as a *pedagogical simplification* for graphical synthesis.
- **Roller Follower** — A roller (small wheel) is mounted at the tip, *rolling* on the cam. This drastically reduces wear and is the *industry standard* (e.g., IC engine valves).

**B. By Path Geometry:**
- **Radial (Inline) Follower** — The line of stroke passes through the cam center (offset = 0).
- **Offset Follower** — The line of stroke is offset from the cam center; the cam rotates about an axis that is *not* on the line of stroke. This is used to obtain a more compact cam or to allow access to a larger valve lift.

## 1.4 The Intuitive Analogy — Why Cams Matter

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. Your hand follows a smooth, deliberate arc — *not* a sudden jerk — even though the swing itself moves in a simple pendulum motion. **You are the cam** (your hand is the cam profile), **the swing seat is the follower**, and the smoothness you choose is the **motion law**. A cam is the engineering "hand" that converts uniform rotation into carefully shaped, *non-uniform* linear motion.

The single most common example in your syllabus is the **camshaft of an internal combustion engine**: as the cam rotates, it pushes the valve open (outstroke), holds it open briefly (dwell), then allows the spring to push it closed (return), and finally rests (dwell). The *shape* of the cam lobe determines whether the valve is opened smoothly or slammed — which is where the choice of motion law matters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Graphical Synthesis Logic — The "Why"

The objective of graphical cam design is straightforward: given a **displacement diagram** (which is a plot of follower lift vs. cam angle for a chosen motion law), convert this *2-D plot* into the *physical 2-D shape* of the cam.

The trick is to use the cam's rotation as a "clock" that maps time (or angle) to angular position on the base circle. The displacement values are then "wrapped" around the base circle like the spokes of a wheel.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Stage Mapping (Memorize This!):**
> 1. **Stage 1:** Map displacement values from the *linear* time axis of the displacement diagram → *angular* positions on the base circle (using angle = $\omega t$).
> 2. **Stage 2:** At each angular position, plot the *radial offset* (for knife edge) or the *roller center location* (for roller) outward from the cam center.

## 2.2 Standard Motion Laws (Syllabus Mandate)

Let $h$ = lift, $T$ = time period of rise (or return), $\beta$ = outstroke angle (or return angle), $\omega$ = cam angular velocity. Then $\theta = \omega t$.

### Motion 1: Uniform Velocity (Simple but Impractical)
Displacement rises as a *straight line*. Mathematically clean but causes **infinite acceleration** at the start and end of the stroke — destructive in practice. **Avoided in high-speed machines.**

$$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{\beta}\theta \quad \text{for } 0 \le \theta \le \beta$$

### Motion 2: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
The follower mimics a pendulum's projection. Smooth start and stop, but **high acceleration** at the beginning and end of stroke.

$$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

### Motion 3: Uniform Acceleration and Deceleration (UAD / Parabolic)
The follower accelerates uniformly for the *first half* and decelerates uniformly for the *second half*. The transition is at the midpoint.

For $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$:
$$y(\theta) = \frac{2h}{\beta^{2}}\theta^{2}$$

For $\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$:
$$y(\theta) = h - \frac{2h}{\beta^{2}}(\beta - \theta)^{2}$$

### Motion 4: Cycloidal Motion (Best for High-Speed)
Combines SHM smoothness with no abrupt acceleration changes. **Used in I.C. engine valve cams.**

$$y(\theta) = h\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam-day lifesaver.** Print it mentally. It contains every formula you will need to draw or numerically verify a cam profile.

| Motion Type | Displacement $y(\theta)$ | Maximum Velocity $v_{max}$ | Maximum Acceleration $a_{max}$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Velocity** | $\dfrac{h\theta}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\infty$ (at start/end) | **Never use for high-speed cams** |
| **Simple Harmonic** | $\dfrac{h}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{\pi h \omega}{2\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2\beta^{2}}$ | Smooth at start/end, peak acc at extremes |
| **Uniform Accel/Decel** | $\dfrac{2h\theta^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ (for first half) | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{4h\omega^{2}}{\beta}$ | Sudden change in acc at midpoint |
| **Cycloidal** | $h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta^{2}}$ | Smoothest of all; **best for high-speed** |

| Configuration | Base Circle $R_b$ | Prime Circle $R_p$ |
|---|---|---|
| Knife-edge (radial) | $R_p$ | $R_b$ (same) |
| Roller, radial | $R_p - r$ | $R_b + r$ |
| Roller, offset | $R_p - r$ | $\text{offset distance}$ |

**Legend:** $h$ = lift, $\beta$ = outstroke/return angle, $\omega$ = cam angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = roller radius.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Camshafts in I.C. engines use cycloidal or polynomial motion for smooth valve operation; the "lobe profile" is precisely a *cam profile* built using these laws.
- **Textile Machinery:** Shuttle cams, cam-actuated picker mechanisms, and needle bar drives all rely on the same graphical synthesis principles.
- **Packaging Industry:** Filling-machine cams require *dwell* periods for stationary bottle indexing — directly enabled by dwell angles in the displacement diagram.
- **Automatic Toys & Clocks:** Cam-driven figures in mechanical toys are miniature cam systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Algorithm: From Displacement Diagram to Cam Profile

The graphical synthesis is the same in *spirit* for all follower types — it is the **execution geometry** that differs. Below is the unified step-by-step logic.

### Stage 0: Establish the Master Frame
- Choose a scale for the displacement diagram. Mark the **lift $h$** on the vertical axis.
- Mark the **outstroke angle $\beta_1$**, **dwell angle $\delta_1$**, **return angle $\beta_2$**, **dwell angle $\delta_2$** on the horizontal axis (cumulative cam angle $\phi$).
- Total angle: $\phi_{total} = \beta_1 + \delta_1 + \beta_2 + \delta_2 = 360°$.
- Subdivide the outstroke and return strokes into $N$ equal parts (typically $N = 6, 8, \text{ or } 12$). Mark vertical lines at each division.
- Plot the displacement curve for the chosen motion law (SHM, UAD, cycloidal) using the formulas in §2.2.
- Measure the displacement $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N$ at each vertical division.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** $N = 6$ for SHM and UAD, $N = 12$ for cycloidal (to capture the sine-wave curvature with reasonable fidelity).

### Stage 1: Lay Out the Base Circle and Angular Sectors
1. Choose a base circle radius $R_b$ (typically 1.5 to 2 times the lift $h$ — never too small, or the cam profile becomes very steep).
2. Draw a horizontal radius to the right (this is the **reference line**, indicating the follower at its lowest position).
3. On the base circle, mark off the four angles $\beta_1, \delta_1, \beta_2, \delta_2$ in sequence using the same scale as the displacement diagram.
4. At each angular mark, draw a radial line from the cam center $O$.

### Stage 2: Project Displacements onto the Base Circle
1. Subdivide the outstroke arc $\beta_1$ on the base circle into $N$ equal angles. (Since we have $N$ points on the displacement diagram, we need $N$ corresponding angular points on the circle.)
2. Repeat for the return arc $\beta_2$.

**Crucial connection:** Point 1 on the displacement diagram (at angle $\beta_1/N$ from the start) corresponds to radial line 1 on the base circle. And so on.

### Stage 3: Construct the Cam Profile (Now Branches by Follower Type)

---

## 3.2 Construction A: Knife-Edge Follower (Radial)

**Step-by-Step:**

1. From the cam center $O$, on the radial line at angular position $\theta_i$, measure a distance equal to the displacement $y_i$ **outward from the base circle** (or, equivalently, mark a point at distance $R_b + y_i$ from $O$).
2. Repeat for all $N$ points in the outstroke and the return.
3. During the dwell angles ($\delta_1$ and $\delta_2$), the cam profile *coincides* with the base circle (since the follower is not moving).
4. Connect the plotted points with a smooth curve — this is the **cam profile**.

**Why this works:** A knife-edge follower touches the cam at a *single point*. The lift $y_i$ *is* the radial distance the cam surface must extend beyond the base circle at angular position $\theta_i$. Hence the radius at each point is simply $R_b + y_i$.

**Geometric Summary for Knife-Edge:**
$$R(\theta_i) = R_b + y_i$$

where $R(\theta_i)$ is the radius from cam center to the cam profile at angular position $\theta_i$.

---

## 3.3 Construction B: Roller Follower (Radial, No Offset)

**Step-by-Step:**

1. Draw the **prime circle** of radius $R_p = R_b + r_{roller}$, concentric with the base circle.
2. On the prime circle, mark off the same angular positions $\theta_i$ (i.e., transfer the radial lines from the base circle outward to the prime circle).
3. At each point $P_i$ on the prime circle (corresponding to angular position $\theta_i$), **draw the roller circle** of radius $r_{roller}$.
4. Join the points $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N$ — this is the **pitch curve** (it is a circle of radius $R_p$ here, since the follower is radial).
5. The **cam profile** is the *inner envelope* (or *outer envelope*, depending on which side the contact is on) of the family of roller circles. Practically: it is a smooth curve that is **tangent to all the roller circles**, lying on the *side away from the cam center*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Construction Trick (KTU's Test of Skill):** To draw the common tangent curve:
> - The curve is **tangent to each roller circle** at a point that lies on the radial line $O P_i$ *extended outward* from $P_i$ by a distance $r_{roller}$ — but only if the cam is rotating *clockwise* and the follower is on the right.
> - In practice, students use a *French curve* or carefully sketch by hand. A common method is to draw a smooth curve that is *externally tangent* to each roller circle on the side away from the cam center.
> - For the dwell regions, the cam profile is a *circular arc of radius $R_b$* (the base circle itself).

**Why this works:** The roller center is always at radial distance $R_p$ from $O$. The cam surface is at distance $r_{roller}$ from the roller center *inward* (toward the cam center). So the cam profile at angle $\theta_i$ is at radius $R_p - r_{roller} = R_b$ from $O$ — which is the base circle! This is consistent.

---

## 3.4 Construction C: Roller Follower with Offset (The Tricky Case)

This is the **most frequently tested** variant in KTU exams, and the source of the most mark-deductions.

**The Key Idea:** The line of stroke is at a perpendicular distance $\epsilon$ (epsilon, the offset) from the cam center $O$. The *prime circle* has radius equal to the offset $R_p = \epsilon$. The roller center always lies on the line of stroke, and at its lowest position, the roller center is at the point on the line of stroke closest to $O$.

**Step-by-Step:**

1. Draw the **base circle** of radius $R_b$ centered at $O$.
2. Draw the **line of stroke** as a vertical line at perpendicular distance $\epsilon$ to the right of $O$ (typical convention: follower is on the right, cam rotates clockwise).
3. Draw the **prime circle** of radius $R_p = \epsilon$ centered at $O$. This circle is **tangent to the line of stroke** at point $A_0$ — the initial position of the roller center.
4. On the base circle (using the same starting reference line $O A_0$), mark off the four angles $\beta_1, \delta_1, \beta_2, \delta_2$.
5. Subdivide the outstroke arc $\beta_1$ into $N$ equal parts. From each division point $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ on the base circle, draw a **line parallel to the line of stroke** (i.e., a vertical line in the standard configuration).
6. On each vertical line, starting from the base circle, measure a vertical distance equal to the displacement $y_i$ **upward** (i.e., away from the cam center in the direction of the follower lift). This gives the roller center positions $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N$ on the prime circle.
7. At each $P_i$, draw the **roller circle** of radius $r_{roller}$.
8. Join the points $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N$ — this is the **pitch curve** (no longer a circle when offset).
9. Draw the **cam profile** as a smooth curve *tangent to all the roller circles* on the side facing the cam center (i.e., the inner envelope).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In step 6, students measure the displacement from the **prime circle** instead of the **base circle**. This is wrong. The displacement $y_i$ is the *additional* vertical distance the roller center has moved above its starting position on the line of stroke. The starting position is on the prime circle (at radius $\epsilon$ from $O$). So the roller center at angular position $i$ is at vertical distance $(R_p + y_i) - \epsilon = y_i$ above the base circle. The total vertical distance from $O$ is $R_b + y_i$ at the initial angular position, where the line of stroke crosses the base circle. Hence, **measuring from the base circle** is correct.

---

## 3.5 The Dwell Region — What Happens in Cam Profile?

During dwell, the follower does not move. Therefore:

- **For knife-edge radial follower:** Cam profile is a circular arc of radius $R_b$ (the base circle itself).
- **For roller radial follower:** Cam profile is a circular arc of radius $R_b$, *concentric* with the cam center. The roller center remains on the prime circle at constant angular position.
- **For roller offset follower:** Cam profile is a circular arc of radius $R_b$, *concentric* with the cam center. The roller center remains at a fixed point on the line of stroke.

## 3.6 Why Use an Offset Follower? — Engineering Justification

1. **Compactness:** The cam can be made smaller because the line of stroke is moved closer to the cam's edge.
2. **Pressure Angle Reduction:** The pressure angle is more uniform throughout the lift, reducing side thrust and wear.
3. **Access:** Allows the follower to clear other mechanical components that may be in the way.

The trade-off is that the *pitch curve* is no longer circular, which makes manufacturing slightly more complex.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cam Synthesis Architecture

The following block diagram captures the complete flow from design inputs to final cam profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Inputs: Type of Follower, Lift h, Angles beta1 delta1 beta2 delta2, Motion Law] --> B0
    B0[Step 1: Draw Displacement Diagram y vs theta using motion law] --> C0
    C0[Step 2: Read off displacement values y1 y2 ... yN at N equal divisions] --> D0
    D0[Step 3: Draw Base Circle radius Rb centered at O] --> E0
    E0[Step 4: Mark outstroke dwell return dwell angles on base circle] --> F0
    F0[Step 5: Subdivide outstroke and return arcs into N equal parts] --> G0{Follower Type}
    G0 -->|Knife Edge Radial| H1[Mark radial distances Rb plus yi on each radial line and join smoothly]
    G0 -->|Roller Radial| H2[Draw prime circle of radius Rb plus r. Mark roller centers. Draw tangent curve to all roller circles]
    G0 -->|Roller Offset| H3[Draw line of stroke at distance offset. Draw prime circle tangent to line of stroke. Mark roller centers on line of stroke. Draw tangent curve to all roller circles]
    H1 --> I0[Step 8: Cam Profile Complete]
    H2 --> I0
    H3 --> I0
    I0[Step 9: Verify by checking tangent and pressure angle constraints]
```

## 4.2 Displacement Diagram Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Choose Motion Law: SHM / UAD / Cycloidal] --> B1[Plot base axis: cumulative cam angle in degrees]
    B1 --> C1[Mark angles beta1 delta1 beta2 delta2 along horizontal axis]
    C1 --> D1[Subdivide outstroke and return into N equal parts]
    D1 --> E1[Apply motion law formula y of theta at each subdivision]
    E1 --> F1[Draw smooth curve through points: this is the Displacement Diagram]
    F1 --> G1[Read off y1 y2 ... yN for the cam profile drawing]
```

## 4.3 Roller Follower Construction — Side-by-Side (Radial vs Offset)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Starting Point: Base Circle radius Rb at Center O] --> SR{Roller Follower Type}
    SR -->|Radial No Offset| SR1[Prime Circle radius Rb plus r concentric with O]
    SR -->|With Offset epsilon| SO1[Line of stroke at distance epsilon from O]
    SO1 --> SO2[Prime Circle radius epsilon tangent to line of stroke]
    SR1 --> SR3[Mark angular positions on prime circle and draw roller circles]
    SO2 --> SO3[Mark roller centers on line of stroke at vertical distance yi above base circle and draw roller circles]
    SR3 --> SR4[Draw common internal tangent curve: Cam Profile]
    SO3 --> SO4[Draw common internal tangent curve: Cam Profile]
    SR4 --> SR5[Add dwell arcs of radius Rb]
    SO4 --> SO5[Add dwell arcs of radius Rb]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Cam Profile Construction — Quick Visual

| Feature | Knife-Edge Radial | Roller Radial | Roller Offset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pitch curve shape | Circle of radius $R_b$ | Circle of radius $R_b + r$ | Not a circle; lies on line of stroke |
| Prime circle radius | $R_b$ | $R_b + r$ | $\epsilon$ (offset) |
| Marking displacement | Radially outward on base circle | On prime circle, then drop down by $r$ | Vertically upward along line of stroke |
| Cam profile relation to roller | Touches knife edge | Internally tangent to rollers | Internally tangent to rollers |
| Construction difficulty | Easiest | Moderate | Most difficult |
| Real-world use | Toy mechanisms | Medium-speed valves | High-speed IC engine valves |

## 4.5 Pressure Angle and the Offset Advantage — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Radial Follower] --> A3[Pressure angle varies: small at start, large at mid stroke]
    B2[Offset Follower] --> B3[Pressure angle more uniform: less side thrust, less wear]
    A3 --> A4[Side thrust causes follower jamming and wear]
    B3 --> B4[Reduced side thrust: smoother operation at high speed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The pressure angle concept is *not* typically asked in Module 3's graphical synthesis, but mentioning it in a 14-mark answer shows the examiner that you understand the *engineering significance* of the offset configuration. This can fetch full marks even if your drawing is slightly off.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

Define the following terms with the help of a neat sketch: (a) Base circle, (b) Pitch curve, (c) Prime circle. How are they related for a knife-edge follower?

**Model Answer:**

The **base circle** is the smallest circle drawn from the cam center that is tangent to the cam profile. The **pitch curve** is the locus of the point of contact on the follower — for a knife-edge follower, this is the cam profile itself. The **prime circle** is the smallest circle drawn from the cam center that is tangent to the pitch curve.

For a **knife-edge follower**, since the pitch curve coincides with the cam profile, the prime circle and the base circle are one and the same: $R_p = R_b$. The three terms refer to the same geometric circle in this case.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

A cam rotating clockwise at a uniform speed is to give SHM to a roller follower with offset. List the steps involved in drawing the cam profile.

**Model Answer:**

The steps for drawing a cam profile for a roller follower with offset are:

1. Draw the displacement diagram $y$ vs. $\theta$ using the SHM formula: $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ for both outstroke and return.
2. Draw the base circle of radius $R_b$ centered at $O$.
3. Draw the line of stroke at a perpendicular distance $\epsilon$ (offset) from $O$.
4. Draw the prime circle of radius $\epsilon$, tangent to the line of stroke.
5. Mark the outstroke angle $\beta_1$, dwell $\delta_1$, return angle $\beta_2$, and dwell $\delta_2$ on the base circle.
6. Subdivide $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ each into $N$ equal parts.
7. From each subdivision, draw a line parallel to the line of stroke and mark the roller centers at distance $y_i$ above the base circle.
8. Draw roller circles of radius $r$ at each roller center.
9. Draw a smooth curve internally tangent to all the roller circles — this is the cam profile.
10. Complete the dwell arcs with radius $R_b$ centered at $O$.

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A cam rotates at **200 rpm clockwise** and operates a **roller follower with an offset of 15 mm**. The roller radius is **20 mm**. The follower lifts by **40 mm** during the outstroke of **90°**, dwells for **45°**, returns during **60°** with **simple harmonic motion**, and dwells for the remaining period. Draw:
- **(a)** The displacement diagram. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The cam profile, clearly showing the base circle, prime circle, line of stroke, and roller positions. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Given Data:**
- $N = 200$ rpm $\Rightarrow$ $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.94$ rad/s
- Offset $\epsilon = 15$ mm
- Roller radius $r = 20$ mm
- Lift $h = 40$ mm
- Outstroke angle $\beta_1 = 90°$
- Dwell $\delta_1 = 45°$
- Return angle $\beta_2 = 60°$
- Dwell $\delta_2 = 360° - 90° - 45° - 60° = 165°$
- Motion law for both outstroke and return: **SHM**

**Part (a) — Displacement Diagram (7 marks):**

Choose scales. Let 1 cm on the horizontal axis = 20° of cam rotation. Let 1 cm on the vertical axis = 10 mm of lift.

The total horizontal length = $\dfrac{360°}{20°/\text{cm}} = 18$ cm.

Mark the outstroke, dwell, return, dwell segments along the horizontal axis.

For **SHM**, the displacement at angle $\theta$ during outstroke is:
$$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_1}\right)\right] = \frac{40}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{90°}\right)\right]$$

Subdivide the 90° outstroke into $N = 6$ equal parts of 15° each. Compute $y_i$:

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $\dfrac{\pi\theta_i}{90°}$ | $\cos(\cdots)$ | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0° | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 15° | $\pi/6$ | 0.866 | 2.68 |
| 2 | 30° | $\pi/3$ | 0.500 | 10.00 |
| 3 | 45° | $\pi/2$ | 0 | 20.00 |
| 4 | 60° | $2\pi/3$ | $-0.500$ | 30.00 |
| 5 | 75° | $5\pi/6$ | $-0.866$ | 37.32 |
| 6 | 90° | $\pi$ | $-1$ | 40.00 |

For the **return stroke** (60°), use the same formula but with $\beta_2 = 60°$:

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $\dfrac{\pi\theta_i}{60°}$ | $\cos(\cdots)$ | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | 0° | 0 | 1 | 40.00 |
| 7 | 10° | $\pi/6$ | 0.866 | 37.32 |
| 8 | 20° | $\pi/3$ | 0.500 | 30.00 |
| 9 | 30° | $\pi/2$ | 0 | 20.00 |
| 10 | 40° | $2\pi/3$ | $-0.500$ | 10.00 |
| 11 | 50° | $5\pi/6$ | $-0.866$ | 2.68 |
| 12 | 60° | $\pi$ | $-1$ | 0 |

Draw a smooth curve through the 12 points — this is the displacement diagram.

**Part (b) — Cam Profile (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Draw the base circle. The base circle radius is:
$$R_b = R_p - r = \epsilon - r = 15 - 20 = -5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(NEGATIVE!)}$$

**⚠ This is a problem!** If $\epsilon < r$, the cam cannot be designed because the base circle would have negative radius. The constraint for a valid offset cam is: $\epsilon > r$, i.e., **15 > 20 is FALSE**.

**Examiner's Note:** In a real exam, if this happens, the student should immediately flag this as a *design infeasibility* and suggest a larger offset. For the purpose of this model solution, we will **assume the student revises the offset to $\epsilon = 25$ mm** (a typical correction).

With corrected $\epsilon = 25$ mm:
$$R_b = \epsilon - r = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$
$$R_p = \epsilon = 25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Draw the line of stroke vertical, at 25 mm to the right of $O$.

**Step 3:** Draw the prime circle of radius 25 mm centered at $O$, tangent to the line of stroke at $A_0$ (the initial position of the roller center).

**Step 4:** Mark the angles on the base circle: starting from $A_0$, go 90° (outstroke), then 45° (dwell), then 60° (return), then 165° (dwell). This divides the base circle into 4 arcs.

**Step 5:** Subdivide the 90° outstroke arc into 6 equal parts of 15° each. Subdivide the 60° return arc into 6 equal parts of 10° each. Draw radial lines from $O$ at each subdivision.

**Step 6:** From the radial line at angular position $\theta_i$, draw a vertical line (parallel to the line of stroke). On this vertical line, mark the roller center at distance $y_i$ above the base circle (i.e., at total distance $R_b + y_i$ from $O$ along the vertical).

For outstroke points:

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $y_i$ (mm) | Roller center vertical distance from $O$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0° | 0 | 5 |
| 1 | 15° | 2.68 | 7.68 |
| 2 | 30° | 10.00 | 15.00 |
| 3 | 45° | 20.00 | 25.00 |
| 4 | 60° | 30.00 | 35.00 |
| 5 | 75° | 37.32 | 42.32 |
| 6 | 90° | 40.00 | 45.00 |

For return points (similar procedure):

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $y_i$ (mm) | Roller center vertical distance from $O$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | 0° | 40.00 | 45.00 |
| 7 | 10° | 37.32 | 42.32 |
| 8 | 20° | 30.00 | 35.00 |
| 9 | 30° | 20.00 | 25.00 |
| 10 | 40° | 10.00 | 15.00 |
| 11 | 50° | 2.68 | 7.68 |
| 12 | 60° | 0 | 5 |

**Step 7:** At each roller center, draw a circle of radius $r = 20$ mm. This is the roller in its position at that instant.

**Step 8:** Draw a smooth curve that is **internally tangent to all the roller circles** on the side facing the cam center. This is the cam profile.

**Step 9:** For the dwell regions (45° and 165°), the cam profile is a circular arc of radius $R_b = 5$ mm centered at $O$.

**Step 10:** Label everything clearly: base circle, prime circle, line of stroke, roller centers, and cam profile.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A cam is to be designed for a **knife-edge follower** with the following specifications:
- Cam rotates at **300 rpm counter-clockwise**
- Lift $h = 30$ mm
- Outstroke angle = **120°** with **uniform acceleration and deceleration (UAD)**
- Dwell = **30°**
- Return stroke angle = **90°** with **SHM**
- Dwell for the remaining angle
- Base circle radius = **45 mm**

Draw:
- **(a)** The displacement diagram. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The cam profile. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Given Data:**
- $\omega = 300$ rpm $= 10\pi$ rad/s $= 31.42$ rad/s
- $h = 30$ mm, $R_b = 45$ mm
- $\beta_1 = 120°$ (UAD), $\delta_1 = 30°$, $\beta_2 = 90°$ (SHM), $\delta_2 = 360° - 120° - 30° - 90° = 120°$

**Part (a) — Displacement Diagram (7 marks):**

For **UAD outstroke** (120°), subdivide into $N = 6$ equal parts of 20° each. The first 3 parts (0° to 60°) are **uniform acceleration**, the next 3 parts (60° to 120°) are **uniform deceleration**.

For acceleration phase ($0 \le \theta \le 60°$):
$$y(\theta) = \frac{2h}{\beta_1^2}\theta^2 = \frac{2 \times 30}{120^2}\theta^2 = \frac{\theta^2}{240}$$

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ (°) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 20 | 1.67 |
| 2 | 40 | 6.67 |
| 3 | 60 | 15.00 |

For deceleration phase ($60° \le \theta \le 120°$):
$$y(\theta) = h - \frac{2h}{\beta_1^2}(\beta_1 - \theta)^2 = 30 - \frac{(\beta_1 - \theta)^2}{240}$$

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ (°) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 80 | 23.33 |
| 5 | 100 | 28.33 |
| 6 | 120 | 30.00 |

For **SHM return** (90°), subdivide into $N = 6$ equal parts of 15° each:
$$y(\theta) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta_2}\right)\right] = 15\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{90°}\right)\right]$$

| Point $i$ | $\theta_i$ (°) | $y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 0 | 30.00 |
| 7 | 15 | 23.30 |
| 8 | 30 | 15.00 |
| 9 | 45 | 6.70 |
| 10 | 60 | 1.70 |
| 11 | 75 | 0.18 |
| 12 | 90 | 0 |

Connect the points smoothly: the outstroke is a parabola, the return is an SHM curve. Dwell periods are horizontal lines at $y = 0$ (lower dwell) and $y = 30$ mm (upper dwell).

**Part (b) — Cam Profile (7 marks):**

1. Draw a horizontal reference line from cam center $O$.
2. Draw the base circle of radius 45 mm.
3. From the reference line, mark the outstroke angle of 120° counter-clockwise (since cam rotates counter-clockwise), then dwell 30°, return 90°, dwell 120°.
4. Subdivide the 120° outstroke arc into 6 equal parts of 20° each; subdivide the 90° return arc into 6 equal parts of 15° each.
5. At each subdivision, draw a radial line from $O$.
6. On each radial line, mark a point at distance $(R_b + y_i)$ from $O$ — i.e., $(45 + y_i)$ mm.
7. Connect the 12 points with a smooth curve — this is the **cam profile**.
8. During the dwell regions, the cam profile is an arc of the base circle (radius 45 mm).

For outstroke:
| Point $i$ | $y_i$ (mm) | $R_b + y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 45.0 |
| 1 | 1.67 | 46.67 |
| 2 | 6.67 | 51.67 |
| 3 | 15.00 | 60.00 |
| 4 | 23.33 | 68.33 |
| 5 | 28.33 | 73.33 |
| 6 | 30.00 | 75.00 |

For return:
| Point $i$ | $y_i$ (mm) | $R_b + y_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 30.00 | 75.00 |
| 7 | 23.30 | 68.30 |
| 8 | 15.00 | 60.00 |
| 9 | 6.70 | 51.70 |
| 10 | 1.70 | 46.70 |
| 11 | 0.18 | 45.18 |
| 12 | 0 | 45.00 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Cam Profile Drawing:**
> 1. **Wrong direction of cam rotation** — Make sure the angles are marked in the same direction as the cam rotates. If the cam rotates clockwise, the outstroke/dwell/return/dwell sectors are marked clockwise from the reference line.
> 2. **Forgetting the dwell arcs** — Dwell regions are not "blank" — they are circular arcs of the base circle radius. Many students leave the cam profile incomplete at the dwell, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing prime circle and base circle for roller followers** — Memorize: $R_p = R_b + r$ for radial roller; $R_p = \epsilon$ for offset roller. Drawing the wrong prime circle is an *instant* 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Wrong tangent direction on the offset cam** — For an offset follower, the cam profile is on the *side of the roller circles closer to the cam center* (the inner envelope), not the outer one.
> 5. **Missing the construction lines** — In KTU 2024 evaluation, *neatness counts*. Always show the construction lines (radial lines, subdivisions, roller circles) in light pencil and the final cam profile in a darker line. This single habit can earn you 1–2 bonus marks for *neatness and clarity*.
> 6. **Offsetting from the prime circle instead of the base circle** — When plotting roller centers in the offset case, measure $y_i$ from the **base circle**, not the prime circle. The prime circle is just a reference for the *initial* roller center position.
> 7. **Using uniform velocity at high speed** — Never use uniform velocity in modern designs. KTU examiners *expect* a justification if you choose it (e.g., "low-speed indexing cam"). Otherwise, use SHM, UAD, or cycloidal.
> 8. **Forgetting the scale** — Always state the scale used (e.g., "1 cm = 10 mm of lift") below the displacement diagram. The examiner will not measure your drawing precisely, but the scale proves you used engineering conventions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Base Circle ($R_b$):** Smallest circle tangent to the cam profile. *This is your drawing's foundation — start here.*
- **Pitch Curve:** Locus of the contact point. For knife-edge = cam profile; for roller = locus of roller center.
- **Prime Circle ($R_p$):** Smallest circle tangent to the pitch curve.
- **Three configurations, three prime circles:**
  - Knife-edge radial: $R_p = R_b$
  - Roller radial: $R_p = R_b + r$
  - Roller offset: $R_p = \epsilon$ (offset)
- **Constraint check for offset cam:** $\epsilon > r$ is **mandatory**, else the cam is physically impossible.
- **Four motion laws (in increasing order of smoothness):** Uniform velocity → SHM → UAD → Cycloidal. Cycloidal is the gold standard.
- **SHM displacement formula:** $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$
- **UAD displacement formula (first half):** $y = \dfrac{2h\theta^2}{\beta^2}$
- **Cycloidal displacement formula:** $y = h\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$
- **Maximum velocities and accelerations:** See the formula cheat sheet in §2.3.
- **Construction rule for offset cam:** Plot roller centers on the *line of stroke*, vertically above the base circle at distance $y_i$. Draw tangent curve to the *inner* side of the roller circles.
- **Dwell = base circle arc.** The cam profile during dwell is a circular arc of radius $R_b$ concentric with the cam center.
- **Direction matters:** Always mark angles in the *direction of cam rotation* from the initial reference line.
- **Always show construction lines** in light strokes; the final cam profile in a darker, single continuous line.
- **Always state the scale** below the displacement diagram and on the cam profile drawing.

**One-line rule of thumb:** "Mark the cam angle, drop a radial, measure $y_i$ outward, smooth curve, repeat. For roller, do the same but offset by $r$ and draw tangent curves. For offset, do the same but the offset is vertical, not radial."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gears - Classification - Terminology of Spur, Helical, Bevel, and Worm Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gears - Classification - Terminology of Spur, Helical, Bevel, and Worm Gear ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Gear A **gear** is a rotating, machine element having cut teeth (or, in the case of a cogwheel, inserted teeth) which mesh with another toothed part to transmit torque and motion. Gears form one of the most critical classes of power-transmission elements in mechanical engineering. They transmit motion and power between two rotating shafts by means of the successive engagement of discrete teeth, ensuring a **positive, non-slip drive** (no belt slip or chain slack). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** A gear is a toothed wheel that, by the successive engagement of its teeth, transmits motion and power from one shaft to another (or to a rack) with a definite velocity ratio (gear ratio). The drive is purely rolling/sliding at the pitch circles, and is classified as a *positive* drive because no slip occurs between the driver and the driven member. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why Gears?" Imagine two **bicycle sprockets** connected by a chain. The chain simply *pulls* the teeth, and every rotation of the big sprocket produces a precise, repeatable rotation of the small sprocket. Now imagine doing the same thing **without a chain** — by making the two wheels' teeth physically *interlock* directly. That is exactly what a gear pair does. - **Bicycle chain system** → Flexible positive drive. - **Direct interlocking toothed wheels** → **Gears** (rigid positive drive, faster, more accurate, no chain to stretch). > [!NOTE] > **The Big Picture (KTU Vision):** Gears are the *backbone* of all gearboxes — from your car's transmission to the tiny hands of a quartz watch. The **shape, orientation, and arrangement** of the teeth define *what kind* of gear it is. ### 1.3 Classification of Gears — Master Overview Gears are classified based on: 1. **Shape of the pitch surface** (cylindrical / conical / hyperboloidal). 2. **Shaft orientation** (parallel / intersecting / non-intersecting, non-parallel). 3. **Tooth profile orientation** (straight / inclined / curved). 4. **Position of teeth** (external / internal). | Category | Gears Covered | Shaft Arrangement | |---|---|---| | **Spur Gears** | Spur gear, Rack & Pinion, Internal gear | Parallel shafts | | **Helical Gears** | Helical gear, Herringbone (double-helical), Crossed helical | Parallel (or skew) shafts | | **Bevel Gears** | Straight bevel, Spiral bevel, Zerol bevel, Hypoid | Intersecting shafts (commonly **90°**) | | **Worm Gears** | Worm & worm wheel, Double-enveloping worm | Non-intersecting, non-parallel shafts (typically **90°**) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Focus:** You must learn **(a)** classification up to the level above, and **(b)** the detailed geometry & terminology of **Spur, Helical, Bevel, and Worm** gears. Rack, hypoid, and planetary sets are typically mentioned only for completeness. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Spur Gears — The "Workhorse" A **spur gear** has straight teeth cut parallel to the axis of rotation of the gear. It is the simplest, cheapest, most widely used gear and transmits power between **two parallel shafts rotating in opposite directions**. **Key Engineering Insights:** - **Why straight teeth?** They are easy to manufacture (hobbing, shaping, milling). - **Failure mode:** Spur gears produce a noticeable *impact noise* at high speeds because the entire face of the tooth engages at once. This is why spur gears are preferred at **low-to-moderate speeds** (up to ~10–15 m/s). - **No axial thrust** is generated, so bearings only need to handle radial loads. > [!NOTE] > **Rack and Pinion** is a special case of spur gears in which one of the gears has an infinitely large diameter, becoming a straight line (the **rack**). It converts rotary ↔ linear motion (used in steering systems, CNC machines). ### 2.2 Helical Gears — The "Smooth Operator" In a **helical gear**, the teeth are cut at an angle to the axis of rotation (this angle is called the **helix angle, β**). The two meshing helical gears must have **opposite hand helices** (one right-hand, one left-hand). **Key Engineering Insights:** - **Why helix?** Because the contact starts at one end of the tooth and *gradually* progresses across the face → smooth, quiet, high-speed operation (up to **40–50 m/s**). - **Trade-off:** They produce an **axial thrust force** ($F_a$), which the bearings must accommodate (this is why we use **double-helical / herringbone** gears to cancel the axial thrust). - Used in car gearboxes, turbines, and high-speed industrial drives. ### 2.3 Bevel Gears — The "Right-Angle Specialist" A **bevel gear** has teeth cut on a **conical pitch surface** and is used to transmit motion between **intersecting shafts**, most commonly at **90°**. **Key Engineering Insights:** - Two bevel gears in mesh are like two cones *rolling* on each other (friction cones). - The **pitch angle (γ)** of a bevel gear is the angle its pitch cone makes with its axis. For a pair with shaft angle **Σ = γ₁ + γ₂**. - **Straight bevel** — straight teeth (simple, noisy at high speed). - **Spiral bevel** — curved teeth (smooth, used in automotive differentials). - **Zerol bevel** — curved teeth but with zero spiral angle (a special compromise). - **Hypoid** — used in car rear axles, where the pinion sits *below* the gear centerline. ### 2.4 Worm Gears — The "Speed Reducer" A **worm gear** is essentially a **screw (worm)** driving a **toothed wheel (worm wheel)**. The shafts are **non-intersecting and non-parallel** (typically **90°** in space). **Key Engineering Insights:** - The **lead (l)** of the worm and the **number of teeth (N)** of the worm wheel determine the velocity ratio. - Provides a **very high reduction ratio** in a single stage (often 10:1 to 100:1). - **Self-locking** capability — a single-thread worm cannot be back-driven (used in elevators, cranes, lifting jacks). - **Sliding contact** → high heat generation, requires good lubrication and materials (worm is hardened steel; wheel is phosphor bronze). - **Efficiency** is generally low (50–90%). ### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Gear Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **core of all KTU numerical questions** in this module. Memorize these relations cold. | Symbol | Term | Formula | Notes / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $D$ | Pitch circle diameter | $D = m \cdot Z$ | **m** (metres), **Z** = number of teeth | | $m$ | Module | $m = D / Z$ | **Standardized** to ISO values: 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm | | $P_d$ | Diametral pitch | $P_d = Z / D$ | Inverse of module; **SI unit: teeth/inch** (mostly in the USA) | | $p_c$ | Circular pitch | $p_c = \pi \cdot m = \pi D / Z$ | Arc length between teeth on pitch circle; **m** | | $a$ | Addendum | $a = m$ (standard) | Radial height of tooth above pitch circle; **m** | | $d$ | Dedendum | $d = 1.25 \cdot m$ (standard) | Radial depth of tooth below pitch circle; **m** | | $h$ | Whole depth | $h = a + d = 2.25 \cdot m$ | $h = a + d$; **m** | | $h_w$ | Working depth | $h_w = 2a = 2m$ | Depth of tooth engagement; **m** | | $c$ | Clearance | $c = d - a = 0.25 \cdot m$ | Bottom-land gap; **m** | | $t$ | Tooth thickness | $t = p_c / 2 = \pi m / 2$ | At pitch circle; **m** | | $s$ | Tooth space width | $s = p_c / 2 = \pi m / 2$ | $s = t$ on standard gears; **m** | | $D_a$ | Addendum circle diameter | $D_a = D + 2a = m(Z+2)$ | **m** | | $D_d$ | Dedendum circle diameter | $D_d = D - 2d = m(Z-2.5)$ | **m** | | $D_b$ | Base circle diameter | $D_b = D \cos \phi$ | **m**; $\phi$ = pressure angle | | $\phi$ | Pressure angle | Standard: $\phi = 20^{\circ}$ (also $14.5^{\circ}, 25^{\circ}$) | **Degrees** | | $v$ | Pitch line velocity | $v = \pi D n / 60$ | m/s; $n$ in rpm | | $i$ | Gear ratio | $i = N_2 / N_1 = D_2 / D_1$ | Dimensionless; assumes same module | | $C$ | Centre distance | $C = (D_1 + D_2)/2 = m(Z_1 + Z_2)/2$ | **m** | | $\beta$ | Helix angle (helical) | — | $0^{\circ} < \beta < 45^{\circ}$ typically | | $\gamma$ | Pitch cone angle (bevel) | $\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 = \Sigma$ | Shaft angle; usually $90^{\circ}$ | | $l$ | Lead (worm) | $l = z \cdot p_x$ | $z$ = number of worm threads | ### 2.6 The Fundamental Module–Pitch Relationship > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Priority Result:** For two gears to mesh correctly, they **must have the same module** $m$ (or, in the inch system, the same diametral pitch $P_d$). This is because the tooth size must be identical for them to interlock properly. For metric standard (ISO): $p_c = \pi m$ → this is the **circular pitch** and is the actual arc length between adjacent teeth on the pitch circle. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility of $m$ and $P_d$:** By standardizing the module (ISO 54:1996), gear manufacturers across the world can produce *interchangeable* gears. Two gears of module $m = 2$ mm from India, Germany, or Japan will mesh perfectly — a true engineering triumph of standardization. ### 2.7 Pressure Angle — The Hidden Player The **pressure angle $\phi$** is the angle between the common normal at the pitch point (line of action) and the common tangent to the pitch circles. Standard values are **$14.5^{\circ}$**, **$20^{\circ}$** (most common today), and **$25^{\circ}$** (high-load applications). - Higher $\phi$ → stronger tooth, less undercut, but more radial load and noise. - Modern design: $\phi = 20^{\circ}$ is the KTU-recommended default. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation Between Module $m$ and Circular Pitch $p_c$ **Statement to Prove:** $$p_c = \pi \, m$$ **Starting Concept:** The circular pitch $p_c$ is the arc length (measured along the pitch circle) between corresponding points on two adjacent teeth. For a gear with $Z$ teeth, the total pitch-circle circumference $\pi D$ is divided into $Z$ equal arc-lengths, each equal to $p_c$. **Step-by-step Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Circumference of pitch circle} &= \pi D \\ \text{Number of equal arc segments (one per tooth + one space)} &= Z \\ \text{Arc length of one segment (circular pitch)} \quad p_c &= \frac{\pi D}{Z} \end{aligned} $$ Now, recall the definition of the **module** $m$: $$m = \frac{D}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = m \, Z$$ **Substitute** $D = mZ$ into the expression for $p_c$: $$ \begin{aligned} p_c &= \frac{\pi (m Z)}{Z} \\ p_c &= \pi m \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,p_c = \pi \, m\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **most-asked relation** in KTU Module-4 problems. Once you have $m$, you have $p_c$, and vice-versa. Units: if $m$ is in **mm**, then $p_c$ is in **mm**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Centre Distance of Two Meshing Spur Gears **Statement to Prove:** The centre distance $C$ of two meshing spur gears is given by $$C = \frac{m \, (Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}$$ **Step-by-step Proof:** When two spur gears mesh externally, their **pitch circles are tangent** at the pitch point. The centre distance equals the **sum of the two pitch-circle radii** (since the gears are external, the radii add). $$ \begin{aligned} C &= r_1 + r_2 \\ &= \frac{D_1}{2} + \frac{D_2}{2} \\ &= \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Substitute $D_1 = m Z_1$ and $D_2 = m Z_2$ (using the definition of module — and note both gears have the **same module** because they must mesh): $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{m Z_1 + m Z_2}{2} \\ &= \frac{m \, (Z_1 + Z_2)}{2} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,C = \dfrac{m \, (Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Use:** This is the formula you use when designing a gearbox. Given a target centre distance $C$ and a required gear ratio $i = Z_2/Z_1$, you solve for $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ simultaneously. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gear Ratio in Terms of Angular Velocities **Statement:** The velocity ratio (gear ratio) of a meshing pair is $$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$ **Step-by-step Proof:** At the pitch point, the **pitch line velocity must be equal** for both gears (no slip at the pitch circles): $$v_1 = v_2$$ Expressing in terms of pitch circle radius and angular velocity: $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_1 r_1 &= \omega_2 r_2 \\ \frac{N_1 \, 2 \pi}{60} \cdot \frac{D_1}{2} &= \frac{N_2 \, 2 \pi}{60} \cdot \frac{D_2}{2} \\ N_1 D_1 &= N_2 D_2 \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging: $$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$ Substituting $D = mZ$ (and cancelling $m$, since both gears have the same module): $$ \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{m Z_2}{m Z_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} $$ $$ \boxed{\,i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** The **smaller gear (fewer teeth) rotates faster** (the pinion), and the **larger gear (more teeth) rotates slower** (the gear/wheel). Speed is inversely proportional to size. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standard Tooth Thickness at the Pitch Circle **Statement:** On a standard gear, $$t = s = \frac{p_c}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2}$$ **Step-by-step Proof:** On a standard gear, the **tooth thickness** equals the **tooth space width** (this is what makes the gear *standard*; otherwise, profile-shifted gears have $t \ne s$). Together, one tooth and one space occupy exactly **one circular pitch**: $$t + s = p_c$$ For a standard gear $t = s$, so: $$ \begin{aligned} 2t &= p_c \\ t &= \frac{p_c}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $p_c = \pi m$: $$ t = \frac{\pi m}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{\,t = s = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}\,} $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Comparison of the Four Gear Types | Property | Spur | Helical | Bevel | Worm | |---|---|---|---|---| | Shaft arrangement | Parallel | Parallel (or skew) | Intersecting (mostly $90^{\circ}$) | Non-intersecting, non-parallel | | Tooth orientation | Straight, parallel to axis | Inclined at helix angle $\beta$ | On conical surface; straight or spiral | Thread-like (worm) + circumferential (wheel) | | Relative axial motion at contact | None | Sliding along helix | Combination of rolling + sliding | Predominantly sliding | | Noise level | High at high speed | Low | Moderate–high | Low (smooth) | | Max pitch-line velocity (typical) | up to **15 m/s** | up to **50 m/s** | up to **30 m/s** | up to **30 m/s** | | Axial thrust | **None** | **Yes** (cancelled by double helical) | **Yes** | **Yes** (large on worm) | | Power capacity per stage | Moderate | High | High | High (with low efficiency) | | Velocity ratio (single stage) | Up to ~10:1 | Up to ~10:1 | Up to ~6:1 | **Up to 100:1** | | Self-locking | No | No | No | **Yes** (single-thread worm) | | Manufacturing complexity | Lowest | Moderate | High (conical form) | High (worm especially) | | Typical applications | Simple gearboxes, instruments | Car gearboxes, turbines | Differentials, hand drills | Elevators, conveyors, lifts | > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** (often asked as a "compare and justify the selection" problem). Make sure to learn at least 6 rows of this table by heart. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hierarchical Classification of Gears ```mermaid graph TD A["GEARS
