> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of undercutting in involute gears. With a neat sketch describe the formation of undercut and its effect on tooth strength. State the role of profile shift in eliminating undercutting.
>
> **(b)** A pair of 14.5° full-depth involute gears has a pinion with 18 teeth and gear with 60 teeth, module 5 mm. Determine (i) whether the pinion will suffer from interference/undercut, (ii) the length of the path of contact, and (iii) the recommended circular backlash.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Explanation of Undercutting:**

When the number of teeth on a pinion is less than the *minimum* value ($t < t_{\min}$), the **cutting tool** (hob or rack-type cutter) removes material from the **dedendum region** of the pinion tooth during manufacture, creating a **notch or "undercut"** at the base of the tooth.

**Sketch description (text-based):**

The involute profile of the pinion starts from the base circle. When the number of teeth is too small, the involute flank does not extend far enough outward to be cut by the tip of the rack-cutter. Instead, the cutter tip sweeps through the **root region**, removing material in a curve that is *not* an involute. This produces a notch below the standard dedendum circle.

**Effects on tooth strength:**

- The **tooth cross-section at the root** is reduced.
- A **stress concentration** forms at the sharp corner where the undercut begins.
- The **bending fatigue strength** of the tooth drops by 10–30% depending on undercut depth.
- The **contact ratio** (length of path of contact / circular pitch) is reduced, lowering load capacity.

**Role of profile shift:**

By applying a **positive profile shift** $+x$ to the pinion (moving the cutting tool outward by $x \cdot m$), the involute region that is being cut is shifted *away* from the base circle, so the cutter tip no longer sweeps through the root. The tooth is then cut with a **full involute profile** even when $t < t_{\min}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $t_1 = 18$ (pinion), $t_2 = 60$ (gear), $\phi = 14.5°$, $m = 5$ mm, $k = 1$.

**Step 1 — Check for undercutting:**

$$
t_{\min} = \frac{2k}{\sin^{2}\phi} = \frac{2 \times 1}{\sin^{2}(14.5°)} = \frac{2}{(0.2504)^{2}} = \frac{2}{0.06270} = 31.90
$$

Since $t_1 = 18 < t_{\min} = 32$, the **pinion will suffer from interference and undercutting.** ✔ (Marks for correct comparison)

**Step 2 — Length of path of contact:**

Pitch radii:

$$
r_1 = \frac{m t_1}{2} = \frac{5 \times 18}{2} = 45 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
r_2 = \frac{m t_2}{2} = \frac{5 \times 60}{2} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

Base radii (with $\cos 14.5° = 0.9681$):

$$
r_{b1} = 45 \times 0.9681 = 43.566 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
r_{b2} = 150 \times 0.9681 = 145.22 \text{ mm}
$$

Addenda ($a_1 = a_2 = km = 5$ mm):

$$
r_{a1} = 45 + 5 = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{a2} = 150 + 5 = 155 \text{ mm}
$$

Length of path of contact:

$$
L_{PC} = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\sqrt{50^{2} - 43.566^{2}} = \sqrt{2500 - 1898.0} = \sqrt{602.0} = 24.54 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\sqrt{155^{2} - 145.22^{2}} = \sqrt{24025 - 21088.85} = \sqrt{2936.15} = 54.19 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L_{PC} = 24.54 + 54.19 = 78.73 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommended circular backlash:**

For ordinary industrial gears:

$$
b_c = 0.06 \cdot m = 0.06 \times 5 = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

(Or, as a range, $b_c = 0.04\,m$ to $0.10\,m \Rightarrow 0.20$ mm to $0.50$ mm.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points (Examiner Warning):**
> 1. **Wrong cosine value** for 14.5°. Use $\cos(14.5°) = 0.9681$, not $0.9703$ (which is $\cos 14°$). A 1° error in angle gives a 0.1 mm error in path of contact — and a mark is deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting the standard addendum** $a = 1 \cdot m$. The problem says "full depth" — you must assume $k = 1$ unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Reporting $L_{PC}$ in the wrong units** (e.g., cm). Always use **mm** for gear geometry.
> 4. **Not concluding whether undercut occurs** — a one-line statement *"Yes, the pinion will under-cut"* is required to get full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4 (Interference / Undercut / Backlash)**

- **Interference** = contact of tip of one tooth with non-involute flank of mating gear. Happens when $t < t_{\min}$.
- **Undercutting** = physical notch at tooth root due to interference. **Wealth of marks lost** if you mix up "interference" (geometric phenomenon) with "undercutting" (manufacturing consequence).
- **Backlash** = small clearance between non-working flanks. *Two types:* circular $b_c$ (on pitch circle) and normal $b_n = b_c \cos\phi$ (on line of action).
- **Master formula:** $t_{\min} = \dfrac{2k}{\sin^{2}\phi}$
- **Standard values to memorize:**
  - 20° full depth: $t_{\min} = 18$ ($k = 1$)
  - 14.5° full depth: $t_{\min} = 32$ ($k = 1$)
  - 20° stub: $t_{\min} = 14$ ($k = 0.8$)
- **Four remedies for interference:** increase $t$, reduce $a$, increase $\phi$, apply positive profile shift.
- **Standard addendum** = $a = 1 \cdot m$ (full depth); $a = 0.8 \cdot m$ (stub tooth).
- **Empirical backlash:** $b_c = 0.04\,m$ (precision) to $0.10\,m$ (heavy machinery).
- **For unequal gears** ($G = t_2/t_1$): $t_{1,\min} = \dfrac{2k}{\sin^{2}\phi}\cdot\dfrac{G+1}{G}$ (gear ratio correction).
- **Always round $t_{\min}$ up** to next integer.
- **Examiner hot-spot:** questions often ask to (i) derive $t_{\min}$, (ii) compute $L_{PC}$ and contact ratio, (iii) recommend backlash. Practice all three together.
- **Profile shift** is the *industrial* solution when fewer teeth are needed (e.g., for compact gearboxes) — be ready to mention it as a remedy.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Interference | Undercut | Backlash**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PBMET404 Mechanics of Machinery*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Trains - Simple and Compound Gear Trains - Planetary Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Trains - Simple and Compound Gear Trains - Planetary Gear Trains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Train** is a mechanical system of two or more meshing gears used to transmit motion and power from one shaft to another. Gear trains are classified based on the relative arrangement of gears and the nature of the axes of rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Hierarchy:**
> 1. **Simple Gear Train** — Each shaft carries only one gear (single-mesh chain).
> 2. **Compound Gear Train** — At least one shaft carries two or more gears (multi-mesh chain).
> 3. **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train** — At least one gear axis revolves about another fixed gear axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle" Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle gear system**:
- The **chainring (front gear)** is the *driver* (input).
- The **rear sprocket** is the *driven* (output).
- When you shift to a *larger* rear sprocket, pedalling becomes easier (speed decreases, torque increases) — this is a **Simple Gear Train** logic.
- A **car gearbox** with multiple countershafts and two meshing pairs is a **Compound Gear Train**.
- An **automatic transmission** or **differential gear** in a car uses **Planetary Gear Trains** where one gear (the sun) sits at the centre and small planet gears orbit around it — this is *epicyclic motion* (epi = upon, cyclic = circle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Planetary Gears?** They provide a **compact, coaxial** arrangement with very high torque density and multiple speed ratios from a single unit — essential in automatic gearboxes, helicopter transmissions, and robot joint actuators.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

- **Train Value (i)** = Ratio of output angular velocity to input angular velocity, $i = \frac{N_{output}}{N_{input}}$.
- **Speed Ratio (e)** = Product of teeth on driven gears / product of teeth on driver gears.
- **Module (m)** = Standard metric parameter, $m = \frac{D}{T}$ where $D$ is the pitch circle diameter in **mm** and $T$ is the number of teeth.
- **Centre Distance (C)** = $C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$ for external meshing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Simple Gear Train — Operational Logic

In a simple gear train, each shaft carries only one gear. The sequence is **Driver → Driven₁ → Driven₂ → ...** with the *last* gear being the final output.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**
- Gear 1 (driver) meshes externally with Gear 2 (driven).
- Linear velocity at the pitch point is identical: $v = \pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$.
- Simplifying: $N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2$ (since $D = mT$ and $m$ is constant for a given module).
- Extending to the full train: $N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2 = N_3 T_3 = \dots$
- Therefore, the **speed ratio** is:
$$e = \frac{N_1}{N_{last}} = \frac{\text{Product of driven teeth}}{\text{Product of driver teeth}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a simple gear train with even number of gears → output rotates **same direction** as input (no reversal).
> For odd number of external meshes → output rotates **opposite direction** to input.

### 2.2 Compound Gear Train — Operational Logic

In a compound gear train, one shaft carries **two rigidly attached gears** (compound gear), allowing speed changes in two (or more) stages. The structure is: Gear 1 (driver) → Gear 2 (driven) and Gear 3 (compound with 2) → Gear 4 (driven), etc.

**Why compound?** A single pair cannot easily achieve very high or very low speed ratios due to practical limits on gear size. Compound trains offer **multi-stage reduction** in a compact space.

### 2.3 Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train — Deep Logic

A planetary gear train has gears whose axes **revolve** about the axis of another fixed gear. The essential parts are:
- **Sun Gear (S)** — Central gear, fixed or input.
- **Planet Gears (P)** — 2 or 3 small gears revolving around the sun.
- **Ring Gear / Annulus (R)** — Outer internal-toothed gear (or can be fixed).
- **Arm / Carrier (A)** — The link on which planet gears are mounted.

**Geometric constraint (for standard configuration):**
$$T_R = T_S + 2 T_P$$

where $T_S$, $T_P$, $T_R$ are teeth on sun, planet, and ring respectively.

**The "Trick" of Analysis — Three Methods:**

| Method | Core Idea | KTU Exam Favourite? |
|---|---|---|
| **Tabular Method** | Two-step: (1) Hold arm fixed, rotate input, (2) Hold input fixed, rotate arm, then add. | ⭐⭐⭐ Most common |
| **Analytical / Relative Velocity Method** | Subtract arm speed from every gear, reducing epicyclic → simple train. | ⭐⭐⭐ Most common |
| **Formula Method** | Direct use of $(ω_1 - ω_a)/(ω_2 - ω_a) = \pm \frac{\text{teeth product}}{\text{teeth product}}$ | ⭐⭐ Quicker but error-prone |

**Sign convention in planetary kinematics:**
- **External mesh** → **negative** sign (reverse rotation).
- **Internal mesh** → **positive** sign (same direction rotation).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Gear Train Speed Ratio | $e = \frac{N_1}{N_n} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdot \dots \cdot T_n}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdot \dots \cdot T_{n-1}}$ | dimensionless | External mesh, same module |
| 2 | Simple Gear Train — Idler Effect | $e = \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_4}$, idler $T_2 = T_3$ cancels? No — idler reverses direction | dimensionless | $T_{idler}$ cancels in magnitude but affects sense |
| 3 | Compound Gear Train Speed | $e = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{T_4}{T_3} \cdot \dots$ | dimensionless | Even drivers, even driven |
| 4 | Compound Train — Direction | Output direction $= (-1)^m$ where $m$ = number of external meshes | dimensionless | Each external mesh reverses direction |
| 5 | Planetary — Geometric Relation | $T_R = T_S + 2 T_P$ | teeth | Standard concentric configuration |
| 6 | Planetary — General Kinematic Equation | $\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_a}{\omega_2 - \omega_a} = \pm \frac{\text{teeth of driven in chain}}{\text{teeth of driver in chain}}$ | rad/s | Sign per mesh type |
| 7 | Planetary — Sun Fixed, Arm Input | $\omega_R = \omega_a \left(1 + \frac{T_S}{T_R}\right)$ | rad/s | $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ |
| 8 | Planetary — Ring Fixed, Sun Input | $\omega_a = \omega_S \cdot \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R}$ | rad/s | Reduction mode |
| 9 | Centre Distance Constraint | $C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$ | mm | External mesh only |
| 10 | Pitch Line Velocity | $v = \pi m N T$ | mm/s | Constant at mesh point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state whether each mesh is *external* or *internal* before applying the sign. Forgetting the sign in planetary problems is the **#1 mark-loser** in board papers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Simple Gear Trains** — Electric fans, mixers, watches.
- **Compound Gear Trains** — Manual car gearboxes, lathe headstocks, machine tool speed reducers.
- **Planetary Gear Trains** — Automatic car transmissions (Toyota, Honda CVT), helicopter main rotor gearboxes (gear ratio ~1:100), wind turbine yaw drives, robotics joint drives, bicycle hub gears (Shimano Nexus), 3D printer extruders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Simple Gear Train Speed Ratio

**Given:** Two meshing spur gears 1 and 2 with $T_1$ and $T_2$ teeth, rotating at $N_1$ and $N_2$ rpm, having pitch diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$.

**Step 1 — Equal pitch line velocity at the mesh point:**
$$v = \pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation $D = mT$ (module is constant for meshing gears):**
$$\pi (mT_1) N_1 = \pi (mT_2) N_2$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms $\pi$ and $m$:**
$$T_1 N_1 = T_2 N_2$$

**Step 4 — Speed ratio $e$:**
$$e = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 5 — Extend to a chain of gears (simple train) where $N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2 = N_3 T_3 = \dots = N_n T_n$:**
$$e = \frac{N_1}{N_n} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} \cdot \frac{T_4}{T_3} \cdots \frac{T_n}{T_{n-1}} = \frac{T_n}{T_1} \quad \text{(telescoping product)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **simple train of 4 gears** where the first and last are coaxial: $N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2 = N_3 T_3 = N_4 T_4$. The intermediate teeth $T_2$ and $T_3$ appear on both sides and **cancel out** — the train value depends only on the **first and last gear**!

### 3.2 Derivation II — Compound Gear Train Speed Ratio

**Given:** Gear 1 (driver, $T_1$) meshes with Gear 2 (driven, $T_2$). Gears 2 and 3 are rigidly fixed on the same shaft (compound shaft). Gear 3 meshes with Gear 4 (driven, $T_4$).

**Step 1 — First mesh (Gear 1 to Gear 2):**
$$T_1 N_1 = T_2 N_2 \implies N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

**Step 2 — Compound shaft constraint:** Gears 2 and 3 rotate at the same angular velocity.
$$N_3 = N_2$$

**Step 3 — Second mesh (Gear 3 to Gear 4):**
$$T_3 N_3 = T_4 N_4 \implies N_4 = N_3 \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_4}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and chain:**
$$N_4 = N_2 \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_4} = N_1 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_4}$$

**Step 5 — Final compound speed ratio:**
$$e_{compound} = \frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3}$$

**Step 6 — Generalising to $k$ stages:**
$$e = \frac{N_1}{N_{last}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{T_{2i}}{T_{2i-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike a simple train, the intermediate teeth in a compound train do **NOT cancel** — the compound shaft's two gears have different tooth counts. This is what allows a compound train to achieve ratios that a simple train cannot (e.g., 1:100 in a small space).

### 3.3 Derivation III — Planetary Gear Train (Relative Velocity / Analytical Method)

**Given:** A simple epicyclic gear train with Sun (S, $T_S$), Planet (P, $T_P$), Ring (R, $T_R$), and Arm (A). 

**Step 1 — The "Subtract the Arm" trick:**
To convert the epicyclic train into a simple fixed-axis train, we **mentally lock the arm** and view motion relative to the arm. This makes every gear rotate with respect to the arm.

Define relative angular velocity:
$$\omega_x^{rel} = \omega_x - \omega_A$$

**Step 2 — Apply simple-train kinematics to the relative velocities:**

The sun (1) meshes externally with the planet (2):
$$(\omega_1 - \omega_A) T_1 = -(\omega_2 - \omega_A) T_2$$

The negative sign is because external mesh reverses rotation.

The planet (2) meshes internally with the ring (3):
$$(\omega_2 - \omega_A) T_2 = +(\omega_3 - \omega_A) T_3$$

Positive sign because internal mesh preserves direction.

**Step 3 — Generalised kinematic equation between sun (1) and ring (3), eliminating planet (2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_A}{\omega_3 - \omega_A} &= -\frac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} \\
&= -\frac{T_3}{T_1} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master equation** for the simple planetary gear train.

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions for typical KTU problems:**

**Case A — Sun fixed ($\omega_S = 0$), Arm input, Ring output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0 - \omega_A}{\omega_R - \omega_A} &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S} \\
-\omega_A &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S}(\omega_R - \omega_A) \\
-\omega_A &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S}\omega_R + \frac{T_R}{T_S}\omega_A \\
\omega_R \left(\frac{T_R}{T_S}\right) &= \omega_A \left(1 + \frac{T_R}{T_S}\right) \\
\omega_R &= \omega_A \cdot \frac{1 + \frac{T_R}{T_S}}{\frac{T_R}{T_S}} = \omega_A \cdot \frac{T_S + T_R}{T_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B — Ring fixed ($\omega_R = 0$), Sun input, Arm output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\omega_S - \omega_A}{0 - \omega_A} &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S} \\
\frac{\omega_S - \omega_A}{-\omega_A} &= -\frac{T_R}{T_S} \\
\omega_S - \omega_A &= \frac{T_R}{T_S}\omega_A \\
\omega_S &= \omega_A\left(1 + \frac{T_R}{T_S}\right) \\
\omega_A &= \frac{\omega_S}{1 + \frac{T_R}{T_S}} = \frac{\omega_S T_S}{T_S + T_R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with geometric constraint $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$:**

For Case B: $T_S + T_R = T_S + T_S + 2T_P = 2T_S + 2T_P = 2(T_S + T_P)$, so $\omega_A = \frac{\omega_S T_S}{2(T_S + T_P)}$ — a clean **speed reduction**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Planetary Configurations

| Configuration | Fixed Member | Input | Output | Speed Relation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Sun ($S$) | Arm ($A$) | Ring ($R$) | $\omega_R = \omega_A \cdot \frac{T_S + T_R}{T_R}$ | Overdrive / Reverse in auto gearbox |
| **B** | Ring ($R$) | Sun ($S$) | Arm ($A$) | $\omega_A = \omega_S \cdot \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R}$ | Forward gear in auto gearbox |
| **C** | Sun ($S$) | Ring ($R$) | Arm ($A$) | $\omega_A = \omega_R \cdot \frac{T_R}{T_S + T_R}$ | Reverse gear in auto gearbox |
| **D** | Arm ($A$) | Sun ($S$) | Ring ($R$) | $\omega_R = -\omega_S \cdot \frac{T_S}{T_R}$ | Speed reversal (negative ratio) |
| **E** | Sun + Ring Coupled | Sun | Arm | $\omega_A = \omega_S \cdot \frac{T_S + T_R}{2T_R + T_S}$ | Compound direct drive |
| **F** | Carrier | Sun + Ring both input | Planet | Differential action | Automotive differential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration F is the differential gear** — when sun and ring rotate at the same speed, the planet does not rotate; when they differ, the planet rotates to balance the path lengths. This is what allows a car to take turns smoothly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Simple Gear Train (Block Functional Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram represents the **kinematic chain flow** of a simple gear train, where each block represents a gear with a defined tooth count and angular velocity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["INPUT SHAFT 1 N1 rpm T1 teeth"] --> m1["EXTERNAL MESH 1 m1 = T1 over T2"]
    m1 --> s2["INTERMEDIATE SHAFT 2 N2 rpm T2 teeth"]
    s2 --> m2["EXTERNAL MESH 2 m2 = T2 over T3"]
    m2 --> s3["SHAFT 3 N3 rpm T3 teeth"]
    s3 --> m3["EXTERNAL MESH 3 m3 = T3 over T4"]
    m3 --> out1["OUTPUT SHAFT 4 N4 rpm T4 teeth"]
    out1 --> eq1["SPEED RATIO e equals T2 T3 T4 over T1 T2 T3 equals T4 over T1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, the intermediate teeth $T_2$ and $T_3$ appear in both numerator and denominator and **cancel** in a simple train. This visually reinforces why simple trains are limited in their reduction capability.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Compound Gear Train (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in2["INPUT SHAFT N1 T1 teeth"] --> cm1["EXTERNAL MESH 1 m1 = T1 over T2"]
    cm1 --> cs1["COMPOUND SHAFT 2 3 Gears 2 and 3 rotate together N2 = N3"]
    cs1 --> cm2["EXTERNAL MESH 2 m2 = T3 over T4"]
    cm2 --> out2["OUTPUT SHAFT N4 T4 teeth"]
    out2 --> eqc["SPEED RATIO e compound equals T1 T3 over T2 T4"]
    cs1 --> note1["NOTE COMPOUND SHAFT has different teeth T2 and T3 so they DO NOT cancel"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sun["SUN GEAR S T_S teeth Central axis input or fixed"]
    ring["RING GEAR R T_R teeth Outer internal teeth"]
    planet["PLANET GEAR P T_P teeth 2 to 3 in number"]
    arm["ARM CARRIER A Holds planet gears rotates about sun axis"]
    planet --> sun
    planet --> ring
    arm --> planet
    sun --> kin["KINEMATIC EQ omega S minus omega A over omega R minus omega A equals minus T_R over T_S"]
    ring --> kin
    arm --> kin
    kin --> apps["APPLICATIONS AUTOMATIC GEARBOX HELICOPTER TRANSMISSION DIFFERENTIAL ROBOTICS"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Tabular Method Template for Planetary Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1["ROW 1 Hold Arm A fixed Rotate input by plus 1 rev Note all gear rotations using train ratio"]
    r2["ROW 2 Hold Input fixed Rotate Arm A by plus 1 rev Note all gear rotations = plus 1 for arm plus T P over T S for sun etc"]
    r3["ROW 3 ADD Row 1 and Row 2 This gives actual rotations in the live mechanism"]
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> outp["OUTPUT Compute desired speed ratio from added row"]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Direction-of-Rotation Logic (External vs Internal Mesh)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ext["EXTERNAL MESH Sign = MINUS Gears rotate in OPPOSITE directions"]
    int["INTERNAL MESH Sign = PLUS Gears rotate in SAME direction"]
    ext --> comp["COMPOUND SIGN Multiply signs of all meshes in chain"]
    int --> comp
    comp --> total["FINAL SIGN OF SPEED RATIO Positive = same direction Negative = opposite direction"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a simple gear train. Derive the expression for the speed ratio of a simple gear train of four gears.**

**Model Answer:**

A simple gear train is one in which each shaft carries only one gear, and the gears mesh in a single continuous chain. For four gears with teeth $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ rotating at $N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$ rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi D_1 N_1 &= \pi D_2 N_2 \\
m T_1 N_1 &= m T_2 N_2 \\
T_1 N_1 &= T_2 N_2 = T_3 N_3 = T_4 N_4
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$e = \frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_3 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3} = \frac{T_4}{T_1}$$

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any three differences between a simple gear train and a planetary gear train.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Simple Gear Train | Planetary Gear Train |
|---|---|---|
| Axis of gears | Fixed in space | At least one axis revolves about another |
| Compactness | Less compact, requires more space | Highly compact, coaxial arrangement |
| Speed ratio range | Limited (single pair or telescoping) | Wide range with single unit, multiple ratios |
| Tooth loading | Higher per tooth | Distributed among multiple planets, lower per tooth |
| Direction change | Requires idler or extra mesh | Achieved by fixing different members |
| Application | Fans, mixers, basic reducers | Automatic transmissions, differentials |

(3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference, up to 3.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Compound Gear Train Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** A compound gear train consists of four gears: Gear A (20 teeth, driver) meshes with Gear B (40 teeth) on the intermediate shaft. Gear C (25 teeth, on the same intermediate shaft) meshes with Gear D (75 teeth, output).

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the kinematic arrangement and determine the speed of the output shaft when the input runs at 1200 rpm.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the input torque is 50 N·m, find the torque on the output shaft (assume 100% efficiency).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Identify the configuration:
- Gear A (driver): $T_A = 20$, $N_A = 1200$ rpm
- Gear B (driven, on compound shaft): $T_B = 40$
- Gear C (driver, on compound shaft with B): $T_C = 25$
- Gear D (driven, output): $T_D = 75$

Step 2 — Apply compound train formula:
$$e = \frac{N_A}{N_D} = \frac{T_B \cdot T_D}{T_A \cdot T_C}$$

Step 3 — Substitute values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{40 \times 75}{20 \times 25} \\
e &= \frac{3000}{500} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compute output speed:
$$N_D = \frac{N_A}{e} = \frac{1200}{6} = 200 \text{ rpm}$$

The output shaft rotates at **200 rpm**.

**Direction check:** Two external meshes → $(-1)^2 = +1$ → output rotates **same direction** as input.

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Use the power-balance equation (assuming 100% efficiency):
$$P_{in} = P_{out} \implies T_A \omega_A = T_D \omega_D$$

Step 2 — Therefore:
$$T_D = T_A \cdot \frac{\omega_A}{\omega_D} = T_A \cdot e = 50 \times 6 = 300 \text{ N·m}$$

The output torque is **300 N·m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some students write $T_D = T_A \cdot e$ but treat $e$ as the **reciprocal** they computed for speed. Always double-check: torque is amplified by the **same** factor by which speed is reduced. Mark deduction: **-2 marks** for sign/direction errors.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Planetary Gear Train Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A simple planetary gear train has a sun gear with 20 teeth, three identical planet gears with 30 teeth each, and a ring gear with 80 teeth. The sun gear is fixed. The arm carrying the planets rotates at 100 rpm clockwise.

**(a) [7 marks]** Verify the geometric constraint and derive the speed of the ring gear.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the speed of each planet gear (magnitude and direction) relative to the ground.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Geometric constraint verification:
$$T_R = T_S + 2T_P \implies 80 \stackrel{?}{=} 20 + 2(30) = 20 + 60 = 80 \;\checkmark$$

The constraint is satisfied.

Step 2 — Apply the master equation for the simple planetary train:
$$\frac{\omega_S - \omega_A}{\omega_R - \omega_A} = -\frac{T_R}{T_S}$$

Step 3 — Substitute $\omega_S = 0$ (sun fixed) and $\omega_A = 100$ rpm (clockwise, taken as positive):
$$\frac{0 - 100}{\omega_R - 100} = -\frac{80}{20} = -4$$

Step 4 — Solve:
$$
\begin{aligned}
-100 &= -4(\omega_R - 100) \\
-100 &= -4\omega_R + 400 \\
4\omega_R &= 500 \\
\omega_R &= 125 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The ring gear rotates at **125 rpm clockwise** (same direction as the arm, since the result is positive).

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Use the sun–planet mesh relation. First, the relative velocity equation for the sun–planet external mesh:
$$(\omega_S - \omega_A) T_S = -(\omega_P - \omega_A) T_P$$

Step 2 — Substitute $\omega_S = 0$, $\omega_A = 100$, $T_S = 20$, $T_P = 30$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(0 - 100)(20) &= -(\omega_P - 100)(30) \\
-2000 &= -30(\omega_P - 100) \\
\omega_P - 100 &= \frac{2000}{30} = 66.67 \\
\omega_P &= 166.67 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — The planet gear rotates at approximately **166.67 rpm**, in the **same direction** as the arm (positive sign).

Step 4 — Verify using ring–planet internal mesh:
$$(\omega_P - \omega_A) T_P = +(\omega_R - \omega_A) T_R$$
$$(166.67 - 100)(30) \stackrel{?}{=} (125 - 100)(80)$$
$$66.67 \times 30 = 2000 \stackrel{?}{=} 25 \times 80 = 2000 \;\checkmark$$

Verified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric constraint** check: KTU examiners award **0–1 marks** if you do not verify $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ before solving.
> 2. **Sign error in the master equation:** The negative sign (external mesh) and positive sign (internal mesh) are NOT interchangeable. Wrong sign → wrong numerical answer → lose **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Using absolute values of angular velocity:** A planetary train's gears can rotate in different directions relative to ground. Always indicate **direction** explicitly.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Some students mix rpm and rad/s in the same equation. KTU standard: stick to **one unit** throughout a problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Gear Trains: Quick-Fire Revision**

- **Simple Gear Train** → Each shaft = one gear. Speed ratio = $T_{last}/T_{first}$ (intermediate teeth cancel).
- **Compound Gear Train** → At least one shaft has TWO gears. Speed ratio = $\prod (T_{driven}/T_{driver})$.
- **Direction Rule** → Each external mesh reverses rotation; sign = $(-1)^m$ where $m$ = number of external meshes.
- **Idler Gear** → Reverses direction but does **NOT** change the **magnitude** of speed ratio.
- **Planetary / Epicyclic Train** → Has a sun, planet(s), ring, and arm. Compact, coaxial, high torque density.
- **Geometric Rule** → $T_R = T_S + 2T_P$ (for simple planetary with external sun-planet + internal planet-ring).
- **Master Equation** → $\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_A}{\omega_2 - \omega_A} = \pm \frac{T_{2,chain}}{T_{1,chain}}$ where signs follow mesh type.
- **Sun Fixed** → Ring output speed = $\omega_A \cdot \frac{T_S + T_R}{T_R}$.
- **Ring Fixed** → Arm output speed = $\omega_S \cdot \frac{T_S}{T_S + T_R}$ (used in **forward gear** of automatic transmissions).
- **Analytical Method** → Subtract $\omega_A$ from all gears to reduce epicyclic → simple train.
- **Tabular Method** → Row 1 (arm fixed, input = +1), Row 2 (input fixed, arm = +1), Row 3 (sum).
- **Differential Gear** → When sun and ring rotate together, planets don't spin; when they differ, planets compensate.
- **Power Transmission** → In a 100% efficient gear train, $T \cdot \omega$ is conserved: $T_{out} = T_{in} \times e$ (where $e$ is the speed reduction ratio).
- **Most-tested KTU patterns:** (i) Find output speed given tooth counts; (ii) Find tooth count given desired ratio; (iii) Direction of rotation with multiple meshes; (iv) Multi-input planetary (compound planetary, two degrees of freedom).
- **Common application question:** "In an automatic car, why is a planetary gear train preferred over a simple train?" → Answer: Compact, coaxial, multiple ratios, lower wear, smoother engagement.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — GEAR TRAINS**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech PBMET404 — Mechanics of Machinery.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tabulation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tabulation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABULATION METHOD (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **Tabulation Method** is a systematic numerical procedure in the design of cam profiles wherein the cam rotation angle (θ) is divided into a finite number of equal angular increments, and the corresponding follower displacement, velocity, and acceleration are computed and tabulated at each increment. It is especially useful when the follower motion is specified as a complex mathematical function, as a graphical curve, or when analytical differentiation of the motion law is cumbersome.

The method essentially converts a continuous kinematic function into a **discrete data table**, from which the cam profile geometry can be generated using either:
- **Coordinate plotting** of displacement versus cam angle, OR
- **Pitch curve generation** by rotating the follower displacement data about the cam center.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Staircase" Approach to Motion**
> Imagine you are walking up a smooth ramp, and you want to know your exact height at every 1-meter step you take along the ramp. Since the ramp is a smooth curve, finding your exact height at every point would require solving a continuous equation. Instead, you decide to "sample" your height at every meter and **write down** those heights in a table. By doing this, you've approximated the smooth continuous motion as a series of small, discrete "steps."
> The **Tabulation Method** does the same thing for a cam-follower system. Instead of trying to derive complex continuous equations for the follower at every angle, the designer **tabulates (records in a table)** the follower displacement at small, fixed angular intervals (say, every 10° or 15° of cam rotation). These tabulated values are then used to construct the cam profile or to verify the smoothness of motion.

## Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Cam rotation angle | θ | Angle turned by cam from start position |
| Angular increment | Δθ or h | Small, fixed angular step used for tabulation |
| Follower displacement | s | Linear travel of follower from base circle |
| Follower velocity | v | Rate of change of displacement (ds/dt) |
| Follower acceleration | a | Rate of change of velocity (d²s/dt²) |
| Angular velocity of cam | ω | Speed of cam rotation (rad/s), usually assumed **constant** |

## Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Convention in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - The cam angular velocity **ω is assumed constant** in all standard problems.
> - Time for one increment: $\Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\omega}$ (in seconds)
> - Standard angular increments used: **5°, 10°, 15°, 20°, 30°**
> - Velocity unit: **m/s** ; Acceleration unit: **m/s²**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic — Why and How the Method Works

The Tabulation Method is built upon the foundational principle of **Numerical Differentiation using Finite Differences**. It bypasses the need to analytically differentiate complex displacement functions, replacing it with discrete numerical approximations.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1: Discretization of the Cam Rotation Cycle**
  - *Why:* Continuous equations are hard to plot and solve at every instant.
  - *How:* The total cam rotation (typically **360°** for a full cycle, or the specific outstroke/return stroke angle) is divided into `n` equal angular steps of magnitude $h = \dfrac{\beta}{n}$, where $\beta$ is the total angle of the motion segment.

- **Step 2: Tabulation of Displacement Values**
  - *Why:* We need a database of follower positions at each sampled angle.
  - *How:* Using the given motion law (SHM, Cycloidal, Uniform Velocity, etc.), calculate the displacement $s$ at each angle $\theta_0, \theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ and tabulate them.

- **Step 3: Computation of Velocity using Numerical Differentiation**
  - *Why:* Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, and analytically differentiating tabular data is impossible.
  - *How:* Apply the **Central Difference Formula** for the first derivative.
  - At any intermediate point $n$:
  $$v_n = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta} \approx \omega \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2h}$$

- **Step 4: Computation of Acceleration using Numerical Differentiation**
  - *Why:* Acceleration determines the dynamic loading and jerk on the follower.
  - *How:* Apply the **Second Central Difference Formula**.
  $$a_n = \frac{d^2s}{dt^2} = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2} \approx \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{h^2}$$

- **Step 5: Boundary Corrections (End Points)**
  - *Why:* The central difference formula cannot be applied at the very first or last row of the table.
  - *How:* Use **Forward Difference** at the start and **Backward Difference** at the end:
  $$v_0 \approx \omega \cdot \frac{-3s_0 + 4s_1 - s_2}{2h}$$
  $$v_n \approx \omega \cdot \frac{3s_n - 4s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{2h}$$
  $$a_0 \approx \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_0 - 2s_1 + s_2}{h^2}$$
  $$a_n \approx \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_n - 2s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{h^2}$$

- **Step 6: Profile Generation (Coordinate Plotting)**
  - *Why:* The tabulated data must be translated into a manufacturable cam profile.
  - *How:* For a radial (disc) cam with a translating roller follower, each tabulated displacement $s_i$ is plotted at the corresponding angle $\theta_i$ on a polar plot. The smooth curve through these points becomes the **pitch curve**, and a circle of roller radius $r_r$ offset from this curve forms the cam profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pattern Insight:** The Tabulation Method question almost always appears as a 7-mark sub-part in a 14-mark cam design problem. Students are expected to construct the full table and **state the formulas used** to compute v and a — not just final numerical values.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Master These for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applies At |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angular increment (radians) | $h = \dfrac{\beta}{n}$ | All points |
| 2 | Time increment | $\Delta t = \dfrac{h}{\omega}$ | All points |
| 3 | Displacement (interior) | $s_n = f(\theta_n)$ from motion law | Interior points |
| 4 | Velocity (interior) | $v_n = \omega \cdot \dfrac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2h}$ | $n = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$ |
| 5 | Acceleration (interior) | $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{h^2}$ | $n = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$ |
| 6 | Velocity (start) | $v_0 = \omega \cdot \dfrac{-3s_0 + 4s_1 - s_2}{2h}$ | $n = 0$ only |
| 7 | Velocity (end) | $v_n = \omega \cdot \dfrac{3s_n - 4s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{2h}$ | $n = n$ only |
| 8 | Acceleration (start) | $a_0 = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{s_0 - 2s_1 + s_2}{h^2}$ | $n = 0$ only |
| 9 | Acceleration (end) | $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{s_n - 2s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{h^2}$ | $n = n$ only |
| 10 | Pitch curve radius | $R_p = R_b + s_n$ | All points (radial cam) |

> **Units Reminder:** $\omega$ in rad/s, $h$ in **radians** (convert from degrees: $h° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$), $s$ in meters.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Use This Method:**
> - **Automotive Camshafts:** Designing valve-lift profiles for IC engine camshafts where motion laws are complex and analytical differentiation is infeasible.
> - **CNC & Automation:** Generating motion profiles for cam-actuated pick-and-place mechanisms.
> - **Textile Machinery:** Designing cam profiles for shuttle and weft-insertion mechanisms, where any acceleration spike can cause yarn breakage.
> - **Robotics:** Computing joint trajectories using sampled data when exact kinematic equations are unavailable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Central Difference Formula for Velocity

We begin with the Taylor series expansion of displacement about point $n$:

$$
s_{n+1} = s_n + h \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}\bigg|_n + \frac{h^2}{2!} \cdot \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_n + \frac{h^3}{3!} \cdot \frac{d^3s}{d\theta^3}\bigg|_n + \dots
$$

$$
s_{n-1} = s_n - h \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}\bigg|_n + \frac{h^2}{2!} \cdot \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_n - \frac{h^3}{3!} \cdot \frac{d^3s}{d\theta^3}\bigg|_n + \dots
$$

**Subtracting the second equation from the first**, the even-order terms cancel out:

$$
s_{n+1} - s_{n-1} = 2h \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}\bigg|_n + \frac{2h^3}{3!} \cdot \frac{d^3s}{d\theta^3}\bigg|_n + \dots
$$

**Neglecting higher-order terms** (third derivative and beyond, since $h$ is small):

$$
s_{n+1} - s_{n-1} \approx 2h \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}\bigg|_n
$$

**Solving for the first derivative:**

$$
\frac{ds}{d\theta}\bigg|_n \approx \frac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2h}
$$

**Multiplying both sides by $\omega$** (since $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$):

$$
\boxed{v_n = \omega \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2h}}
$$

> This is the **central difference formula for velocity**, which has a truncation error of order $h^2$ — making it more accurate than forward or backward differences.

## Derivation 2: Central Difference Formula for Acceleration

**Adding the two Taylor series expansions**, the odd-order terms cancel out:

$$
s_{n+1} + s_{n-1} = 2s_n + h^2 \cdot \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_n + \frac{2h^4}{4!} \cdot \frac{d^4s}{d\theta^4}\bigg|_n + \dots
$$

**Rearranging and neglecting higher-order terms:**

$$
\frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_n \approx \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{h^2}
$$

**Multiplying by $\omega^2$** (since $a = \dfrac{d^2s}{dt^2} = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}$):

$$
\boxed{a_n = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{h^2}}
$$

## Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU ESE Pattern):**
> A cam rotating at **$\omega = 20$ rad/s** (uniform) drives a knife-edge follower through a total lift of **30 mm** during an outstroke of **60°** following **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**. The follower dwells for the remaining **30°** of the cam rotation. Use the **Tabulation Method** with an interval of **15°** to determine the velocity and acceleration of the follower at the end of each 15° interval.

### Solution Setup

- Total outstroke angle: $\beta = 60°$
- Number of intervals: $n = \dfrac{60°}{15°} = 4$ intervals → 5 points (0°, 15°, 30°, 45°, 60°)
- Angular increment: $h = 15° = \dfrac{\pi}{12} = 0.2618$ rad
- Lift: $S = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- SHM displacement formula: $s = \dfrac{S}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$

### Step A: Tabulate Displacement

At $\theta = 0°$: $\quad s_0 = \dfrac{0.030}{2}\left[1 - \cos(0)\right] = 0$

At $\theta = 15°$: $\quad s_1 = 0.015\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot 15°}{60°}\right)\right] = 0.015[1 - \cos(45°)]$

$$
s_1 = 0.015 \cdot [1 - 0.7071] = 0.015 \cdot 0.2929 = 0.004394 \text{ m} = 4.394 \text{ mm}
$$

At $\theta = 30°$: $\quad s_2 = 0.015\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot 30°}{60°}\right)\right] = 0.015[1 - \cos(90°)]$

$$
s_2 = 0.015 \cdot [1 - 0] = 0.015 \text{ m} = 15.000 \text{ mm}
$$

At $\theta = 45°$: By symmetry of SHM about the midpoint, $s_3 = S - s_1 = 30 - 4.394 = 25.606$ mm

At $\theta = 60°$: $\quad s_4 = 0.015[1 - \cos(180°)] = 0.015 \cdot 2 = 0.030$ m $= 30.000$ mm ✓

| Point | $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | 15 | 0.004394 |
| 2 | 30 | 0.015000 |
| 3 | 45 | 0.025606 |
| 4 | 60 | 0.030000 |

### Step B: Compute Velocity at Interior Points ($n = 1, 2, 3$)

**At $n = 1$ (15°):**

$$
v_1 = \omega \cdot \frac{s_2 - s_0}{2h} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.0150 - 0.0000}{2 \cdot 0.2618}
$$

$$
v_1 = 20 \cdot \frac{0.0150}{0.5236} = 20 \cdot 0.02865 = 0.5730 \text{ m/s}
$$

**At $n = 2$ (30°):**

$$
v_2 = 20 \cdot \frac{s_3 - s_1}{2h} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.025606 - 0.004394}{0.5236}
$$

$$
v_2 = 20 \cdot \frac{0.021212}{0.5236} = 20 \cdot 0.04051 = 0.8102 \text{ m/s}
$$

**At $n = 3$ (45°):**

$$
v_3 = 20 \cdot \frac{s_4 - s_2}{2h} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.0300 - 0.0150}{0.5236} = 20 \cdot 0.02865 = 0.5730 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step C: Compute Velocity at End Points

**At $n = 0$ (0°) — Forward Difference:**

$$
v_0 = \omega \cdot \frac{-3s_0 + 4s_1 - s_2}{2h} = 20 \cdot \frac{0 + 4(0.004394) - 0.0150}{0.5236}
$$

$$
v_0 = 20 \cdot \frac{0.017576 - 0.0150}{0.5236} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.002576}{0.5236} = 0.0984 \text{ m/s}
$$

> *Note: For pure SHM with zero velocity at start, the forward difference gives a small non-zero numerical artifact due to truncation error.*

**At $n = 4$ (60°) — Backward Difference:**

$$
v_4 = \omega \cdot \frac{3s_4 - 4s_3 + s_2}{2h} = 20 \cdot \frac{3(0.0300) - 4(0.025606) + 0.0150}{0.5236}
$$

$$
v_4 = 20 \cdot \frac{0.0900 - 0.102424 + 0.0150}{0.5236} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.002576}{0.5236} = 0.0984 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step D: Compute Acceleration at Interior Points

**At $n = 1$ (15°):**

$$
a_1 = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_2 - 2s_1 + s_0}{h^2} = 400 \cdot \frac{0.0150 - 2(0.004394) + 0}{(0.2618)^2}
$$

$$
a_1 = 400 \cdot \frac{0.0150 - 0.008788}{0.06854} = 400 \cdot \frac{0.006212}{0.06854} = 400 \cdot 0.0906 = 36.25 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**At $n = 2$ (30°):**

$$
a_2 = 400 \cdot \frac{s_3 - 2s_2 + s_1}{h^2} = 400 \cdot \frac{0.025606 - 0.0300 + 0.004394}{0.06854}
$$

$$
a_2 = 400 \cdot \frac{0}{0.06854} = 0 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

> *This correctly reflects the zero acceleration at mid-stroke for SHM motion.*

**At $n = 3$ (45°):**

$$
a_3 = 400 \cdot \frac{s_4 - 2s_3 + s_2}{h^2} = 400 \cdot \frac{0.0300 - 0.051212 + 0.0150}{0.06854}
$$

$$
a_3 = 400 \cdot \frac{-0.006212}{0.06854} = -36.25 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

### Final Compiled Table

| $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (mm) | $v$ (m/s) | $a$ (m/s²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.0984 | — |
| 15 | 4.394 | 0.5730 | 36.25 |
| 30 | 15.000 | 0.8102 | 0.00 |
| 45 | 25.606 | 0.5730 | -36.25 |
| 60 | 30.000 | 0.0984 | — |

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The peak velocity (**0.8102 m/s**) occurs at mid-stroke (30°), and the acceleration passes through zero at the same point — confirming the **smoothness and symmetry** of the SHM motion law, validated through the tabulation method.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Tabulation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input Cam Parameters] --> B[Identify Motion Law and Angles]
    B --> C[Divide Cam Rotation into Equal Intervals h]
    C --> D[Compute Displacement s at each angle]
    D --> E[Construct Displacement Table]
    E --> F{Is the point an interior point?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Central Difference for Velocity]
    F -- No --> H[Apply Forward or Backward Difference]
    G --> I[Apply Central Difference for Acceleration]
    H --> J[Apply Forward or Backward Difference for Acceleration]
    I --> K[Tabulate Final v and a Values]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Plot Displacement Curve s vs theta]
    L --> M[Plot Velocity Curve v vs theta]
    M --> N[Plot Acceleration Curve a vs theta]
    N --> O[Generate Cam Pitch Curve using Rp = Rb + s]
    O --> P[Construct Cam Profile with Roller Offset]
    P --> Q[END: Validate Smoothness and No Jerk Spikes]
```

## Schematic Representation of the Cam-Follower Sampling Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAM[Cam Rotation Cycle]
        A0[theta 0 deg] --> A1[theta 15 deg]
        A1 --> A2[theta 30 deg]
        A2 --> A3[theta 45 deg]
        A3 --> A4[theta 60 deg]
    end
    subgraph DISP[Follower Displacement Samples]
        B0[s0 equals 0 mm] --> B1[s1 equals 4.39 mm]
        B1 --> B2[s2 equals 15.00 mm]
        B2 --> B3[s3 equals 25.61 mm]
        B3 --> B4[s4 equals 30.00 mm]
    end
    subgraph KINE[Kinematic Quantities]
        C0[Central Diff Velocity v] --> D0[Central Diff Acceleration a]
        D0 --> E0[Tabulated Output Table]
    end
    A0 --- B0
    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
    A4 --- B4
    B2 --> C0
    C0 --> D0
    D0 --> E0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The schematic above shows the **three-stage data pipeline** of the Tabulation Method: (1) Angular sampling from the cam cycle, (2) Displacement evaluation at each sample, and (3) Numerical differentiation to obtain velocity and acceleration, finally compiled into a single tabulated output that drives the cam profile construction.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State the formulas used in the Tabulation Method to calculate the velocity and acceleration of a follower at the start and end points of the motion segment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **velocity at the start point** (n = 0) is calculated using the **forward difference formula**:
> $$v_0 = \omega \cdot \frac{-3s_0 + 4s_1 - s_2}{2h}$$
>
> The **velocity at the end point** (n = n) is calculated using the **backward difference formula**:
> $$v_n = \omega \cdot \frac{3s_n - 4s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{2h}$$
>
> The corresponding **acceleration formulas** at the boundaries are:
> $$a_0 = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_0 - 2s_1 + s_2}{h^2} \qquad a_n = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{s_n - 2s_{n-1} + s_{n-2}}{h^2}$$
>
> where $h$ is the angular increment in radians, $\omega$ is the cam angular velocity, and $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n$ are the tabulated follower displacements.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is the Tabulation Method preferred for cam profile generation when the follower motion is defined graphically or by a complex function?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Tabulation Method is preferred because:
> 1. It **avoids analytical differentiation** of complex or graphically-defined motion functions, which may be mathematically intractable. **[1 Mark]**
> 2. It uses **simple finite-difference numerical formulas** that can be applied uniformly across any motion law (SHM, cycloidal, polynomial, etc.). **[1 Mark]**
> 3. It produces **discrete tabulated data points** of displacement, velocity, and acceleration that can be directly plotted to generate the cam profile coordinates, making the method **graphically intuitive and computer-friendly**. **[1 Mark]**

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**A cam rotates at a uniform speed of 300 rpm and operates a roller follower through a total lift of 40 mm during an outstroke of 90° with simple harmonic motion. The follower dwells for the remaining 30° of the cam rotation. Using the Tabulation Method with an interval of 15°, determine the displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the follower at the end of each interval. Also, draw the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams.**

#### Part (a): Tabulate Displacement Values (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416$ rad/s
- Lift $S = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- Outstroke angle $\beta = 90°$
- Number of intervals $n = \dfrac{90°}{15°} = 6$ → 7 points (0°, 15°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 75°, 90°)
- $h = 15° = \dfrac{\pi}{12} = 0.2618$ rad
- SHM displacement: $s = \dfrac{S}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$

**Calculations:**

$$
s_0 = 0.020[1 - \cos(0°)] = 0
$$

$$
s_1 = 0.020[1 - \cos(30°)] = 0.020[1 - 0.8660] = 0.020 \times 0.1340 = 0.002680 \text{ m}
$$

$$
s_2 = 0.020[1 - \cos(60°)] = 0.020[1 - 0.5] = 0.010 \text{ m}
$$

$$
s_3 = 0.020[1 - \cos(90°)] = 0.020[1 - 0] = 0.020 \text{ m}
$$

$$
s_4 = 0.020[1 - \cos(120°)] = 0.020[1 + 0.5] = 0.030 \text{ m}
$$

$$
s_5 = 0.020[1 - \cos(150°)] = 0.020[1 + 0.8660] = 0.037320 \text{ m}
$$

$$
s_6 = 0.020[1 - \cos(180°)] = 0.040 \text{ m}
$$

| Point | $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (m) | $s$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0.000000 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 15 | 0.002680 | 2.680 |
| 2 | 30 | 0.010000 | 10.000 |
| 3 | 45 | 0.020000 | 20.000 |
| 4 | 60 | 0.030000 | 30.000 |
| 5 | 75 | 0.037320 | 37.320 |
| 6 | 90 | 0.040000 | 40.000 |

#### Part (b): Compute Velocity and Acceleration, Draw Diagrams (7 Marks)

**Velocity at Interior Points (n = 1 to 5):**

$$v_n = 31.416 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2 \times 0.2618} = 60.0 \cdot (s_{n+1} - s_{n-1})$$

$$
v_1 = 60.0 \times (0.0100 - 0) = 0.6000 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_2 = 60.0 \times (0.0200 - 0.00268) = 1.0392 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_3 = 60.0 \times (0.0300 - 0.0100) = 1.2000 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_4 = 60.0 \times (0.03732 - 0.0200) = 1.0392 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_5 = 60.0 \times (0.0400 - 0.0300) = 0.6000 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Velocity at End Points (Forward & Backward):**

$$
v_0 = 31.416 \cdot \frac{-3(0) + 4(0.00268) - 0.0100}{0.5236} = 31.416 \cdot \frac{0.00072}{0.5236} = 0.0432 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_6 = 31.416 \cdot \frac{3(0.040) - 4(0.03732) + 0.0300}{0.5236} = 31.416 \cdot \frac{0.00072}{0.5236} = 0.0432 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Acceleration at Interior Points:**

$$a_n = (31.416)^2 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{(0.2618)^2} = 986.96 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{0.06854}$$

$$
a_1 = 986.96 \cdot \frac{0.0100 - 0.00536 + 0}{0.06854} = 986.96 \times 0.0676 = 66.74 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
a_2 = 986.96 \cdot \frac{0.0200 - 0.0200 + 0.00268}{0.06854} = 986.96 \times 0.0391 = 38.59 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
a_3 = 986.96 \cdot \frac{0.0300 - 0.0400 + 0.0100}{0.06854} = 0 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
a_4 = 986.96 \cdot \frac{0.03732 - 0.0600 + 0.0200}{0.06854} = -38.59 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
a_5 = 986.96 \cdot \frac{0.0400 - 0.07464 + 0.0300}{0.06854} = -66.74 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Final Compiled Table:**

| $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (mm) | $v$ (m/s) | $a$ (m/s²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.0432 | — |
| 15 | 2.680 | 0.6000 | 66.74 |
| 30 | 10.000 | 1.0392 | 38.59 |
| 45 | 20.000 | 1.2000 | 0.00 |
| 60 | 30.000 | 1.0392 | -38.59 |
| 75 | 37.320 | 0.6000 | -66.74 |
| 90 | 40.000 | 0.0432 | — |

> **Diagrams (to be drawn on graph paper):**
> 1. **Displacement Diagram:** Plot $s$ (mm) vs $\theta$ (°) — smooth sinusoidal curve from 0 to 40 mm.
> 2. **Velocity Diagram:** Plot $v$ (m/s) vs $\theta$ (°) — bell-shaped curve peaking at 45°.
> 3. **Acceleration Diagram:** Plot $a$ (m/s²) vs $\theta$ (°) — crosses zero at 45°, positive during first half, negative during second half.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**A cam rotates at 200 rpm and gives a uniform acceleration and retardation to a knife-edge follower during the outstroke of 50°. The lift is 25 mm. Use the Tabulation Method with 10° intervals to determine the velocity and acceleration of the follower at each interval. Sketch the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams.**

#### Part (a): Compute Displacement Table (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944$ rad/s
- Lift $S = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Outstroke $\beta = 50°$, with uniform acceleration for first 25° and uniform retardation for next 25°.
- Intervals: 5 points (0°, 10°, 20°, 30°, 40°, 50°) → $h = 10° = 0.1745$ rad
- For **uniform acceleration (0° to 25°):** $s = \dfrac{2S}{(\beta/2)^2} \cdot \theta^2 = \dfrac{2 \times 0.025}{(25°)^2} \cdot \theta^2 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \theta^2$ (with $\theta$ in degrees)
- For **uniform retardation (25° to 50°):** $s = S - \dfrac{2S}{(\beta/2)^2} \cdot (\beta - \theta)^2$

**Calculations:**

| Point | $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (m) | $s$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 10 | $8 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 = 0.008$ | 8.000 |
| 2 | 20 | $8 \times 10^{-5} \times 400 = 0.032$ (clamped to ≤ S/2 = 0.0125) | 12.500 |
| 3 | 30 | $0.025 - 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 400 = 0.025 - 0.032 \to$ use formula $s = S - 8\times 10^{-5}(50-30)^2$ | 17.000 |
| 4 | 40 | $s = 0.025 - 8 \times 10^{-5}(10)^2$ | 23.000 |
| 5 | 50 | $0.025$ | 25.000 |

> *Note: For uniform accel/retard, parabolic segments must be capped at the midpoint displacement of $S/2$ to prevent overshoot.*

**Recomputed corrected displacement values:**

| Point | $\theta$ (°) | $s$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0.000 |
| 1 | 10 | 2.000 |
| 2 | 20 | 8.000 |
| 3 | 30 | 17.000 |
| 4 | 40 | 23.000 |
| 5 | 50 | 25.000 |

> (Using correct parabolic coefficients: $s = 2S \cdot \theta^2 / \beta^2$ for accel half, with $\beta = 50°$ gives $s = (0.05/2500) \theta^2 \cdot 1000 = 0.020 \theta^2$ mm, where $\theta$ is in degrees.)

#### Part (b): Compute Velocity and Acceleration (7 Marks)

**Velocity at Interior Points:**

$$v_n = 20.944 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}}{2 \times 0.1745} = 60.0 \cdot (s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}) \text{ m/s}$$

$$
v_1 = 60.0 \times (0.0080 - 0) = 0.480 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_2 = 60.0 \times (0.0170 - 0.0020) = 0.900 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_3 = 60.0 \times (0.0230 - 0.0080) = 0.900 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
v_4 = 60.0 \times (0.0250 - 0.0170) = 0.480 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Velocity at End Points (Forward/Backward):**

$$
v_0 = 20.944 \cdot \frac{-3(0) + 4(0.0020) - 0.0080}{0.3491} = 20.944 \cdot \frac{0}{0.3491} = 0 \text{ m/s} \checkmark
$$

$$
v_5 = 20.944 \cdot \frac{3(0.0250) - 4(0.0230) + 0.0170}{0.3491} = 20.944 \cdot \frac{0.0}{0.3491} = 0 \text{ m/s} \checkmark
$$

> *Zero velocity at the boundaries is correctly captured — characteristic of uniform accel/retard motion.*

**Acceleration at Interior Points:**

$$a_n = (20.944)^2 \cdot \frac{s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}}{0.03046}$$

For the **acceleration half (0° to 25°)**, the acceleration is theoretically **constant** at $a = 4S\omega^2/\beta^2$. For points in this region, the finite-difference formula should yield the same constant value.

$$
a_1 = 438.6 \cdot \frac{0.0080 - 0.0040 + 0}{0.03046} = 438.6 \times 0.1313 = 57.62 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

For the **retardation half (25° to 50°)**, the acceleration is constant and **negative**:

$$
a_4 = 438.6 \cdot \frac{0.0250 - 0.0460 + 0.0170}{0.03046} = 438.6 \times (-0.1313) = -57.62 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

> **Diagrams (to be drawn on graph paper):**
> 1. **Displacement Diagram:** Two parabolic arcs joining at the midpoint, forming a smooth "S" shape from 0 to 25 mm.
> 2. **Velocity Diagram:** Two straight-line segments forming a triangle, peaking at the midpoint (25°).
> 3. **Acceleration Diagram:** A **rectangular step function** — constant positive value during acceleration phase, then constant negative value during retardation phase.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians:** The angular increment $h$ must be in radians when used in the formulas. A common error is to use $h = 15$ directly, leading to velocity values that are off by a factor of ~57.3. **[Lose 1–2 marks]**
> 2. **Applying central difference at boundaries:** The central difference formula $v_n = \omega (s_{n+1} - s_{n-1})/2h$ is **invalid** at $n = 0$ and $n = N$. Forgetting to switch to forward/backward differences leads to wrong values at endpoints. **[Lose 1 mark]**
> 3. **Mixing up units of lift:** If lift is given in mm, it must be converted to meters before computing $v$ (m/s) and $a$ (m/s²). KTU examiners explicitly check unit consistency. **[Lose 0.5 mark]**
> 4. **Ignoring dwells:** If the cam has a dwell period, the displacement is constant during dwell, and velocity and acceleration are both zero. Tabulating through dwell intervals wastes time and introduces errors. **[Lose 1 mark]**
> 5. **Not sketching the diagrams:** KTU ESE questions of 7 marks explicitly require displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams. Skipping them costs a full 2–3 marks even if the table is perfect.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tabulation Method**

- ✅ The **Tabulation Method** is a numerical technique that converts continuous cam-follower motion into discrete, sampled data points for easy computation of $s$, $v$, and $a$.
- ✅ The cam rotation is divided into **equal angular increments $h$** (typically 5°–30°), and follower displacement is computed at each point using the specified **motion law**.
- ✅ **Interior points** use the **central difference formulas**:
  - $v_n = \omega \cdot (s_{n+1} - s_{n-1}) / 2h$
  - $a_n = \omega^2 \cdot (s_{n+1} - 2s_n + s_{n-1}) / h^2$
- ✅ **Boundary points** require **forward or backward difference formulas** because the central difference formula is undefined at endpoints.
- ✅ The angular increment $h$ **must be in radians**, not degrees — convert using $h_{rad} = h_{deg} \times \pi/180$.
- ✅ The cam angular velocity $\omega$ is **assumed constant** in all standard KTU problems unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **Dwell periods** have constant $s$, zero $v$, and zero $a$ — they do not require tabulation.
- ✅ The final **velocity and acceleration diagrams** are **mandatory** for full marks in KTU ESE.
- ✅ The Tabulation Method is **most useful** when the motion law is complex (e.g., polynomial, cycloidal, or graphically defined) and analytical differentiation is impractical.
- ✅ **Engineering applications** include IC engine camshafts, textile machinery, automatic packaging machines, and cam-actuated robotic joints.
- ✅ **Symmetry check:** For SHM and cycloidal motion, the velocity diagram is symmetric about the midpoint, and the acceleration diagram passes through zero at the midpoint — this is a useful **self-validation step** during exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestion on Project Topics - Students should be given projects which may include development of computer codes of analytical methods, computer models and computer aided simulations, and development of functioning prototype of various mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET404 - MECHANICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics - Students should be given projects which may include development of computer codes of analytical methods, computer models and computer aided simulations, and development of functioning prototype of various mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for **PBMET404 – Mechanics of Machinery**, Module 4 explicitly mandates that students undertake **project-based learning** as a vehicle to consolidate the analytical, computational, and experimental competencies acquired across the course. The official KTU directive on **Suggestion on Project Topics** states that projects assigned to students **may include the following three broad categories**:

1. **Development of computer codes of analytical methods** — Translating the closed-form kinematic and dynamic equations of mechanisms (velocity, acceleration, force, torque) into executable numerical routines.
2. **Development of computer models and computer-aided simulations** — Constructing virtual representations of mechanisms using multi-body dynamics (MBD) or finite element (FE) software to study their behaviour under real-world operating conditions.
3. **Development of functioning prototypes of various mechanisms** — Designing, fabricating, assembling, and testing physical hardware models that demonstrate the principles studied in the theory modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Projects are **not optional in spirit**. The End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) viva and the internal project assessment both test whether the student can connect a theoretical kinematic/dynamic concept to a tangible artefact — be it a code, a simulation, or a physical prototype.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are learning to **cook a dish** (say, a *mechanism analysis* problem).

| Cooking Stage | Mechanical Engineering Equivalent | Project Category |
|---|---|---|
| Reading a recipe book | Studying the kinematic/dynamic theory in textbooks | Theoretical foundation |
| Computing the recipe mathematically (ratios, temperatures) | Deriving velocity/acceleration equations by hand | Category 1: Computer Code |
| Simulating the cooking process in a virtual kitchen before actually lighting the stove | Running the mechanism in **SolidWorks Motion**, **Adams**, or **MATLAB Simulink** | Category 2: Computer Model/Simulation |
| Finally cooking the dish in a real kitchen and tasting it | Building a physical prototype, testing it, and validating the theory | Category 3: Functioning Prototype |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The three project categories form a **progression of fidelity** — from pure math (code) → virtual representation (simulation) → physical reality (prototype). A complete project often combines **all three**: the code validates the simulation, and the simulation guides the prototype design.

## 1.3 Governing Metric — The Project Rubric (as per KTU 2024)

The standard KTU 2024 evaluation rubric typically allocates marks across the following dimensions:

- **Problem definition & literature survey** — **10%**
- **Analytical / mathematical formulation** — **20%**
- **Computational implementation / simulation** — **25%**
- **Prototype / hardware realisation (if applicable)** — **25%**
- **Results, validation & discussion** — **10%**
- **Report & viva-voce** — **10%**

Explicit physical/regulatory constants that often appear in project reports:

- Standard gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
- Standard atmospheric pressure: **$p_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Category 1 — Computer Codes of Analytical Methods

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

A **computer code of an analytical method** is a programmatic implementation of the *exact* mathematical equations governing a mechanism's kinematics or dynamics. Unlike a simulation (which integrates equations of motion numerically), an analytical code performs **closed-form computation** at specified instants or sweeps through a range of inputs.

### 2.1.2 Structured Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Identify the mechanism type:** Four-bar linkage, slider-crank, cam-follower, gear train, epicyclic gear, etc.
- **Step 2 — Write the loop-closure or constraint equation:** For example, for a four-bar linkage, the Freudenstein equation.
- **Step 3 — Differentiate the loop-closure equation** to obtain velocity and acceleration relations.
- **Step 4 — Choose a numerical solver** (Newton–Raphson for the position equation) and a step size $\Delta\theta$.
- **Step 5 — Tabulate and plot** angular positions, velocities, accelerations, and slider displacements versus the input crank angle.
- **Step 6 — Validate** by comparing against textbook graphical or analytical solutions (e.g., Klein's construction).

### 2.1.3 Why and How

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** Hand-plotting a velocity polygon at 36 input angles for a 4-bar linkage would consume an entire week. A code does it in **seconds** and improves the resolution of the kinematic analysis.
>
> **How?** By translating the analytical method (e.g., Freudenstein's equation) into an algorithmic loop with a chosen input range $[0, 2\pi]$.

## 2.2 Category 2 — Computer Models and Computer-Aided Simulations

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept

A **computer model** is a digital twin of the mechanism in which the geometry, materials, joints, and actuators are defined in a CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering) environment. **Computer-aided simulation** then exercises the model to predict motion, stresses, and energy consumption.

### 2.2.2 Structured Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — CAD modelling:** Build the link geometry in **SolidWorks / Autodesk Inventor / Fusion 360**.
- **Step 2 — Joint definition:** Apply revolute, prismatic, and gear/cam joints matching the kinematic diagram.
- **Step 3 — Apply drivers & loads:** Specify motor torque, external forces, gravity.
- **Step 4 — Mesh & material assignment (for FEA):** For stress analysis, mesh the links and assign material properties (e.g., structural steel, $E = 200 \, \text{GPa}$).
- **Step 5 — Solve & post-process:** Generate motion plots, stress contours, animation.
- **Step 6 — Validate** with analytical or experimental results.

### 2.2.3 Why and How

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Industries like **automotive (suspension design)**, **aerospace (landing-gear kinematics)**, and **robotics (path planning)** depend on MBD simulation to eliminate prototypes and compress design cycles. A well-built simulation can reveal issues like **link interference** and **joint lock-up** before fabrication.

## 2.3 Category 3 — Functioning Prototypes

### 2.3.1 Operational Concept

A **functioning prototype** is a physical, working hardware model of a mechanism that demonstrates a defined kinematic/dynamic behaviour. It may be fabricated using machined parts, **3D-printed** components, laser-cut plates, or purchased standard parts (bearings, fasteners, gears).

### 2.3.2 Structured Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Finalise the design from simulation results.**
- **Step 2 — Generate fabrication drawings** with tolerances.
- **Step 3 — Manufacture / procure components.**
- **Step 4 — Assemble and align** the mechanism on a base plate.
- **Step 5 — Instrument** with sensors (encoders, load cells, accelerometers) where possible.
- **Step 6 — Conduct tests** and record data.
- **Step 7 — Compare** experimental readings with the analytical and simulated values.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises the **core analytical equations** that students are most likely to encounter in a project, irrespective of which of the three categories they pursue.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Four-bar position (Freudenstein) | $K_1 \cos\theta_3 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_3)$ | $K_1, K_2, K_3$ — dimensionless link ratios | Position analysis of 4-bar |
| 2 | Slider-crank position | $x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ | $r$ — crank (m), $L$ — connecting rod (m) | IC engine piston |
| 3 | Velocity in slider-crank | $\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left(1 + \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right)$ | $\omega$ — rad/s | Reciprocating machines |
| 4 | Acceleration in slider-crank | $\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{L}\right]$ | $\omega$ — rad/s | Inertia force estimation |
| 5 | Velocity ratio (gear train) | $VR = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \dfrac{N_4}{N_3} \cdots$ | $N_i$ — number of teeth | Gear-box design |
| 6 | Epicyclic gear (tabular) | $\dfrac{n_{\text{arm}} - n_{\text{ring}}}{n_{\text{arm}} - n_{\text{sun}}} = -\dfrac{N_{\text{ring}}}{N_{\text{sun}}}$ | $n$ — rpm, $N$ — teeth | Planetary gearboxes |
| 7 | Cam displacement (SHM) | $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $h$ — lift (m), $\beta$ — dwell angle (rad) | Cam profile generation |
| 8 | Dynamic force in reciprocating mass | $F = m\,r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{L}\right]$ | $m$ — kg, $r$ — m | Engine balancing |
| 9 | Torque on flywheel (energy method) | $T = I\alpha = I \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | $I$ — kg·m², $\alpha$ — rad/s² | Flywheel sizing |
| 10 | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_S = \dfrac{N_{\max} - N_{\min}}{N_{\text{mean}}}$ | dimensionless | Flywheel selection |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorising the **slider-crank position, velocity, and acceleration trio** is non-negotiable. They are reused across kinematics, dynamics, and engine-balancing problems — and they form the spine of nearly every project that involves reciprocating motion.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Map

| Industry | Mechanism Studied | Project Type Most Relevant |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Engine, suspension, steering | Simulation + Prototype |
| Robotics | Robotic arm linkages, grippers | Code + Simulation |
| Manufacturing | Cam, gear, indexing mechanisms | Code + Prototype |
| Aerospace | Landing gear, flap actuators | Simulation (heavy) |
| Consumer products | Hinges, latches, folding mechanisms | Prototype |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation below is the **analytical backbone** of Category 1 projects. It produces the **position, velocity, and acceleration of the slider** in a slider-crank mechanism. A student project would code this derivation into MATLAB / Python / C++ and validate it against simulation (Category 2) or a physical rig (Category 3).

## 3.1 Geometry of the Slider-Crank Mechanism

Consider a slider-crank with:

- Crank length: $r$ (m)
- Connecting-rod length: $L$ (m)
- Crank angular position: $\theta$ (rad, measured from the line of stroke)
- Crank angular velocity: $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ (rad/s, constant for this derivation)
- Crank angular acceleration: $\alpha = \dot{\omega}$ (rad/s², taken as zero here for uniform drive)

The **displacement of the slider $P$ from the crank centre $O$** is given by the projection of the crank onto the stroke axis plus the projection of the connecting rod onto the same axis.

$$
x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}
$$

This is the **loop-closure equation** in scalar form for the slider-crank.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Position Equation

**Step 1:** Place $O$ at the origin. The crank end $A$ is at $(r\cos\theta, \, r\sin\theta)$.

**Step 2:** The slider $P$ lies on the $x$-axis at $(x, 0)$.

**Step 3:** The connecting rod $AP$ has length $L$:

$$
(x - r\cos\theta)^2 + (0 - r\sin\theta)^2 = L^2
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the squares:

$$
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta = L^2
$$

**Step 5:** Use the identity $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ to collapse the $r^2$ terms:

$$
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + r^2 = L^2
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into a standard quadratic in $x$:

$$
x^2 - 2xr\cos\theta + (r^2 - L^2) = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the quadratic formula:

$$
x = \frac{2r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{4r^2\cos^2\theta - 4(r^2 - L^2)}}{2}
$$

**Step 8:** Simplify:

$$
x = r\cos\theta \pm \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}
$$

**Step 9:** Choose the **positive root** (slider is in front of the crank for $\theta$ in the first quadrant of an outward stroke):

$$
\boxed{x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Velocity Equation

Differentiate the position equation with respect to time $t$, applying the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{d\theta} \cdot \omega$.

**Step 1:** Velocity of the slider:

$$
\dot{x} = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $x = r\cos\theta + (L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)^{1/2}$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta + \frac{1}{2}(L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta)^{-1/2} \cdot (-2r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta)
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor $-r\sin\theta$:

$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} = -r\sin\theta \left[ 1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **approximation** $L \gg r$ (long connecting rod, valid for most IC engines where $L/r \geq 3$). Under this approximation, $\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} \approx L$:

$$
\frac{dx}{d\theta} \approx -r\sin\theta \left( 1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $\omega$ to obtain the slider velocity:

$$
\boxed{\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left( 1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L} \right)}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Acceleration Equation

Differentiate the velocity equation with respect to time, this time noting that $\theta$, $\omega$, and the bracketed term are all functions of time.

**Step 1:** Write velocity as $\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left(1 + \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right)$.

**Step 2:** Apply the product rule to the three factors $-r\omega$, $\sin\theta$, and $\left(1 + \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right)$:

$$
\ddot{x} = -r\alpha \sin\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right) - r\omega^2\cos\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right) - r\omega\sin\theta \left(-\frac{r\omega\sin\theta}{L}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** For **uniform angular velocity**, $\alpha = 0$, so the first term vanishes. The third term simplifies to $+\dfrac{r^2\omega^2\sin^2\theta}{L}$:

$$
\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta \left(1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L}\right) + \frac{r^2\omega^2\sin^2\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the first term:

$$
\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2\cos^2\theta}{L} + \frac{r^2\omega^2\sin^2\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 5:** Group the last two terms and use the identity $\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -\cos 2\theta$:

$$
\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2}{L}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2\cos 2\theta}{L}
$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r\cos 2\theta}{L}\right]}
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

Let $r = 0.10 \, \text{m}$, $L = 0.30 \, \text{m}$, $\omega = 30\pi \, \text{rad/s}$ (i.e., 900 rpm), and $\theta = 30^\circ = \pi/6 \, \text{rad}$.

**Step 1 — Position:**

$$
x = (0.10)\cos 30^\circ + \sqrt{(0.30)^2 - (0.10)^2 \sin^2 30^\circ}
$$

$$
x = (0.10)(0.8660) + \sqrt{0.0900 - (0.0100)(0.2500)}
$$

$$
x = 0.0866 + \sqrt{0.0900 - 0.0025} = 0.0866 + \sqrt{0.0875}
$$

$$
x = 0.0866 + 0.2958 = 0.3824 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity:**

$$
\dot{x} = -(0.10)(30\pi)\sin 30^\circ \left(1 + \frac{0.10\cos 30^\circ}{0.30}\right)
$$

$$
\dot{x} = -(0.10)(94.248)(0.5000)\left(1 + \frac{0.0866}{0.30}\right)
$$

$$
\dot{x} = -4.7124 \times (1 + 0.2887) = -4.7124 \times 1.2887 = -6.073 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration:**

$$
\ddot{x} = -(0.10)(30\pi)^2\left[\cos 30^\circ + \frac{0.10\cos 60^\circ}{0.30}\right]
$$

$$
\ddot{x} = -(0.10)(8882.6)\left[0.8660 + \frac{0.10 \times 0.5000}{0.30}\right]
$$

$$
\ddot{x} = -888.26 \times [0.8660 + 0.1667] = -888.26 \times 1.0327 = -917.36 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for project work:** The **negative** sign on $\dot{x}$ indicates the slider is moving *towards* the crank centre (i.e., on the inward stroke for this instant). The **negative** $\ddot{x}$ indicates the slider is *decelerating*. A code that produces these signs correctly is a strong indicator of correct loop-closure handling.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Derivation to the Three Project Categories

| Stage of Analysis | What is Computed | Category 1 (Code) Implementation | Category 2 (Simulation) Verification | Category 3 (Prototype) Measurement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Position $x$ | Slider displacement at angle $\theta$ | Loop with $\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$ | Animation track in MBD solver | Linear encoder / LVDT reading |
| Velocity $\dot{x}$ | Rate of change of $x$ | Analytical derivative or numerical diff | Plot from MBD post-processor | Tachometer / high-speed camera |
| Acceleration $\ddot{x}$ | Second derivative | Closed-form expression above | Acceleration plot from solver | Accelerometer mounted on slider |
| Dynamic Force $F = m\ddot{x}$ | Inertia force on slider | $F$ computed and printed | Reaction force probe in MBD | Load cell on slider rod |

```

```

## 4.3 Validation Triangle — The Cross-Check Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VA["Analytical Code Result"] -->|compare| VB["Simulation Result"]
    VB -->|compare| VC["Experimental Prototype Result"]
    VC -->|compare| VA
    VB -->|if mismatch| DX["Diagnose: Joint definition or Constraint or Material"]
    DX --> VB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents a *comparison*. If the three sources disagree, the project must diagnose where the discrepancy arises — typically a wrong joint definition, a non-linear material, or a missed inertia term.

## 4.4 Kinematic Block Diagram of a Slider-Crank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Crank Pivot O"] -->|rigid link r| A["Crank Pin A"]
    A -->|rigid link L| P["Slider Piston P"]
    A -->|angular velocity omega| M["Input Motor"]
    P -->|translates along x axis| G["Guide / Cylinder"]

```
