# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** When answering project-related questions, students **routinely lose marks** for the following reasons:
> 1. **No clear problem statement** — a project without a "what problem am I solving?" loses marks on problem-definition.
> 2. **Confusing code with simulation** — writing a MATLAB script and calling it a "simulation" is wrong. A *code* solves analytical equations; a *simulation* uses a CAE solver.
> 3. **Skipping validation** — presenting a code result without comparing it to a textbook or simulation result is treated as incomplete work.
> 4. **Units & sign errors** in slider-crank and cam derivations are the most common silent killers of a 14-mark answer.
> 5. **No limitations / future scope** section in the project report deducts 5–10% from the final mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the development of a computer code of an analytical method and a computer-aided simulation in the context of a mechanics-of-machinery project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **computer code of an analytical method** implements *closed-form kinematic or dynamic equations* (e.g., the Freudenstein equation, the slider-crank acceleration expression) in a high-level language or numerical computing environment. It returns **deterministic numerical results** at the points the user requests and requires the user to *supply* the governing equations.

A **computer-aided simulation**, in contrast, relies on a **multi-body dynamics (MBD) or CAE solver** (e.g., Adams, SolidWorks Motion, ANSYS) in which the user supplies the **geometry, joints, materials, and loading**, and the solver **automatically assembles and integrates the equations of motion** numerically over time.

Key contrasts:

| Aspect | Computer Code | CAE Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| Source of equations | User-written (analytical) | Built-in solver (numerical) |
| Input | Number, units, equations | Geometry, joints, materials |
| Time to set up | Short (minutes) | Long (hours to days) |
| Validation level | Against textbook / hand-calc | Against code / experiment |

*(Award full 3 marks if all four contrasts are given and at least one example is correctly cited.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three categories of project topics recommended by KTU for Module 4 of Mechanics of Machinery. For each, give one example topic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three recommended categories are:

1. **Development of computer codes of analytical methods** — *Example:* MATLAB code for the kinematic and dynamic analysis of a slider-crank mechanism.
2. **Development of computer models and computer-aided simulations** — *Example:* Multi-body simulation of a four-bar linkage in SolidWorks Motion or Adams.
3. **Development of functioning prototypes of various mechanisms** — *Example:* Fabrication of a crank-rocker demonstrator rig and validation against analytical results.

*(1 mark per correctly-named category with example. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**As part of your Module-4 project, you are required to perform a complete kinematic and dynamic analysis of a slider-crank mechanism using an analytical computer code and to validate the same against a computer-aided simulation.**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Develop the analytical expressions for the position, velocity, and acceleration of the slider for a slider-crank with crank radius $r$, connecting-rod length $L$, and constant crank angular velocity $\omega$. Clearly state all assumptions.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Describe a step-by-step validation strategy that compares the analytical code output with a computer-aided simulation (e.g., Adams or SolidWorks Motion) and explain the criteria for accepting the validation.

---

**Model Solution (Part a) — 7 marks**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
- The crank rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ (so $\alpha = 0$).
- The connecting rod is rigid and massless.
- The slider moves along a frictionless, horizontal, straight guide.
- The mechanism operates in a single plane.

*Position equation (2 marks):*

Using the loop-closure relation, the slider position $x$ measured from the crank centre $O$ along the line of stroke is:

$$
x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}
$$

*Velocity equation (2 marks):*

Differentiating with respect to time and using the long-rod approximation $L \gg r$:

$$
\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta \left( 1 + \frac{r\cos\theta}{L} \right)
$$

*Acceleration equation (2 marks):*

Differentiating the velocity and simplifying:

$$
\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r\cos 2\theta}{L} \right]
$$

The inertia force on a reciprocating mass $m$ at the slider is $F = m\ddot{x}$.

---

**Model Solution (Part b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Geometry and inputs (1 mark):* Build the same slider-crank geometry ($r$, $L$, slider position) in the CAE tool and apply the same crank angular velocity $\omega$ as in the code.

*Step 2 — Joint definition (1 mark):* Apply a revolute joint at the crank pivot, a revolute joint at the crank-pin, and a prismatic joint at the slider-guide. Match the centre of mass and mass $m$ of the slider.

*Step 3 — Solve the simulation (1 mark):* Run the simulation for one full revolution ($0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$) and export the slider position, velocity, and acceleration versus the crank angle.

*Step 4 — Extract code output at the same crank-angle sampling (1 mark):* Ensure both the code and the simulation export data at *identical* crank angles (e.g., every $5^\circ$).

*Step 5 — Compute the percentage error (1 mark):*

$$
\% \text{Error at } \theta_i = \frac{\vert \ddot{x}_{\text{code}}(\theta_i) - \ddot{x}_{\text{sim}}(\theta_i) \vert}{\vert \ddot{x}_{\text{sim}}(\theta_i) \vert} \times 100
$$

*Step 6 — Acceptance criteria (2 marks):* The validation is **accepted** if (i) the maximum absolute percentage error in $x$, $\dot{x}$, and $\ddot{x}$ is **below 2%** across the full revolution, and (ii) the phase and sign of all three quantities match at every sampled point. If either criterion fails, the model must be re-checked for joint definition, mass, or solver settings.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]`

**You are assigned a project to develop a *functioning physical prototype* of a four-bar crank-rocker mechanism and to validate its motion experimentally.**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Describe, in detail, the procedure to design, fabricate, and assemble the prototype. Include the selection of materials, the method of joint realisation, and the instrumentation for measurement.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Explain how the experimental measurements of the coupler-point trajectory are validated against the analytical position equation (Freudenstein) and against a CAE simulation.

---

**Model Solution (Part a) — 7 marks**

*Design stage (2 marks):*
- Choose the four link lengths to satisfy the **Grashof condition** $s + l \leq p + q$ (where $s$ is the shortest, $l$ the longest) to ensure a crank-rocker.
- A typical set: crank $a = 50 \, \text{mm}$, coupler $b = 120 \, \text{mm}$, follower $c = 80 \, \text{mm}$, fixed link $d = 100 \, \text{mm}$. Verify $50 + 120 = 170 \leq 80 + 100 = 180$ → satisfies the Grashof inequality.
- Compute the range of the output rocker angle using the Freudenstein equation.

*Material and joint selection (2 marks):*
- Links: **mild steel (AISI 1020)** or **aluminium 6061-T6** for light weight; manufactured by CNC milling or laser cutting.
- Joints: **Rolling-element bearings** (e.g., 8 mm ID deep-groove ball bearings) mounted in housings on each link, fixed to a base plate (12 mm acrylic or aluminium).
- Fasteners: M6 cap screws, locking nuts.

*Drive and instrumentation (3 marks):*
- Drive: a **24 V DC gear motor** with an encoder (e.g., 500 pulses/revolution) coupled to the crank via a flexible coupling. Control speed through a PWM driver.
- Instrumentation:
  - **Rotary encoder** on the crank to record $\theta_2$.
  - **Tilt sensor / digital protractor** on the rocker to record $\theta_4$.
  - **High-speed camera or webcam** with image-processing to capture the coupler-point trajectory (optional).
- Data acquisition via Arduino Uno / ESP32, with a sampling rate of 100 Hz.

*Fabrication and assembly (read from design + material sections; full marks already given above).*

---

**Model Solution (Part b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Set up the experiment (1 mark):* Mount the prototype on a vibration-isolated table. Switch on the drive motor at a low, constant speed. Verify all joints move freely without binding.

*Step 2 — Calibrate sensors (1 mark):* Calibrate the encoder zero with the crank at the inner dead-centre, and the protractor zero with the rocker at its mid-position.

*Step 3 — Record data (1 mark):* Sweep the crank over a full revolution at increments of $10^\circ$ (36 readings). Record $\theta_2$ and the corresponding $\theta_4$ at each step.

*Step 4 — Analytical reference (2 marks):* For each $\theta_2$, solve the Freudenstein equation for $\theta_4$:

$$
K_1 \cos\theta_4 - K_2 \cos\theta_2 + K_3 = \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)
$$

where

$$
K_1 = \frac{d}{a}, \quad K_2 = \frac{d}{c}, \quad K_3 = \frac{a^2 - b^2 + c^2 + d^2}{2ac}
$$

Use a numerical root-finder (e.g., Newton–Raphson) to obtain $\theta_4^{\text{analytical}}$.

*Step 5 — Simulation reference (1 mark):* In SolidWorks Motion or Adams, build the same link lengths, apply the same crank rotation, and export $\theta_4^{\text{sim}}$.

*Step 6 — Compare and accept (1 mark):* Compute the error

$$
\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert \theta_4^{\text{exp}} - \theta_4^{\text{analytical}} \vert}{\vert \theta_4^{\text{analytical}} \vert} \times 100
$$

If the error is **below 5%** for every sample (allowing for manufacturing tolerances and joint clearances), the prototype is considered validated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the viva!)**

- **Three project categories** are sanctioned by KTU: **Code, Simulation, Prototype**. A strong project combines at least two.
- **Analytical codes** solve *closed-form* equations (Freudenstein, slider-crank, cam SHM). **Simulations** solve *numerical* equations of motion in a CAE solver. Do not confuse them.
- The **slider-crank position, velocity, and acceleration** trio is the most-cited derivation across project work. Memorise all three.
- The **Freudenstein equation** is the standard tool for four-bar position analysis in any code-based project.
- The **Grashof condition** $s + l \leq p + q$ decides whether a four-bar is a crank-rocker, double-crank, or double-rocker — this is the *first check* in any four-bar project.
- A **validation triangle** (Code ↔ Simulation ↔ Experiment) is mandatory. Without it, the project loses up to 25% of marks.
- **Sign convention** in slider-crank: the positive $x$ direction is conventionally taken as the *outward* stroke; velocity is negative on the inward stroke.
- **Long-rod approximation** ($L \gg r$) simplifies $\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$ to $L$ — but you must *state the assumption*.
- **Project report must contain**: problem statement, literature survey, methodology, results, validation, limitations, and future scope.
- **Viva questions** often probe: "Why did you choose this category?", "What error did you get between code and simulation?", "What would you do differently if given more time?" — prepare crisp answers.
- **Standard values to remember**: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $E_{\text{steel}} = 200 \, \text{GPa}$, $E_{\text{Al}} = 70 \, \text{GPa}$.
- **Acceptance criteria for validation** (typical KTU benchmark): code-vs-simulation error < 2%; experiment-vs-code error < 5% (the looser bound accounts for manufacturing tolerances).
